# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Hydraulic Cylinder Force and Velocity from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the derivation most commonly asked for 7 marks in KTU University Exams.** Walk through every step explicitly.

### Problem Statement
A single-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter $D = 50$ mm and is supplied with oil at a gauge pressure $P = 100$ bar. The pump delivers flow rate $Q = 10$ L/min. Calculate:
- (a) The maximum linear force the cylinder can produce.
- (b) The extension velocity of the piston.
- (c) The hydraulic power delivered to the cylinder.

### Given
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 10^7$ Pa
- $Q = 10$ L/min $= \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s $= 1.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

### Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Piston

The piston is circular, so its area is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}
$$

Substituting $D = 0.05$ m:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Conversion logic:** Diameter is converted from mm to m by multiplying by $10^{-3}$, then squared (giving $10^{-6}$ factor) and divided by 4 — yielding an area of order $10^{-3}$ m², which is sensible for a cylinder of this size.

### Step 2 — Linear Force from Pascal's Law

Pascal's Law states that pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and the walls of the container. Therefore, the force on the piston face equals pressure times area:

$$
F = P \cdot A
$$

Substituting:

$$
F = 10^7 \text{ Pa} \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F = 19{,}635 \text{ N} \approx 19.63 \text{ kN}
$$

**Interpretation:** A small piston (5 cm diameter) at 100 bar produces nearly 2 tonnes of force — this is why hydraulics dominate heavy-duty automotive applications.

### Step 3 — Piston Velocity from Continuity

The volumetric flow rate equals the rate of change of cylinder volume:

$$
Q = A \cdot v \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

Substituting:

$$
v = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
v = 0.0849 \text{ m/s} \approx 84.9 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The ratio of $10^{-4}$ (flow) over $10^{-3}$ (area) gives a velocity of order $10^{-1}$ m/s, which is realistic for a hydraulic actuator of this size.

### Step 4 — Hydraulic Power

Hydraulic power is the product of pressure drop and volumetric flow rate:

$$
P_{\text{hyd}} = P \cdot Q
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{\text{hyd}} = 10^7 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-4} = 1666.7 \text{ W} \approx 1.67 \text{ kW}
$$

### Verification via Universal Power Equation

Using $P = F \cdot v$:

$$
P = 19{,}635 \text{ N} \times 0.0849 \text{ m/s} = 1666.97 \text{ W} \approx 1.67 \text{ kW} \; \checkmark
$$

Both methods agree — the derivation is dimensionally and numerically consistent.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: DC Motor Torque-Speed Characteristics

### Problem Statement
A 12 V automotive DC motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ and back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad. When the motor draws a current of $I_a = 10$ A, find:
- (a) The angular speed $\omega$.
- (b) The torque developed $T$.
- (c) The mechanical power output $P_{\text{out}}$.
- (d) The no-load speed $\omega_0$.
- (e) The stall torque $T_{\text{stall}}$.

### Given
- $V = 12$ V
- $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$
- $K_e = K_t = 0.05$ (in SI, $K_e$ in V·s/rad and $K_t$ in N·m/A have equal magnitude)
- $I_a = 10$ A

### Step 1 — Back-EMF at Operating Point

From the voltage equation:

$$
V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a
$$

Substituting:

$$
E_b = 12 - (10)(0.5) = 12 - 5 = 7 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 2 — Angular Speed

From $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$:

$$
\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_e} = \frac{7}{0.05} = 140 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Convert to RPM:

$$
N = \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 140 \times 9.5493 = 1336.9 \text{ RPM}
$$

### Step 3 — Developed Torque

From $T = K_t \cdot I_a$:

$$
T = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 4 — Mechanical Power Output

$$
P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega = 0.5 \times 140 = 70 \text{ W}
$$

Alternatively, $P_{\text{out}} = E_b \cdot I_a = 7 \times 10 = 70$ W ✓

### Step 5 — No-Load Speed

At no load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V$:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e} = \frac{12}{0.05} = 240 \text{ rad/s} \; (\approx 2292 \text{ RPM})
$$

### Step 6 — Stall Torque

At stall, $\omega = 0$, so $E_b = 0$:

$$
I_{\text{stall}} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{12}{0.5} = 24 \text{ A}
$$

$$
T_{\text{stall}} = K_t \cdot I_{\text{stall}} = 0.05 \times 24 = 1.2 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Analytical Result

The motor's torque-speed line is **linear** (since the only loss is ohmic):

$$
T(\omega) = T_{\text{stall}} - \frac{T_{\text{stall}}}{\omega_0} \cdot \omega
$$

$$
T(\omega) = 1.2 - 0.005\,\omega \quad \text{[N·m]}
$$

This line passes through $(\omega_0, 0) = (240, 0)$ and $(0, T_{\text{stall}}) = (0, 1.2)$. Maximum power transfer occurs at $\omega = \omega_0 / 2$:

$$
P_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_{\text{stall}} \cdot \omega_0}{4} = \frac{1.2 \times 240}{4} = 72 \text{ W}
$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Solenoid Force on Plunger

### Problem Statement
A fuel injector solenoid produces a flux density $B = 1.2$ T across a plunger face of area $A = 50$ mm². Calculate the magnetic pulling force on the plunger.

### Given
- $B = 1.2$ T
- $A = 50$ mm² $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m² $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

### Step 1 — Maxwell Stress Equation

The magnetic pressure (force per unit area) on a ferromagnetic surface in a uniform field is derived from energy density considerations. The energy density stored in a magnetic field is:

$$
u_B = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}
$$

This energy density equals the magnetic pressure pulling the plunger into the field:

$$
p_{\text{mag}} = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}
$$

### Step 2 — Force Calculation

$$
F = p_{\text{mag}} \cdot A = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2\mu_0}
$$

Substituting:

$$
F = \frac{(1.2)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
F = \frac{1.44 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
F = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{-5}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} = 28.65 \text{ N}
$$

### Interpretation
A 50 mm² plunger at 1.2 T produces approximately **28.6 N** — enough to lift a fuel injector needle against a high fuel rail pressure. The force scales with the **square** of flux density, which is why solenoid designers obsess over minimizing air gaps to maximize $B$.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Gear Train Speed-Torque Transformation

### Problem Statement
A 3-stage gear train has the following gear pairs: (1) 20-tooth driver, 80-tooth driven; (2) 25-tooth driver, 75-tooth driven; (3) 30-tooth driver, 90-tooth driven. The input shaft rotates at 3000 RPM with a torque of 5 N·m. Find the output speed, output torque, and overall efficiency assuming 95% efficiency per stage.

### Step 1 — Individual Stage Ratios

$$
R_1 = \frac{Z_{\text{driven},1}}{Z_{\text{driver},1}} = \frac{80}{20} = 4
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{Z_{\text{driven},2}}{Z_{\text{driver},2}} = \frac{75}{25} = 3
$$

$$
R_3 = \frac{Z_{\text{driven},3}}{Z_{\text{driver},3}} = \frac{90}{30} = 3
$$

### Step 2 — Overall Gear Ratio

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 4 \times 3 \times 3 = 36
$$

### Step 3 — Output Speed

$$
N_{\text{out}} = \frac{N_{\text{in}}}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{3000}{36} = 83.33 \text{ RPM}
$$

### Step 4 — Output Torque (Ideal, 100% Efficient)

$$
T_{\text{out,ideal}} = T_{\text{in}} \times R_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 36 = 180 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 5 — Actual Output Torque (With Losses)

Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\text{total}} = 0.95^3 = 0.857
$$

$$
T_{\text{out,actual}} = T_{\text{out,ideal}} \times \eta_{\text{total}} = 180 \times 0.857 = 154.3 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Interpretation
The gear train **reduces** speed by a factor of 36 (from 3000 to 83.3 RPM) and **multiplies** torque by the same factor (with efficiency losses). This is exactly the principle behind automotive transmissions and final-drive differentials.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Pneumatic Cylinder Force (Air Brake Actuator)

### Problem Statement
A truck air brake actuator has a piston diameter of 120 mm and is supplied with air at 8 bar gauge pressure. Find the maximum clamping force generated on the brake pad.

### Given
- $D = 120$ mm $= 0.12$ m
- $P = 8$ bar $= 8 \times 10^5$ Pa

### Step 1 — Piston Area

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.12^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0144}{4} = 0.01131 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Actuator Force

$$
F = P \cdot A = 8 \times 10^5 \times 0.01131 = 9047.8 \text{ N} \approx 9.05 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 3 — Comparison with Hydraulic
At the same piston size and the same 8 bar pressure, pneumatic force equals hydraulic force (the formula is identical). The reason hydraulics are preferred in cars is that cars use ~100 bar brake pressure, not 8 bar. Trucks use pneumatics because compressed air is safer (no flammable oil leaks) and air tanks can be re-pressurized by the engine-driven compressor.

---

## 3.6 MASTER ANALYTICAL MATRIX — "Which Actuator Do I Choose?"

| Application | Force Needed | Speed Needed | Precision Needed | **Best Actuator** | Why? |
|-------------|--------------|--------------|------------------|-------------------|------|
| Engine valve actuation | Moderate | Very high | High timing accuracy | **Mechanical (cam)** | Synchronized to crank, ultra-reliable |
| Window lift | Low | Moderate | Position | **DC motor + lead screw** | Precise position control |
| Throttle body | Low | Moderate | Precise angle | **DC motor or stepper** | Closed-loop PID control |
| Disc brake caliper | **Very high** | Fast | Moderate | **Hydraulic cylinder** | Force density unbeatable |
| ABS modulator valve | Moderate | Very fast | High-speed on/off | **Solenoid** | Millisecond response |
| Truck air brake | Very high | Moderate | Moderate | **Pneumatic cylinder** | Air is safe, abundant, regenerative |
| HVAC blend door | Very low | Slow | Angular position | **Small DC/stepper motor** | Low power, position control |
| Power steering assist | Very high | Moderate | Force feedback | **Hydraulic motor/cylinder** | High force at low speed |
| Starter pinion engagement | Moderate | Fast (one-shot) | On/off | **Solenoid** | Single linear push action |
| Active suspension strut | High | Fast | Continuous | **Hydraulic cylinder + servo valve** | High force + bandwidth |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Actuator Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ACTUATORS] --> B[MECHANICAL]
    A --> C[ELECTRICAL]
    A --> D[HYDRAULIC]
    A --> E[PNEUMATIC]

    B --> B1[Cams and Followers]
    B --> B2[Gear Trains]
    B --> B3[Linkages and Levers]
    B --> B4[Springs]
    B --> B5[Brakes and Clutches]

    C --> C1[DC Motor]
    C --> C2[Stepper Motor]
    C --> C3[Solenoid]
    C --> C4[Relay]

    D --> D1[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    D --> D2[Hydraulic Motor]
    D --> D3[Directional Control Valve]
    D --> D4[Pressure Relief Valve]

    E --> E1[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    E --> E2[Pneumatic Motor]
    E --> E3[Compressor]
    E --> E4[Air Reservoir and FRL Unit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The root node `ACTUATORS` branches into the four KTU-mandated families. Each family then expands into its sub-types. This hierarchical structure directly maps to the KTU 2024 syllabus Module 1 topic heading.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Automotive Actuator Control (Electronic Throttle Control as Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ECU1[ECU - Desired Throttle Angle] --> COMP[Comparator]
    TPS1[Throttle Position Sensor TPS] --> COMP
    COMP --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID --> DRIVER[Motor Driver H-Bridge]
    DRIVER --> DCMOTOR[DC Motor in Throttle Body]
    DCMOTOR --> THROTTLE[Throttle Plate Rotation]
    THROTTLE --> INTAKE[Engine Air Intake]
    THROTTLE -.->|Mechanical Feedback| TPS1
    TPS1 -.->|Electrical Signal| ECU1
    INTAKE --> ENGINE[Engine Torque Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the canonical closed-loop actuator control system. The driver presses the accelerator pedal, the ECU calculates the desired throttle angle, the DC motor rotates the throttle plate, and the TPS sensor feeds the actual angle back to the ECU — closing the loop. Without the **actuator (DC motor)**, the loop is broken and the system cannot function.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Hydraulic Brake Actuation Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    PEDAL[Brake Pedal] --> MC[Master Cylinder]
    MC --> PROPORTION[Proportioning Valve]
    PROPORTION --> FL[Front Left Wheel Cylinder]
    PROPORTION --> FR[Front Right Wheel Cylinder]
    PROPORTION --> RL[Rear Left Wheel Cylinder]
    PROPORTION --> RR[Rear Right Wheel Cylinder]
    FL --> DISC1[Front Disc Brake]
    FR --> DISC2[Front Disc Brake]
    RL --> DRUM1[Rear Drum Brake]
    RR --> DRUM2[Rear Drum Brake]
    ABS[ABS Hydraulic Modulator] -.->|Pressure Modulation| FL
    ABS -.->|Pressure Modulation| FR
    ABS -.->|Pressure Modulation| RL
    ABS -.->|Pressure Modulation| RR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Engineering Insight:** The master cylinder is the **hydraulic actuator** that converts the mechanical pedal force into hydraulic pressure. The proportioning valve is a **mechanical (passive) actuator**. The ABS modulator is an **electro-hydraulic actuator** containing both solenoid valves and an electric pump motor — all three actuator families in one assembly.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Pneumatic Air Brake System (Heavy Vehicle)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ENG[Engine] --> COMP[Air Compressor]
    COMP --> DRYER[Air Dryer]
    DRYER --> TANK1[Primary Reservoir]
    DRYER --> TANK2[Secondary Reservoir]
    TANK1 --> GOVERNOR[Governor Unloader]
    TANK2 --> GOVERNOR
    TANK1 --> FOOT[Brake Foot Valve]
    TANK2 --> FOOT
    FOOT --> RCHAMBER[Rear Chamber Actuator]
    FOOT --> FCHAMBER[Front Chamber Actuator]
    RCHAMBER --> RSTOP[Rear Wheel Brake]
    FCHAMBER --> FSTOP[Front Wheel Brake]
    SPRING[Spring Brake Actuator] -.->|Parking/Emergency| RSTOP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The spring brake actuator is a **fail-safe mechanical actuator** — a large coil spring is held compressed by air pressure during normal operation. If air pressure is lost (engine off or leak), the spring releases and applies the brakes. This is why trucks with no air pressure cannot be moved — the parking brake is always engaged by default.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Stepper Motor Microstepping Actuator Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    MCU[Microcontroller ECU] --> DIR[Direction Signal]
    MCU --> STEP[Step Pulse Signal]
    MCU --> ENABLE[Enable Signal]
    STEP --> DRVIC[Stepper Driver IC]
    DIR --> DRVIC
    ENABLE --> DRVIC
    DRVIC --> COILA[Coil A H-Bridge]
    DRVIC --> COILB[Coil B H-Bridge]
    COILA --> STEPPER[Stepper Motor]
    COILB --> STEPPER
    STEPPER --> GEAR[Reduction Gearbox]
    GEAR --> OUTPUT[Output Shaft Throttle Valve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each step pulse from the MCU advances the motor by exactly one step angle (e.g., 1.8°). The driver IC handles the current chopping and sequencing. The reduction gearbox multiplies torque and reduces speed, giving the throttle plate smooth, precise angular positioning.

## 4.6 Block Diagram: Solenoid Valve Operation (Fuel Injector)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ECU[ECU Injector Pulse] --> DRIVER[Injector Driver]
    DRIVER --> COIL[Solenoid Coil]
    COIL -->|Magnetic Field| PLUNGER[Armature Plunger]
    PLUNGER -->|Linear Pull| NEEDLE[Valve Needle Lifts]
    NEEDLE --> SPRAY[Atomized Fuel Spray]
    SPRAY --> CYL[Engine Cylinder]
    RAIL[High Pressure Fuel Rail] --> NEEDLE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Insight:** A fuel injector is one of the highest-speed actuators in a modern engine — it may fire 8 times per combustion cycle at 6000 RPM, meaning the solenoid must open and close the needle in **sub-millisecond** times. The ECU precisely controls the **pulse width** (typically 1–10 ms) to meter fuel quantity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define an actuator. List any four types of actuators used in automotive mechatronics with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks):**
> An **actuator** is a device that converts a control signal — typically electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy — into a physical output such as motion, force, or torque. It is the final control element in any mechatronic system, executing the commands issued by the controller (ECU). Without actuators, the controller has no way to influence the physical world.

**Four Types with Examples (1 mark):**

| # | Type | Automotive Example |
|---|------|---------------------|
| 1 | Mechanical | Engine camshaft operating intake valve |
| 2 | Electrical | DC motor driving the windshield wiper |
| 3 | Hydraulic | Master cylinder in disc brake system |
| 4 | Pneumatic | Air brake actuator in a heavy truck |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Compare hydraulic and pneumatic actuators based on (i) working medium, (ii) force capability, and (iii) response time.*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Hydraulic Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **(i) Working medium** | Incompressible oil (mineral-based) at ~100–200 bar | Compressible air at ~6–10 bar |
| **(ii) Force capability** | Very high force density; small cylinder produces large force | Moderate force; requires larger piston for same force |
| **(iii) Response time** | Slower (10–100 ms) due to oil inertia and valve lag | Faster (5–50 ms) due to low air density, but compliance reduces precision |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** A common student mistake is to say "pneumatic is faster AND more precise." This is **wrong** — pneumatic is faster but **less** precise because compressed air is springy. Hydraulic is slower but stiffer and more precise. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of any **two** types of mechanical actuators used in automobiles. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of **80 mm** and is supplied with oil at a pressure of **120 bar**. The pump flow rate is **15 L/min**. Calculate:
1. The linear force produced by the cylinder.
2. The extension velocity of the piston.
3. The hydraulic power delivered. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to (a)

**Mechanical Actuator 1: Camshaft and Follower (3.5 marks)**

A cam is a rotating disc with an irregular (non-circular) profile. A follower rests on the cam profile. As the cam rotates, the profile pushes the follower up (lift) and lets it drop (return), converting continuous rotary motion of the camshaft into oscillating linear motion of the follower.

$$
\text{Follower Lift} = h = R_{\max} - R_{\text{base circle}}
$$

The valve event timing (when the valve opens, how long it stays open, and how quickly it opens) is entirely defined by the cam profile shape. In modern engines, the cam profile is a complex polynomial called the **polydyne cam**, designed to optimize acceleration, jerk, and valve timing for smooth engine operation.

**Automotive Use:** Intake and exhaust camshafts in 4-stroke engines.

**Mechanical Actuator 2: Brake Pedal Lever and Linkage (3.5 marks)**

The brake pedal is a Class 1 lever pivoted at the firewall. The driver applies a small force $F_{\text{foot}}$ at a long moment arm $L_{\text{foot}}$ from the pivot, and the master cylinder pushrod receives a much larger force $F_{\text{rod}}$ at a short moment arm $L_{\text{rod}}$.

$$
F_{\text{foot}} \cdot L_{\text{foot}} = F_{\text{rod}} \cdot L_{\text{rod}}
$$

$$
F_{\text{rod}} = F_{\text{foot}} \cdot \frac{L_{\text{foot}}}{L_{\text{rod}}}
$$

If the lever ratio is **5:1**, a 200 N foot force produces 1000 N on the master cylinder pushrod.

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- $P = 120$ bar $= 120 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 1.2 \times 10^7$ Pa
- $Q = 15$ L/min $= \dfrac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Piston Area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Linear Force**

$$
F = P \cdot A = 1.2 \times 10^7 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F = 60{,}318 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Piston Velocity**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0497 \text{ m/s} \approx 49.7 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic Power**

$$
P_{\text{hyd}} = P \cdot Q = 1.2 \times 10^7 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 3000 \text{ W} = 3 \text{ kW}
$$

**Verification via $F \cdot v$:**

$$
P = 60{,}318 \times 0.0497 = 2997.8 \text{ W} \approx 3 \text{ kW} \;\checkmark
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $F = 60.32$ kN
2. $v = 49.7$ mm/s
3. $P_{\text{hyd}} = 3$ kW

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an **electronic throttle control system** using a DC motor actuator. Discuss the role of the TPS sensor in the closed loop. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **pneumatic brake actuator** in a truck has a piston diameter of **150 mm** and operates at an air pressure of **8 bar**. The brake shoe coefficient of friction is **$\mu = 0.4$** and the effective brake drum radius is **200 mm**. Calculate:
1. The actuator clamping force.
2. The braking torque developed at the wheel.
3. The mechanical braking power if the wheel rotates at **500 RPM**. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to (a)

**Electronic Throttle Control (ETC) — Block Diagram Description (4 marks):**

The Electronic Throttle Control (also called "drive-by-wire") replaces the traditional mechanical cable between the accelerator pedal and the throttle body. The system consists of:

1. **Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APPS)** — Two potentiometers (for redundancy) that convert pedal position into a voltage signal.
2. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** — Receives APPS signal, applies pedal map and traction-control corrections, and outputs a desired throttle angle.
3. **DC Motor Actuator** — A 12 V brushed DC motor geared down to drive the throttle plate shaft. Typical motors deliver 0.5–2 N·m of torque.
4. **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** — Two redundant potentiometers mounted on the throttle shaft that report the actual throttle plate angle.
5. **Return Spring** — A fail-safe spring that closes the throttle to idle if power is lost.

**Closed-Loop Operation (3 marks):**

The ECU implements a PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) control loop. The **comparator** inside the ECU subtracts the desired angle (setpoint) from the actual TPS reading (feedback) to produce an **error signal**. The PID controller converts this error into a Pulse-Width-Modulated (PWM) signal that drives the H-bridge motor driver. The H-bridge applies forward or reverse current to the DC motor, which rotates the throttle plate. As the plate rotates, the TPS reading changes, reducing the error.

When error = 0, the throttle is at the commanded angle. The loop typically runs at **100 Hz to 1 kHz**, providing smooth, precise throttle response.

**Why Closed-Loop?**
- Compensates for spring non-linearities, motor dead-zone, and friction.
- Enables traction control, cruise control, and stability control integration.
- Detects faults (e.g., stuck throttle) by monitoring TPS-APPS correlation.

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
- $P = 8$ bar $= 8 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\mu = 0.4$
- $r_{\text{eff}} = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m
- $N = 500$ RPM

**Step 1 — Actuator Clamping Force**

Piston area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2
$$

Clamping force on brake shoe:

$$
F = P \cdot A = 8 \times 10^5 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2} = 14{,}137 \text{ N} \approx 14.14 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Braking Torque**

$$
T_{\text{brake}} = \mu \cdot F \cdot r_{\text{eff}} = 0.4 \times 14{,}137 \times 0.2
$$

$$
T_{\text{brake}} = 1130.97 \text{ N·m} \approx 1.13 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical Braking Power**

Convert RPM to rad/s:

$$
\omega = 2\pi \cdot \frac{N}{60} = 2\pi \times \frac{500}{60} = 52.36 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Braking power:

$$
P_{\text{brake}} = T_{\text{brake}} \cdot \omega = 1130.97 \times 52.36 = 59{,}217 \text{ W} \approx 59.22 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $F = 14.14$ kN
2. $T_{\text{brake}} = 1130.97$ N·m
3. $P_{\text{brake}} = 59.22$ kW

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Dedicated 1-mark loss per error):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — leaving pressure in bar without converting to Pa, or flow rate in L/min without converting to m³/s. The numerical answer will be off by factors of 10⁵ or 60.
> 2. **Mixing up the lever-arm direction** — in a brake pedal problem, students often write $F_{\text{foot}} = F_{\text{rod}} \times (\text{ratio})$, flipping the ratio. Remember: the longer arm (foot side) gives the smaller force, the shorter arm (rod side) gives the larger force.
> 3. **Treating pneumatic force as lower than hydraulic** at the same pressure — they are **identical** by $F = PA$. The difference is operating pressure, not the formula.
> 4. **Using $K_e \neq K_t$ in SI** — always $K_e = K_t$ in SI units. Examiners will deduct marks for using them as different.
> 5. **Omitting the "automotive example"** when asked to "classify actuators" — examiners specifically want real car components as examples, not generic ones like "industrial robot arm."
> 6. **Wrong RPM to rad/s conversion** — the correct factor is $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$, not $\pi N / 60$ or $N / 60$. A full 1-mark deduction applies.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Read it 30 minutes before the exam.**

### 🔹 Mechanical Actuators
- ✅ Convert stored/mechanical energy into motion
- ✅ **Cam** → rotary → linear (valve actuation)
- ✅ **Gear train** → trades speed for torque: $N_1/N_2 = Z_1/Z_2$
- ✅ **Lever** → amplifies force: $F_{\text{out}} d_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{in}} d_{\text{in}}$
- ✅ **Spring** → stores energy: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$
- ✅ **Friction brake/clutch** → $T = \mu F_N r$

### 🔹 Electrical Actuators
- ✅ **DC Motor** → $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, $E_b = K_e \omega$, $T = K_t I_a$
- ✅ **Stepper Motor** → discrete step angle, open-loop position control
- ✅ **Solenoid** → linear pull from magnetic field, $F = B^2 A / (2\mu_0)$
- ✅ **Relay** → low-power signal switches high-power circuit

### 🔹 Hydraulic Actuators
- ✅ **Cylinder force** → $F = P A = P \pi D^2 / 4$
- ✅ **Cylinder velocity** → $v = Q / A = 4Q / (\pi D^2)$
- ✅ **Motor torque** → $T = \Delta P \cdot V_D / (2\pi)$
- ✅ **Power** → $P = \Delta P \cdot Q = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega$
- ✅ Working fluid: **oil** at **100–200 bar** (cars), **incompressible**, **high force**, **slow but stiff**
- ✅ Automotive use: **brakes**, **power steering**, **active suspension**, **clutch actuation**

### 🔹 Pneumatic Actuators
- ✅ Same force formula as hydraulic: $F = P A$
- ✅ Working fluid: **air** at **6–10 bar**, **compressible**, **moderate force**, **fast but compliant**
- ✅ Automotive use: **truck air brakes**, **air suspension**, **HVAC**, **exhaust brakes**
- ✅ Spring-applied, air-released = **fail-safe parking brake**

### 🔹 Universal Power Equation (Memorize This!)
$$
P = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega
$$
**Applies to ALL four actuator families** — use it as a verification check on every calculation.

### 🔹 Energy Domain Selection Cheat Sheet
- Need **highest force in small space**? → **Hydraulic**
- Need **fastest precision control**? → **Electrical (DC/stepper)**
- Need **fail-safe, on/off, no fluid leak**? → **Pneumatic or mechanical spring**
- Need **synchronization to engine**? → **Mechanical (cam)**

### 🔹 Conversion Factors You WILL Use
- 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 100 kPa
- 1 L/min = $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s
- 1 mm = $10^{-3}$ m
- $\omega$ (rad/s) = $2\pi N / 60$ where $N$ is in RPM
- $K_e = K_t$ in SI units (V·s/rad = N·m/A)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working of Sensors and Actuators in Automobile - MAF, Throttle Position, Crank Shaft Position, Steering Torque, Lambda, Knock Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Working of Sensors and Actuators in Automobile - MAF, Throttle Position, Crank Shaft Position, Steering Torque, Lambda, Knock Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Sensors** are electro-mechanical transducers that convert a physical quantity (mass flow, position, rotation, torque, gas concentration, vibration) into a standardized electrical signal (voltage, frequency, or digital pulse train) that the Engine Control Unit (ECU) or Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) can interpret. **Automotive Actuators** are electro-mechanical devices that convert an ECU command signal back into a physical action (movement, fluid regulation, or energy release) to close the control loop.

In the context of this KTU module, the six sensors under study belong to two functional clusters:

| Cluster | Sensors | Primary Control Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Powertrain (Engine) | MAF, Throttle Position, Crankshaft Position, Lambda, Knock | Air–Fuel Mixture, Ignition Timing, Idle Speed |
| Chassis (Steering) | Steering Torque | Electric Power Steering (EPS) Assist |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR)** for gasoline is **14.7 : 1** by mass. This is the cornerstone number for Lambda sensor operation and is a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car as a Human Body

Imagine the modern automobile as a **human body**:

- The **ECU (Engine Control Unit)** is the **brain**.
- The **sensors** are the **five senses** (eyes, ears, skin) — they continuously report what is happening inside and outside the engine.
- The **actuators** are the **muscles** — they execute the brain's commands (open throttle, inject fuel, adjust spark).

**Real-world mapping:**
- The **MAF sensor** is like the **lungs' intake monitor** — it tells the brain how much air the body is breathing in.
- The **Throttle Position Sensor** is like the **foot pedal feedback** — it tells the brain how hard the driver is pushing the accelerator.
- The **Crankshaft Position Sensor** is like the **heart-rate monitor** — it tracks every rotation of the engine's "heart" (crankshaft).
- The **Steering Torque Sensor** is like the **hand-grip pressure sensor** — it tells the power-steering system how much help the driver needs.
- The **Lambda (Oxygen) Sensor** is like the **blood-oxygen monitor** — it reports whether the engine's "blood" (exhaust) is clean or rich/lean.
- The **Knock Sensor** is like the **ear listening for abnormal sounds** — it hears pinging inside the engine before damage occurs.

This sensory–actuator feedback loop is what makes a modern car a **"Mechatronic system"** — the seamless integration of Mechanical + Electronic + Computer + Control engineering.

---

## 1.3 Standardized Automotive Signal Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The ECU works on a **5 V analog reference** for most resistive sensors, a **0–5 V analog range** for output, and **5 V or 12 V digital pulse-width-modulated (PWM)** signals for switching actuators. The standard operating temperature range for under-hood sensors is **−40 °C to +150 °C**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Sensor-by-Sensor Breakdown

### A. Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor

**Location:** Between the air filter and the throttle body, inside the intake duct.

**Construction & Working (Hot-Wire Type):**
1. A **platinum hot-wire** sensing element is placed in the intake airstream.
2. The ECU supplies current to keep the wire at a constant temperature **~200 °C above incoming air temperature**.
3. As incoming air flows past the wire, it **cools** the wire (convective heat loss).
4. The control circuit **increases the supply current** to maintain the temperature differential.
5. The change in current is directly proportional to the mass of air flowing past the wire.

**Output Signal:** **Analog voltage** between **0 V and 5 V** (higher voltage = more air mass).

**Key ECU Action:** The ECU uses MAF signal to calculate **precise fuel injector pulse width** for stoichiometric combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hot-Wire and not Hot-Film?** Hot-film sensors use a thin resistive film on a ceramic substrate instead of a platinum wire. They are **more durable** (no wire breakage from contamination) but work on identical physics.

---

### B. Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)

**Location:** Mounted on the throttle body shaft.

**Construction & Working (Potentiometric / Hall-Effect type):**
1. A **potentiometric track** (or a Hall-effect IC) is mechanically coupled to the throttle shaft.
2. As the driver presses the accelerator pedal, the throttle plate rotates from **0° (idle)** to **~85° (wide-open throttle, WOT)**.
3. This rotation changes the resistance of the potentiometer (or the magnetic flux density at the Hall sensor).
4. The output voltage varies linearly from **~0.45 V (closed)** to **~4.5 V (fully open)** — a **5 V reference** divided by the pot track.

**Signal Type:** **Linear analog voltage** (or **dual-track redundant** signal for fail-safe).

**ECU Use:**
- Determines **driver demand** (load request).
- Inputs to **acceleration enrichment** logic.
- Works with **MAF** to compute **transient fueling corrections**.

> [!WARNING]
> A faulty TPS can cause **hunting idle**, **stalling**, **hesitation on acceleration**, or trigger **limp-home mode** (ECU limits RPM).

---

### C. Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)

**Location:** Near the crankshaft pulley, flywheel, or camshaft area.

**Construction & Working (Variable Reluctance / Hall-Effect type):**

**Variable Reluctance (VR) Type:**
1. A **reluctor wheel** with **60 teeth** (minus 2 missing = 58 teeth) is fixed to the crankshaft.
2. One tooth represents **6° of crankshaft rotation** (360° ÷ 60).
3. The **missing 2 teeth** create a reference gap of **12°** to mark TDC of cylinder 1.
4. A **permanent magnet with a pickup coil** sits near the wheel.
5. As each tooth passes, magnetic flux changes, inducing an **AC voltage pulse** in the coil.

**Hall-Effect Type:** Uses a stationary Hall IC and a **trigger wheel with a defined pattern**; outputs a **clean 0–5 V or 12 V digital square wave**.

**Output:** **Frequency proportional to RPM** (pulses per second).

$$f_{CKP} = \frac{N_{teeth} \cdot RPM}{60}$$

where the RPM is the engine rotational speed.

**ECU Use:**
- Determines **engine speed (RPM)**.
- Calculates **ignition timing** (firing angle).
- Controls **fuel injection sequencing**.
- Detects **misfire** (sudden drop in expected pulse train).

---

### D. Steering Torque Sensor

**Location:** Inside the **Electric Power Steering (EPS) column**, between the steering wheel and the rack/pinion.

**Construction & Working:**
1. A **torsion bar** mechanically couples the upper steering shaft (driver side) to the lower steering shaft (rack side).
2. When the driver turns the wheel, the torsion bar **twists** by a small angle proportional to the applied torque.
3. Two magnetic rings (one on each shaft section) rotate by slightly different angles.
4. The relative angular displacement is sensed by a **Hall-effect IC** or a **magneto-resistive sensor**.
5. The output is a **linear analog voltage (0–5 V)** proportional to the steering torque, typically in the range of **±6 Nm to ±10 Nm**.

**ECU (EPS Control Unit) Use:**
- Computes the **required assist torque**.
- Sends a **PWM command** to the EPS electric motor.
- Provides **steering feel** (variable ratio).

> [!NOTE]
> Modern EPS systems integrate the torque sensor with a **steering angle sensor** to support **Lane Keep Assist (LKA)** and **Auto-Park** features.

---

### E. Lambda (Oxygen / O₂) Sensor

**Location:** In the **exhaust manifold** (upstream, "Sensor 1") and sometimes after the catalytic converter (downstream, "Sensor 2").

**Construction & Working (Zirconia Narrow-Band Type):**
1. A **zirconium dioxide (ZrO₂)** ceramic element is shaped like a **thimble** coated inside and outside with porous platinum electrodes.
2. The **exhaust gas** flows on the outer side; the **ambient air** is on the inner side.
3. At operating temperature (**>350 °C**), ZrO₂ becomes a solid-state **oxygen-ion conductor**.
4. If the exhaust is **rich** (low O₂), oxygen ions migrate from inside to outside, generating a **voltage up to ~0.9 V**.
5. If the exhaust is **lean** (high O₂), the polarity reverses, generating **~0.1 V**.

**Output:** **Switching voltage signal** — either **~0.1 V (lean)** or **~0.9 V (rich)** for a narrow-band sensor.

**The Lambda (λ) Definition:**
$$\lambda = \frac{\text{Actual AFR}}{\text{Stoichiometric AFR}}$$

For gasoline: $\lambda = 1$ when AFR = 14.7; $\lambda > 1$ means lean; $\lambda < 1$ means rich.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wide-band Lambda sensors** (5-wire type) can measure $\lambda$ continuously from **0.7 to 32** and are used in advanced engines with **closed-loop stoichiometric control** within ±1% of AFR 14.7.

**ECU Use:**
- **Closed-loop fuel trim** — ECU adjusts injector pulse width every ~10 ms to keep AFR at stoichiometry.
- Enables the **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** to simultaneously reduce **CO, HC, and NOx**.

---

### F. Knock Sensor (KS / Detonation Sensor)

**Location:** On the **engine block** (typically between cylinders 2 and 3 on inline engines).

**Construction & Working (Piezoelectric Type):**
1. The sensor body contains a **piezoelectric crystal** (lead zirconate titanate, PZT) bonded to a **seismic mass**.
2. When **engine knock** occurs (abnormal auto-ignition producing shock waves of **~7 kHz**), the seismic mass vibrates.
3. The vibration exerts pressure on the piezoelectric crystal, generating a **small AC voltage** (millivolts range).
4. The ECU amplifies this signal through a **band-pass filter** centered at the engine's knock frequency (typically **6–15 kHz**).
5. If the signal amplitude exceeds a calibrated threshold, the ECU **retards ignition timing** by **3°–12° crankshaft degrees** in real time.

**Why Knock Detection Matters:**
Knock (uncontrolled detonation) causes **extreme cylinder pressures**, leading to **piston ring damage, overheating, and catastrophic engine failure**. Knock sensors protect the engine while allowing the ECU to operate the engine near the **knock limit** for maximum efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engine knock frequency** is generally: $f_{knock} \approx 6\text{–}15 \text{ kHz}$, depending on cylinder bore size. Larger engines resonate at lower knock frequencies.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Alert:** Memorize the formulas below. KTU examiners frequently test these in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | $\lambda$ — dimensionless; $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$ (gasoline) | Defines rich/lean/stoich exhaust |
| 2 | $AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Mass ratio | Stoichiometric combustion |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_{displ} \cdot \dfrac{RPM}{60} \cdot \eta_{vol}$ | kg/s | Volumetric efficiency-based air mass |
| 4 | $f_{CKP} = \dfrac{N_{teeth} \cdot RPM}{60}$ | Hz | Crankshaft pulse frequency |
| 5 | $V_{TPS}(\theta) = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | Volts; $\theta$ in degrees | TPS linear output |
| 6 | $\tau_{sensor} = k \cdot \Delta\theta_{torsion}$ | Nm; $k$ — bar stiffness | Steering torque from torsion |
| 7 | $V_{knock} \propto a_{vib}$ | Volts proportional to vibration g | Piezoelectric output |
| 8 | $\eta_{comb} \uparrow \Rightarrow T_{cyl} \uparrow \Rightarrow P_{knock}$ risk | Knock timing retardation needed | ECU protection logic |
| 9 | $V_{O_2} \approx 0.1 \text{ V (lean)} \text{ or } 0.9 \text{ V (rich)}$ | Switching voltage | Closed-loop fueling |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{inj} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj,max}}$ | Injector pulse width in ms | Fuel injection duration |

**Notes on Units:**
- Mass flow: **kg/s**
- Temperature: **°C or K**
- Torque: **Nm**
- Frequency: **Hz**
- Voltage: **V**
- Crankshaft angle: **degrees (°CA)**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel economy improvement** of **10–25%** vs. carburetted engines (Lambda closed-loop control).
- **Emission compliance** with **BS-VI / Euro 6** norms is impossible without MAF + Lambda closed-loop.
- **Power steering feel** is fully programmable via steering torque sensor mapping.
- **Engine longevity** is extended by knock-sensor-based **real-time timing retard**.
- **Drive-by-wire** systems replace mechanical throttle cables with **electronic TPS** for traction control integration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lambda (λ) Computation from Sensor Voltage

The narrow-band Lambda sensor output is non-linear and switches between two states. However, the **Wideband Lambda sensor** produces a linear pump current $I_p$ proportional to $\lambda$. We derive the relationship.

### Step 1 — Nernst Equation for Zirconia Sensor
The electromotive force (EMF) generated across the zirconia element is given by the Nernst equation:

$$E = \frac{R \cdot T}{4F} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,ref}}{P_{O_2,exh}}\right)$$

where:
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ — Universal gas constant
- $T$ — Absolute temperature of the sensor (K)
- $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$ — Faraday's constant
- $P_{O_2,ref}$ — Partial pressure of oxygen in reference air (~0.21 atm)
- $P_{O_2,exh}}$ — Partial pressure of oxygen in exhaust

### Step 2 — Solve for Oxygen Partial Pressure in Exhaust
We rearrange the equation to isolate $P_{O_2,exh}$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,ref}}{P_{O_2,exh}}\right) = \frac{4F \cdot E}{R \cdot T}$$

$$\frac{P_{O_2,ref}}{P_{O_2,exh}} = e^{\frac{4F \cdot E}{R \cdot T}}$$

$$P_{O_2,exh} = P_{O_2,ref} \cdot e^{-\frac{4F \cdot E}{R \cdot T}}$$

### Step 3 — Relate $P_{O_2,exh}$ to Air–Fuel Ratio
For a hydrocarbon fuel C₈H₁₈ (octane approximation), the combustion equation in lean-burn conditions leaves excess O₂:

$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5\,O_2 + N_2 \rightarrow 8\,CO_2 + 9\,H_2O + \text{excess } O_2 + N_2$$

The relationship between excess O₂ partial pressure and AFR for small lean deviations is approximately linear:

$$AFR \approx AFR_{stoich} \cdot \left(1 + k \cdot P_{O_2,exh}\right)$$

where $k$ is a fuel-specific constant.

### Step 4 — Compute Lambda
Substituting back into the Lambda definition:

$$\lambda = \frac{AFR}{AFR_{stoich}} = 1 + k \cdot P_{O_2,ref} \cdot e^{-\frac{4F \cdot E}{R \cdot T}}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda(E, T) = 1 + C \cdot e^{-\alpha E / T}}$$

where $C = k \cdot P_{O_2,ref}$ and $\alpha = \frac{4F}{R}$.

This exponential dependence is why **wideband sensors use a closed-loop pump cell** to maintain $P_{O_2,exh}$ at a fixed reference, with the **pump current $I_p$ becoming the linear indicator of λ**.

### Step 5 — Typical Numerical Example
Let $T = 873 \text{ K}$ (600 °C), $E = 0.45 \text{ V}$ (rich):

$$\frac{4F \cdot E}{R \cdot T} = \frac{4 \cdot 96485 \cdot 0.45}{8.314 \cdot 873} = \frac{173673}{7258} \approx 23.93$$

$$e^{-23.93} \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-11}$$

For stoichiometric operation, $E \approx 0.5 \text{ V}$ gives the **characteristic voltage step** seen in narrow-band sensors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fuel Injector Pulse Width Calculation

### Step 1 — Required Fuel Mass per Cycle
For each engine cycle, the cylinder draws in a known mass of air:

$$m_{air} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{N_{cycles/sec}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot 60}{RPM / 2} \text{ (for 4-stroke, cycles/sec = RPM/120)}$$

Wait, correcting: for a 4-stroke engine, the number of cycles per second is RPM/120:

$$m_{air,cycle} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot 120}{RPM}$$

### Step 2 — Required Fuel Mass per Cycle
For target $\lambda = 1$ (stoichiometric):

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air,cycle}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{m_{air,cycle}}{14.7}$$

### Step 3 — Injector Pulse Width
If the injector delivers $\dot{m}_{inj,max}$ (kg/s) at full duty:

$$\boxed{\Delta t_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{inj,max}} = \frac{m_{air,cycle}}{14.7 \cdot \lambda \cdot \dot{m}_{inj,max}}}$$

**Units check:**
- $m_{air,cycle}$: kg
- $\dot{m}_{inj,max}$: kg/s
- $\Delta t_{inj}$: seconds (typically converted to **ms**)

### Step 4 — Numerical Verification
Given: $\dot{m}_{air} = 0.05 \text{ kg/s}$, $RPM = 3000$, $\dot{m}_{inj,max} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$, $\lambda = 1$.

$$m_{air,cycle} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 120}{3000} = 0.002 \text{ kg} = 2 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{2}{14.7} = 0.136 \text{ g}$$

$$\Delta t_{inj} = \frac{0.136 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0272 \text{ s} = 27.2 \text{ ms}$$

> This is in the typical range of **2–30 ms** for port-fuel-injected gasoline engines.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Signal Conditioning Summary

| Sensor | Transduction Principle | Output Range | ECU Filter / Processing | Fault Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MAF (Hot-Wire) | Convective heat loss → resistance change | 0–5 V analog | Moving average over 10–50 ms | Limp mode uses MAP fallback |
| TPS | Potentiometric / Hall | 0.45–4.5 V analog | Dual-track redundancy check | Limp mode limits RPM to 2500 |
| CKP | Variable reluctance / Hall | AC pulse / digital sq. wave | Missing-tooth detection for TDC | Engine will not start if absent |
| Steering Torque | Hall-effect / Magneto-resistive | 0–5 V analog (linear) | Torque ramp + speed-based gain | Mechanical backup steering |
| Lambda (Narrow) | Nernst (ZrO₂) | 0.1 V or 0.9 V switching | Hysteresis comparator | Open-loop fueling table |
| Lambda (Wide) | Pump-current (5-wire) | $I_p$ = ±mA linear | Closed-loop PID control | Open-loop table |
| Knock | Piezoelectric vibration | mV AC at 7 kHz | Band-pass + windowed integration | Retard timing by fixed map |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vehicle Mechatronic Control Loop — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    driver[Driver Input Pedal Steering] --> tps1[Throttle Position Sensor]
    driver --> st1[Steering Torque Sensor]
    air[Inlet Air] --> maf1[MAF Sensor]
    engine[Engine Block Vibration] --> ks1[Knock Sensor]
    crank[Crankshaft Rotation] --> ckp1[CKP Sensor]
    exh[Exhaust Gas] --> lam1[Lambda O2 Sensor]

    tps1 --> ecu[ECU VCU Central Processor]
    st1 --> ecu
    maf1 --> ecu
    ks1 --> ecu
    ckp1 --> ecu
    lam1 --> ecu

    ecu --> act1[Fuel Injector Actuator]
    ecu --> act2[Ignition Coil Actuator]
    ecu --> act3[Throttle Motor Drive by Wire]
    ecu --> act4[EPS Electric Motor Actuator]

    act1 --> engine
    act2 --> engine
    act3 --> engine
    act4 --> steer[Rack Steering Output]

    engine --> exh
```

## 4.2 MAF Sensor Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    air1[Ambient Air] --> hwire[Hot Wire Platinum Element 200C Above Air]
    hwire --> bridge[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    bridge --> amp[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    amp --> adc[ADC 10 bit Resolution]
    adc --> ecuf[ECU Air Mass Calculation Block]
    ecuf --> fuel[Injector Pulse Width Generator]
```

## 4.3 Knock Sensor Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    engvib[Engine Block Vibration 7 kHz] --> pzt[Piezo Crystal PZT]
    pzt --> preamp[Charge Amplifier Gain 40 dB]
    preamp --> bpf[Band Pass Filter 6 to 15 kHz]
    bpf --> rect[Rectifier and Integrator]
    rect --> thresh[Comparator Threshold Check]
    thresh --> ecuk[ECU Knock Detection Window]
    ecuk --> retard{Is Knock Present}
    retard --> yes[Retard Spark by 3 to 12 degrees]
    retard --> no[Maintain MBT Timing]
```

## 4.4 Lambda Sensor Closed Loop Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    exhgas[Exhaust Gas Mixture] --> zircon[Zirconia ZrO2 Thimble]
    zircon --> nernst[Nernst Voltage 0.1V Lean or 0.9V Rich]
    nernst --> comp[ECU Comparator Target Lambda 1.0]
    comp --> pid[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    pid --> pulse[Adjust Injector Pulse Width]
    pulse --> engine[Engine Combustion]
    engine --> exhgas
```

## 4.5 EPS Steering Torque Sensor — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sw1[Steering Wheel Driver Force] --> tb1[Torsion Bar Twist]
    tb1 --> mring1[Magnetic Ring Upper Shaft]
    tb1 --> mring2[Magnetic Ring Lower Shaft]
    mring1 --> hall[Hall IC or MR Sensor]
    mring2 --> hall
    hall --> eps1[EPS ECU Torque Computation]
    eps1 --> motor[EPS Motor PWM Command]
    motor --> rack[Steering Rack Movement]
    rack --> wheel[Front Wheel Turn]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use the **alphanumeric node-identifier convention** (e.g., `driver`, `tps1`, `ecuf`) to ensure **Mermaid compatibility** without reserved-keyword conflicts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] 
**List any three engine management sensors and state the physical quantity each one measures.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **MAF Sensor** — Measures the **mass of air** entering the engine intake (kg/s).
2. **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** — Measures the **angular position** of the throttle plate (degrees) or driver pedal demand.
3. **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)** — Measures the **rotational speed and angular position** of the crankshaft (RPM / degrees CA).

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correctly named sensor + its measured quantity)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the function of a Lambda sensor in a modern petrol engine? Mention the stoichiometric AFR value for gasoline.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Lambda (oxygen) sensor**, located in the exhaust manifold, measures the **residual oxygen content** in the exhaust gas. The ECU uses this feedback to **continuously adjust the fuel injector pulse width** so that the air–fuel mixture remains at the **stoichiometric ratio**, which is **14.7 : 1** for gasoline. This **closed-loop fuel control** is essential for efficient operation of the **three-way catalytic converter**, which removes CO, HC, and NOx emissions simultaneously only when AFR is at stoichiometry.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for function, 1 mark for closed-loop concept, 1 mark for stoichiometric value)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE convention: Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **7 + 7 marks** for sub-parts (a) and (b). Students must answer **ONE full question** (both sub-parts) from each module pair.

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor** of the hot-wire type. How does the ECU interpret its output?

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The hot-wire MAF sensor consists of:
- A **platinum hot-wire sensing element** placed in the intake airstream.
- A **temperature-compensating cold-wire** placed upstream (for air temperature compensation).
- A **Wheatstone bridge** electronic control circuit.
- A housing mounted between the **air filter and throttle body**.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. The bridge circuit energizes the hot-wire to maintain it at a constant **~200 °C above** the incoming air temperature.
2. As intake air flows past the wire, **convective cooling** reduces the wire temperature.
3. The bridge **increases the supply current** to restore the temperature differential.
4. The increase in current (or equivalently, voltage across a sense resistor) is **directly proportional to the mass airflow rate** ($\dot{m}_{air}$).
5. This voltage is conditioned and sent to the ECU as a **0–5 V analog signal**.

**ECU Interpretation (1 mark):**
The ECU reads the MAF voltage, looks up a calibrated transfer curve (Voltage → kg/s), computes the **mass of air per cycle**, divides by **AFR = 14.7** to determine the required fuel mass, and generates the corresponding **injector pulse width**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **Knock Sensor** in a petrol engine. Why is knock detection essential, and how does the ECU respond?

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- The knock sensor contains a **piezoelectric crystal** (PZT — lead zirconate titanate) bonded to a **seismic mass**.
- It is mounted on the engine block in a position sensitive to cylinder knock vibrations.
- When **engine knock** (abnormal auto-ignition) occurs, the resulting shock waves (**~7 kHz**) vibrate the seismic mass.
- The vibration exerts pressure on the piezoelectric crystal, generating a **small AC voltage** (millivolts).

**Signal Path (2 marks):**
1. The signal is amplified through a **charge amplifier** (~40 dB gain).
2. It passes through a **band-pass filter** centered at the engine's knock frequency (typically **6–15 kHz**).
3. The ECU samples the filtered signal **only during a specific crank-angle window** (knock-detection window).
4. If the signal exceeds a calibrated threshold, knock is declared.

**Why Essential (1 mark):**
Engine knock causes **extreme cylinder pressures and temperatures**, leading to piston-ring scoring, overheating, and potential **catastrophic engine failure**.

**ECU Response (1 mark):**
The ECU **retards ignition timing** by **3°–12° CA** to suppress knock, then gradually advances timing back toward MBT (Minimum advance for Best Torque) until knock recurs — this is the **"knock-limited spark advance"** strategy.

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a **Lambda (O₂) sensor** based on the Nernst principle. How is the output used in closed-loop fuel control?

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- A **zirconium dioxide (ZrO₂)** ceramic element shaped like a thimble.
- Both inner and outer surfaces are coated with **porous platinum electrodes**.
- The outer surface is exposed to **exhaust gas**; the inner surface is exposed to **ambient air** (reference).
- A heating element (in modern sensors) ensures **>350 °C** operating temperature.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. At high temperature, ZrO₂ becomes a solid-state **oxygen-ion conductor**.
2. When the O₂ concentration on the exhaust side differs from the reference air side, oxygen ions migrate, generating an **electromotive force (EMF)** described by the **Nernst equation**:

$$E = \frac{R \cdot T}{4F} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,ref}}{P_{O_2,exh}}\right)$$

3. For rich exhaust ($P_{O_2,exh} \to 0$): $E \to 0.9 \text{ V}$.
4. For lean exhaust ($P_{O_2,exh}$ high): $E \to 0.1 \text{ V}$.
5. The output **switches sharply** near the stoichiometric point, giving the **characteristic step response**.

**Closed-Loop Use (2 marks):**
- ECU uses a **comparator** with a reference of **~0.45 V** to determine rich/lean state.
- If rich → ECU **reduces injector pulse width**.
- If lean → ECU **increases injector pulse width**.
- This **PI/PID control loop** keeps AFR within **±1%** of 14.7, maximizing catalytic converter efficiency.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a block diagram how a **Steering Torque Sensor** works in an Electric Power Steering (EPS) system. How is the assist torque computed?

**Model Answer:**

**Construction & Working (4 marks):**
1. A **torsion bar** mechanically couples the **upper steering shaft** (connected to the steering wheel) to the **lower steering shaft** (connected to the rack).
2. When the driver applies torque, the torsion bar **twists** by a small angle proportional to the applied torque:

$$\tau = k_{bar} \cdot \Delta\theta_{torsion}$$

3. Two **magnetic rings** — one on each shaft section — rotate by slightly different angles.
4. The **relative angular displacement** is sensed by a **Hall-effect IC** or **magneto-resistive sensor**.
5. The sensor output is a **linear analog voltage (0–5 V)** proportional to torque, typically calibrated for **±6 to ±10 Nm**.

**Assist Torque Computation (3 marks):**
1. The EPS ECU also reads the **steering angle sensor** and **vehicle speed sensor**.
2. Assist torque is computed using a **2-D lookup table**:

$$\tau_{assist} = f(\tau_{driver}, V_{vehicle})$$

3. At **low vehicle speeds**, assist is **high** (e.g., parking maneuver → ease of turning).
4. At **high vehicle speeds**, assist is **low** (highway stability → driver feel).
5. The EPS ECU sends a **PWM command** to the electric motor, which applies torque through a **worm gear or ball-screw reduction** to the steering rack.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Mentioning sensor name without its measured quantity** → loses 1 mark per instance.
> 2. **Forgetting stoichiometric value 14.7** in any Lambda question → loses 1 mark guaranteed.
> 3. **Confusing MAF vs. MAP** → MAP = Manifold *Absolute Pressure* (kPa); MAF = Mass Air *Flow* (kg/s). Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 4. **TPS without mentioning 5 V reference** → mention the reference voltage and the **~0.45 V (idle) to ~4.5 V (WOT)** range.
> 5. **Knock sensor answer without ignition retard action** → the ECU response (retarding timing) is **mandatory** to earn full marks.
> 6. **Writing Nernst equation without explaining variables** → examiners expect **R, T, F, P** to be defined.
> 7. **Missing block diagram in a 7-mark question** → always include a **neat sketch or block diagram** to score full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **MAF Sensor:** Hot-wire principle → measures **mass airflow (kg/s)** → output **0–5 V analog** → used to compute **injector pulse width**.
- **TPS:** Potentiometric / Hall → measures **throttle angle (°)** → output **0.45 V (idle) to 4.5 V (WOT)** → input to acceleration enrichment.
- **CKP Sensor:** VR / Hall → **60−2 teeth reluctor wheel** → output **pulse train** → gives **RPM and TDC reference**.
- **Steering Torque Sensor:** Torsion bar + Hall effect → measures **driver torque (Nm)** → used by **EPS ECU** for **variable assist**.
- **Lambda (O₂) Sensor:** ZrO₂ + Nernst principle → output switches **0.1 V (lean) ↔ 0.9 V (rich)** → **stoichiometric AFR = 14.7** → enables **closed-loop fuel control + TWC operation**.
- **Knock Sensor:** Piezoelectric → detects **7 kHz vibration** → ECU **retards ignition** by 3–12° to prevent engine damage.
- **Golden Numbers to Memorize:**
  - Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline): **14.7 : 1**
  - Lambda at stoich: **λ = 1**
  - Crankshaft teeth: **60 − 2 = 58**
  - Knock frequency: **6–15 kHz**
  - Lambda sensor switching voltages: **0.1 V (lean) / 0.9 V (rich)**
  - Standard sensor reference: **5 V**
- **Closed-Loop vs. Open-Loop:**
  - **Closed-loop:** Lambda sensor feedback active → used during cruise / part-load.
  - **Open-loop:** Lambda sensor cold or during WOT → ECU uses pre-calibrated fueling map.
- **Always** mention both the **physical principle** AND the **ECU action** in long-answer questions to fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microprocessor Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microprocessor Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microprocessor Architecture (KTU 2024 OEMUT834 – Module 2 Definition)**
> A **microprocessor** is a single-chip, programmable, clock-driven, register-based, binary-compatible, von-Neumann (or Harvard) Central Processing Unit (CPU) fabricated using Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) technology. Its **architecture** refers to the *internal organisation*, *register set*, *instruction set*, *addressing modes*, *bus structure*, *interrupt mechanism*, and *interfacing philosophy* that collectively define how the chip fetches, decodes, and executes machine instructions.

In the **automotive mechatronics** context, the microprocessor is the *computational heart* of every **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** — the embedded "brain" that samples sensor data (crankshaft position, MAP, lambda, wheel speed), executes control algorithms (fuel injection, ABS, traction), and drives actuators (injectors, solenoid valves, ignition coils).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Hook:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically asks for the **8085 / 8086 family** microprocessor architecture with reference to **automotive ECU applications**. Students must master the pin-out, internal block diagram, register model, and timing — not the latest ARM Cortex, although the *concepts* map 1:1.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Post Office"

Imagine a **post office sorting room**:

| Post Office Element | Microprocessor Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Mail bags (unsorted) | External **Memory** (RAM / ROM) |
| Sorting desk (where clerk works) | **ALU** (Arithmetic Logic Unit) |
| Desk drawers for temporary papers | **Registers** (A, B, C, H, L, PC, SP) |
| Memo from Postmaster General | **Control Unit (CU)** with Instruction Register |
| Front door / dispatch window | **I/O Ports & Bus Interface** |
| Customer queue (priority mail) | **Interrupt Request lines (INTR, RST 7.5 …)** |
| Master clock striking every minute | **System Clock (CLK)** driving the timing diagram |

When a **letter (instruction)** arrives → the clerk fetches it from the bag → reads it in the **Instruction Register** → the Postmaster's memo (decoded micro-operations) tells the clerk which desk drawer to fetch numbers from → clerk does the math → puts the result back → dispatches the answer. **That is the entire Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle** of a microprocessor.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **System clock frequency (8085A):** **3.072 MHz** (internal) → **6.144 MHz** with internal clock generator.
- **Instruction cycle (8085):** 1 to 6 **machine cycles**, each 3 to 6 **T-states** (clock periods).
- **Address bus width:** **16-bit** → can address **65,536 (64 KB)** memory locations.
- **Data bus width:** **8-bit** → one byte per transfer.
- **Register width:** **8-bit** (general purpose), **16-bit** (PC, SP, address pairs).
- **Supply voltage (Vcc):** **+5 V DC** (TTL-compatible).
- **Power-down standby current:** **< 10 mA** for CMOS variants (80C85).

> [!NOTE]
> These numbers are **exam-favourites**. KTU boards routinely test the relationship *Number of addressable locations = 2^n* where *n* is the address bus width.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Microprocessor Architecture

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — The Register Set
Registers are the **fastest, on-chip, single-byte storage** the CPU touches. They are physically inside the ALU area, with zero wait-state access.

For the **Intel 8085A** (KTU reference microprocessor):

| Register | Width | Purpose | Special Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A (Accumulator)** | 8-bit | Primary operand store for all ALU ops | Result of every arithmetic op lands here |
| **B, C, D, E, H, L** | 8-bit each (×6) | General purpose, can pair as **B-C, D-E, H-L** for 16-bit ops | H-L pair = default *Memory Pointer* (M) |
| **PC (Program Counter)** | 16-bit | Holds address of *next* instruction to fetch | Increments automatically; loaded on **CALL, JMP, RET** |
| **SP (Stack Pointer)** | 16-bit | Points to *top* of the stack in RAM | Decremented on PUSH; incremented on POP |
| **Flags (F)** | 8-bit | Status register: S, Z, AC, P, CY (bits 7,6,4,2,0) | Bits 1, 3, 5 are undocumented "don't care" bits |

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — The Bus Architecture (3-bus model)

1. **Unidirectional Address Bus (A15–A0):** 16 lines, CPU → Memory/I/O. Carries the *location* of data/instruction.
2. **Bidirectional Data Bus (D7–D0):** 8 lines, multiplexed for I/O and memory data transfer.
3. **Control Bus (RD, WR, IO/M, ALE, S1, S0, READY, HOLD, HLDA, RESET, INTR, INTA, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, TRAP, CLK, X1, X2):** Synchronises operation and handles requests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** is the demultiplexing key. In the **8085**, the lower address byte (AD0–AD7) is multiplexed with the data bus. A **74LS373** latch triggered by ALE demultiplexes them. KTU loves this question.

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — The Control Unit (CU) & Timing

The CU is **hardwired (or micro-programmed)** logic that converts each opcode into a sequence of **micro-operations** executed across machine cycles. Each **machine cycle = 3 to 6 T-states**, and each **T-state = one clock period (T)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Golden Rule:**
> *Execution time of a program = Σ (instruction count × cycles per instruction × T)*
> *where T = 1 / fCLK*

## 2.2 Fetch–Decode–Execute (FDE) Cycle — The Heartbeat

Every instruction follows the same five-stage pipeline logically:

1. **Fetch Opcode:** PC → Address Bus → Memory → Data Bus → Instruction Register (IR); PC ← PC + 1.
2. **Decode Opcode:** IR → Instruction Decoder → Control Unit generates micro-operation sequence.
3. **Fetch Operands:** If required, read from registers or memory using the addressing mode.
4. **Execute:** ALU performs operation; flags updated.
5. **Store Result:** Written back to register / memory / output port.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive ECU Parallel:** The Engine Control Module (ECM) repeats this loop at **5–10 kHz** for a single cylinder, sampling crank angle, computing injection pulse-width via lookup-table + PI controller, and firing the injector — all within one tooth time of the 60-2 trigger wheel.

## 2.3 Interrupt Structure of 8085 (Critical for ECU Real-Time Response)

| Interrupt | Type | Vector Address | Trigger | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TRAP** | Non-maskable, edge+level | **0024H** | Rising edge + high level (latched) | 1 (highest) |
| **RST 7.5** | Maskable, edge-triggered | **003CH** | Rising edge, latched | 2 |
| **RST 6.5** | Maskable, level-high | **0034H** | High level | 3 |
| **RST 5.5** | Maskable, level-high | **002CH** | High level | 4 |
| **INTR** | Maskable, level-high | Vect. from external device (8-bit) | High level | 5 (lowest) |

> [!NOTE]
> **SIM (Set Interrupt Mask) instruction** controls mask bits of RST 7.5, 6.5, 5.5. **RIM (Read Interrupt Mask)** reads the current mask. KTU frequently tests the SIM instruction format.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{mem} = 2^{n}$ | Addressable memory locations from *n*-bit address bus | locations |
| 2 | $T_{exec} = N_{inst} \times C_{avg} \times T_{CLK}$ | Total program execution time | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | Clock period | seconds |
| 4 | $N_{MC} = 1\text{ to }6$ | Machine cycles per instruction (8085) | MC |
| 5 | $T_{MC} = 3\text{ to }6 \times T_{CLK}$ | Duration of one machine cycle | seconds |
| 6 | $B_{throughput} = \dfrac{8 \times f_{CLK}}{C_{avg}}$ | Data throughput (bytes/sec) for streaming | bytes/s |
| 7 | $V_{IH} \ge 2.0\text{ V}, \;\; V_{IL} \le 0.8\text{ V}$ | TTL logic levels for 8085 inputs | V |
| 8 | $I_{OH} = -400\;\mu\text{A}, \;\; I_{OL} = 1.6\text{ mA}$ | Drive capability of one output pin | A |
| 9 | $P_{diss} = V_{CC} \times I_{CC} \approx 5\text{ V} \times 170\text{ mA} = 0.85\text{ W}$ | Power dissipation (max) | W |
| 10 | $A_{15..8} \mid A_{7..0} = 2\text{-byte address}$ | Address demultiplexing (high + low byte) | — |

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Automotive Mechatronics

- **Hard real-time constraints** (e.g., electronic diesel injection must fire within ±2 µs of commanded angle) → demands a deterministic FDE pipeline.
- **Interrupt-driven I/O** lets the ECU service a 60-2 crank sensor event *immediately* over less-urgent tasks like CAN message transmission.
- **Bus multiplexing** mirrors the modern **CAN, LIN, FlexRay** philosophy — sharing a single physical medium for multiple logical signals.
- **Register set** directly maps to the **AUTOSAR Run-Time Environment** register model that compiles to PowerPC / TriCore / ARM cores in production ECUs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Addressable Memory from Bus Width

**Given:** A microprocessor has an *n*-bit address bus.
**To find:** Maximum addressable memory locations.

**Step 1:** Every address line can take binary value **0 or 1** — i.e., 2 states.
**Step 2:** With *n* independent address lines, total distinct patterns =

$$N_{mem} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} = 2^{n}$$

**Step 3:** For **8085**, *n* = 16, therefore:

$$N_{mem} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4:** Address range spans from **0000H** to **FFFFH**.

> **Conversion logic:** Each unique binary pattern on the address bus corresponds to exactly one byte-addressable memory cell. The "kibi-byte" unit = $2^{10}$ = 1024 bytes, so $2^{16} / 2^{10} = 2^6 = 64$ KB.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Memory Map Decoding for 16 KB ROM + 16 KB RAM

**Given:** Design a memory map using an 8085 (16-bit address). Allocate **16 KB ROM** at lower addresses and **16 KB RAM** at higher addresses. Use 3-to-8 decoder **74LS138**.

**Step 1:** Size of each memory chip = 16 KB = $2^{14}$ bytes → needs **14 address lines (A0–A13)**.
**Step 2:** Remaining lines = A15, A14 → used for chip-select decoding.
**Step 3:** Build truth table:

| A15 | A14 | A13 | Region | Decimal Range | Hex Range | Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0–1 | Low 16 KB | 0 – 16383 | **0000H – 3FFFH** | ROM (CS1) |
| 0 | 1 | 0–1 | Next 16 KB | 16384 – 32767 | **4000H – 7FFFH** | (unused) |
| 1 | 0 | 0–1 | Next 16 KB | 32768 – 49151 | **8000H – BFFFH** | (unused) |
| 1 | 1 | 0–1 | High 16 KB | 49152 – 65535 | **C000H – FFFFH** | RAM (CS2) |

**Step 4:** Use **A15 and A14** as inputs to a NAND/ex-NOR combination:

$$\overline{CS}_{ROM} = \overline{\overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}}} = A_{15} + A_{14}$$

$$CS_{RAM} = A_{15} \cdot A_{14}$$

> **Conversion logic:** When both A15 and A14 are LOW, the lower 16 KB is selected. When both are HIGH, the upper 16 KB is selected. The middle two 16 KB bands are unmapped — typical automotive ECU leaves gaps for **peripheral-mapped I/O** or **memory-mapped CAN registers**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Execution Time of a 200-Instruction Program

**Given:** An 8085A runs at **fCLK = 3.072 MHz**. A program has **N = 200 instructions** with an *average* cycle count of **Cavg = 4.5 T-states per instruction**. Find execution time.

**Step 1:** Clock period:

$$T_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \dfrac{1}{3.072 \times 10^{6}} = 325.52 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2:** Total T-states consumed:

$$T_{total} = N \times C_{avg} = 200 \times 4.5 = 900 \text{ T-states}$$

**Step 3:** Execution time:

$$T_{exec} = T_{total} \times T_{CLK} = 900 \times 325.52 \text{ ns}$$

$$\boxed{T_{exec} = 292{,}968 \text{ ns} \approx 293 \;\mu\text{s}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Multiply the dimensionless count of clock ticks (T-states) by the physical time of one tick. The µs order of magnitude matches typical automotive control loop time at idle (5–10 ms). For high-RPM injection control, one-shot times must be < 100 µs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Wait-State Insertion for Slow EPROM

**Given:** An 8085A accesses an EPROM with access time **tACC = 450 ns**. 8085's memory read T-state at 5 MHz has no wait state. Find required **wait states** if CLK = 5 MHz.

**Step 1:** Without wait, the data must be valid by the **sampling edge** of the read cycle. Time available from address valid to data sample in a standard opcode fetch (4 T-states):

$$T_{avail} = 4 \times T_{CLK} = 4 \times \dfrac{1}{5 \times 10^6} = 800 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with EPROM access time:

$$T_{avail} = 800 \text{ ns} \;>\; t_{ACC} = 450 \text{ ns} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0 \text{ wait states sufficient.}$$

**Step 3:** Now slow the clock to **fCLK = 2 MHz** and find wait states.

$$T_{avail} = 4 \times 0.5\;\mu\text{s} = 2.0\;\mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{avail} > t_{ACC} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0 \text{ wait states still OK.}$$

**Step 4:** Counter-example: a **1 MHz** clock with **4 T-states** for memory read:

$$T_{avail} = 4 \times 1.0\;\mu\text{s} = 4.0\;\mu\text{s}$$

$$t_{ACC} = 450 \text{ ns} = 0.45\;\mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{avail} \gg t_{ACC} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{no wait state required.}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Wait states are required **only when** the device's access time *exceeds* the CPU's sampling window. Use the **READY** pin of 8085 to insert wait states. One wait state = 1 extra T-state = $T_{CLK}$ additional delay.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Stack Operation Depth Calculation

**Given:** Initial SP = **FFFFH** (top of 64 KB RAM). The program executes **15 PUSH** operations. Find final SP and the number of bytes occupied on the stack.

**Step 1:** Each PUSH decrements SP by **2** (because it stores a 16-bit register pair).

$$SP_{final} = SP_{initial} - 2 \times N_{PUSH} = \text{FFFFH} - 2 \times 15$$

**Step 2:** Compute:

$$2 \times 15 = 30_{10} = 001\text{E}_{H}$$

$$SP_{final} = \text{FFFF}_{H} - 001\text{E}_{H} = \text{FFE1}_{H}$$

**Step 3:** Bytes stored on stack:

$$B_{stack} = 2 \times N_{PUSH} = 30 \text{ bytes}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The stack grows **downward** in 8085. Each PUSH writes the high byte at (SP − 1) and low byte at (SP − 2), then updates SP ← SP − 2. The stack must never underflow below 0000H, or the program will wrap around and corrupt the interrupt vector table at 0000H–0038H — a common bug in early automotive ECUs.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Addressing Modes of 8085

| # | Addressing Mode | Format | Example | Operand Location | T-states (typical) | Bytes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | `OPR DATA` | `MVI A, 32H` | Data embedded in instruction | 7 | 2 |
| 2 | **Direct** | `OPR addr` | `LDA 2050H` | Address embedded in instruction | 13 | 3 |
| 3 | **Register** | `OPR r1` | `MOV A, B` | In register | 4 | 1 |
| 4 | **Register Indirect** | `OPR M` | `MOV A, M` | Address in H-L pair | 7 | 1 |
| 5 | **Implied/Implicit** | `OPR` | `STC, CMA` | Operation on accumulator/flags | 4 | 1 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of Intel 8085A (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The physical drawing of a 40-pin DIP is replaced with a **block-level functional architecture flow** that KTU boards accept. Each block shows its connections and data flow direction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ExtBus["EXTERNAL 16-BIT ADDRESS BUS A15-A0"] --> AddrMux
    ExtData["EXTERNAL 8-BIT DATA BUS D7-D0"] --> AddrMux
    AddrMux["ADDRESS-DATA DEMULTIPLEXER + 74LS373 LATCH driven by ALE"] --> AddrReg["16-BIT ADDRESS REGISTER / INCREMENTER"]
    AddrReg --> PCreg["16-BIT PROGRAM COUNTER PC"]
    AddrReg --> Spreg["16-BIT STACK POINTER SP"]
    AddrReg --> HLpair["16-BIT H-L ADDRESS PAIR"]
    InstReg["8-BIT INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR"] --> Dec["INSTRUCTION DECODER + CONTROL LOGIC"]
    Dec --> CUMicro["CONTROL UNIT MICRO-OPERATION SEQUENCER"]
    CUMicro --> RegArray["8-BIT REGISTER ARRAY: A,B,C,D,E,H,L + FLAGS"]
    CUMicro --> ALU["8-BIT ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT ALU"]
    RegArray --> ALU
    ALU --> RegArray
    CUMicro --> TimingGen["TIMING AND CONTROL GENERATOR"]
    TimingGen --> CtrlBus["CONTROL BUS: RD WR IO-M S1 S0 HLDA RESET INTR INTA RST TRAP"]
    InterruptLogic["INTERRUPT CONTROL: TRAP RST7.5 RST6.5 RST5.5 INTR"] --> CUMicro
    SIM_RIM["SIM-RIM INSTRUCTION LOGIC"] --> InterruptLogic
    ClockGen["CLOCK GENERATOR X1 X2 + internal divide-by-2"] --> TimingGen
    HoldLogic["HOLD-HLDA DMA LOGIC"] --> TimingGen
    SerialIO["SOD SID SERIAL I-O LINE"] --> CUMicro
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (left-to-right flow):** Address & Data buses enter the chip → demultiplexed → routed to PC, SP, H-L pair → IR fetches opcode → Decoder fires micro-ops → ALU works on register operands → Control bus drives external memory & I/O. This is the **canonical 8085A architecture** shown on every KTU module-2 question paper.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU almost always asks *"List the demultiplexed address lines of 8085"* — answer: **A15–A8** (pins 21–28) are *only* address. The lines **AD7–AD0** (pins 12–19) are *time-multiplexed* with the data bus and require an external latch (74LS373) to demultiplex, controlled by the **ALE** signal (pin 30).

---

## 4.3 Fetch–Decode–Execute Timing State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> T1
    T1 --> T2 : Send PC content to address bus; ALE HIGH; IO-M HIGH
    T2 --> T3 : Memory places opcode on data bus; RD LOW
    T3 --> T4 : Opcode latched into IR; PC incremented
    T4 --> T1 : Decoder generates micro-ops for next machine cycle
    T1 --> Decode : If all bytes fetched, transition to DECODE
    Decode --> Execute : Instruction decoder fires ALU / branch micro-ops
    Execute --> T1 : Return to T1 of next instruction
    Execute --> WAIT : If READY LOW, insert WAIT state
    WAIT --> Execute : When READY HIGH again, resume
    Execute --> INT : If INTR HIGH and interrupts enabled, accept at INTA
    INT --> Execute : Vector fetched and ISR executed
```

> [!NOTE]
> **State diagram reading guide:** Each node is a T-state (one clock period). For the **opcode fetch (M1) machine cycle**, exactly 4 T-states are used. **WAIT** and **INT** are conditional branches — the bus is held until external events resolve. This matches the *tristate* bus arbitration seen on **CAN bus** during error frames in modern automotive networks.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1.** *List the three status signals S1, S0 and IO/M̄ of the 8085 microprocessor and state what they collectively indicate about the current machine cycle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three status signals collectively identify the type of current machine cycle:

| IO/M̄ | S1 | S0 | Machine Cycle Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Halt |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | Memory Write |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | Memory Read |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Opcode Fetch |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | I/O Write |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | I/O Read |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Interrupt Acknowledge |

**(1 mark** for listing the three signals, **1 mark** for the truth table, **1 mark** for correct interpretation of at least four cycles**.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2.** *Explain the function of the **ALE** pin in the 8085 microprocessor. What external IC is used to demultiplex the address-data bus?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **ALE (Address Latch Enable, pin 30):** A *high-going* pulse that occurs during the **first T-state of every machine cycle**. It indicates that the lower 8-bit address (A7–A0) is currently valid on the multiplexed AD7–AD0 bus and must be latched into an external register before the data phase begins. **(2 marks)**
