- **External IC used:** The **74LS373** octal transparent D-latch (or 74LS245) controlled by the ALE pulse. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMUT834 Module 2]**
> *Answer ANY ONE full question from the pair.*

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Draw the internal block diagram of the **8085 microprocessor** and explain the function of the following blocks:
   (i) Instruction Register & Decoder (4 marks)
   (ii) Arithmetic Logic Unit (3 marks)

**(b)** An 8085A-based system uses an EPROM of size **8 KB** mapped at address **0000H–1FFFH** and a RAM of size **8 KB** mapped at address **4000H–5FFFH**. Design the address decoding circuit using a **3-to-8 decoder (74LS138)**. Draw the memory map and list the address lines used for each chip-select. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Instruction Register (IR) and Instruction Decoder (4 marks):**
- The **Instruction Register** is an 8-bit latch that holds the **opcode byte** just fetched from memory during the first machine cycle (M1).
- The opcode bits are then routed to the **Instruction Decoder**, a combinational logic matrix that decodes the binary pattern into a **unique one-hot micro-operation enable line**.
- The decoder output drives the **Control Unit sequencer** which generates the precise timing signals (RD, WR, IO/M̄) for subsequent machine cycles.
- Example: opcode `MOV A, B` = `78H` = `0111 1000` → decoder activates "copy B to A" micro-line → one machine cycle, 4 T-states, no external data fetch. **(1 mark per bullet)**

**(ii) Arithmetic Logic Unit (3 marks):**
- The **ALU** is an 8-bit parallel arithmetic-and-logic circuit. It accepts two 8-bit operands — one always from the **Accumulator (A)** and the other from a temporary register **fed by the register array**.
- It performs **8 arithmetic operations** (ADD, ADC, SUB, SBB, INR, DCR, CMA, DAA) and **4 logical operations** (AND, OR, XOR, CMP) plus rotate.
- The result is **always placed back in the Accumulator** and **flags register (F)** is updated with Sign, Zero, Auxiliary Carry, Parity and Carry flags. **(1 mark per bullet)**

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Determine chip size to address-line mapping:**
- 8 KB = $2^{13}$ bytes → needs **13 address lines (A0–A12)** for each chip.
- Remaining 3 lines (**A13, A14, A15**) are used for chip-select decoding.

**Step 2 — Truth table for chip-select (using 74LS138 with active-LOW outputs):**

| A15 | A14 | A13 | Hex Address | Active Output (CS̄) | Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0000H–1FFFH | Y0̄ (CS̄0) | **EPROM** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 2000H–3FFFH | Y1̄ (CS̄1) | — |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 4000H–5FFFH | Y2̄ (CS̄2) | **RAM** |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 6000H–7FFFH | Y3̄ (CS̄3) | — |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 8000H–9FFFH | Y4̄ (CS̄4) | — |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | A000H–BFFFH | Y5̄ (CS̄5) | — |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | C000H–DFFFH | Y6̄ (CS̄6) | — |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | E000H–FFFFH | Y7̄ (CS̄7) | — |

**Step 3 — Decoder connections:**
- **A, B, C** inputs of 74LS138 = **A13, A14, A15** respectively.
- **G1 = +5V**, **G2Ā = G2B̄ = GND** (always enabled).
- **Y0̄ → CS̄ of EPROM**, **Y2̄ → CS̄ of RAM**.

**Step 4 — Memory map (ASCII art for board copy):**

```
+--------------------+ FFFFH
|    UNUSED (32K)    |
+--------------------+ C000H
|    UNUSED (16K)    |
+--------------------+ A000H
|    UNUSED (16K)    |
+--------------------+ 8000H
|    UNUSED (8K)     |
+--------------------+ 6000H
|  RAM 8K (CS2)      | 5FFFH
+--------------------+ 4000H
|    UNUSED (8K)     |
+--------------------+ 2000H
|  EPROM 8K (CS0)    | 1FFFH
+--------------------+ 0000H
```

**(1 mark for chip-size line-allocation, 2 marks for truth table, 2 marks for 74LS138 pin connections, 1 mark for memory map, 1 mark for chip-select polarity.)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle of the 8085 microprocessor. Show the **T-state timing diagram for an opcode fetch machine cycle** and list the operations in each T-state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An interrupt-driven automotive ECU uses the **RST 7.5** input of the 8085. The RST 7.5 line is connected to a **crankshaft position sensor** that generates a pulse every 6° of crank rotation. Calculate:
  (i) The number of times RST 7.5 will be triggered per second at an engine speed of **3000 RPM**. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) The minimum time between two consecutive triggers at this speed. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) If each ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) takes **90 µs** to execute, what percentage of CPU time is consumed by the ISR? **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Stage 1 — Fetch (2 marks):**
- The content of the **Program Counter (PC)** is placed on the **16-bit address bus** during T1.
- **ALE goes HIGH** during T1; the lower address byte is latched into the 74LS373.
- During T2, the memory places the opcode on the **data bus** and the **RD̄** line goes LOW.
- During T3, the CPU latches the data bus byte into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
- During T4, the **PC is incremented** by 1.

**Stage 2 — Decode (2 marks):**
- The IR feeds the **Instruction Decoder**, a hardwired logic matrix.
- The decoder activates a specific control line corresponding to the operation.
- The Control Unit generates the micro-operation sequence for the remaining machine cycles.

**Stage 3 — Execute (2 marks):**
- The micro-operations may involve:
  - Reading operand(s) from registers or memory (extra machine cycles).
  - ALU performing arithmetic/logic.
  - Storing result in accumulator/register/memory.
  - Updating flags (S, Z, AC, P, CY).
  - Branching (modifying PC) for JMP / CALL / RET.

**T-state diagram (1 mark):**

```
       T1         T2         T3         T4
       |          |          |          |
CLK  __|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__
                   
ALE    ‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
                 
AD-A15-A8  [    PC(15-8)   |  TRI-STATE              ]
A7-A0   [ PC(7-0) |        |        |        ]
ALE latch -> A7-A0 stays for rest

RD̄                  ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
                                
D7-D0  [    X    |    OPCODE BYTE FROM MEMORY    ]
```

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Triggers per second at 3000 RPM (2 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert RPM to revolutions per second:

$$N_{rev/s} = \dfrac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2:** Each revolution = 360°; sensor fires every 6°, so triggers per revolution:

$$N_{trig/rev} = \dfrac{360°}{6°} = 60 \text{ pulses/rev}$$

**Step 3:** Triggers per second:

$$N_{trig/s} = 50 \times 60 = 3000 \text{ interrupts/s}$$

---

**(ii) Minimum time between triggers (2 marks):**

$$T_{gap} = \dfrac{1}{N_{trig/s}} = \dfrac{1}{3000} = 333.33\;\mu\text{s}$$

---

**(iii) CPU utilisation by ISR (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total ISR time per second:

$$T_{ISR/s} = N_{trig/s} \times t_{ISR} = 3000 \times 90\;\mu\text{s} = 270{,}000\;\mu\text{s} = 0.27\text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** CPU utilisation percentage:

$$\eta_{CPU} = \dfrac{T_{ISR/s}}{1\text{ s}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.27}{1} \times 100\% = 27\%$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** **27 % CPU load** is high but acceptable for an idling engine control. At **6000 RPM**, triggers double → CPU load = 54 %, which would starve background tasks like CAN communication. This is why production ECUs use **hardware timer-capture** peripherals instead of pure software ISR servicing.

**(1 mark each for correct formulas in (i) and (ii), 1 mark for (i) numerical answer, 1 mark for (ii) numerical answer, 2 marks for (iii) calculation, 1 mark for engineering interpretation.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Microprocessor Architecture:**
> 1. **Forgetting ALE demultiplexing.** Saying "8085 has 16-bit address bus" is incomplete — must mention the 8-bit **multiplexed** lines AD7–AD0 and the role of **74LS373 + ALE**.
> 2. **Confusing stack growth direction.** Stack grows **downward** in 8085 — PUSH decrements SP, POP increments. A wrong answer loses 2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Forgetting PC auto-increment timing.** PC is incremented *during T3 of the opcode fetch*, not after the entire instruction. Drawing the timing diagram with PC increment at the end of T4 loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mis-stating interrupt vector addresses.** RST 7.5 = **003CH**, not 0038H (that is TRAP-1 confusion). Memorise the 8 × n table: 0008H, 0010H, 0018H, 0020H, 0028H, 0030H, 0038H, then add 4 for the next family. Specifically: TRAP = 0024H, RST 5.5 = 002CH, RST 6.5 = 0034H, RST 7.5 = 003CH.
> 5. **Not converting address to binary before drawing memory map.** KTU examiners deduct marks if the memory map is drawn without binary/Hex justification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **8085 = 8-bit data, 16-bit address, 40-pin DIP, +5 V supply, NMOS technology.**
- [ ] **Address bus = 2^16 = 64 KB** addressable; **data bus = 8 bits.**
- [ ] **PC is 16-bit, always points to the NEXT instruction.**
- [ ] **SP is 16-bit, stack grows DOWNWARD in RAM.**
- [ ] **ALE** demultiplexes AD7–AD0 with help of **74LS373** latch.
- [ ] **Five interrupt sources** of 8085: **TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, INTR** — priority order is fixed.
- [ ] **Vector addresses:** TRAP=24H, RST5.5=2CH, RST6.5=34H, RST7.5=3CH, INTR=8-bit from device.
- [ ] **SIM** sets the interrupt mask; **RIM** reads it.
- [ ] **Status signals (IO/M̄, S1, S0)** identify machine cycle type.
- [ ] **HOLD-HLDA** pair enables DMA-style bus release (modern CAN controllers use this principle).
- [ ] **H-L pair** is the *implicit* memory pointer; `MOV A, M` reads from address HL.
- [ ] **Flags register bits:** **S(7), Z(6), AC(4), P(2), CY(0)** — only five defined.
- [ ] **8085 clock = external crystal ÷ 2** (internal divide-by-2).
- [ ] **Automotive context:** ECU = μP + Memory + A/D + Actuator Drivers + CAN transceiver.
- [ ] **Quick formula:** Memory locations = $2^{\text{address bus width}}$.
- [ ] **Quick formula:** Time = (Instructions × T-states × Period).
- [ ] **Quick formula:** 1 PUSH = 2 bytes on stack; SP decreases by 2.
- [ ] **Demultiplexing pin pair to remember:** AD7–AD0 ↔ ALE ↔ 74LS373 → separate A7–A0 and D7–D0.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open and Closed Loop Control Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Open and Closed Loop Control Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Open Loop Control Strategy — Formal Definition

An **Open Loop Control System** is a non-feedback control architecture in which the control action is entirely independent of the actual system output. The controller generates a command signal based solely on a pre-programmed reference input or a fixed timing sequence, and this signal drives the actuator without any mechanism to verify whether the desired output has actually been achieved.

In the context of **Automotive Mechatronics**, open loop control is used in systems where:
- The load is predictable and constant.
- Precision is not a critical design requirement.
- Cost, simplicity, and reliability under harsh environments outweigh the need for accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEMUT834 – Module 2):**
> Open-loop systems have **no feedback path**. The defining equation is simply:
>
> $$\text{Output} = f(\text{Input})$$
>
> They are also called **non-feedback** or **non-automatic** systems.

## Closed Loop Control Strategy — Formal Definition

A **Closed Loop Control System** is a feedback-based control architecture in which the actual output of the system is continuously measured by a sensor, compared against the desired reference value, and the resulting **error signal** is used by the controller to generate a corrective command. This loop of measurement → comparison → correction → actuation runs continuously in real-time, allowing the system to self-regulate against disturbances and component ageing.

In **Automotive Mechatronics**, closed loop control is fundamental to virtually every modern subsystem — from the Engine Control Unit (ECU) regulating air-fuel ratio, to Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS), Electronic Stability Control (ESC), and adaptive cruise control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> A closed loop system is characterized by a **feedback transfer function** $H(s)$ and a **forward path transfer function** $G(s)$. The canonical closed-loop transfer function is:
>
> $$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$
>
> This is the single most important equation in control engineering for the KTU board exam.

## Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

**Analogy 1 — The Toaster (Open Loop):**
Imagine you set a toaster to "3 minutes" and walk away. The heating coil runs for exactly 180 seconds regardless of how brown the bread actually is. If the bread is frozen, you get a pale slice; if the room is cold, the toaster runs cooler. The toaster **does not look at the result** and does not adjust. This is **open loop** — a pre-set command, no feedback.

**Analogy 2 — The Cruise Control (Closed Loop):**
Now imagine driving a car on a highway. You set cruise control to **100 km/h**. The ECU continuously reads the actual vehicle speed from the wheel speed sensor, compares it to the setpoint of 100 km/h, and if you start going uphill (speed drops to 95), the ECU increases throttle opening to push speed back up. If a tailwind helps you overshoot to 105, the ECU cuts throttle. The system **constantly watches itself and self-corrects**. This is **closed loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:**
> **O**pen = **O**nce-only (no feedback check).
> **C**losed = **C**ontinuous-**C**orrection (feedback always running).

## Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Required Vocabulary)

The following metrics are the **exam-relevant yardsticks** used by KTU examiners to grade any control-system question:

- **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** The persistent difference between desired and actual output as $t \to \infty$. Open loop has no mechanism to reduce this; closed loop aims to drive it to **zero**.
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time for the output to go from **10% to 90%** of its final value.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time for the output to remain within $\pm 2\%$ (or $\pm 5\%$) of the final value.
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** Maximum peak value above the steady-state value, expressed as a percentage.
- **Bandwidth ($\omega_{bw}$):** Frequency range over which the system responds adequately.
- **Disturbance Rejection:** Ability to maintain output despite external disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constant:** The exam value **$2\%$** is the universally accepted KTU/IEEE settling-time tolerance band unless otherwise stated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Open Loop System — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Reference Input ($r$)** is generated by the user, a timer, or a fixed program.
2. **Controller ($G_c$)** processes the input (often just a scaling or amplification stage).
3. **Actuator ($G_a$)** — e.g., a motor, solenoid, or relay — converts the electrical command into mechanical action.
4. **Process/Plant ($G_p$)** performs the physical work — e.g., the engine, wiper motor, or lamp.
5. **Output ($c$)** is the result. **Crucially, $c$ is NOT routed back** to the input.
6. **Disturbances ($d$)** — load changes, temperature drift, ageing — affect the process but are **invisible** to the controller.

**Why does this matter in a car?**
A basic HVAC blower fan set to "speed 3" is open loop. If the cabin is at $-5^\circ\text{C}$ vs. $+35^\circ\text{C}$, the same voltage reaches the motor and the airflow is identical — the system cannot adapt. This is acceptable for non-critical comfort loads.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Closed Loop System — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Reference Input ($r$):** The setpoint — e.g., 100 km/h for cruise control, 14.7:1 AFR (Air-Fuel Ratio) for the engine.
2. **Comparator / Summing Junction:** Computes **error** $e = r - b$, where $b$ is the feedback.
3. **Controller ($G_c$):** Often a **PID** (Proportional + Integral + Derivative) controller implemented inside the ECU.
4. **Actuator ($G_a$):** Drives the plant — e.g., the drive-by-wire throttle motor.
5. **Process/Plant ($G_p$):** The engine, brake modulator, etc.
6. **Sensor / Feedback Element ($H$):** Measures actual output — e.g., lambda sensor, wheel speed sensor, throttle position sensor.
7. **Feedback Signal ($b$):** Scaled sensor reading, returned to the comparator.

**Why does this matter in a car?**
A wide-band oxygen (lambda) sensor measures exhaust oxygen concentration **100 times per second**. The ECU compares actual AFR to stoich (14.7:1) and adjusts injector pulse width in real time — this is the heart of the modern fuel-injection system. Without closed loop, fuel economy, emissions, and catalytic converter life would all collapse.

## 2.3 The Four Control Actions (PID Family)

The controller block $G_c(s)$ in a closed loop can take one of four canonical forms:

1. **P — Proportional:** Output is proportional to error.
2. **I — Integral:** Output is proportional to the *integral* (cumulative history) of error. Eliminates steady-state error.
3. **D — Derivative:** Output is proportional to the *rate of change* of error. Anticipates future error and damps oscillations.
4. **PID — Combined:** Industrial and automotive gold standard.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Description | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---------|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Open Loop Output | $c(t) = f[r(t)]$ | Output depends only on input | Conceptual question |
| 2 | Closed Loop T.F. | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Standard negative feedback | Draw block diagram problems |
| 3 | Open Loop T.F. | $T_{OL}(s) = G(s)H(s)$ | Loop gain (used in stability) | Routh-Hurwitz, Nyquist |
| 4 | Error Signal | $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ | Comparator output | Derive $E(s)/R(s)$ |
| 5 | Error / Input T.F. | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Steady-state error via Final Value Theorem | $e_{ss}$ problems |
| 6 | Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ | Final Value Theorem | Step/ramp/parabolic input |
| 7 | Step Input $e_{ss}$ | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | Position error constant $K_p$ | Type-0 systems |
| 8 | Ramp Input $e_{ss}$ | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | Velocity error constant $K_v$ | Type-1 systems |
| 9 | Characteristic Eq. | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Stability condition | Find $K$ for marginal stability |
| 10 | PID Controller | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Time-domain | Cruise/throttle design |
| 11 | PID T.F. | $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | s-domain | Bode/root locus |
| 12 | Sensitivity Function | $S(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Sensitivity to disturbances | Disturbance rejection |
| 13 | Damping Ratio $\zeta$ | $M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Overshoot → damping | Suspension design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline tip:** In KTU valuation, $G(s) = G_c(s)\,G_a(s)\,G_p(s)$ is the *forward path*, while $G(s)H(s)$ is the *loop gain*. Marking key phrases like "forward path" and "feedback path" exactly as written here is worth **2 of 14 marks** in ESE questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Automotive Examples

- **Open Loop:** Windshield wiper interval timer, simple interior lamp, basic indicator flasher (older electro-mechanical), basic fuel injection (older carburetor-era systems), AC compressor clutch.
- **Closed Loop:** Electronic Throttle Control (ETC/DBW), Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Traction Control System (TCS), Electronic Stability Control (ESC), Active Suspension, Cruise Control, Engine Idle Speed Control, Lambda-based AFR control, Automatic Transmission Shift Quality Control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed Loop Transfer Function from First Principles

Consider a standard **negative feedback** closed loop with forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback path transfer function $H(s)$.

**Given:**

$$\text{Forward path output: } C(s) = G(s)\,E(s)$$

$$\text{Feedback signal: } B(s) = H(s)\,C(s)$$

**Comparator definition:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $B(s)$ into the comparator equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(s) &= R(s) - H(s)\,C(s) \\
     &= R(s) - H(s)\bigl[G(s)\,E(s)\bigr] \\
     &= R(s) - G(s)H(s)\,E(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Collect $E(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(s) + G(s)H(s)\,E(s) &= R(s) \\
E(s)\bigl[1 + G(s)H(s)\bigr] &= R(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $E(s)/R(s)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} &= \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use $C(s) = G(s)\,E(s)$ to obtain $C(s)/R(s)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} &= G(s) \cdot \frac{E(s)}{R(s)} \\
                  &= G(s) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \\
\boxed{\;T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} &= \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical closed loop transfer function** used in every KTU board exam problem on this module.

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error Using the Final Value Theorem

The Final Value Theorem states that if all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the open left-half $s$-plane (system is stable), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) \\
       &= \lim_{s \to 0} s\,E(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting** $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \\
       &= \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s\,R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For a unit step input** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot (1/s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \\
       &= \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)} \\
       &= \frac{1}{1 + K_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)$ is the **position error constant**.

**For a unit ramp input** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot (1/s^2)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \\
       &= \frac{1}{\lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,G(s)H(s)} \\
       &= \frac{1}{K_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,G(s)H(s)$ is the **velocity error constant**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs. Closed Loop

| Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Feedback** | Absent | Present (negative feedback) |
| **Accuracy** | Poor; depends on calibration | High; self-correcting |
| **Steady-State Error** | Large and uncorrected | Near zero (with integral action) |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | None | Excellent |
| **Sensitivity to Parameter Variation** | High | Low |
| **Bandwidth** | Wide but uncontrolled | Tunable |
| **Stability** | Always stable (no loop) | Conditional — may oscillate if $G(s)H(s)$ is poorly designed |
| **Complexity & Cost** | Low | High (sensors + controller) |
| **Bandwidth Cost** | Cheap | Sensor + wiring + ECU pins |
| **Examples in Cars** | Wiper, simple flasher, interior lamp | Cruise, ABS, ESC, ETC, AFR control |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | Conceptual (3–7 marks) | Heavy (full 14-mark derivations) |
| **Equation** | $c(t) = f[r(t)]$ | $T(s) = G(s)/[1 + G(s)H(s)]$ |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A closed loop speed control system has $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$ and $H(s) = 1$. Determine:
(a) The closed loop transfer function.
(b) The steady-state error for a unit step input.
(c) The type number of the system.

**Solution:**

**(a) Closed loop T.F.:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(s) &= \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \\
     &= \frac{10/[s(s+2)]}{1 + 10/[s(s+2)]} \\
     &= \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10} \\
     &= \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Steady-state error for unit step:**

Comparing $s^2 + 2s + 10$ with the standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:
$\omega_n^2 = 10 \Rightarrow \omega_n = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$ rad/s
$2\zeta\omega_n = 2 \Rightarrow \zeta = 1/\sqrt{10} \approx 0.316$

The system is **Type-1** (one pure integrator in $G(s)H(s)$). For a Type-1 system, the step-input steady-state error is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = 0 \quad \text{(since } K_p = \lim_{s\to 0} G(s)H(s) = \infty\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Type Number:** Count pure integrators (poles at $s=0$) in $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$ → **Type-1** system.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open Loop Control — Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode["Reference Input r"] --> sumJunction["Controller Gc"]
    sumJunction --> actuatorNode["Actuator Ga"]
    actuatorNode --> processNode["Process Plant Gp"]
    processNode --> outputNode["Final Output c"]
    distNode["Disturbance d"] -.->|unmeasured| processNode
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The arrows flow strictly **left to right**. There is no return path.
- The dashed arrow from `distNode` represents an **unmeasured** disturbance that open loop cannot reject.

## 4.2 Closed Loop Control — Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refNode["Reference r setpoint"] --> compNode["Comparator e=r-b"]
    compNode --> ctrlNode["Controller Gc PID"]
    ctrlNode --> actNode["Actuator Ga"]
    actNode --> plantNode["Process Plant Gp"]
    plantNode --> outNode["Output c measured"]
    outNode --> fbNode["Feedback Sensor H"]
    fbNode --> compNode
    distNode["Disturbance d"] -.->|attenuated by loop| plantNode
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The arrow from `fbNode` back to `compNode` is the **feedback path** — the defining feature of a closed loop.
- The feedback sensor $H(s)$ typically has unity gain ($H(s)=1$) in introductory problems.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Automotive Throttle Control (Drive-by-Wire)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    driver["Driver Pedal Position"] --> accelMod["Accelerator Pedal Module Sensor"]
    accelMod --> ecuNode["Engine Control Unit ECU"]
    ecuMod["Engine Speed and Lambda Sensors"] --> ecuNode
    ecuNode --> throttleMotor["Throttle Body Drive Motor"]
    throttleMotor --> throttlePlate["Throttle Plate Actual Angle"]
    throttlePlate --> intakeMan["Intake Manifold Air Mass"]
    intakeMan --> engineOut["Engine Torque and Speed"]
    engineOut --> ecuMod
    throttlePlate --> tpsNode["TPS Throttle Position Sensor"]
    tpsNode --> ecuNode
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This is a **dual-loop** architecture — *outer loop* (driver demand → torque/speed) and *inner loop* (throttle plate angle → TPS).
- Both loops are closed loop. If TPS feedback fails, the ECU flags a **P0120** diagnostic trouble code and enters **limp-home mode** — a classic KTU-style discussion point.

## 4.4 PID Controller — Internal Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    errSig["Error e t"] --> pBlock["Proportional Kp times e"]
    errSig --> iBlock["Integral Ki times integral e dt"]
    errSig --> dBlock["Derivative Kd times de by dt"]
    pBlock --> sumBlock["Summing Junction"]
    iBlock --> sumBlock
    dBlock --> sumBlock
    sumBlock --> ctrlOut["Controller Output u t"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The three branches (P, I, D) operate **in parallel** on the same error signal.
- The final output is the **sum** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between open loop and closed loop control systems with two suitable automotive examples for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback | No feedback path | Continuous feedback path |
| Accuracy | Low; depends on calibration | High; self-correcting |
| Disturbance handling | Cannot reject disturbances | Rejects disturbances automatically |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditional — may oscillate |

**Automotive Examples:**
- *Open Loop:* Windshield wiper timer, simple turn-indicator flasher relay.
- *Closed Loop:* Cruise control, Anti-lock Braking System (ABS).

(1 mark – definition distinction; 1 mark – two examples each; 1 mark – any additional valid distinguishing parameter such as accuracy/cost.)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is steady-state error? Write the expression for steady-state error of a Type-1 unity feedback system for a unit ramp input.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Steady-state error $e_{ss}$ is the difference between the desired reference input and the actual measured output as time $t \to \infty$, assuming the system is stable.
- **Expression (1.5 marks):** For a Type-1 unity feedback system with unit ramp input $R(s) = 1/s^2$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{\lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,G(s)}$$

For Type-1 systems, $K_v$ is finite, hence $e_{ss}$ is a finite non-zero constant. (For Type-0 system, $e_{ss} = \infty$ for ramp input.)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Derive the transfer function of a closed loop control system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* State the block diagram relations:

$$C(s) = G(s)\,E(s) \quad \text{and} \quad B(s) = H(s)\,C(s)$$

*Step 2:* Comparator equation (negative feedback):

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

*Step 3:* Substitute $B(s)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(s) &= R(s) - H(s)\bigl[G(s)\,E(s)\bigr] \\
E(s) + G(s)H(s)\,E(s) &= R(s) \\
E(s)\bigl[1 + G(s)H(s)\bigr] &= R(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4:* Solve for $E(s)/R(s)$:

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

*Step 5:* Multiply by $G(s)$ to get $C(s)/R(s)$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\;}$$

(Marks: 1 mark each for steps 1–4; 3 marks for the final boxed result, derivation justification, and stability implication that poles of the closed loop are roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$.)

**(b) For a unity feedback system with $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+5)}$, find the value of $K$ for which the damping ratio $\zeta = 0.707$. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Closed loop transfer function with $H(s) = 1$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K/[s(s+5)]}{1 + K/[s(s+5)]} = \frac{K}{s^2 + 5s + K}$$

*Step 2:* Compare with standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:

$$2\zeta\omega_n = 5 \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_n^2 = K$$

*Step 3:* Substitute $\zeta = 0.707$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(0.707)\omega_n &= 5 \\
\omega_n &= \frac{5}{2 \times 0.707} = \frac{5}{1.414} \approx 3.536 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4:* Solve for $K$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \omega_n^2 = (3.536)^2 \approx 12.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\;K \approx 12.5\;}$$

(Marks: 2 marks for setting up closed loop T.F.; 2 marks for standard-form comparison; 2 marks for $\omega_n$ calculation; 1 mark for final $K$ value.)

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) Explain the PID controller with a neat block diagram. State one automotive application where it is used. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Block Diagram:* (See SECTION 4.4 above for the Mermaid functional architecture — refer to the P-I-D parallel structure.)

*Theory (5 marks):*
The PID controller combines three control actions applied in parallel to the error signal $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$:

1. **Proportional (P):** $u_P(t) = K_p\, e(t)$ — produces an output proportional to the *current* error. Provides fast response but cannot eliminate steady-state error on its own. (1.5 marks)
2. **Integral (I):** $u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$ — integrates the error over time. Drives the steady-state error to **zero** but may introduce overshoot and slow response. (1.5 marks)
3. **Derivative (D):** $u_D(t) = K_d\, \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ — acts on the *rate of change* of error, anticipating future error and providing damping. (1 mark)

*Combined Output:*

$$u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

*Automotive Application (1 mark):* **Electronic Throttle Control (Drive-by-Wire)** — the ECU uses a PID controller to drive the throttle plate motor so that actual throttle angle tracks the driver's demanded angle. **Cruise control** and **active suspension** are also valid examples.

**(b) Compare open loop and closed loop control systems on the basis of any seven parameters. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution (7 marks = 1 mark per well-explained parameter):**

1. **Feedback:** Open loop — absent; Closed loop — present.
2. **Accuracy:** Open loop — low, depends on calibration; Closed loop — high, self-correcting.
3. **Steady-state error:** Open loop — finite and uncorrected; Closed loop — can be made zero with integral action.
4. **Disturbance rejection:** Open loop — none; Closed loop — excellent.
5. **Sensitivity:** Open loop — high sensitivity to component drift; Closed loop — low (by the sensitivity function $1/[1+GH]$).
6. **Stability:** Open loop — always stable; Closed loop — must be analyzed (Bode, Routh-Hurwitz, Nyquist).
7. **Complexity & cost:** Open loop — simple, cheap; Closed loop — sensors + controller add cost.
8. **Examples in automotive:** Open loop — wiper timer, simple flasher; Closed loop — ABS, ESC, cruise, ETC.

(For 7 marks, any 7 of the above parameters each with a one-line description earn full credit.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in feedback:** $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ (negative feedback). Writing $E = R + B$ costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting $H(s) = 1$ for unity feedback:** If the problem says "unity feedback", you **must** substitute $H(s) = 1$ explicitly, not omit it.
> 3. **Not writing the Final Value Theorem condition:** When using $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$, you **must** state "assuming the system is stable" — otherwise the theorem is invalid and you lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up P, I, D definitions:** Proportional acts on the *current* error, Integral on the *past* (cumulative) error, Derivative on the *future* (predicted) error. Getting this backwards costs up to **2 marks** in the PID question.
> 5. **Skipping units in the worked example:** $K$ is dimensionless, but $\omega_n$ must have units of rad/s. Always state units to be safe.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Open Loop = No Feedback.** Output is a function of input only: $c(t) = f[r(t)]$.
- ☐ **Closed Loop = Negative Feedback.** Canonical T.F.: $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$.
- ☐ **Characteristic Equation** is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$; its roots determine closed-loop stability.
- ☐ **Steady-State Error** uses the Final Value Theorem: $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ (valid only for stable systems).
- ☐ **Error Constants:** $K_p = \lim_{s\to 0} GH$ (step), $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} sGH$ (ramp), $K_a = \lim_{s\to 0} s^2 GH$ (parabolic).
- ☐ **System Type** = number of pure integrators (poles at $s=0$) in $G(s)H(s)$. Type-0 cannot track ramp with finite error; Type-1 cannot track parabolic input.
- ☐ **PID = $K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$.** All three branches act in parallel; output is the sum.
- ☐ **P** acts on *current* error; **I** on *past* (cumulative); **D** on *future* (rate of change).
- ☐ **Sensitivity to disturbance** is governed by $S(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ — high loop gain = low sensitivity.
- ☐ **Automotive closed loop examples:** Cruise, ABS, ESC, ETC, Lambda-based AFR, Active Suspension, Idle Speed Control.
- ☐ **Automotive open loop examples:** Wiper timer, basic flasher relay, interior lighting, simple horn circuit.
- ☐ **Always state "negative feedback assumed"** at the start of any derivation unless the problem states otherwise.
- ☐ **Unity feedback** means $H(s) = 1$ — write this substitution explicitly in your solution.
- ☐ **Damping ratio** from standard form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$: $\zeta = 0.707$ is the standard KTU value for "optimal" Butterworth response.
- ☐ **Examiners' hot keywords:** "forward path", "feedback path", "error detector", "summing junction", "controller", "actuator", "plant", "sensor", "disturbance", "setpoint" — use these terms verbatim to gain easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PID Control and Look Up Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PID Control and Look Up Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 PID Control — Formal Definition

A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) Controller** is a closed-loop feedback control mechanism widely used in automotive mechatronic systems. It continuously calculates an *error value* $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired setpoint (SP) and a measured process variable (PV), and applies a corrective action based on three mathematical terms: **proportional**, **integral**, and **derivative**.

Mathematically, the controller output is expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

In the Laplace (s-domain) representation, the PID transfer function is:

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

where:
- $K_p$ = **Proportional Gain**
- $K_i$ = **Integral Gain**
- $K_d$ = **Derivative Gain**
- $e(t) = \text{Setpoint} - \text{Process Variable}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** PID control is treated as the canonical feedback algorithm in *OEMUT834 — Automotive Mechatronics*, particularly for **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC)**, **Cruise Control**, **Active Suspension**, and **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** wheel slip regulation.

### 1.2 Look-Up Tables (LUTs) — Formal Definition

A **Look-Up Table (LUT)** is a pre-computed data array stored in the ECU's non-volatile memory (typically Flash/EEPROM) that maps one or more input variables to a pre-calculated output value. The ECU retrieves output values by **indexing** into the table based on real-time sensor inputs, rather than computing them through complex equations at runtime.

In automotive ECUs, LUTs are the practical bridge between:
- **Calibration data** (developed on dynamometers and chassis rolls)
- **Real-time embedded execution** (microcontroller cycle budgets in microseconds)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A PID controller **computes** its output continuously using a mathematical algorithm; a LUT **retrieves** its output by table-lookup. Many production ECUs use **LUT + PID hybrid** architectures — the LUT provides the *feedforward* baseline, and the PID corrects *feedback* errors.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

#### The Cruise Control Analogy (PID)

Imagine you are driving on a highway and want to maintain exactly **80 km/h**:

| Driver Action | PID Term | What it Does |
|---|---|---|
| The harder the speed drops below 80, the more you press the accelerator | **Proportional (P)** | Reacts to *current* error magnitude |
| If you keep being slightly slow for a long time, you press the accelerator a bit harder each second | **Integral (I)** | Eliminates *steady-state* error over time |
| If you suddenly see a hill coming, you preemptively ease off the gas before speed even drops | **Derivative (D)** | Reacts to *rate of change* of error |

#### The Chef's Recipe Book Analogy (LUT)

Think of a chef's recipe book. The chef doesn't calculate cooking time using calculus — he looks up: *"If the chicken weighs 1.5 kg at 180 °C, cook for 90 minutes."* The recipe book is the **LUT**; the input is the weight; the output is the cook time. Similarly, an Engine Control Unit (ECU) doesn't compute injection duration using live chemistry — it looks up: *"If RPM = 3000 and MAP = 60 kPa, then fuel pulse width = 4.2 ms."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Automotive Metric:** LUTs in modern ECUs are typically **2D** (one input, e.g., coolant temperature vs. idle target RPM) or **3D** (two inputs, e.g., RPM × Load → injection time), stored as **16-bit signed integers** with linear interpolation between adjacent cells. The standard SAE J1979 calibration format stores LUTs in the **.A2L** and **.HEX** (Intel HEX) formats.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Terms of PID — Operational Logic

#### 2.1.1 Proportional Term (P)
- **Logic:** Output is *proportional* to the instantaneous error.
- **Equation:** $P_{out} = K_p \cdot e(t)$
- **Effect:** Faster response, but causes **steady-state offset (residual error)**.
- **Tuning behavior:** Increasing $K_p$ reduces rise time but increases overshoot and oscillation.

#### 2.1.2 Integral Term (I)
- **Logic:** Output is proportional to the *accumulated* past error.
- **Equation:** $I_{out} = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$
- **Effect:** Eliminates steady-state error completely.
- **Tuning behavior:** Increasing $K_i$ reduces offset but causes **integrator windup** if not bounded.

#### 2.1.3 Derivative Term (D)
- **Logic:** Output is proportional to the *rate of change* of error (predictive action).
- **Equation:** $D_{out} = K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$
- **Effect:** Adds damping, reduces overshoot, improves stability.
- **Tuning behavior:** Sensitive to sensor noise; usually passed through a **low-pass filter** before differentiation.

### 2.2 Why Use All Three Together? — The Engineering Rationale

| Goal | Term That Solves It |
|---|---|
| Fast response to error | **P** |
| Zero steady-state error | **I** |
| Minimal overshoot & oscillation | **D** |

A **PI controller** is used where derivative noise is problematic (e.g., position control of slow actuators).
A **PD controller** is used where steady-state error is acceptable (e.g., rate damping in active suspension).
A **full PID** is used where both precision *and* stability are critical (e.g., electronic throttle body angle tracking).

### 2.3 Discrete (Digital) PID Implementation

Because automotive ECUs are digital microcontrollers, the continuous PID must be **discretized** using forward-Euler approximation:

$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n] + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

where $T_s$ is the sampling period (typically **5 ms to 20 ms** in powertrain ECUs).

### 2.4 Anti-Windup (Integral Clamping)

When the actuator saturates (e.g., throttle at 100 %), the integrator keeps accumulating error → huge overshoot on recovery. The standard automotive solution is **back-calculation anti-windup**:

$$I_{sat}[k] = I[k-1] + K_i T_s e[k] + K_{aw} (u_{sat} - u[k-1])$$

where $K_{aw}$ is the anti-windup gain and $u_{sat}$ is the saturated actuator command.

### 2.5 Look-Up Tables — Architectural Types

#### 1D LUT (Single-Input Map)
- Used when one input drives one output.
- Example: **Coolant Temperature → Idle Speed Target RPM**.

#### 2D LUT (Two-Input Map)
- Most common in ECUs.
- Inputs: two axes (e.g., RPM and Air Mass).
- Example: **RPM × Air Mass → Base Injection Pulse Width (ms)**.

#### 3D LUT (Three-Input Map) / Multi-Dimensional
- Used with interpolated surface fitting.
- Example: **RPM × Boost Pressure × EGT → VGT Vane Position %**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Unit / Type | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuous PID Output | $u(t)=K_p e + K_i\!\int\! e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ | Scalar | General controller design |
| 2 | PID Transfer Function | $C(s)=K_p+\frac{K_i}{s}+K_d s$ | Dimensionless | Laplace-domain analysis |
| 3 | Error Signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Same as PV | Feedback loop math |
| 4 | Discrete P-Term | $K_p e[k]$ | % or DAC units | ECU code implementation |
| 5 | Discrete I-Term (Rectangular) | $K_i T_s \sum e[n]$ | %·sample | ECU integration |
| 6 | Discrete D-Term (Backward Diff) | $K_d \frac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_s}$ | %/s | ECU differentiator |
| 7 | Anti-Windup Term | $K_{aw}(u_{sat}-u)$ | % | Saturation handling |
| 8 | LUT 1D Interpolation | $y = y_1 + \frac{(x-x_1)(y_2-y_1)}{(x_2-x_1)}$ | Output units | Between adjacent cells |
| 9 | LUT Storage Size | $N = \prod_{i=1}^{d} n_i$ | cells | Memory planning |
| 10 | Ziegler-Nichols $K_p^*$ | $0.6 K_u$ | Gain | Empirical tuning |
| 11 | Ziegler-Nichols $T_i$ | $0.5 T_u$ | seconds | Empirical tuning |
| 12 | Ziegler-Nichols $T_d$ | $0.125 T_u$ | seconds | Empirical tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In automotive ECUs, LUTs often encode **factory calibration values** that took **6–18 months of dyno testing** to develop. They cannot be computed at runtime because the underlying thermodynamic/chemical relationships are too complex for the ECU's limited MIPS budget. PID adds the *adaptive correction* on top of the LUT's *feedforward baseline*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Continuous-Time PID Controller

We begin with the generic closed-loop control law. Let:

- $r(t)$ = reference (setpoint)
- $y(t)$ = measured process variable
- $e(t)$ = tracking error
- $u(t)$ = controller output (commanded actuator action)

**Step 1 — Define the error signal**

By the fundamental principle of negative feedback:

$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

This is the deviation between what we *want* and what we *have*.

**Step 2 — Apply the linear combination principle**

A PID controller assumes the corrective signal is a *linear* combination of three error-based terms:

$$u(t) = u_P(t) + u_I(t) + u_D(t)$$

**Step 3 — Define the Proportional Term**

The proportional term responds to the *magnitude* of current error:

$$u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$$

A larger $K_p$ means a *stronger* reaction to the same error. Physically: a bigger torque command from the throttle for the same speed deficit.

**Step 4 — Define the Integral Term**

The integral term accumulates *all past* errors. By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

Even a tiny persistent error (e.g., +0.5 km/h for 10 seconds) grows over time and forces the controller to keep pushing harder until the error vanishes — guaranteeing **zero steady-state offset**.

**Step 5 — Define the Derivative Term**

The derivative term reacts to *how fast* the error is changing. By the definition of derivative:

$$u_D(t) = K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This is a *predictive* term: it damps the response before the error grows too large.

**Step 6 — Combine the three terms (final continuous PID law)**

$$\boxed{u(t) = K_p\, e(t) \;+\; K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Laplace Domain for Analysis**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\}=E(s)$, $\mathcal{L}\!\left\{\int e\,dt\right\}=\frac{E(s)}{s}$, $\mathcal{L}\{\dot e\}=sE(s)$:

$$U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)$$

Thus the PID transfer function is:

$$\boxed{C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}}$$

This shows the PID adds **two zeros** and **one pole at the origin** to the open-loop transfer function.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Discrete-Time PID (Digital Implementation)

In an ECU running at sampling period $T_s$, derivatives and integrals must be approximated.

**Step 1 — Discretize the Integral (Forward Rectangular Rule)**

$$\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n]$$

**Step 2 — Discretize the Derivative (Backward Difference)**

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

**Step 3 — Combine into the discrete control law**

$$u[k] = K_p\, e[k] + K_i\, T_s \sum_{n=0}^{k} e[n] + K_d \frac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Express in *velocity (incremental) form* (industry-standard ECU form)**

Subtract $u[k-1]$ from $u[k]$ to eliminate the running sum:

$$\Delta u[k] = u[k] - u[k-1]$$

After algebraic expansion:

$$\boxed{\Delta u[k] = K_p \bigl(e[k]-e[k-1]\bigr) + K_i T_s\, e[k] + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl(e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2]\bigr)}$$

This **velocity-form** PID is preferred in ECUs because:
- It is **bumpless** during setpoint changes.
- It has **built-in anti-windup** (the integrator naturally saturates with $\Delta u$).
- It only requires the last three error samples → minimal RAM.

### 3.3 Derivation of LUT Linear Interpolation (1D)

For a 1D LUT with breakpoints $x_1 < x_2$ and corresponding values $y_1, y_2$, given an arbitrary query $x$ where $x_1 \le x \le x_2$:

**Step 1 — Similar triangles principle**

$$\frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $y$**

$$\boxed{y = y_1 + (y_2 - y_1) \cdot \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}}$$

For a 2D LUT, this is extended using **bilinear interpolation** along the row and column axes sequentially.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tuning Trade-offs

| Controller Action | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $K_p$ | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | Small change | ↓ Decreases (not zero) | ↓ Degrades |
| Increase $K_i$ | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | **→ 0 (Eliminates)** | ↓ Degrades |
| Increase $K_d$ | Small ↓ | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | No effect | ↑ Improves |
| Increase $T_s$ | No direct effect | No direct | No direct | No direct | ↓ Degrades (aliasing) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-aliasing requirement:** The **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem** mandates $T_s < \frac{T_{process}}{2}$. For electronic throttle control with mechanical time constant $\approx 50$ ms, automotive ECUs typically sample at $T_s = 5$ ms (200 Hz), comfortably exceeding the Nyquist floor.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PID Closed-Loop Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spNode[Setpoint r t] --> sumNode((Sum))
    fbNode[Process Variable y t] --> sumNode
    sumNode --> errNode[Error e t]
    errNode --> pBlock[P Gain Kp]
    errNode --> iBlock[I Gain Ki integral]
    errNode --> dBlock[D Gain Kd derivative]
    pBlock --> sumOut((Add))
    iBlock --> sumOut
    dBlock --> sumOut
    sumOut --> uNode[Actuator Command u t]
    uNode --> plantNode[Plant Automotive System]
    plantNode --> yNode[Sensor Measured y t]
    yNode --> fbNode
```

### 4.2 PID + LUT Hybrid Architecture (Automotive ECU Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    driverNode[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> spNode[Setpoint RPM or Torque]
    spNode --> ffBlock[Feedforward LUT Lookup]
    ffBlock --> baseCmd[Base Command from LUT]
    baseCmd --> sumFF((Add))
    spNode --> fbSum((Sum))
    sensorNode[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> fbSum
    fbSum --> errNode[Error e t]
    errNode --> pidBlock[PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    pidBlock --> corrCmd[Correction Term]
    corrCmd --> sumFF
    sumFF --> finalCmd[Final Actuator Command]
    finalCmd --> actuatorNode[Throttle Body or Injector]
    actuatorNode --> engineNode[Internal Combustion Engine]
    engineNode --> sensorNode
```

### 4.3 Look-Up Table Internal Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[Input Sensor Reading x] --> idxBlock[Index Calculator]
    idxBlock --> lowPtr[Lower Bound Index i]
    idxBlock --> highPtr[Upper Bound Index i plus 1]
    lowPtr --> memNode[LUT Array in Flash]
    highPtr --> memNode
    memNode --> y1Node[Value at lower y1]
    memNode --> y2Node[Value at upper y2]
    y1Node --> interpBlock[Linear Interpolator]
    y2Node --> interpBlock
    idxBlock --> interpBlock
    interpBlock --> outNode[Interpolated Output y]
```

### 4.4 ECU Software Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1[ASAM MCD Layer Calibration Tools]
    layer2[A2L File LUT and Axis Definitions]
    layer3[ECU Flash Memory LUT Data]
    layer4[Real Time OS Task Scheduler 5 to 10 ms tick]
    layer5[LUT Read and Interpolate Function]
    layer6[PID Compute Function]
    layer7[Actuator Driver PWM Output]
    layer1 --> layer2 --> layer3 --> layer4 --> layer5 --> layer6 --> layer7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node identifiers are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `spNode`, `pidBlock`). No reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` are used as standalone labels. Double-quoted labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold, italics, or embedded HTML.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a PID controller used in an automotive electronic throttle body.

**Model Answer:**

A PID controller regulates the throttle plate angle to track the driver's pedal demand. The three terms function as follows:

- **Proportional (P):** Produces an output proportional to the instantaneous error between the desired and actual throttle angle. It provides the *primary* corrective torque to the DC motor.
- **Integral (I):** Accumulates the angular error over time and eliminates the steady-state offset (e.g., spring preload that prevents the plate from reaching the exact commanded angle).
- **Derivative (D):** Responds to the rate of change of angle error, damping overshoot when the plate swings past the setpoint.

A typical ETC implementation uses **velocity-form discrete PID** with sampling $T_s = 5$ ms and **back-calculation anti-windup** because the motor voltage is bounded between 0 V and 12 V.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a Look-Up Table (LUT) and a PID controller. Give one automotive example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | LUT | PID |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Stores pre-computed values; retrieves by indexing | Computes output from error in real time |
| Output type | Static (no learning) | Dynamic (adapts to error) |
| Memory vs Compute | Requires Flash memory | Requires CPU cycles |
| Automotive Example | **RPM × Air Mass → Base Fuel Injection Time** | **Cruise Control — Speed Error → Throttle Command** |

LUTs are best for **feedforward** static calibration maps; PID is best for **feedback** correction of dynamic errors.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the mathematical expression for the continuous-time PID controller. Explain the role of each term with reference to an automotive cruise control system.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a block diagram, describe how **anti-windup** is implemented using the back-calculation method. Why is it essential in production ECUs?

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The PID controller law in the time domain is derived from the principle that the corrective signal $u(t)$ is a linear combination of three error-dependent terms.

**Step 1 — Define the error.** Let $r(t)$ be the driver-set cruise speed and $y(t)$ be the measured wheel speed from the ABS sensor:

$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define the proportional term.** The proportional component reacts instantaneously to the magnitude of error:

$$u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$$

In cruise control, a larger speed deficit (e.g., on an incline) demands a *proportionally* larger throttle opening.

**Step 3 — Define the integral term.** The integral term sums the historical error to eliminate steady-state offset:

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

On a long hill, even a small persistent speed deficit grows the integral and forces the throttle to open more until equilibrium is restored.

**Step 4 — Define the derivative term.** The derivative term anticipates the future trajectory of error:

$$u_D(t) = K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

When the vehicle approaches the setpoint, the derivative slows the throttle closing to prevent overshoot.

**Step 5 — Combine into the final PID law:**

$$u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 6 — Laplace transform:**

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Anti-windup via back-calculation:**

When the PID output $u(t)$ exceeds the actuator's physical limits (e.g., 0 %–100 % throttle duty cycle), the integrator must *not* continue accumulating error, otherwise it will cause massive overshoot when the system finally leaves saturation.

The back-calculation method feeds the saturation *excess* back into the integrator:

$$I[k] = I[k-1] + K_i T_s\, e[k] + K_{aw}\,\bigl(u_{sat} - u[k-1]\bigr)$$

Here $u_{sat}$ is the clamped actuator value and $K_{aw}$ is the anti-windup gain (typically $K_{aw} = \frac{1}{T_t}$ where $T_t$ is the tracking time constant, e.g., $T_t = 0.5$ s).

**Block diagram (textual):**

```
e[k] ──► [Kp] ───────────────►(Σ)──► u[k] ──►[Saturation]──► u_sat ──► Plant
          ▲                       ▲                          │
          │                       │                          │
          │                [Integrator I] ◄─── + K_aw·(u_sat − u) ◄──┘
          │                       ▲
          └────── + ──────────────┘
```

**Why it is essential in production ECUs:**
1. The throttle body can only open 0–90 °; without anti-windup, the integrator builds up huge stored error during wide-open-throttle transients, then dumps it on the actuator → passenger-perceptible "jerk".
2. ASAM/ISO 26262 functional safety standards require **bounded actuator behavior** under all fault conditions.
3. Anti-windup ensures smooth recovery from saturation, critical for NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) refinement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] What is a Look-Up Table in an automotive ECU? With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **2D LUT** for engine fuel injection mapping. Show the bilinear interpolation formula.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **1D**, **2D**, and **3D LUTs** in terms of memory usage, accuracy, and real-time computational load. Give one example of each from a modern passenger car ECU.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Definition:** A Look-Up Table (LUT) in an automotive ECU is a pre-calibrated data structure stored in Flash memory that maps input sensor readings to pre-computed actuator commands. The ECU performs a memory read and (optionally) interpolation rather than computing the value from first principles.

**2D LUT Architecture for Fuel Injection:**

A typical 2D LUT for base injection pulse width uses two input axes:

- **X-axis (rows):** Engine Speed (RPM) — e.g., breakpoints at [800, 1200, 2000, 3000, 4000, 5500]
- **Y-axis (columns):** Air Mass Flow (mg/stroke) — e.g., breakpoints at [10, 30, 60, 100, 150, 220]
- **Cell values:** Base injection time in milliseconds (e.g., 1.2 ms, 2.5 ms, 4.8 ms ...)

```
            Air Mass (mg/stroke) →
        ┌─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐
  RPM   │ 1.2 │ 2.5 │ 4.8 │ 7.1 │
   ↓    ├─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┤
  800   │ 1.5 │ 3.0 │ 5.5 │ 8.0 │
 1200   │ 1.8 │ 3.5 │ 6.0 │ 8.8 │
 2000   │ 2.0 │ 4.0 │ 6.8 │ 9.5 │
 3000   │ 2.3 │ 4.5 │ 7.5 │10.2 │
 4000   │ 2.6 │ 5.0 │ 8.0 │11.0 │
 5500   ├─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┤
        │ 2.6 │ 5.0 │ 8.0 │11.0 │
        └─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘
```

**Bilinear Interpolation Formula:**

Given measured inputs (RPM*, MAF*) falling inside the cell bounded by (RPM$_i$, MAF$_j$), (RPM$_{i+1}$, MAF$_j$), (RPM$_i$, MAF$_{j+1}$), (RPM$_{i+1}$, MAF$_{j+1}$) with cell values $V_{ij}, V_{i+1,j}, V_{i,j+1}, V_{i+1,j+1}$:

First interpolate along RPM at MAF$_j$:

$$V_a = V_{i,j} + (V_{i+1,j} - V_{i,j}) \cdot \frac{\text{RPM}^* - \text{RPM}_i}{\text{RPM}_{i+1} - \text{RPM}_i}$$

Then interpolate along RPM at MAF$_{j+1}$:

$$V_b = V_{i,j+1} + (V_{i+1,j+1} - V_{i,j+1}) \cdot \frac{\text{RPM}^* - \text{RPM}_i}{\text{RPM}_{i+1} - \text{RPM}_i}$$

Final interpolated value:

$$\boxed{V = V_a + (V_b - V_a) \cdot \frac{\text{MAF}^* - \text{MAF}_j}{\text{MAF}_{j+1} - \text{MAF}_j}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

| Feature | 1D LUT | 2D LUT | 3D LUT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Inputs | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| Memory (typical cells) | 8–32 | 64–1024 | 4096–32768 |
| Flash Footprint | ~64 B | ~2–8 KB | ~64–256 KB |
| Real-Time Compute | Negligible | 1 multiply per axis | 2 multiplies per axis |
| Accuracy | Lowest | High | Highest |
| Example | Coolant Temp → Idle RPM Target | RPM × MAF → Injection Time | RPM × Boost × EGT → VGT Vane % |
| ECU Use Case | Engine warm-up | Base fueling | Advanced turbo calibration |

**Real-world Example — 1D LUT:** In a Maruti Suzuki petrol ECU, coolant temperature is mapped to target idle RPM via a 1D LUT with 16 breakpoints.

**Real-world Example — 2D LUT:** In a Bosch MED17 ECU, base fuel injection pulse width is stored as a 16×16 RPM × MAF table.

**Real-world Example — 3D LUT:** In a Bosch EDC17 diesel ECU, Variable Geometry Turbo (VGT) vane position is stored as a 16×16×8 RPM × Boost × EGT map for emissions compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the PID *algorithm* with the LUT *data structure* — they are different mechanisms; mixing them in a single answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Do not** omit units in the bilinear interpolation derivation (e.g., forgetting to state that $T_s$ is in seconds) — KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Do not** write "PID reduces error" without specifying *which* error (steady-state vs transient). Always name the error type.
> 4. For **anti-windup**, students often write only "clamp the integrator" — full marks require the **back-calculation formula** and the diagram.
> 5. **LUT memory calculation**: $N = \prod n_i$. A 16×16×8 LUT has $N = 2048$ cells, NOT $16+16+8 = 40$. This is a common KTU trap question.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PID = Proportional + Integral + Derivative.** The output is $u(t) = K_p e + K_i\!\int\! e\,dt + K_d \dot e$. Laplace form: $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
- **P** handles current error; **I** eliminates steady-state offset; **D** provides damping against overshoot.
- **Velocity-form discrete PID** is preferred in ECUs: $\Delta u[k]$ depends only on the last 3 error samples — no running sum, built-in anti-windup, **bumpless transfer**.
- **Anti-windup via back-calculation** is mandatory when the actuator saturates — without it, integrator windup causes dangerous overshoot in throttle/body control.
- **Look-Up Table (LUT)** is a pre-calibrated data array indexed by sensor inputs, used to avoid real-time computation of complex functions.
- **LUT types:** 1D (1 input), 2D (2 inputs), 3D (3 inputs). Memory grows as $N = \prod n_i$.
- **Bilinear interpolation** is performed between adjacent LUT cells using the formula: $V = V_a + (V_b - V_a)\cdot\frac{x^*-x_j}{x_{j+1}-x_j}$.
- **LUT + PID hybrid** is the dominant ECU pattern: LUT gives the **feedforward baseline**; PID corrects the **feedback error**.
- **Ziegler-Nichols tuning:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$, where $K_u$ and $T_u$ are the ultimate gain and period from relay-feedback tests.
- **Sampling theorem:** $T_s < T_{process}/2$. For electronic throttle ($T \approx 50$ ms), use $T_s \approx 5$ ms.
- **Calibration standards:** ASAM MCD-2MC (.A2L), CCP/XCP for measurement & calibration over CAN.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Modern Control Strategies like Fuzzy Logic and Adaptive Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Modern Control Strategies like Fuzzy Logic and Adaptive Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Modern Control Strategies"?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Modern Control Strategies** refer to a class of intelligent control algorithms that go beyond the conventional linear PID controllers covered in earlier modules. They are specifically designed to handle **non-linearity, uncertainty, and time-varying parameters** — all of which are abundant in real automotive systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 OEMUT834):**
> *Modern Control Strategies* are computational intelligence-based control methodologies (such as Fuzzy Logic, Adaptive Control, Neural Networks, and Neuro-Fuzzy hybrids) that mimic human reasoning or self-learn the plant dynamics in real time, without requiring an exact analytical model of the system being controlled.

### Why do we need them in Automotive Mechatronics?

Conventional PID controllers assume:
- The plant is **linear** or linearizable.
- Parameters are **time-invariant**.
- A reasonably accurate **mathematical model** is available.

But an automotive vehicle is subjected to:
- Changing load (passenger count, cargo).
- Aging components (engine wear, brake pad wear).
- Nonlinear phenomena (tyre slip saturation, engine torque non-linearity).
- Environmental disturbances (road gradient, temperature, altitude).

Modern strategies address these challenges directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two strategies emphasized in Module 2:**
> 1. **Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC)** — rule-based reasoning using linguistic variables.
> 2. **Adaptive Control (AC)** — controllers whose parameters self-tune online as the plant changes.

---

## 1.2 Fuzzy Logic Control — Intuition

### Real-World Analogy

Imagine an experienced car driver shifting gears of a manual transmission. They do not solve differential equations to decide when to shift. Instead, they use **rules of thumb** learned through experience:

> *"If engine speed is high AND road speed is moderate, THEN shift up."*
> *"If engine speed is very low AND accelerator pedal is pressed deeply, THEN shift down."*

This is exactly how **Fuzzy Logic** works. It encodes expert knowledge into **IF–THEN rules** using *linguistic* (word-based) variables like "high", "moderate", "low".

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Fuzzy Logic replaces the binary Boolean logic (0 or 1) with a continuous **membership degree** between 0 and 1. This allows statements to be "partially true" — just like in human reasoning.

### Crisp vs Fuzzy Set (Conceptual)

- **Crisp Set:** A temperature of 39.9 °C is *cold*, 40.0 °C is *hot* — an abrupt jump.
- **Fuzzy Set:** 39.9 °C has a *membership degree* of 0.01 in "cold" and 0.99 in "hot" — a smooth, human-like transition.

---

## 1.3 Adaptive Control — Intuition

### Real-World Analogy

Consider a **cruise control system** in a car. A fixed-gain PID controller may work fine on a flat road but will overshoot on a steep uphill climb. An **Adaptive Controller** continuously watches the tracking error and *adjusts its own gains online* to maintain performance — much like a human driver who subtly changes throttle habits when climbing a hill versus driving on the highway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Adaptive Control):**
> Adaptive Control is a feedback control technique in which the controller parameters are **automatically and continuously updated** in real time to compensate for variations in plant dynamics or the environment, thereby maintaining a desired closed-loop performance.

---

## 1.4 A Quick Comparison (Foundational)

| Feature | Classical PID | Fuzzy Logic Control | Adaptive Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requires accurate plant model? | Yes (linear) | No | Partial (reference model) |
| Handles non-linearity well? | Poor | Excellent | Moderate to Good |
| Self-tuning? | No | No (rules are fixed) | Yes |
| Implementation cost | Low | Medium | Medium–High |
| Typical automotive use | Speed loops | ABS, transmission shift | Active suspension, fuel injection |

---

## 1.5 Why this Topic is High-Yield for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners routinely test this topic because it merges **control theory** with **practical automotive relevance**. Questions typically focus on:
> - The architecture / block diagram of a Fuzzy Logic Controller.
> - The three main steps: **Fuzzification, Inference, Defuzzification**.
> - The conceptual difference between **MRAC** (Model Reference Adaptive Control) and **STR** (Self-Tuning Regulator).
> - Automotive applications such as **ABS, traction control, and active suspension**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)

The FLC has **four principal blocks**, as outlined in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### Block 1 — Fuzzification Interface
- Converts each crisp numerical input (e.g., slip = 0.12) into a **fuzzy linguistic value** with an associated **membership degree**.
- Uses pre-defined **Membership Functions (MFs)**.

### Block 2 — Knowledge Base
Consists of two parts:
1. **Database** — declares the MFs for each linguistic variable.
2. **Rule Base** — a set of IF–THEN linguistic rules supplied by a human expert.

### Block 3 — Inference Engine
- Combines the fuzzified inputs with the rule base.
- Performs fuzzy reasoning (typically using **Mamdani** or **Sugeno** inference).
- Produces a fuzzy output set.

### Block 4 — Defuzzification Interface
- Converts the fuzzy output back into a **single crisp numerical value** suitable for the actuator.
- Common methods: **Centroid (Centre of Gravity)**, **Mean of Maximum**, **Largest of Maximum**, **Bisector of Area**.

> [!NOTE]
> The most commonly used method in automotive KTU problems is the **Centroid (COG)** defuzzification.

---

## 2.2 Membership Functions (MFs)

A Membership Function $\mu_A(x)$ maps every element $x$ of the universe of discourse to a real number in $[0,1]$, representing the degree to which $x$ belongs to the fuzzy set $A$.

### Standard MF Forms

| MF Type | Formula (Mathematical Description) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Triangular | $\mu(x)=\max\!\left(0,\;1-\dfrac{\vert x-c \vert}{w}\right)$ | Real-time control (low computation) |
| Trapezoidal | $\mu(x)=\max\!\left(0,\;\min\!\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a},\;1,\;\dfrac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$ | Smooth control surfaces |
| Gaussian | $\mu(x)=\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | Smooth, differentiable rules |
| Singleton | $\mu(x)=1$ if $x=c$, else $0$ | Sugeno-style output MFs |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU formula sheet above, the notation $\vert x-c \vert$ represents absolute value. The vertical bar `|` symbol is intentionally avoided in markdown table cells to keep the table syntax valid.

---

## 2.3 The Defuzzification Step (Centroid Method) — Theory

The crisp output $y^{*}$ using the **Centre of Gravity** is:

$$y^{*} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\int_{Y} y \cdot \mu_{B}(y)\, dy}{\displaystyle\int_{Y} \mu_{B}(y)\, dy}$$

where:
- $Y$ is the universe of discourse of the output.
- $\mu_{B}(y)$ is the aggregated membership function of the fuzzy output set $B$.
- The numerator is the *first moment of area*, and the denominator is the *total area under the MF*.

---

## 2.4 Adaptive Control — Two Principal Architectures

### 2.4.1 Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)

In MRAC:
- A **reference model** defines the *desired* closed-loop response.
- The **plant output** is compared to the **model output**.
- The **error** drives an **adjustment mechanism** that updates the controller gains in real time.

### 2.4.2 Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)

In STR:
- A **parameter estimator** (often a Recursive Least Squares block) continuously identifies the plant model.
- The estimated model parameters are used to **re-design the controller** (e.g., pole placement, LQR) at every step.

### 2.4.3 Gain Scheduling (a simpler adaptive technique)

- The controller gains are pre-computed for several operating points.
- A scheduling variable (e.g., vehicle speed) selects the appropriate gain set.

---

## 2.5 The MIT Rule — The Heart of MRAC

The **MIT rule** is the classical gradient-descent law used in MRAC to adjust a controller parameter $\theta$:

$$\dot{\theta} \;=\; -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

where:
- $e(t) = y(t) - y_{m}(t)$ is the output error between plant and reference model.
- $\gamma > 0$ is the **adaptation gain** (a design constant).
- $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ is the **sensitivity derivative** of the error with respect to the adjustable parameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MIT rule is derived by descending along the gradient of the cost function $J(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{2} e^{2}$.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Membership degree | $0 \le \mu_A(x) \le 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Centroid defuzzification | $y^{*}=\dfrac{\int y\,\mu_B(y)\,dy}{\int \mu_B(y)\,dy}$ | Crisp output value |
| 3 | Mean of Maximum (MOM) | $y^{*}=\dfrac{\sum y_{i}^{*}}{N}$ over max-cut region | Less smooth than COG |
| 4 | Triangular MF | $\mu(x)=\max\!\left(0,\;1-\dfrac{\vert x-c \vert}{w}\right)$ | $c$ = centre, $w$ = half-width |
| 5 | Gaussian MF | $\mu(x)=\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | $\sigma$ = standard deviation |
| 6 | MIT rule | $\dot{\theta}=-\gamma\, e\, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ | $\gamma$ = adaptation gain (s⁻¹) |
| 7 | MRAC cost function | $J(\theta)=\dfrac{1}{2}e^{2}$ | Minimised by gradient descent |
| 8 | STR parameter update | $\hat{\theta}(t)=\hat{\theta}(t-1)+K(t)\,[y(t)-\phi^{T}(t)\hat{\theta}(t-1)]$ | RLS estimator form |
| 9 | Slip ratio (ABS) | $\lambda=\dfrac{v_{veh}-v_{whl}}{v_{veh}}$ | Used as FLC input |
| 10 | Lyapunov stability (qualitative) | $\dot{V} \le 0$ for stability | Used in robust adaptive design |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Where Modern Control is Used |
|---|---|
| **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** | FLC decides brake pressure based on slip & slip-rate |
| **Automatic Transmission** | FLC selects gear from engine load & vehicle speed |
| **Active Suspension** | Adaptive Control adjusts damper coefficient online |
| **Electronic Stability Program (ESP)** | Adaptive yaw-moment control |
| **Engine Fuel Injection** | Adaptive CRDI compensates for altitude & wear |
| **EV Battery Management** | Adaptive SOC estimation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Centroid (COG) Defuzzification Formula

### Step 0 — Statement of the Problem

Given an aggregated fuzzy output set $B$ with membership function $\mu_B(y)$ over a universe $Y = [y_{\min}, y_{\max}]$, derive the crisp value $y^{*}$ that best represents $B$.

### Step 1 — Consider the Continuous First Moment of Area

The classical "centre of mass" of a planar lamina of density $\mu_B(y)$ distributed along $Y$ is, by the definition of moments:

$$M \;=\; \int_{Y} y \cdot \mu_B(y)\, dy$$

This integral represents the *first moment* of the area under $\mu_B(y)$ about the origin.

### Step 2 — Consider the Total Area

$$A \;=\; \int_{Y} \mu_B(y)\, dy$$

This is the area "covered" by the membership function.

### Step 3 — Apply the Centre-of-Mass Definition

By definition, the centroid of a 1-D density is the first moment divided by the total mass:

$$y^{*} \;=\; \frac{M}{A} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\int_{Y} y \cdot \mu_B(y)\, dy}{\displaystyle\int_{Y} \mu_B(y)\, dy}$$

This is the standard COG formula used by the KTU examiner.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The MIT Adaptive Rule for a First-Order Plant

### Step 0 — Plant Description

Consider a simple first-order plant with a single adjustable controller gain $\theta$:

$$G(s) \;=\; \frac{K}{s+a}$$

The closed-loop system with reference input $r$ produces the output $y$. The reference model is:

$$\frac{y_m(s)}{r(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_m}{s+a_m}$$

### Step 1 — Define the Tracking Error

$$e(t) \;=\; y(t) - y_m(t)$$

### Step 2 — Define the Cost Function

Following the KTU/Lyapunov-style formulation, define a quadratic cost:

$$J(\theta) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\, e^{2}(t)$$

The aim is to choose $\dot{\theta}$ so that $J$ decreases with time.

### Step 3 — Gradient Descent Along the Cost Surface

The MIT rule prescribes that $\theta$ should change in the direction opposite to the gradient of $J$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$\dot{\theta} \;=\; -\gamma \, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$$

Substitute the expression for $J$:

$$\dot{\theta} \;=\; -\gamma \, \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left[\frac{1}{2}\, e^{2}\right]$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Chain Rule

Differentiating $J$ with respect to $\theta$ using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} \;=\; e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

Substitute back:

$$\dot{\theta} \;=\; -\gamma \, e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

This is the **MIT rule**.

### Step 5 — Heuristic Interpretation (For Intuition)

- If error $e$ is large, $\dot{\theta}$ becomes large in magnitude → parameter changes faster.
- If $\partial e / \partial \theta$ is large → this parameter strongly influences the error, so update it faster.
- The sign $-\gamma$ ensures the update *reduces* the error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering note:** Pure MIT rule can become unstable if $\gamma$ is too large. Modern automotive MRAC implementations often use **Lyapunov-based** or **Normalised MIT** variants to guarantee boundedness.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Example: FLC for Anti-lock Braking (Conceptual Numerical)

### Problem Setup (Typical KTU 3-Mark Style)

A fuzzy ABS controller uses two inputs:
- $e$ = slip error (difference between actual slip and target slip of **0.2**).
- $\Delta e$ = rate of change of slip error.

Output: $u$ = brake-pressure increment (in bar).

Membership functions (simplified):
- $e$ is **Negative (N)**, **Zero (Z)**, or **Positive (P)**.
- $\Delta e$ is **Negative (N)** or **Positive (P)**.
- Output $u$ is **Decrease (D)**, **Hold (H)**, **Increase (I)**.

Rule Base (Mamdani):

| Rule # | IF ($e$) | AND ($\Delta e$) | THEN ($u$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N | N | I |
| 2 | N | P | H |
| 3 | Z | N | H |
| 4 | Z | P | H |
| 5 | P | N | D |
| 6 | P | P | D |

### Step 1 — Suppose current readings

- Actual slip = **0.25** → $e = 0.25 - 0.20 = +0.05$ (Positive, but not strongly).
- $\Delta e = +0.01$ per cycle (Positive).

### Step 2 — Fuzzification

$\mu_P(e) = 0.6$, $\mu_Z(e) = 0.4$, $\mu_N(e) = 0$
$\mu_P(\Delta e) = 1.0$, $\mu_N(\Delta e) = 0$

### Step 3 — Apply Rules (Rule Strength via MIN operator)

- Rule 5: $\min(0.6, 0) = 0$ → inactive
- Rule 6: $\min(0.6, 1.0) = 0.6$ → **D** with strength 0.6
- Rule 3: $\min(0.4, 0) = 0$ → inactive
- Rule 4: $\min(0.4, 1.0) = 0.4$ → **H** with strength 0.4

### Step 4 — Defuzzification (using singletons)

Suppose singletons are: D = −2 bar, H = 0 bar, I = +2 bar.

$$u^{*} \;=\; \frac{(0.6)(-2) + (0.4)(0)}{0.6 + 0.4} \;=\; \frac{-1.2}{1.0} \;=\; -1.2 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Decision

The brake pressure must be **decreased by 1.2 bar** in this cycle to bring slip back to 0.2.

> [!NOTE]
> This short numerical example captures the structure expected in KTU Part A or short Part B sub-parts. Examiners reward clear labelling of the four FLC stages.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Core Equation | Physical Meaning | Stability Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Membership | $\mu_A(x) \in [0,1]$ | Degree of belonging | N/A |
| Centroid COG | $y^{*}=\dfrac{\int y \mu_B dy}{\int \mu_B dy}$ | Crisp control action | Always bounded |
