| MIT rule | $\dot{\theta}=-\gamma e \partial e/\partial\theta$ | Online gain update | Stable only if $\gamma$ small |
| MRAC error | $e=y-y_m$ | Model-tracking deviation | Lyapunov proof available |
| RLS estimator | $\hat{\theta}(k)=\hat{\theta}(k-1)+K(k)\varepsilon(k)$ | Online parameter ID | Converges if PE condition met |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the full architecture of a generic FLC as it appears in KTU textbook treatments.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput1[/Input 1: Error e/] --> nodeFuzz1[Fuzzification 1]
    nodeInput2[/Input 2: Delta e/] --> nodeFuzz2[Fuzzification 2]
    nodeFuzz1 --> nodeKnowledgeBase
    nodeFuzz2 --> nodeKnowledgeBase
    nodeKnowledgeBase[Knowledge Base: Rules and Membership Functions] --> nodeInference[Inference Engine]
    nodeInference --> nodeDefuzz[Defuzzification Interface]
    nodeDefuzz --> nodeOutput[/Crisp Output u/]
    nodeOutput --> nodeActuator[Actuator: Brake Valve or Gear Actuator]
    nodeActuator --> nodePlant[Plant: Vehicle Dynamics]
    nodePlant --> nodeFeedback[Feedback Sensor]
    nodeFeedback --> nodeComparator[Comparator with Reference]
    nodeComparator --> nodeInput1
    nodeComparator --> nodeInput2
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRef[/Reference Input r/] --> nodeComparator[Comparator]
    nodeRefModel[Reference Model] --> nodeComparator
    nodeComparator --> nodeError[e = y minus ym]
    nodeError --> nodeAdjMech[Adjustment Mechanism: MIT Rule]
    nodeAdjMech --> nodeController[Adaptive Controller with Gain theta]
    nodeRef --> nodeController
    nodeController --> nodePlant[Plant: Engine or Suspension]
    nodePlant --> nodeOutput[/Plant Output y/]
    nodeOutput --> nodeRefModel
    nodeOutput --> nodeComparator
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput[/Reference r/] --> nodeController[Controller with Gain K]
    nodeController --> nodePlant[Plant]
    nodePlant --> nodeOutput[/Output y/]
    nodeOutput --> nodeEstimator[Parameter Estimator RLS]
    nodeEstimator --> nodeDesign[Controller Design Block]
    nodeDesign --> nodeController
    nodeInput --> nodeDesign
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Application Mapping: Fuzzy Logic in an Automotive ABS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeWhlSpd[Wheel Speed Sensor] --> nodeSlipCalc[Slip Calculator]
    nodeVehSpd[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> nodeSlipCalc
    nodeSlipCalc --> nodeInput1[Fuzzification of Slip]
    nodeSlipCalc --> nodeInput2[Fuzzification of Slip Rate]
    nodeInput1 --> nodeFLC[Fuzzy Logic Controller]
    nodeInput2 --> nodeFLC
    nodeFLC --> nodeHydraulic[Hydraulic Modulator]
    nodeHydraulic --> nodeBrake[Wheel Brake]
    nodeBrake --> nodeWhlSpd
```

---

## 4.5 Conceptual Hierarchy of Modern Control Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop[Modern Control Strategies] --> nodeFLC[Fuzzy Logic Control]
    nodeTop --> nodeAC[Adaptive Control]
    nodeTop --> nodeNN[Neural Network Control]
    nodeTop --> nodeNF[Neuro Fuzzy Control]
    nodeFLC --> nodeF1[Mamdani Type]
    nodeFLC --> nodeF2[Sugeno Type]
    nodeAC --> nodeA1[MRAC]
    nodeAC --> nodeA2[STR]
    nodeAC --> nodeA3[Gain Scheduling]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in KTU exams:** Examiners will often give you a block diagram with one blank box and ask you to identify it. Remember — in FLC the order is always *Fuzzification → Knowledge Base → Inference → Defuzzification*, and in MRAC the loop is always *Reference Model → Comparator → Adjustment Mechanism → Controller → Plant*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]

**Q: Differentiate between classical PID control and Fuzzy Logic Control. List any two automotive applications of FLC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Classical PID | Fuzzy Logic Control |
|---|---|---|
| Model dependency | Requires accurate linear model | No mathematical model needed |
| Knowledge representation | Numerical gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ | Linguistic IF–THEN rules |
| Non-linearity handling | Poor (linear by design) | Excellent (built-in non-linearity) |
| Tuning | Ziegler-Nichols or empirical | Expert rules + MF tuning |

**Two automotive applications (1 mark each):**
1. Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) — slip & slip-rate FLC.
2. Automatic transmission shift scheduling.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]

**Q: What is meant by the term "adaptation gain" in the MIT rule? What happens if it is chosen too large?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **adaptation gain** $\gamma$ in the MIT rule

$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

controls the **speed** at which the controller parameter $\theta$ updates in response to the tracking error.

- A larger $\gamma$ means faster adaptation but smaller stability margin.
- If $\gamma$ is **too large**, the parameter may overshoot, oscillate, or even cause **closed-loop instability** because the system does not have enough time to "settle" between successive updates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "more $\gamma$ = better response". The examiner expects the answer to mention the **stability trade-off**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice Pattern (KTU ESE 2024 Scheme)

> Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2023 Style]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Fuzzy Logic Controller. Discuss each block in detail and explain how the centroid method is used for defuzzification.**

#### (a) Architecture and Block-by-Block Explanation — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

A Fuzzy Logic Controller consists of **four major blocks** as shown below:

1. **Fuzzification Interface:** Converts crisp numerical inputs into fuzzy linguistic values using pre-defined **Membership Functions**. Each input $x_i$ is mapped to a degree $\mu_A(x_i) \in [0,1]$ for every fuzzy set $A$.

2. **Knowledge Base:** This block stores:
   - The **Database** (definitions of MFs for all linguistic variables).
   - The **Rule Base** (a set of expert IF–THEN rules). For example: `IF slip is High AND slip-rate is Positive THEN brake-pressure is Decrease`.

3. **Inference Engine:** Performs fuzzy reasoning. It takes the fuzzified inputs, evaluates every rule's antecedent, applies an implication operator (typically MIN), and aggregates the conclusions using a union operator (typically MAX) to produce the overall fuzzy output.

4. **Defuzzification Interface:** Converts the aggregated fuzzy output into a single crisp numerical value $y^*$ suitable to drive the actuator.

*(A neat block diagram — refer to Section 4.1 above — should accompany this answer. Award full marks if the student provides a labelled diagram with all four blocks.)*

#### (b) Centroid Defuzzification — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

The Centroid (Centre of Gravity) method computes the crisp output as:

$$y^{*} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{y_{\min}}^{y_{\max}} y \cdot \mu_B(y) \, dy}{\displaystyle\int_{y_{\min}}^{y_{\max}} \mu_B(y) \, dy}$$

where $\mu_B(y)$ is the aggregated membership of the output fuzzy set $B$.

**Working Steps:**
1. Compute the area under the aggregated MF — this is the denominator.
2. Compute the first moment of area about the origin — this is the numerator.
3. Take the ratio to obtain the crisp value.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Smooth and continuous output.
- Considers the *entire* shape of the MF, not just peaks.
- Most widely used in industrial and automotive controllers.

**Disadvantages (½ mark):**
- Computationally intensive due to integration.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: Explain the Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) scheme with a block diagram. Derive the MIT rule for adapting a single controller parameter and discuss its limitations.**

#### (a) MRAC Architecture and Block Diagram — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

In MRAC, a **reference model** specifies the desired closed-loop response. The controller parameters are adjusted so that the plant output $y(t)$ tracks the model output $y_m(t)$ as closely as possible.

**Block Diagram Description (refer to Section 4.2 above):**
- Reference input $r(t)$ is fed simultaneously to the reference model and the controller.
- The plant output $y(t)$ is compared to $y_m(t)$ to form the error $e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$.
- The error drives the **adjustment mechanism**, which updates the controller gain $\theta$ online using the MIT rule.
- The closed-loop adaptively minimises $\dfrac{1}{2}e^2$.

#### (b) Derivation of the MIT Rule and Its Limitations — 7 Marks

**Model Solution — Derivation:**

Define the cost function:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} e^{2}(t)$$

Take the time derivative:

$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$$

By chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

Therefore:

$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

This is the MIT rule.

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Stability:** The MIT rule guarantees convergence only when the adaptation gain $\gamma$ is *sufficiently small* and the plant is slowly varying. For large $\gamma$, the system may become unstable.
2. **Sensitivity dependence:** It assumes the sensitivity derivative $\partial e / \partial \theta$ is computable and bounded.
3. **Lack of robustness:** Unmodelled dynamics or disturbances can drive the parameters to drift.
4. **Modern alternative:** Lyapunov-based MRAC and Normalised MIT rule address these issues and are preferred in production automotive ECUs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **Fuzzification** (input → fuzzy) with **Defuzzification** (fuzzy → crisp). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks.
> 2. Drawing the FLC block diagram but forgetting to label the **Knowledge Base** as consisting of both *Rule Base* and *Database*.
> 3. For MRAC derivations: forgetting to define the cost function $J$ *before* applying gradient descent — this costs 1 mark.
> 4. Using the symbol `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables — broken tables = 0 marks for that sub-part in the answer sheet.
> 5. Failing to mention the **stability limitation** of the MIT rule — examiners specifically test this in 14-mark questions.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC)** is rule-based; uses **linguistic variables** instead of numerical equations.
- ✅ The **four blocks** of an FLC (in order) are: **Fuzzification → Knowledge Base → Inference → Defuzzification**.
- ✅ **Membership functions** map crisp values to degrees of belonging $\mu \in [0,1]$; common shapes are **triangular**, **trapezoidal**, and **Gaussian**.
- ✅ The **Centroid (COG)** defuzzification formula is:
$$y^{*} = \dfrac{\int y \cdot \mu_B(y) \, dy}{\int \mu_B(y) \, dy}$$
- ✅ **Adaptive Control** self-tunes its parameters online; the two main types are **MRAC** and **STR**; **Gain Scheduling** is a simpler related technique.
- ✅ The **MIT Rule** is:
$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$
- ✅ **MIT Rule stability**: large $\gamma$ can cause instability; prefer **Lyapunov-based** or **Normalised MIT** variants in safety-critical automotive systems.
- ✅ **Automotive applications**: FLC in **ABS, traction control, automatic transmission**; Adaptive Control in **active suspension, fuel injection, EV battery management**.
- ✅ In KTU answers, always **label diagrams clearly** and **state the cost function** before deriving adaptive laws — these are the two biggest single mark-grabbers.
- ✅ Remember the slip ratio definition for ABS problems: $\lambda = (v_{veh} - v_{whl}) / v_{veh}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Mechatronics in Modern Vehicles like ABS, Airbag, Active Suspension, Rain Sensing Wipers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of Mechatronics in Modern Vehicles like ABS, Airbag, Active Suspension, Rain Sensing Wipers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Automotive Mechatronics — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Mechatronics (KTU 2024 Definition):** The synergistic integration of **mechanical engineering**, **electronics**, **control engineering**, and **computer science** within a vehicle, wherein embedded sensors, microcontrollers, and actuators operate in closed-loop feedback to perform dynamic safety, comfort, and drivability functions that would otherwise be impossible by any single discipline alone.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, automotive mechatronics is treated as a *system-of-systems* discipline — every modern vehicle subsystem is viewed as a **Sense → Decide → Actuate** triad governed by real-time embedded firmware.

### 1.1.1 The Generic Mechatronic Block

$$S_{\text{out}}(t) = f\!\left( K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right)$$

where $e(t) = S_{\text{ref}} - S_{\text{meas}}$ is the feedback error and $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are the controller gains. This **PID paradigm** unifies ABS, Airbag, Active Suspension, and Rain-Sensing Wipers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogies (Plain-English Intuition)

| Subsystem | Layman's Analogy | Key Mechatronic Insight |
|---|---|---|
| **ABS** | A driver pumping the brake pedal 15 times/sec on ice | Electronic *pumping* via solenoid valves — replaces human reflex |
| **Airbag** | A cannon firing a soft pillow at 200 mph *just before* you hit the windshield | Crash *prediction* (must fire in **< 30 ms**) |
| **Active Suspension** | A waiter balancing a tray of water while walking | Sensors detect body motion; actuators cancel it in real time |
| **Rain-Sensing Wiper** | A friend who wipes your windshield *only when* it starts to drizzle | Optical sensing of water-film refractive index |

---

## 1.3 The Four Target Systems — One-Line Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **KTU 2024 Module-2 exam-favorite definitions**. Memorize verbatim.

1. **Antilock Braking System (ABS):** A closed-loop hydraulic braking control system that prevents wheel lock-up by modulating brake-line pressure based on wheel-speed feedback.
2. **Airbag System (SRS — Supplemental Restraint System):** A pyrotechnic safety system that uses crash-deceleration sensors to inflate a nylon cushion with nitrogen gas in under **30 ms**.
3. **Active Suspension System:** A computer-controlled suspension employing hydraulic/electromechanical actuators to actively counteract chassis disturbances and maintain a *constant ride height*.
4. **Rain-Sensing Wiper System:** An optical (infra-red) sensor-based automatic wiper controller that detects water droplets on the windshield and triggers the wiper motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Hint:** When asked "is X a mechatronic system?", justify using the triad — **Sensor (electrical) + Microprocessor (computing) + Actuator (mechanical)**. If all three are present, the system qualifies.

---

## 1.4 Why These Four Systems Together?

These four systems were chosen by the KTU syllabus committee because they span the **full control-time spectrum**:

- **ABS** → milliseconds control ($\approx 10$–$100$ ms loop time)
- **Airbag** → sub-millisecond actuation ($\approx 5$–$30$ ms)
- **Active Suspension** → tens of milliseconds ($10$–$50$ Hz)
- **Rain Wiper** → slow adaptive control ($100$ ms–$1$ s)

They also span **passive-to-active** spectrum and **safety-to-comfort** spectrum — making them a pedagogically complete set.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ABS — Antilock Braking System

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

The ABS continuously compares the **wheel deceleration** $\dot{\omega}_w$ with a reference deceleration $a_{\text{ref}} \approx 0.3g$ to $0.5g$ (where $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$). When impending lock-up is detected, the **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** commands three solenoid valves per wheel:

| Valve State | Hydraulic Effect | Brake Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Increase** | Inlet valve open | **Rises** |
| **Pressure Hold** | Inlet + outlet closed | **Constant** |
| **Pressure Decrease** | Outlet valve open | **Drops** |

This 3-state cycle occurs **15–20 times per second** — far faster than a human's $\approx 2$ Hz reaction.

### 2.1.2 Slip Ratio Definition

$$\lambda = \frac{v_{\text{veh}} - v_{\text{wheel}}}{v_{\text{veh}}} = 1 - \frac{\omega_w \, R_w}{v_{\text{veh}}}$$

where:

- $v_{\text{veh}}$ = vehicle body speed (m/s)
- $v_{\text{wheel}} = \omega_w R_w$ = wheel linear speed
- $\omega_w$ = wheel angular velocity (rad/s)
- $R_w$ = effective wheel radius (m)

### 2.1.3 μ-λ Curve (Tire-Road Friction)

The maximum braking force is achieved in the **stable region** ($\lambda \approx 8$–$30\%$). The ABS controller's job is to maintain $\lambda$ in this window.

$$\mu(\lambda) = a + b\,\lambda - c\,\lambda^2$$

This is the **Pacejka Magic Formula** simplified form, where $a, b, c$ are tire-specific empirical constants.

### 2.1.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — ABS

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slip ratio | $\lambda = 1 - \dfrac{\omega_w R_w}{v_{\text{veh}}}$ | dimensionless | Tire slip state |
| Friction coefficient | $\mu = \dfrac{F_{\text{brake}}}{F_z}$ | dimensionless | Braking efficiency |
| Max slip (dry asphalt) | $\lambda_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.15$ | — | Optimal braking point |
| Max slip (icy road) | $\lambda_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.08$ | — | Reduced grip window |
| Wheel deceleration | $a_w = R_w \dfrac{d\omega_w}{dt}$ | m/s² | Lock-up detection signal |
| Stopping distance | $s = \dfrac{v_0^2}{2 \mu g}$ | m | ABS reduces this by **≈ 30 %** |
| Cycle frequency | $f_{\text{ABS}} = 15$–$20$ Hz | Hz | Solenoid switching rate |

---

## 2.2 Airbag System (SRS — Supplemental Restraint System)

### 2.2.1 Crash Sensing Mechanism

Two sensor classes are used in modern vehicles:

1. **Mechanical (ball-in-tube) sensor** — a steel ball held by a permanent magnet; under deceleration exceeding threshold ($\approx 5g$ to $10g$), the ball overcomes magnetic force and closes a contact.
2. **Electronic accelerometer** — MEMS capacitive sensor outputting voltage proportional to $a(t)$.

### 2.2.2 Inflation Chemistry

The inflator contains **sodium azide** ($\text{NaN}_3$), which undergoes rapid pyrotechnic decomposition:

$$2\,\text{NaN}_3 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{Na} + 3\,\text{N}_2\uparrow$$

This produces approximately **$60$–$120$ litres of nitrogen gas** within **$25$–$30$ ms**, sufficient to inflate a cushion of volume $\approx 60$ L.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** Students often confuse *Airbag* with *Seatbelt*. The Seatbelt is the **primary** restraint; the Airbag is **supplemental** — hence the name *SRS*.

### 2.2.3 Deployment Timing

| Phase | Time | Event |
|---|---|---|
| T₀ | 0 ms | Crash initiated |
| T₁ | $\approx 5$–$15$ ms | Sensors detect deceleration threshold |
| T₂ | $\approx 20$ ms | Squib (igniter) fires |
| T₃ | $\approx 30$ ms | Bag fully inflated |
| T₄ | $\approx 50$–$80$ ms | Bag begins deflation (vents) |

> [!WARNING]
> A deployed airbag cannot be reused — the entire **SRS module**, including inflator, must be replaced after one firing.

### 2.2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Airbag

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deceleration threshold | $a_{\text{thr}} \approx 5g$–$10g$ | m/s² | $1g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| Gas produced | $V_{\text{N}_2} = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$ | m³ | Ideal gas |
| Inflation time | $t_{\text{infl}} \le 30$ ms | ms | Mandated by FMVSS 208 |
| Decel of head impact | $a_{\text{head}} = \dfrac{v^2}{2\,d}$ | m/s² | $d$ = bag thickness cushioning |
| Sodium azide mass | $m_{\text{NaN}_3} = M \cdot n$ | g | $M = 65$ g/mol |

---

## 2.3 Active Suspension System

### 2.3.1 Sky-Hook Control Concept

A passive suspension can only dissipate energy; an *active* suspension injects energy to *cancel road disturbances*. The **sky-hook damping** strategy mimics a damper connected to an imaginary "sky" reference:

$$F_{\text{skyhook}} = -C_{\text{sky}} \cdot \dot{z}_{\text{b}}$$

where $\dot{z}_{\text{b}}$ is the body (sprung mass) vertical velocity. This is contrasted with the standard passive damper force:

$$F_{\text{passive}} = -C \cdot (\dot{z}_{\text{b}} - \dot{z}_{\text{u}})$$

### 2.3.2 Quarter-Car Model

The standard 2-DOF model:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
-\dfrac{k_s}{m_s} & \dfrac{k_s}{m_s} & -\dfrac{c_s}{m_s} & \dfrac{c_s}{m_s} \\
\dfrac{k_s}{m_u} & -\dfrac{k_s+k_t}{m_u} & \dfrac{c_s}{m_u} & -\dfrac{c_s}{m_u}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \dfrac{k_t}{m_u} \end{bmatrix} \, r(t)$$

where:

- $m_s$ = sprung mass (body), $m_u$ = unsprung mass (wheel)
- $k_s$ = spring stiffness, $k_t$ = tire stiffness
- $c_s$ = damping coefficient
- $r(t)$ = road input disturbance

### 2.3.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Active Suspension

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_s}{m_s}}$ | Hz | Body bounce mode |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c_s}{2\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$ | dimensionless | Comfort vs. handling trade-off |
| Sky-hook force | $F_{\text{sh}} = C_{\text{sky}}\,\dot{z}_b$ | N | Active control law |
| Road isolation | $T(j\omega) = \dfrac{\omega_n^2}{(j\omega)^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n(j\omega) + \omega_n^2}$ | — | Transfer function |
| Comfort band | $f < 5$ Hz | Hz | Human sensitivity range |

---

## 2.4 Rain-Sensing Wipers

### 2.4.1 Optical Detection Principle

A pair of **infrared LEDs** projects IR light ($\lambda \approx 940$ nm) onto the inside of the windshield at a critical angle (total internal reflection geometry). A photodiode receives the reflected light:

- **Dry glass** → high reflection (signal high)
- **Wet glass** → scattered/refracted light (signal drops)

The signal change $\Delta V$ is mapped to wiper speed via a look-up table.

### 2.4.2 Sensitivity Equation

$$V_{\text{photo}} = V_0 \, R(\theta) \, e^{-\alpha d_{\text{water}}}$$

where $R(\theta)$ is the reflection coefficient, $\alpha$ is the water absorption coefficient, and $d_{\text{water}}$ is the effective water-film thickness.

### 2.4.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Rain Sensor

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| IR wavelength | $\lambda_{\text{IR}} \approx 940$ nm | nm | Near-IR band |
| Critical angle (glass/air) | $\theta_c = \arcsin(n_2 / n_1)$ | degrees | TIR threshold |
| Refractive index, glass | $n_1 \approx 1.5$ | — | Soda-lime glass |
| Refractive index, water | $n_2 \approx 1.33$ | — | |
| Response time | $t_r \approx 100$–$500$ ms | ms | Detection latency |
| Sensitivity levels | $5$–$7$ discrete steps | — | Driver-adjustable |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Slip Ratio → Optimal Braking Torque

### Step 1 — Define the Kinematic Slip Ratio

We start from first principles. Let $v_v$ be the linear velocity of the vehicle chassis and $\omega_w$ the angular velocity of the wheel. The contact patch linear speed is:

$$v_{\text{contact}} = \omega_w \, R_w$$

The **slip ratio** $\lambda$ is the normalized difference between vehicle speed and wheel speed:

$$\lambda = \frac{v_v - v_{\text{contact}}}{v_v} = \frac{v_v - \omega_w R_w}{v_v}$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange the Equation

We can also express this in the inverted form commonly used in ECU code:

$$\lambda = 1 - \frac{\omega_w R_w}{v_v}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For pure rolling, $\lambda = 0$. For locked wheel ($\omega_w = 0$), $\lambda = 1$ (**100 % slip**).

### Step 3 — Compute Maximum Friction

Using the simplified Pacejka expression with $a, b, c$ as tire constants:

$$\mu(\lambda) = a + b\,\lambda - c\,\lambda^2$$

Differentiate to find the optimal slip:

$$\frac{d\mu}{d\lambda} = b - 2c\lambda = 0$$

$$\lambda_{\text{opt}} = \frac{b}{2c}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Maximum Braking Force

$$F_{\text{brake,max}} = \mu_{\text{max}} \, F_z = \left(a + \frac{b^2}{4c}\right) F_z$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Braking Torque

$$T_{\text{brake,opt}} = F_{\text{brake,max}} \cdot R_w = \mu_{\text{max}} \, F_z \, R_w$$

This is the **target torque** the ABS ECU attempts to deliver — never exceeding the slip ratio $\lambda_{\text{opt}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ECU never commands $F_{\text{brake}} > \mu_{\text{max}} F_z$ because doing so would push the tire into the unstable lock-up region.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Airbag Inflation — Gas Dynamics

### Step 1 — Reaction Stoichiometry

$$2\,\text{NaN}_3(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{Na}(s) + 3\,\text{N}_2(g)$$

Molar mass of $\text{NaN}_3$ is $M = 65$ g/mol. For 100 g of sodium azide:

$$n_{\text{NaN}_3} = \frac{100}{65} = 1.538 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{3}{2} \times 1.538 = 2.308 \, \text{mol}$$

### Step 2 — Ideal Gas Law at Standard Conditions

Assume $T = 1500$ K (post-combustion), $P = 1.2 \times 10^5$ Pa:

$$V_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{n R T}{P}$$

where $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Substituting:

$$V_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{2.308 \times 8.314 \times 1500}{1.2 \times 10^5}$$

$$V_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{28{,}778}{120{,}000}$$

$$V_{\text{N}_2} \approx 0.240 \, \text{m}^3 = 240 \, \text{L}$$

This exceeds the **60 L cushion volume** by 4× — the excess vents harmlessly through calibrated exhaust ports.

### Step 3 — Cushioning Deceleration of Head

If a 5 kg head strikes the inflated bag and decelerates from $v = 25$ m/s ($\approx 90$ km/h) over a stopping distance $d = 0.15$ m:

$$a = \frac{v^2}{2d} = \frac{25^2}{2 \times 0.15} = \frac{625}{0.30}$$

$$a \approx 2083 \, \text{m/s}^2 \approx 212\,g$$

This is still **far below** the $\approx 1000\,g$ limit for survivable head injury — illustrating why bag thickness is engineered to such precision.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Active Suspension — Sky-Hook Damping

### Step 1 — Passive Damper Force (Reference)

For a standard passive damper between sprung mass ($m_s$) and unsprung mass ($m_u$):

$$F_{\text{damp}} = -c_s (\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$

### Step 2 — Sky-Hook Active Force

Imagine the damper connected to a stationary inertial frame ("the sky") rather than to the unsprung mass:

$$F_{\text{sky}} = -C_{\text{sky}} \cdot \dot{z}_s$$

### Step 3 — Total Active Damper Force

A real active actuator must deliver the *difference* between the sky-hook force and what a passive damper would have done:

$$F_{\text{actuator}} = F_{\text{sky}} - F_{\text{passive}} = -C_{\text{sky}}\,\dot{z}_s + c_s(\dot{z}_s - \dot{z}_u)$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{actuator}} = -(C_{\text{sky}} - c_s)\,\dot{z}_s - c_s\,\dot{z}_u}$$

### Step 4 — Body-Bounce Natural Frequency

The body's vertical resonant frequency in the comfort band:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}}$$

For typical $k_s = 25{,}000$ N/m and $m_s = 400$ kg per corner:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{25000}{400}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{62.5} = \frac{7.91}{6.283}$$

$$f_n \approx 1.26 \, \text{Hz}$$

This is **below** the human comfort threshold of $5$ Hz — well-engineered.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Rain Sensor Threshold Logic

### Step 1 — Photodiode Output Voltage

$$V_{\text{photo}} = V_0 \, e^{-\alpha d_w}$$

where $V_0 \approx 4.5$ V (dry reference), $\alpha \approx 50$ /mm, $d_w$ = effective water-film thickness in mm.

### Step 2 — Threshold Mapping Table

| $d_w$ (µm) | $V_{\text{photo}}$ (V) | ECU Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (dry) | 4.50 | Wiper OFF |
| 50 | 3.27 | Intermittent (slow) |
| 150 | 1.24 | Intermittent (fast) |
| 500 | 0.0008 | Continuous (high speed) |

### Step 3 — Wiper Delay

To prevent jitter, a **debounce filter** is added:

$$V_{\text{filt}}(t) = \frac{1}{\tau}\int_0^t V_{\text{photo}}(\tau')\,e^{-(t-\tau')/\tau}\,d\tau'$$

where $\tau \approx 200$ ms is the filter time constant. This ensures the wiper does not chatter in misty/drizzle conditions.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | ABS | Airbag | Active Suspension | Rain Wiper |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Loop time** | 10–100 ms | 5–30 ms | 10–50 ms | 100–500 ms |
| **Sensor** | Wheel-speed encoder | MEMS accelerometer | Body-height, accel | IR photodiode |
| **Actuator** | Hydraulic solenoid | Pyrotechnic inflator | Hydraulic/electromotor | DC wiper motor |
| **Controller** | 16-bit MCU | ASIC + safety MCU | 32-bit DSP | 8-bit MCU |
| **Control law** | Sliding mode / PID | Threshold (discrete) | Sky-hook / LQR | Lookup table |
| **Power level** | $\sim 500$ W | $\sim 100$ kW (pulse) | $\sim 200$ W | $\sim 50$ W |
| **Safety class** | Active safety | Passive safety | Comfort + safety | Comfort |
| **Failure mode** | Reverts to conventional brake | Warning lamp on | Reverts to passive | Manual mode |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Antilock Braking System (ABS). Mention any three advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A typical ABS consists of: (i) **wheel speed sensors** (magnetic pick-up / Hall-effect) at each wheel, (ii) an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** that processes sensor signals and computes slip ratio $\lambda = 1 - \omega_w R_w / v_{\text{veh}}$, and (iii) a **hydraulic modulator** with three solenoid valves per wheel (pressure-increase, pressure-hold, pressure-decrease).

**Working:** When the driver applies the brake, if the ECU detects an impending wheel lock (high $\lambda$ or sudden deceleration), it cycles the solenoid valves 15–20 times/second to modulate brake-line pressure, keeping the tire in the high-friction zone.

**Three advantages:**
1. Prevents wheel lock-up — maintains **steerability** during emergency braking.
2. Reduces stopping distance on slippery surfaces by **≈ 30 %**.
3. Minimizes tire wear due to skidding.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**List the main components of a Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) airbag. What is the role of the squib?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Main components:**
1. **Crash sensor(s)** — mechanical ball-in-tube and/or MEMS accelerometer.
2. **SRS ECU** — dual-channel microcontroller with self-diagnostic capability.
3. **Squib (igniter)** — pyrotechnic initiator inside the inflator.
4. **Inflator** — sodium azide ($\text{NaN}_3$) propellant that generates $\text{N}_2$ gas.
5. **Airbag cushion** — nylon fabric, volume $\approx 60$ L, with calibrated vent holes.
6. **SRS warning lamp** — driver dashboard indicator.

**Role of the squib:** The squib is a thin resistive wire (bridge-wire) that, on receiving a current pulse from the ECU's capacitor discharge circuit, ignites and triggers the sodium azide propellant, producing nitrogen gas for inflation within 25–30 ms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 marks each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of an **Active Suspension System** with a sky-hook control strategy. Derive the expression for the natural frequency of the sprung mass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the working of a **Rain-Sensing Wiper System**. State two limitations of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a): Active Suspension with Sky-Hook Control**

**Construction (3 marks):**

An active suspension replaces (or augments) the conventional damper with a force-generating actuator — either a hydraulic cylinder with servo-valve, an electro-hydraulic actuator, or a linear electromagnetic motor. The system comprises:

- **Sprung mass** ($m_s$) — vehicle body
- **Unsprung mass** ($m_u$) — wheel/axle assembly
- **Coil spring** ($k_s$) — supports static load
- **Active actuator** — delivers controlled force $F_a$
- **Sensors** — body accelerometer, ride-height sensor, suspension stroke sensor
- **ECU** — 32-bit DSP running the control algorithm

**Working — Sky-Hook Control (2 marks):**

The sky-hook control law emulates a virtual damper connected between the sprung mass and an inertial "sky" reference:

$$F_{\text{skyhook}} = -C_{\text{sky}} \, \dot{z}_s$$

The ECU commands the actuator to deliver this force, achieving **superior low-frequency road-isolation** compared to passive damping.

**Derivation of natural frequency (2 marks):**

For free vibration of the sprung mass on the spring:

$$m_s \, \ddot{z}_s + k_s \, z_s = 0$$

Standard second-order ODE with natural angular frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}}$$

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_s}{m_s}}$$

For $k_s = 25{,}000$ N/m, $m_s = 400$ kg: $f_n \approx 1.26$ Hz (within human comfort band).

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(b): Rain-Sensing Wiper System**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.4** Mermaid diagram. The components are:

- IR LED emitter (940 nm)
- Photodiode receiver
- Transimpedance amplifier
- Microcontroller with look-up table
- Wiper motor + relay driver

**Working (2 marks):**

The IR LED projects light onto the windshield at the critical angle for total internal reflection. In dry conditions, the photodiode receives a high reflected signal. When water droplets accumulate, light scatters and the photodiode voltage drops. The ECU maps this voltage drop to one of 5–7 wiper-speed settings via a look-up table, with a debounce filter ($\tau \approx 200$ ms) preventing jitter.

**Limitations (1 mark):**

1. The sensor may be fooled by **dust, dirt, or insect splatter** — triggering false wipes.
2. Performance degrades when the windshield has **cracks or stone-chips** that scatter IR light.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working of an **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** with a block diagram. Define **slip ratio** and derive the relationship between vehicle speed, wheel speed, and slip ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **Automotive Airbag System**. Discuss the chemistry of inflation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a): ABS — Slip Ratio Derivation**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1** Mermaid diagram. The major blocks are: wheel-speed sensors → ECU → slip-ratio calculator → solenoid-driver → hydraulic modulator → wheel cylinders.

**Definition of Slip Ratio (2 marks):**

Slip ratio $\lambda$ is the dimensionless measure of how much the wheel is sliding relative to the road:

$$\lambda = \frac{v_v - \omega_w R_w}{v_v}$$

where $v_v$ = vehicle body speed (m/s), $\omega_w$ = wheel angular velocity (rad/s), $R_w$ = effective tire radius (m).

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Starting from the definition of slip:
- Pure rolling → $v_v = \omega_w R_w$ → $\lambda = 0$
- Locked wheel → $\omega_w = 0$ → $\lambda = 1$
- Rearranging: $\omega_w R_w = v_v (1 - \lambda)$

The ECU computes $\lambda$ in real time by reading each wheel-speed sensor and inferring $v_v$ from the *fastest-spinning, non-driven wheel* (or via a longitudinal accelerometer).

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(b): Airbag System**

**Construction (3 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** Mermaid diagram. Major components: crash sensor (MEMS accel), SRS ECU, squib, sodium azide inflator, nylon cushion, vent holes, warning lamp.

**Working (2 marks):**

Upon impact exceeding $\approx 5$–$10g$, the sensor closes a contact. The ECU confirms (via dual-channel voting), then triggers the squib capacitor discharge. The squib ignites the sodium azide pellets, producing nitrogen gas within 25–30 ms. The cushion inflates, and vent holes release excess gas to absorb occupant kinetic energy gently.

**Chemistry (2 marks):**

$$2\,\text{NaN}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{Na} + 3\,\text{N}_2\uparrow$$

The solid Na by-product is reacted with $\text{KNO}_3$ to form harmless silicates:

$$10\,\text{Na} + 2\,\text{KNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{K}_2\text{O} + 5\,\text{Na}_2\text{O} + \text{N}_2$$

This two-step chemistry ensures **non-toxic effluents**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Exam):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Active" and "Passive" Suspension:** A passive suspension only dissipates energy; an *active* one *adds* energy. If a student writes "active suspension uses only springs and dampers", they will lose **3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Every numerical answer must include units. Missing unit on slip ratio (dimensionless) is acceptable; missing unit on natural frequency (Hz) costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Airbag Chemistry:** Writing "airbag uses compressed air" is **wrong**. It must be a *chemical generation* of nitrogen via sodium azide decomposition.
> 4. **ABS Slip Ratio Sign Convention:** Some textbooks define slip as $(v_w - v_v)/v_v$. KTU follows the **ECE R13** convention: $\lambda = (v_v - v_w)/v_v$. Using the wrong sign costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Rain-Sensor Mechanism:** Writing "uses raindrops to complete a circuit" is incorrect. It uses **optical IR reflection**, not conductivity.
> 6. **Block Diagram:** Always label **every block** and **every signal arrow**. A Mermaid/text diagram without arrows between blocks gets **0/3 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ABS** maintains tire slip ratio $\lambda$ in the **8–30 %** range via 15–20 Hz solenoid cycling.
- **Airbag** inflates in **≤ 30 ms** using **$\text{NaN}_3$ decomposition** producing $\text{N}_2$ gas.
- **Active suspension** uses the **sky-hook** control law to cancel sprung-mass vertical velocity.
- **Rain-sensing wiper** uses **IR total internal reflection** with photodiode detection.
- All four are **Sense → Decide → Actuate** closed-loop mechatronic systems.
- **Loop time hierarchy:** Airbag (5–30 ms) < ABS (10–100 ms) < Suspension (10–50 ms loop) < Rain wiper (100–500 ms).
- **Safety class:** Airbag & ABS = **active/passive safety**; Suspension & Wiper = **comfort/safety**.
- **Failure mode:** All systems must **fail safely** to a passive/manual mode.
- **Refractive indices to memorize:** Glass $n_1 \approx 1.5$, Water $n_2 \approx 1.33$, Critical angle $\theta_c \approx 62.5°$.
- **Molar mass:** Sodium azide $M(\text{NaN}_3) = 65$ g/mol; produces 1.5 mol $\text{N}_2$ per mol $\text{NaN}_3$.
- **Pacejka constants $a, b, c$** are **tire-specific** — different for dry, wet, snow, ice.
- **Body natural frequency** must stay **below 5 Hz** for human comfort ($\approx 1$–$1.5$ Hz typical).
- **Deceleration threshold for airbag:** $5g$ to $10g$ (where $g = 9.81$ m/s²).
- **Sodium azide toxicity:** modern inflators add $\text{KNO}_3$ to neutralize Na byproducts.
- **Mechatronic triad test:** Any system qualifies as "mechatronic" if it has *sensor + processor + actuator*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ADAS Driverless Cars - Working with Sensing and Actuating Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)** is an umbrella term for a cluster of electronic, sensor-based, and mechatronic subsystems integrated into a vehicle that assist the human driver in monitoring the driving environment, reducing cognitive load, and actively performing dynamic driving tasks to enhance **safety**, **comfort**, and **fuel economy**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: OEMUT834)** syllabus, ADAS is the bridge between conventional driver-controlled vehicles and fully autonomous (driverless) vehicles. The term **"Driverless Car"** (also called an **Autonomous Vehicle (AV)** or **Self-Driving Car**) refers to a vehicle capable of performing all dynamic driving tasks (longitudinal control, lateral control, OEDR — Object and Event Detection and Response) without any human intervention.

The performance of ADAS is graded by the **SAE J3016 Standard** which defines **6 Levels of Driving Automation** (Level 0 to Level 5).

## 1.2 SAE J3016 — Levels of Driving Automation

| Level | Name | Driver Involvement | System Capability |
|-------|------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **0** | No Automation | Full (100%) | Only warnings (e.g., blind-spot alert) |
| **1** | Driver Assistance | Continuous | Either steering **OR** acceleration/braking |
| **2** | Partial Automation | Continuous | Steering **AND** acceleration/braking together |
| **3** | Conditional Automation | Fallback-ready | Full driving; driver must take back on request |
| **4** | High Automation | Not required in geo-fenced area | Full driving without driver in defined zones |
| **5** | Full Automation | None | Driverless in **all** conditions |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** A common trick question is *"At which SAE level does a vehicle become truly 'driverless'?"* — The precise answer is **Level 5** (Full Automation). Level 4 is driverless **only within a restricted operational design domain (ODD)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car is a Human"

Think of a driverless car exactly like a **human being driving a vehicle**:

- **Eyes and Ears = Sensors (Sensing Layer)**
  - The car "sees" using **cameras, LiDAR, Radar, and Ultrasonic sensors**.
- **Brain = ECU / Domain Controllers (Processing Layer)**
  - Data from sensors is fused, processed, and decisions are made using algorithms (sensor fusion, AI/ML).
- **Hands and Legs = Actuators (Actuating Layer)**
  - The car "acts" by controlling **steering, throttle, and brakes** via mechatronic actuators.

Just as a human can drive only when senses (vision), brain (decision-making), and limbs (action) all work together, a driverless car requires a tight, real-time integration of **Sensing → Processing → Actuating** — a mechatronic loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of ADAS / Driverless Car Architecture:**
> 1. **SENSE** — Collect environmental data (cameras, radar, LiDAR, GPS, IMU).
> 2. **THINK** — Process and interpret the data (ECUs, AI/ML, sensor fusion).
> 3. **ACT** — Execute vehicle control (EPS, electronic throttle, brake-by-wire).

---

## 1.4 Key Functional Components in ADAS / Driverless Cars

**A. Sensing Components (Perception Layer)**

- **Cameras** — Visible + IR imaging (object/lane/sign recognition).
- **Radar (RAdio Detection And Ranging)** — Long-range object detection, adaptive cruise control.
- **LiDAR (Light Detection And Ranging)** — High-resolution 3D point cloud, 360° mapping.
- **Ultrasonic Sensors** — Short-range (< 5 m) parking/low-speed obstacle detection.
- **GPS + GNSS** — Absolute vehicle position (latitude/longitude).
- **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — Accelerometer + gyroscope for orientation and motion.
- **Wheel Speed Sensors / Encoders** — Ground speed and yaw rate.
- **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** — Communication with other cars and infrastructure.

**B. Processing / Decision-Making Components**

- **ADAS Domain Controller (SoC)** — e.g., NVIDIA Drive PX, Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride.
- **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** — Embedded microcontrollers running RTOS.
- **Sensor Fusion Algorithms** — Kalman Filter, Bayesian Networks, Deep Learning.

**C. Actuating Components (Vehicle Motion Control)**

- **EPS (Electric Power Steering)** — Lateral / steering control.
- **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC / Drive-by-Wire)** — Longitudinal acceleration.
- **Brake-by-Wire / EHB (Electro-Hydraulic Brakes) / EMB (Electro-Mechanical Brakes)** — Deceleration.
- **Drive-by-Wire Throttle** — Engine torque control.
- **Active Suspension** — Ride height/comfort control.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Sensors:** **"CURL GPS-V"**
> **C**amera, **U**ltrasonic, **R**adar, **L**iDAR, **G**PS, **P**osition(Encoder), **S**peed, **V**2X.

---

## 1.5 Functional Subsystems Commonly Categorized as ADAS

| Function | Description | Key Sensors | SAE Level |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) | Auto-apply brakes before collision | Radar, Camera | L1/L2 |
| ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) | Maintains set distance to lead vehicle | Radar, Lidar | L1 |
| LDW / LKA (Lane Departure Warning / Keeping) | Keeps vehicle within lane | Front Camera | L1/L2 |
| BSD (Blind Spot Detection) | Detects vehicles in blind spot | Side Radar | L0/L1 |
| PA (Park Assist) | Self-parking | Ultrasonic, Camera | L2 |
| ACC + LKA combined | Highway assist | Multiple | **L2** |
| Traffic Jam Pilot | Full control in slow traffic | Full sensor suite | L3 |
| Robotaxi (Waymo, Cruise) | No driver in geo-fence | Full sensor suite + HD maps | **L4** |

---

## 1.6 Why ADAS / Driverless Cars Matter (KTU Context)

- **Safety** — 90%+ of road accidents are due to human error (WHO data). ADAS reduces fatalities.
- **Comfort** — Reduces driver fatigue in traffic.
- **Fuel Efficiency** — Optimized longitudinal control saves **5–15% fuel**.
- **Emission Reduction** — Smoother driving reduces CO₂.
- **Foundation for Autonomous Mobility** — Building block for future **L4/L5** mobility-as-a-service.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of ADAS / Driverless Cars

The operational flow of any ADAS / driverless car function follows a closed-loop mechatronic sequence, often called the **"Sense–Plan–Act" (SPA) cycle**. Understanding this loop is essential for KTU board exam answers.

### Step 1: SENSE (Perception / Data Acquisition)

Sensors mounted on the vehicle continuously scan the surroundings.

- **Long-Range Radar (77 GHz)** — Detects objects up to **250 m**.
- **LiDAR (905 nm / 1550 nm laser)** — Detects objects up to **200 m**, generates 3D point cloud.
- **Stereo / Mono Cameras** — Detect lane lines, traffic signs, pedestrians, traffic lights.
- **Ultrasonic (40–70 kHz)** — Detects obstacles within **0.1 m to 5 m**.
- **GPS + IMU** — Provides vehicle's own position and orientation (pose).
- **Wheel Speed Sensor** — Provides ground-truth speed for sensor fusion.

The sensor outputs are **raw signals** (point clouds, image frames, RF echoes) which must be cleaned and time-synchronized.

### Step 2: PROCESS (Perception + Decision-Making)

The raw sensor data is fed into a **Domain Controller (high-performance SoC)** where:

1. **Object Detection** — Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) such as YOLO, SSD identify cars, pedestrians, cyclists.
2. **Sensor Fusion** — Combines Radar + Camera + LiDAR data (typically using **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Bayesian Filter**) to get a robust, single-track estimate of each object (position, velocity, class).
3. **Tracking & Prediction** — Predicts future trajectories of obstacles (e.g., using Constant Turn Rate and Acceleration — CTRA model).
4. **Path Planning** — Global planner (route on HD map) + Local planner (lane changes, obstacle avoidance).
5. **Behavior Decision** — Yield, overtake, stop, accelerate — a finite-state machine decides the maneuver.

### Step 3: ACT (Vehicle Control / Actuation)

The control commands generated (e.g., "steer 2° left, brake at 0.3 g") are sent to the **drive-by-wire** actuators.

- **Steering Control** — Done by **Electric Power Steering (EPS)**. Closed-loop with EPS motor torque.
- **Throttle Control** — Done by **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)**. DC motor drives the throttle plate.
- **Brake Control** — Done by **Brake-by-Wire (BBW)** with EHB or EMB. Hydraulic pressure / electric caliper activation.
- **Drive Control** — Torque vectoring, gear shifting in EV/automatic transmission.

The vehicle responds, sensors re-scan, and the **SPA loop continues at 10–100 Hz** depending on the function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:**
> The end-to-end latency (sensing → processing → actuation) for an emergency maneuver should be **< 100 ms** to be considered safe. This is called the **"Time-to-Act"** budget.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Models of the Sense–Act Loop

### 2.2.1 Time-of-Flight (ToF) Principle for LiDAR and Radar

Distance is measured by emitting a signal and measuring the time until the reflected echo returns.

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where:
- $d$ = distance to the object (m)
- $c$ = speed of the wave ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s for radar, $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for LiDAR laser)
- $t$ = round-trip time of flight (s)
- Division by 2 — because $t$ is the **round-trip** time.

### 2.2.2 Time-to-Collision (TTC) — Critical for AEB and ACC

The **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** is the time available before the ego vehicle collides with a lead object, assuming both maintain current velocity vectors.

$$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} = \frac{d_{obj} - d_{ego}}{v_{ego} - v_{obj}}$$

where:
- $d_{obj}$ = distance of the lead object from the ego vehicle (m)
- $d_{ego}$ = distance of ego from a reference (m), so $d_{rel} = d_{obj} - d_{ego}$
- $v_{ego}$, $v_{obj}$ = velocities of ego and object respectively (m/s)
- $v_{rel} = v_{ego} - v_{obj}$ = relative velocity (m/s)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** If the object is **stationary** ($v_{obj} = 0$), TTC = $d_{obj}/v_{ego}$. If the object is **moving at the same speed**, $v_{rel}=0$ and TTC = **infinity** (no collision possible) — but KTU expects you to state this explicitly.

### 2.2.3 Required Deceleration for Collision Avoidance

For an AEB system, the minimum deceleration required to stop just before a collision is:

$$a_{req} = \frac{v_{rel}^2}{2 \cdot d_{rel}}$$

The system can apply deceleration $a_{applied}$. The condition for safe stopping is:

$$a_{applied} \geq a_{req}$$

If $a_{applied} < a_{req}$, a collision is unavoidable.

### 2.2.4 Braking Distance (Kinematic Model)

$$d_{brake} = \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}$$

where:
- $v$ = current vehicle speed (m/s)
- $a_{max}$ = maximum achievable deceleration (typically 7–9 m/s² for road cars, or about **0.8 g to 0.9 g**)

### 2.2.5 Sensor Resolution (LiDAR)

For a LiDAR with horizontal field of view $FOV_h$ and number of channels $N$:

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{FOV_h}{N}$$

Lower $\theta_{res}$ → better resolution. A 128-channel LiDAR with 360° FOV gives $\theta_{res} = 2.81°$.

### 2.2.6 Camera Frame-Rate and Processing Latency

For a vision pipeline at $f$ FPS, the **frame period** is:

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1}{f}$$

Typical ADAS cameras run at **30–60 FPS**, so $T_{frame} = 16.6$–$33.3$ ms. This bounds the perception latency.

### 2.2.7 Sensor Fusion — Kalman Filter Update

The discrete Kalman filter predicts and updates the state vector $\mathbf{x}$ (position, velocity) and covariance $\mathbf{P}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1|k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1|k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$$

$$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T (\mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k|k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k|k-1})$$

where:
- $\mathbf{F}_k$ = state-transition matrix
- $\mathbf{H}_k$ = measurement matrix
- $\mathbf{Q}_k, \mathbf{R}_k$ = process and measurement noise covariances
- $\mathbf{K}_k$ = Kalman gain
- $\mathbf{z}_k$ = measurement vector from sensor

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, you are typically expected to state the **purpose** of the Kalman filter (optimal recursive estimator for fusing noisy sensor data) and write the 5 update equations in sequence. Memorize the **predict–update** split.

### 2.2.8 Actuator Dynamics — DC Motor for EPS / Throttle

For a brushed DC motor driving an actuator (e.g., EPS), the armature voltage equation and mechanical equation are:

$$V_a = R_a \cdot i_a + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$$

$$e_b = K_b \cdot \omega$$

$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega = T_m - T_L = K_t \cdot i_a - T_L$$

where:
- $V_a$ = armature voltage
- $R_a, L_a$ = armature resistance and inductance
- $e_b$ = back-EMF
- $K_b, K_t$ = motor constants (V·s/rad and N·m/A)
- $J$ = moment of inertia
- $B$ = viscous friction coefficient
- $T_L$ = load torque

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** In EPS, $T_L$ is the **self-aligning torque (SAT)** of the tire/road contact patch. The control loop uses torque feedback to give the driver a feel of the road.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save these formulas — these are the most-asked in KTU Module-2 ADAS questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | LiDAR / Radar distance | $d = ct/2$ | $c$ in m/s, $t$ in s, $d$ in m |
| 2 | Time-to-Collision (TTC) | $TTC = d_{rel}/v_{rel}$ | $d$ in m, $v$ in m/s, TTC in s |
| 3 | Required deceleration | $a_{req} = v_{rel}^2 / (2 d_{rel})$ | $a$ in m/s² |
| 4 | Braking distance | $d_{brake} = v^2 / (2 a_{max})$ | $d$ in m |
| 5 | Reaction distance | $d_{react} = v \cdot t_{react}$ | $t_{react} \approx 1.5$ s |
| 6 | Stopping distance | $d_{stop} = d_{react} + d_{brake}$ | m |
| 7 | Frame period (camera) | $T = 1/f$ | $f$ in Hz, $T$ in s |
| 8 | LiDAR angular resolution | $\theta_{res} = FOV_h / N$ | degrees |
| 9 | Latency budget | $t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{process} + t_{act}$ | s, target < 100 ms |
| 10 | Free-space optical freq (LiDAR) | $c = f \lambda$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 11 | Camera focal length relation | $1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i$ | m |
| 12 | Doppler Radar velocity | $v = (f_d \cdot \lambda) / 2$ | $f_d$ Doppler shift, $\lambda$ wavelength |
| 13 | Tire slip ratio (traction ctrl) | $\kappa = (v - \omega r) / \max(v, \omega r)$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Yaw rate (bicycle model) | $\dot{\psi} = v \cdot \tan(\delta) / L$ | rad/s |
| 15 | Centripetal acceleration | $a_c = v^2 / R$ | m/s² |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use **SI units**: m, s, m/s, m/s², rad, Hz, W.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** uses **TTC** to determine when to brake and by how much.
- **AEB** triggers when **TTC < ~1.5 s** (Euro NCAP threshold).
- **LiDAR-equipped Robotaxis (Waymo, Cruise)** use a 360° spinning LiDAR with up to **128 channels** for $0.1°$ angular resolution.
- **Tesla FSD** relies on **vision-only (camera)** perception — controversial because cameras lose accuracy in low light/rain.
- **Bosch, Continental, ZF, Aptiv** are Tier-1 suppliers of production ADAS sensors and ECUs.
- **Aircraft autopilots** use the same SPA architecture and Kalman filter-based sensor fusion — the technology is not new; cars are catching up.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, step-by-step derivations and analytical worked examples that are commonly asked in KTU board exams.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Stopping Distance of an ADAS-Equipped Vehicle

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the total stopping distance $d_{stop}$ of a vehicle equipped with an AEB system, accounting for the system latency $t_{lat}$ and the human/auto reaction time $t_{react}$.

### Step 1 — Define the system phases.

The stopping process has **three distinct phases**:

1. **Perception + Processing Phase (Latency $t_{lat}$):** The sensor detects the obstacle, ECU processes the data, and brake command is sent. During this time the vehicle continues at constant velocity.
2. **Reaction Phase ($t_{react}$):** Time between brake command received at the brake-by-wire controller and the brake pads beginning to clamp the rotor.
3. **Braking Phase ($t_{brake}$):** Time during which the brake decelerates the vehicle to rest.

### Step 2 — Distance during the latency phase.

During the latency phase, the vehicle travels at constant speed $v$.

$$d_{lat} = v \cdot t_{lat}$$

### Step 3 — Distance during the reaction phase.

After the brake command is received, the brake system has a finite response time $t_{react}$. During this brief interval, the vehicle is still approximately at constant speed $v$ (or sometimes slightly under deceleration $a_{react}$).

$$d_{react} = v \cdot t_{react} - \frac{1}{2} a_{react} \cdot t_{react}^2$$

> In KTU exam simplifications, we usually assume $a_{react} = 0$ (no deceleration during reaction), giving:

$$d_{react} \approx v \cdot t_{react}$$

### Step 4 — Distance during the braking phase.

The brake applies a deceleration $a_{max}$. The vehicle starts this phase at speed $v$ and decelerates to rest. Using the kinematic equation $v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2 a \cdot d$ with $v_f = 0$:

$$0 = v^2 - 2 \cdot a_{max} \cdot d_{brake}$$

Solving for $d_{brake}$:

$$d_{brake} = \frac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$$

### Step 5 — Sum the three phases.

$$d_{stop} = d_{lat} + d_{react} + d_{brake}$$

$$\boxed{d_{stop} = v \cdot t_{lat} + v \cdot t_{react} + \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot a_{max}}}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical verification.

Let $v = 30$ m/s (≈ 108 km/h), $t_{lat} = 0.05$ s, $t_{react} = 0.2$ s, $a_{max} = 8$ m/s².

$$d_{lat} = 30 \times 0.05 = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{react} = 30 \times 0.2 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{brake} = \frac{30^2}{2 \times 8} = \frac{900}{16} = 56.25 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{stop} = 1.5 + 6.0 + 56.25 = 63.75 \text{ m}$$

✅ Result: A vehicle at 108 km/h needs about **63.75 m** to stop in a fully autonomous emergency brake maneuver.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Time-to-Collision (TTC) for an AEB-Triggered Scenario

**Statement:** An ego vehicle is moving at $v_e = 25$ m/s. A stationary obstacle is detected at $d = 40$ m ahead. Find (a) TTC, (b) the minimum required deceleration to avoid collision, (c) verify whether a brake system capable of $a_{max} = 6$ m/s² can avoid the collision.

### Step 1 — Compute relative distance and velocity.

The obstacle is stationary, so $v_{obj} = 0$.

$$d_{rel} = d_{obj} - d_{ego} = 40 - 0 = 40 \text{ m}$$

$$v_{rel} = v_{ego} - v_{obj} = 25 - 0 = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute TTC.

$$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} = \frac{40}{25} = 1.6 \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the required deceleration.

Using $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a \cdot d$, set $v_f = 0$, $d = 40$ m, $v_i = 25$ m/s:

$$0 = 25^2 - 2 \cdot a_{req} \cdot 40$$

$$a_{req} = \frac{625}{80} = 7.8125 \text{ m/s}^2$$

### Step 4 — Compare with available deceleration.

$$a_{max} = 6 \text{ m/s}^2 < a_{req} = 7.8125 \text{ m/s}^2$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion.

The brake system **cannot** avoid the collision by braking alone. The ADAS must also:

- Issue an **early warning** to the driver (visual/haptic).
- Possibly combine braking with **evasive steering** (L2+).
- If neither works, **mitigate** the impact (e.g., pre-tension seatbelts, raise brake pressure in advance).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why modern AEB systems trigger at TTC ≈ 1.5 s but combine brake pre-fill, audible warning, and seatbelt pretensioning — they cannot rely on braking alone if the distance is short.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Angular Resolution of a Spinning LiDAR

**Statement:** A mechanical spinning LiDAR has 128 laser channels, a horizontal field of view (FOV) of $360°$, and a vertical FOV of $40°$. Compute (a) horizontal angular resolution, (b) vertical angular resolution, (c) the number of points generated per revolution if each channel fires 20 times per revolution.

### Step 1 — Horizontal angular resolution.

$$\theta_h = \frac{FOV_h}{N_h} = \frac{360°}{128} = 2.8125°$$

### Step 2 — Vertical angular resolution.

$$\theta_v = \frac{FOV_v}{N_v} = \frac{40°}{128} = 0.3125°$$

### Step 3 — Points per revolution.

Each of 128 channels fires 20 times per revolution, so:

$$N_{points} = 128 \times 20 = 2560 \text{ points/revolution}$$

If the LiDAR spins at 10 Hz (10 revolutions per second):

$$\text{Points/sec} = 2560 \times 10 = 25{,}600 \text{ pts/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Modern automotive LiDARs (e.g., Velodyne VLP-32C, Hesai Pandar XT-32) generate **600,000 to 2.4 million points per second** for high-resolution 3D mapping.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Sensor Fusion with a 1-D Kalman Filter (Radar + Camera for Distance)

**Statement:** A radar measures distance to a lead vehicle with noise standard deviation $\sigma_R = 0.5$ m, and a camera (via stereo vision) measures the same distance with $\sigma_C = 1.0$ m. The radar gives $z_R = 30$ m and the camera gives $z_C = 28$ m. Find the fused estimate $\hat{x}$ using a simple weighted average (a special case of the Kalman filter for two sensors with independent noise).

### Step 1 — Measurement noise variances.

$$R_R = \sigma_R^2 = 0.5^2 = 0.25$$

$$R_C = \sigma_C^2 = 1.0^2 = 1.0$$

### Step 2 — Compute the inverse-variance weights.

$$w_R = \frac{1/R_R}{1/R_R + 1/R_C} = \frac{1/0.25}{1/0.25 + 1/1.0} = \frac{4}{4 + 1} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

$$w_C = \frac{1/R_C}{1/R_R + 1/R_C} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

### Step 3 — Fused estimate.

$$\hat{x} = w_R \cdot z_R + w_C \cdot z_C = 0.8 \times 30 + 0.2 \times 28 = 24 + 5.6 = 29.6 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4 — Fused variance.

$$\frac{1}{\hat{R}} = \frac{1}{R_R} + \frac{1}{R_C} = 4 + 1 = 5 \implies \hat{R} = 0.2$$

$$\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{0.2} = 0.447 \text{ m}$$

✅ The fused estimate is **closer to the more-accurate radar measurement**, with a **lower uncertainty** than either sensor alone. This is the essence of sensor fusion.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison Table (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Camera | Radar | LiDAR | Ultrasonic | GPS |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|------------|-----|
| Range | 0–250 m | 0–250 m | 0–200 m | 0.1–5 m | Global |
| Works in fog/rain | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Yes |
| Works at night | ❌ Limited (IR helps) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Angular res. | High | Moderate | Very High | Low | N/A |
| Depth info | No (mono) / Yes (stereo) | Yes | Yes (3D) | Yes | No |
| Cost | Low | Med | High | Very Low | Low |
| Latency | 30–50 ms | 50 ms | 100 ms | <10 ms | 100 ms (1 Hz update) |
| Use | Lane/signs | ACC/AEB | HD mapping | Parking | Position |
| Wavelength/freq | 400–700 nm | 24 / 77 GHz | 905 / 1550 nm | 40–70 kHz | 1.575 GHz (L1) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** No single sensor is sufficient. **Sensor fusion** is mandatory — cameras give rich semantic info, radar gives reliable velocity, LiDAR gives 3D geometry, GPS gives global position, and ultrasonic fills the low-speed blind spot.

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Derivation: Closed-Loop Control of Brake-by-Wire (BBW)

**Statement:** A brake-by-wire system uses a PID controller to regulate hydraulic pressure $P$ (bar) in the brake caliper, targeting a desired deceleration $a_{des}$. Derive the closed-loop transfer function and the steady-state error.

### Step 1 — Plant model.

The hydraulic brake system (actuator + brake pad/rotor) is approximately first-order:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

where $K_p$ is the static gain (bar per command) and $\tau_p$ is the time constant (~10–30 ms).

### Step 2 — Controller.

PID controller:

$$G_c(s) = K_p^{ctrl} + \frac{K_i^{ctrl}}{s} + K_d^{ctrl} s$$

### Step 3 — Forward path.

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \left(K_p^{ctrl} + \frac{K_i^{ctrl}}{s} + K_d^{ctrl} s\right) \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

### Step 4 — Closed-loop transfer function.

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

### Step 5 — Steady-state error for a step input $A_{des}(s) = A/s$.

Using Final Value Theorem for a system with integral action ($K_i^{ctrl} \neq 0$):

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{A_{des}(s)}{1 + G(s)} = 0$$

✅ The **integral term** in the PID guarantees **zero steady-state error** for a step demand in deceleration — the brake pressure will eventually settle to the exact value needed to produce the requested deceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **PID control is universal** in ADAS actuation: integral action removes steady-state error, and derivative action damps oscillations. P-only control would leave a persistent offset.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP] 

**Define ADAS. List any four ADAS features currently available in production vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** are electronic and mechatronic subsystems in a vehicle that use sensors, ECUs, and actuators to assist the human driver in longitudinal control, lateral control, and situational awareness, thereby improving safety, comfort, and efficiency.

Four production ADAS features:

1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** — Maintains a set distance from the lead vehicle using long-range radar.
2. **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA) / Lane Departure Warning (LDW)** — Uses a front camera to detect lane lines and provides steering correction.
3. **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** — Radar + camera detect imminent collision and apply brakes automatically.
4. **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)** — Side-mounted radar warns of vehicles in the blind spot during lane change.
5. *(Optional)* Park Assist, Traffic Sign Recognition, Driver Drowsiness Detection, 360° Surround View.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]

**Explain the differences between LiDAR, Radar, and Camera as perception sensors in ADAS. State one advantage and one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sensor | Principle | Advantage | Limitation |
|--------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| **LiDAR** | Time-of-flight of laser pulses (905 / 1550 nm) | High-resolution 3D point cloud, accurate depth | Expensive; performance degrades in heavy rain/fog |
| **Radar** | RF wave reflection (24 / 77 GHz) | Works in all weather; measures velocity directly via Doppler | Low angular resolution; no semantic (class) info |
| **Camera** | Visible/IR image capture | Rich semantic info (signs, lanes, objects); low cost | Fails in low light/rain; no direct depth (mono) |

> The three sensors are **complementary**, and modern ADAS uses **sensor fusion** to combine their strengths.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choice 1

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]

**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Driverless / Autonomous Car. Clearly show the Sense–Plan–Act (SPA) loop and the role of each block.**

**Model Answer:**

A driverless car operates in a closed-loop **Sense–Plan–Act (SPA)** cycle:

**1. Sense (Perception Layer):**
Sensors — cameras, radar, LiDAR, ultrasonic, GPS, IMU — collect data about the surroundings and the vehicle's own state (speed, position, heading). Raw signals are pre-processed (noise filtering, time synchronization).

**2. Plan (Decision Layer):**
- **Perception** runs object detection (CNN/YOLO), lane detection, sign recognition.
- **Sensor Fusion** (EKF) combines multi-sensor data into a single robust estimate of each obstacle (position, velocity, class).
- **Tracking & Prediction** uses a motion model (e.g., CTRA) to forecast obstacle trajectories.
- **Path Planning** computes global route (HD map) and local trajectory.
- **Behavior FSM** decides maneuver (yield, overtake, brake, stop).

**3. Act (Actuation Layer):**
Commands are sent to **drive-by-wire** actuators:
- **EPS** for lateral (steering) control.
- **Electronic Throttle** for longitudinal acceleration.
- **Brake-by-Wire (EHB/EMB)** for deceleration.

The vehicle responds, sensors re-scan, and the loop repeats at **10–100 Hz**. Total latency target: **< 100 ms**.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Environment]
     |
     v
[Sensors: Camera, Radar, LiDAR, GPS, IMU]
     |
     v
[Pre-processing & Time Sync]
     |
     v
[Perception: Object/Lane/Sign Detection - CNN]
     |
     v
[Sensor Fusion: EKF]
     |
     v
[Tracking & Prediction: CTRA]
     |
     v
[Path & Behavior Planner: FSM + A*]
     |
     v
[Controller: PID / MPC]
     |
     v
[Actuators: EPS, Throttle, Brake-by-Wire]
     |
     v
[Vehicle Motion] ---> back to Environment
```

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]

**A vehicle moving at 90 km/h detects a stationary obstacle at 50 m. (i) Calculate the Time-to-Collision (TTC). (ii) Calculate the minimum deceleration required to avoid collision. (iii) If the AEB system can apply a maximum deceleration of 7 m/s², will the collision be avoided?**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $v_{ego} = 90$ km/h $= 90/3.6 = 25$ m/s, $d_{rel} = 50$ m, $v_{obj} = 0$, $a_{max} = 7$ m/s².

**(i) Time-to-Collision:**

$$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}} = \frac{50}{25 - 0} = 2.0 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Minimum required deceleration:**

$$a_{req} = \frac{v_{rel}^2}{2 \cdot d_{rel}} = \frac{25^2}{2 \times 50} = \frac{625}{100} = 6.25 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(iii) Will collision be avoided?**

$$a_{max} = 7 \text{ m/s}^2 \geq a_{req} = 6.25 \text{ m/s}^2$$

✅ **Yes, the collision will be avoided** with a margin of $7 - 6.25 = 0.75$ m/s². The AEB will bring the vehicle to a stop in:

$$d_{brake} = \frac{25^2}{2 \times 7} = \frac{625}{14} = 44.64 \text{ m} < 50 \text{ m}$$

The vehicle will stop in 44.64 m, leaving a **safety margin of 5.36 m**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Choice 2 (Internal Choice)

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model QP]

**Explain the working of the following sensing components used in ADAS, with a focus on the physical principle and a typical use case:**
**(i) Automotive Radar (77 GHz)**
**(ii) LiDAR**
**(iii) Ultrasonic Sensor**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) 77 GHz Automotive Radar:**
- **Principle:** Emits **Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW)** radio waves at 77 GHz (λ ≈ 3.9 mm). The reflected wave from a target is mixed with the transmitted signal; the frequency difference (beat frequency) gives **range**, and the **Doppler shift** gives **relative velocity**.
- **Range:** Up to 250 m.
- **Use case:** Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), AEB, Blind Spot Detection.
- **Advantage:** Works in all weather (rain, fog, dust); robust to lighting conditions.

**(ii) LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging):**
- **Principle:** Emits **laser pulses** (905 nm or 1550 nm) and measures the round-trip time of flight:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

- A 360° mechanical or solid-state spinning LiDAR generates a **3D point cloud** of the surroundings.
- **Range:** Up to 200 m; angular resolution 0.1°–0.5°.
- **Use case:** HD mapping, Robotaxi (Waymo, Cruise), 3D obstacle detection.

**(iii) Ultrasonic Sensor:**
- **Principle:** Emits **acoustic pulses at 40–70 kHz**. The echo from a nearby object is received and time-of-flight gives distance.
- **Range:** 0.1 m to 5 m.
- **Use case:** Low-speed parking assist, blind-spot warning at very close range.
- **Advantage:** Very cheap; works in any lighting; ideal for the close-proximity perimeter of the vehicle.

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]

**With a block diagram, explain the working of an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system. Show how the controller uses Time-to-Collision (TTC) to decide throttle and brake commands.**

**Model Answer:**

**Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** is an L1 ADAS function that maintains a driver-set speed **and** automatically adjusts speed to keep a safe following distance from a slower lead vehicle.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Sensing:** A **long-range radar** (77 GHz, mounted behind the grille) measures $d_{rel}$ (distance to lead vehicle) and $v_{rel}$ (relative velocity). Some systems also use a front camera for object classification (to filter out non-vehicles like manhole covers).

2. **Computation of TTC and Time Headway:**

$$TTC = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{rel}}, \quad THW = \frac{d_{rel}}{v_{ego}}$$

A typical safety threshold: $THW \geq 1.5$ s.

3. **Decision Logic (FSM):**
   - If $d_{rel} > d_{safe}$ (no lead vehicle or far lead) → maintain set speed → **throttle control**.
   - If $d_{rel} \leq d_{safe}$ and $v_{rel} > 0$ (lead vehicle slower) → **brake command** proportional to $(v_{rel} \cdot k_{brake})$.
   - If $d_{rel} \leq d_{critical}$ → maximum brake (AEB-level intervention).

4. **Actuation:**
   - **Throttle:** Electronic Throttle Body (ETB) adjusts throttle plate to control engine torque.
   - **Brake:** Brake-by-Wire (EHB) applies hydraulic pressure.

5. **Closed Loop:** The radar re-measures $d_{rel}$ at **20 Hz** and the loop repeats.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Driver sets v_set]    [Lead Vehicle]
        |                  |
        v                  v
        +------[Radar]-----+         (senses d_rel, v_rel)
                |
                v
        [Time-to-Collision TTC = d_rel / v_rel]
                |
                v
        [Controller: PID or Fuzzy]
                |
        +-------+--------+
        |                |
        v                v
   [Throttle ETC]   [Brake-by-Wire EHB]
        |                |
        v                v
   [Engine Torque]  [Brake Deceleration]
        |                |
        +-------+--------+
                |
                v
       [Ego Vehicle Motion]
                |
                v
       [Sensors re-measure] ---> back to Radar
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing L2 and L3:** Many students write that *Level 2 is "driverless."* It is **not** — at L2, the driver must keep hands on the wheel and eyes on the road. L3 is the first level where the system is "conditional driverless."
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF:** Radar and LiDAR measure **round-trip** time, so the distance formula is $d = ct/2$, not $d = ct$. Losing 1 mark for this is common.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion:** $v = 90$ km/h = **25 m/s**, not 90. Always divide by 3.6.
> 4. **TTC = ∞ when speeds are equal:** When $v_{rel} = 0$, the formula $d_{rel}/v_{rel}$ is undefined. State this explicitly.
> 5. **Sensor fusion ≠ just averaging:** Many students write "fused = (Radar + Camera) / 2." The correct method is **inverse-variance weighting** or **Kalman filter** — sensors with lower noise get higher weight.
> 6. **Actuator omission:** In "Driverless Car" questions, marks are reserved for mentioning **EPS, ETC, and Brake-by-Wire** explicitly. Students often describe sensors only and forget the actuation side.
> 7. **No discussion of latency:** Always quote the **end-to-end latency budget** (< 100 ms) — it shows awareness of real-time constraints, a favorite KTU examiner's mark-grabber.
> 8. **"Driverless" in L4 vs L5:** Be precise. L4 is driverless **within a geo-fenced ODD** (Operational Design Domain). L5 is driverless **everywhere, in all conditions**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **ADAS** = cluster of mechatronic systems aiding the driver. Goal: **safety, comfort, efficiency**.
- [x] **SAE Levels 0–5** — L0 = none, L5 = full driverless in all conditions.
- [x] **L4** is driverless only in a **geo-fenced ODD**. L5 is unrestricted.
- [x] The three pillars: **SENSE → PROCESS → ACT** (Sense–Plan–Act or SPA loop).
- [x] **Sensors** — Camera, Radar (77 GHz), LiDAR, Ultrasonic, GPS, IMU, Wheel Speed Encoder, V2X.
- [x] **Actuators** — EPS (steering), ETC (throttle), Brake-by-Wire EHB/EMB (brakes), Drive motor/transmission.
- [x] **LiDAR range formula:** $d = c \cdot t / 2$ (round-trip).
- [x] **Time-to-Collision:** $TTC = d_{rel} / v_{rel}$.
- [x] **Required deceleration:** $a_{req} = v_{rel}^2 / (2 d_{rel})$.
- [x] **Braking distance:** $d_{brake} = v^2 / (2 a_{max})$.
- [x] **Total stopping distance:** $d_{stop} = v \cdot t_{lat} + v \cdot t_{react} + v^2 / (2 a_{max})$.
- [x] **Frame period:** $T_{frame} = 1/f$ (typical ADAS camera = 30 FPS).
- [x] **Sensor fusion** uses **Kalman Filter (EKF)** — not a simple average.
- [x] **Inverse-variance weight:** $w_i = (1/R_i) / \sum (1/R_i)$.
- [x] **Doppler radar velocity:** $v = f_d \lambda / 2$.
- [x] **LiDAR angular resolution:** $\theta_{res} = FOV_h / N_{channels}$.
- [x] **AEB threshold:** Trigger when $TTC \leq 1.5$ s (Euro NCAP).
- [x] **ACC uses PID** (or MPC) control — integral action ensures **zero steady-state error**.
- [x] **End-to-end latency** target: **≤ 100 ms** for safety-critical functions (ISO 26262 ASIL-D).
- [x] **Brake-by-wire** has two variants: **EHB** (electro-hydraulic) and **EMB** (electro-mechanical).
- [x] **HD Maps** are pre-built centimeter-accurate maps used by L3+ cars for lane-level localization.
- [x] **Tesla** uses **vision-only (no LiDAR)**, while **Waymo** uses **LiDAR + Radar + Camera**. This is a key industrial difference.
- [x] **AEB** + **FCW** (Forward Collision Warning) + **ACC** are the most common production ADAS features.
- [x] **Euro NCAP** and **NHTSA** mandate AEB in new cars since 2022–2024.
- [x] Always quote units in **SI (m, s, m/s, m/s²)** in numerical answers.
- [x] Always add a **"sense" diagram** + an **"act" diagram** in long answers for full marks.

---

### ✅ End of Module 2 Notes — ADAS & Driverless Cars
