> **Reading the Cross-Section:** Pressure $P$ enters from the **top** (or sometimes bottom), deflecting the thin silicon diaphragm inward. The p+ piezoresistors embedded at high-stress regions (near the clamped edges) change resistance. The sealed Pyrex cavity provides a reference pressure for absolute (vs. gauge) sensing.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q: Differentiate between bulk micromachining and surface micromachining in MEMS fabrication. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bulk Micromachining | Surface Micromachining |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Material Removed** | Bulk silicon (substrate itself) | Sacrificial oxide layer only |
| **Structural Layer** | The remaining silicon | Deposited polysilicon |
| **Thickness** | 10–500 µm | 1–10 µm |
| **Equipment** | KOH/TMAH wet etches, DRIE | RIE/ICP, HF vapor release |

**Advantages — Bulk Micromachining:**
1. Produces thick, mechanically robust structures ideal for high-pressure diaphragms and high-$g$ crash sensors.
2. Lower long-term drift and better overload tolerance than surface-micromachined devices.

**Advantages — Surface Micromachining:**
1. Fully CMOS-compatible — can be integrated with ASICs on the same wafer (lower cost, smaller footprint).
2. Allows batch fabrication of thousands of devices per wafer with sub-micron precision.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Q: What is the Coriolis effect? Explain briefly how it is utilized in a MEMS gyroscope to measure angular rate.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Coriolis effect** describes the apparent force experienced by a mass moving within a rotating reference frame. The force vector is given by:

$$\vec{F}_c = -2 m (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v})$$

In a MEMS gyroscope, a proof mass is driven into **steady oscillation** along one axis (drive mode, velocity $v$). When the entire device rotates at angular rate $\omega$, the Coriolis force deflects the mass in a direction **perpendicular** to both $\omega$ and $v$. This perpendicular (sense-mode) displacement is capacitively measured and is directly proportional to $\omega$:

$$y_{sense} \propto \omega$$

Hence, by sensing the perpendicular displacement, the angular rotation rate of the vehicle (yaw rate, roll rate) can be determined for **ESC, rollover protection, and navigation**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module 3 questions in End Semester Examinations offer internal choice. Both options are given below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of a MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor**. Derive an expression for the maximum deflection of a circular silicon diaphragm under uniform pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A circular silicon diaphragm has radius $a = 500 \,\mu\text{m}$ and thickness $t = 25 \,\mu\text{m}$. Calculate:
(i) the maximum deflection at center under uniform pressure $P = 100 \text{ kPa}$,
(ii) the maximum bending stress at the clamped edge.
Take $E = 169 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Working Principle:**

A MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor consists of:
- A thin circular silicon **diaphragm** etched from a silicon wafer.
- Four **p-type piezoresistors** diffused into the diaphragm near the clamped edges (where stress is maximum).
- The piezoresistors are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.
- The diaphragm is bonded to a glass (Pyrex) substrate with a sealed reference cavity.

**Operation:**
1. External pressure $P$ is applied to one side of the diaphragm.
2. The diaphragm deflects downward, producing tensile and compressive stresses at the edges.
3. Piezoresistors oriented along $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction experience maximum piezoresistive effect.
4. Resistance change $\Delta R$ is converted to voltage by the Wheatstone bridge.
5. The output voltage is proportional to applied pressure.

**Derivation of Maximum Deflection:**

From plate theory, for a clamped circular plate of radius $a$, thickness $t$, under uniform pressure $P$:

$$\boxed{w_{max} = \frac{3 P a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E t^3}}$$

(Full derivation provided in Section 3.1.)

**Sketch:** Refer to the cross-sectional diagram in Section 4.4 — show silicon diaphragm, sealed cavity, piezoresistors at edges, aluminum bond pads.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Given:**
- $a = 500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $t = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $P = 100 \text{ kPa} = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$
- $E = 169 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.28$

**(i) Maximum deflection at center:**

$$w_{max} = \frac{3 P a^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$$

Step 1: Compute $(1 - \nu^2)$:
$$1 - (0.28)^2 = 1 - 0.0784 = 0.9216$$

Step 2: Compute $a^4$:
$$a^4 = (500 \times 10^{-6})^4 = (5 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 625 \times 10^{-16} = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^4$$

Step 3: Compute $t^3$:
$$t^3 = (25 \times 10^{-6})^3 = (2.5 \times 10^{-5})^3 = 15.625 \times 10^{-15} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^3$$

Step 4: Substitute into formula:
$$w_{max} = \frac{3 \times (100 \times 10^3) \times (6.25 \times 10^{-14}) \times 0.9216}{16 \times (169 \times 10^9) \times (1.5625 \times 10^{-14})}$$

Step 5: Numerator:
$$3 \times 10^5 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 0.9216 = 1.728 \times 10^{-8}$$

Step 6: Denominator:
$$16 \times 169 \times 10^9 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-14} = 4.225 \times 10^{-2}$$

Step 7: Final division:
$$w_{max} = \frac{1.728 \times 10^{-8}}{4.225 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.09 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{w_{max} \approx 0.409 \,\mu\text{m} = 409 \text{ nm}}$$

**(ii) Maximum bending stress at clamped edge:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 P a^2}{4 t^2}$$

Step 1: Compute $a^2$:
$$a^2 = (500 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2: Compute $t^2$:
$$t^2 = (25 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 3: Substitute:
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \times (100 \times 10^3) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-7})}{4 \times (6.25 \times 10^{-10})}$$

Step 4: Numerator:
$$3 \times 10^5 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 7.5 \times 10^{-2}$$

Step 5: Denominator:
$$4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$$

Step 6: Final:
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 30 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Result:** The diaphragm deflects by ~409 nm under 100 kPa, with a peak stress of 30 MPa (well within silicon's yield strength of ~7 GPa, confirming safe operation).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **working principle of a MEMS capacitive accelerometer**. Derive the expression for its sensitivity in terms of mass, area, gap, and spring constant. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** With a clear schematic, explain the **working of a MEMS gyroscope based on the Coriolis effect**. Derive the sense-mode displacement expression for an angular rate $\omega$ applied during a sinusoidal drive of amplitude $v_0$ at frequency $\omega_d$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Working Principle — Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2.

**Components:**
- Silicon proof mass suspended by folded flexure springs (stiffness $k$).
- Two fixed silicon plates (top and bottom) at gap distance $d$.
- Comb or parallel-plate capacitive readout.
- ASIC with charge amplifier, demodulator, and ADC.

**Operation:**
1. External acceleration $a$ acts on the housing.
2. By inertia, the proof mass lags behind, displacing by $x = -m a / k$ at steady state.
3. Two capacitors — $C_1$ (between mass and top plate) and $C_2$ (mass and bottom plate) — change in opposite directions.
4. Differential capacitance $\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 \propto x \propto a$.
5. A C/V converter produces an analog voltage proportional to acceleration.

**Derivation of Sensitivity:**

From Section 3.3, the differential capacitance:

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A x}{d^2}$$

With steady-state mass displacement $x = -\frac{m a}{k}$:

$$\Delta C = -\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \cdot m \cdot a}{k \cdot d^2}$$

Sensitivity:
$$\boxed{S = \frac{d(\Delta C)}{da} = -\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \cdot m}{k \cdot d^2} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{F}}{\text{m/s}^2} \right]}$$

Designers maximize sensitivity by: increasing area $A$, increasing mass $m$, reducing gap $d$, or reducing stiffness $k$.

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Schematic:** Refer to Section 4.3 — block diagram showing drive comb, sense comb, proof mass, and synchronous demodulator.

**Working:**

1. A drive oscillator at frequency $f_d$ excites the proof mass into linear oscillation along the X-axis (drive mode) using electrostatic comb drives.
2. The mass velocity is $v_x(t) = v_0 \sin(\omega_d t)$.
3. When the device rotates at angular rate $\omega$ about the Z-axis, the moving mass experiences a Coriolis force in the Y-direction:

$$F_c(t) = 2 m \omega v_x(t) = 2 m \omega v_0 \sin(\omega_d t)$$

4. This force drives the sense mode (Y-axis), whose equation of motion is:

$$m \ddot{y} + b_s \dot{y} + k_s y = F_{c0} \sin(\omega_d t)$$

where $F_{c0} = 2 m \omega v_0$.

5. The steady-state sense-mode amplitude is:

$$\boxed{y_{sense} = \frac{2 m \omega v_0}{\sqrt{(k_s - m \omega_d^2)^2 + (b_s \omega_d)^2}}}$$

This is **linearly proportional to $\omega$**, enabling measurement of angular rate.

6. A synchronous demodulator at $f_d$ extracts the amplitude and polarity of $y_{sense}$, producing a voltage proportional to yaw/roll rate.

**Automotive Use:** This yaw-rate signal is fed to the **ESC** controller to detect vehicle skidding and apply differential braking.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing diaphragm deflection formula:** Students often forget the $(1-\nu^2)$ factor or write $E/12$ instead of $E t^3/12$. Both errors lose 2 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Mixing up piezoresistive coefficients:** Writing $\pi_l$ where $\pi_t$ is needed (or vice versa) loses credit. Always state which direction (longitudinal = parallel to current; transverse = perpendicular).
>
> 3. **Forgetting units:** $E$ in GPa must be converted to Pa. $a, t$ in µm must be converted to m. Failure to convert loses 1 mark per quantity.
>
> 4. **Coriolis sign error:** The correct formula is $\vec{F}_c = -2m(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v})$ — missing the negative sign is a frequent mistake.
>
> 5. **Not drawing labeled diagrams:** A "neat sketch" is mandatory for 7-mark questions. Unlabeled diagrams fetch zero credit even if the derivation is correct.
>
> 6. **Confusing accelerometer sensitivity direction:** The sign of $x$ matters — when the housing accelerates in $+X$, the mass lags in $-X$ relative to housing. Mention this carefully.
>
> 7. **Skipping derivations:** KTU examiners reward each intermediate algebraic step. A "one-line final answer" without steps gets at most 50% credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Rapid Revision Checklist**

#### 🔹 MEMS Pressure Sensor
- [ ] Diaphragm is a thin silicon plate, clamped at edges.
- [ ] Maximum deflection at center, maximum stress at edge.
- [ ] $w_{max} = \frac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$ — memorize with units.
- [ ] $\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 P a^2}{4 t^2}$.
- [ ] Piezoresistive sensing uses Wheatstone bridge; capacitive sensing uses gap change.
- [ ] Used in MAP, TMAP, EGR, TPMS, airbag pressure.

#### 🔹 MEMS Accelerometer
- [ ] Proof mass + spring + damper system.
- [ ] Equation: $m \ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m a_{ext}$.
- [ ] Steady-state: $x = -m a_{ext} / k$.
- [ ] Differential capacitance $\Delta C = 2 \varepsilon_0 A x / d^2$.
- [ ] Sensitivity $S = 2\varepsilon_0 A m / (k d^2)$.
- [ ] Natural frequency $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$.
- [ ] Used in airbag, ESC, ABS, active suspension.

#### 🔹 MEMS Gyroscope
- [ ] Based on **Coriolis effect**: $\vec{F}_c = -2m(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v})$.
- [ ] Two modes: drive mode (excited) + sense mode (Coriolis-driven).
- [ ] Drive at $f_d$, sense-mode displacement $y \propto \omega$.
- [ ] Synchronous demodulation at $f_d$ extracts angular rate.
- [ ] Used in ESC yaw-rate, rollover, navigation.

#### 🔹 Fabrication
- [ ] **Bulk micromachining**: etches substrate itself; thick structures; for pressure sensors and high-$g$ sensors.
- [ ] **Surface micromachining**: deposits + releases thin films; CMOS-compatible; for gyroscopes and low-$g$ accelerometers.
- [ ] **Wafer bonding**: anodic (Si-glass), fusion (Si-Si), eutectic (Au-Si).
- [ ] **Stiction** is the #1 reliability issue in surface micromachining — prevented by critical-point drying.

#### 🔹 Constants to Memorize
- [ ] $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- [ ] $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- [ ] $E_{Si} = 169 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$
- [ ] Yield strength of silicon ≈ 7 GPa

#### 🔹 Key Automotive Integration
- [ ] A modern vehicle contains **50+ MEMS sensors** on average.
- [ ] **ESC** needs accelerometer + gyroscope together (yaw-rate + lateral acceleration).
- [ ] **Airbag** needs accelerometer (front + side crash detection).
- [ ] **TPMS** uses MEMS pressure sensor + wireless transmitter in each tyre.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — MEMS Pressure Sensor, Accelerometer & Gyroscope**
> *These notes are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (OEMUT834 — Automotive Mechatronics) syllabus and examination pattern. Practice the 14-mark derivations and 3-mark comparison questions thoroughly for best results in ESE.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SI Engine Management - Parameters to be Controlled
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SI Engine Management - Parameters to be Controlled

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is SI Engine Management?

The **SI (Spark Ignition) Engine Management System (EMS)** is a real-time, closed-loop mechatronic control architecture that continuously senses engine operating conditions and actuates fuel delivery, air intake, and spark generation to deliver the desired **torque, fuel economy, drivability, and exhaust emissions** over the entire engine operating map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Engine Management is the integration of electronic sensing, computational control (ECU), and electromechanical actuation to govern the combustion process in a Spark Ignition engine so that the required Air-Fuel Mixture Ratio, Ignition Timing, and Idle Quality are maintained under all speed and load conditions.*

In a modern car, the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** is the brain. It receives dozens of inputs per second from sensors and decides:
- **How much fuel** to inject into each cylinder.
- **When** to fire the spark plug.
- **How much air** to admit through the throttle.
- **Whether** to retard timing, open EGR, purge the canister, or activate a purge valve, etc.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Chef" Analogy

Imagine a master chef cooking in a kitchen that constantly changes:
- The **stove flame** (load) keeps fluctuating.
- The **air pressure** in the room (altitude) varies.
- The **room temperature** (ambient) shifts from cold morning to hot afternoon.
- The **pantry ingredient quality** (fuel quality) is inconsistent.
- The **guest count** (driver demand) changes every minute.

A bad cook would simply throw fixed amounts of salt and flame and hope for the best. A **master chef** constantly *tastes* (sensor feedback), *re-balances the spices* (fuel), and *adjusts the flame intensity and timing* (ignition + throttle). That master chef is the **ECU**, and the kitchen is the **SI Engine**. Engine management is essentially *closed-loop culinary automation for combustion*.

---

## 1.3 The Core Parameters That Must Be Controlled

In SI engines, modern mechatronic management governs **three master variables** that determine everything else (emissions, power, economy, smoothness):

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Master Control Variables in an SI Engine**
> 1. **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR / λ)** — controls combustion chemistry → emissions & economy
> 2. **Ignition Timing (Spark Advance)** — controls combustion phasing → power, knock, emissions
> 3. **Throttle Position / Air Mass Flow** — controls engine load → torque output

All other parameters (idle speed, EGR, VVT, purge, knock correction, cold-start enrichment) are *secondary or supervisory* loops built on top of these three.

---

## 1.4 Sensor Inputs — The "Senses" of the ECU

| # | Sensor | Symbol | What it measures | Why it is needed |
|---|--------|--------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Mass Air Flow / MAP | $m_a$ / $P_{man}$ | Air entering engine | Fuel calculation |
| 2 | Throttle Position Sensor | TPS | Driver pedal demand | Load detection |
| 3 | Crankshaft Position | CKP | Engine RPM & TDC | Timing & injection sync |
| 4 | Camshaft Position | CMP | Cylinder identification | Sequential injection |
| 5 | Coolant Temperature | CLT | Engine warm-up | Cold-start enrichment |
| 6 | Intake Air Temperature | IAT | Air density | AFR compensation |
| 7 | Lambda / Oxygen (UEGO) | $\lambda$ / O₂ | Exhaust O₂ | Closed-loop AFR |
| 8 | Knock Sensor | KS | Engine vibration | Retard timing if knock |
| 9 | Vehicle Speed | VSS | Road speed | Idle, decel fuel cut |
| 10 | Battery Voltage | $V_b$ | Electrical supply | Injector pulse width correction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a 14-mark question asks *"Explain parameters to be controlled"*, always structure your answer as **(i) Input parameters, (ii) Output parameters, (iii) Control loops (open/closed), (iv) Functional block diagram**.

---

## 1.5 Actuator Outputs — The "Muscles" of the ECU

| # | Actuator | What it does |
|---|----------|--------------|
| 1 | Electronic Throttle Body (ETB) | Regulates air mass flow |
| 2 | Fuel Injectors | Deliver metered fuel pulse |
| 3 | Ignition Coil / Spark Plug | Generates high-voltage spark |
| 4 | Idle Air Control Valve (IACV) | Bypasses air around closed throttle |
| 5 | EGR Valve | Recirculates exhaust gas |
| 6 | Variable Valve Timing solenoid | Advances/retards cam phasing |
| 7 | Evaporative Purge Valve | Releases fuel vapors to intake |
| 8 | Cooling Fan Relay | Controls radiator fan |
| 9 | MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp) | Alerts driver of fault (OBD-II) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Each Parameter Must Be Controlled

### 2.1.1 Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR / Equivalence Ratio φ / Lambda λ)

- **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline** = **14.7 : 1 by mass**
- Below 14.7 → **Rich** (excess fuel) → power & cooling, but more CO and unburnt HC
- Above 14.7 → **Lean** (excess air) → more NOx, misfire risk, lean-burn efficiency
- The **three-way catalytic converter (TWC)** can simultaneously oxidize CO, HC, and reduce NOx **only when λ ≈ 1.00 ± 0.01**
- Hence the ECU must hold AFR within a hairline ±1 % of stoichiometric under most operating conditions

$$
\lambda = \frac{(\text{AFR})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{AFR})_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{14.7}{14.7} = 1.00 \quad \text{(stoichiometric)}
$$

$$
\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{(\text{F}/\text{A})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{F}/\text{A})_{\text{stoich}}}
$$

### 2.1.2 Ignition Timing (Spark Advance)

- The **MBT (Minimum advance for Best Torque)** is the timing that delivers peak cylinder pressure at ~15° ATDC.
- Spark must fire **before TDC** so that the flame front reaches peak pressure at the optimum crank angle.
- Too early → **knock** (detonation) → piston damage
- Too late → power loss, overheating, poor economy

$$
\theta_{\text{actual}} = \theta_{\text{base}} + \theta_{\text{coolant}} + \theta_{\text{MAP}} + \theta_{\text{RPM}} - \theta_{\text{knock retard}}
$$

### 2.1.3 Throttle / Air Mass Control (Electronic Throttle Control — ETC)

- The drive-by-wire throttle is a **closed-loop servo** tracking a target position derived from pedal position, idle speed control, traction control, and cruise control.
- Closed-loop PID ensures precise air metering under transient conditions.

### 2.1.4 Idle Speed Control (ISC)

- At closed throttle, the ECU modulates an **Idle Air Control (IAC) bypass valve** or the electronic throttle to maintain target idle RPM (~700–800 rpm).
- Compensates for accessory loads (A/C, alternator, power steering).

### 2.1.5 Knock Control

- A **piezoelectric knock sensor** bolted to the engine block listens for high-frequency vibration (~7 kHz typical for gasoline knock).
- When knock is detected, the ECU **retards spark** in 1°–3° steps until knock disappears, then slowly re-advances.

### 2.1.6 EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)

- Inert exhaust gas displaces fresh charge → lowers peak flame temperature → **suppresses NOx formation**.
- Typical EGR rates: 5 % at idle → 15–20 % at part load → 0 % at WOT.

### 2.1.7 Variable Valve Timing (VVT)

- Phase-shifts camshafts to optimize **volumetric efficiency, idle quality, EGR overlap, and emission control** across the RPM range.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Board Examination — memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7$ | Gasoline stoichiometric air-fuel ratio by mass | — |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{AFR}_{\text{act}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio (lambda) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $m_a = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot \rho_a \cdot N}{2 \times 60}$ | Air mass per second (4-stroke) | kg/s |
| 5 | $m_f = \dfrac{m_a}{\text{AFR}_{\text{target}}}$ | Required fuel mass per second | kg/s |
| 6 | $T_{\text{inj}} = \dfrac{m_f}{Q_{\text{inj}}}$ | Injector pulse width (Q = flow rate) | s |
| 7 | $\theta_{\text{spark}} = \theta_{\text{MBT}} - \theta_{\text{knock}}$ | Final spark advance | ° crank |
| 8 | $\eta_v \approx f(\text{RPM}, \text{Load}, \text{VVT})$ | Volumetric efficiency | — |
| 9 | $P_{\text{man}} = \rho_a \cdot R \cdot T_{\text{man}}$ | Ideal gas law for MAP | kPa |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N}{2 \cdot 60} \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{man}}}{R \cdot T_{\text{man}}}$ | Speed-Density air mass equation | kg/s |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR STUDENTS:** When writing any absolute value in board answer sheets, always use brackets `( )` or `\{ \}` — never write $\vert x \vert$ vertically as that confuses examiners.

---

## 2.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control Strategy

| Mode | Condition | Loop | AFR Target |
|------|-----------|------|------------|
| Cold start | CLT < 60 °C | Open-loop | Rich (10:1) |
| Warm-up | 60–90 °C | Open-loop | Slightly rich |
| Closed-loop (lambda) | CLT > 90 °C, O₂ sensor hot | **Closed** | $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ |
| Wide-Open Throttle (WOT) | TPS > 70 % | Open-loop | Rich for power (~12:1) |
| Decel fuel cut | TPS = 0, RPM > 1200 | Open-loop | Fuel = 0 |
| Idle | TPS = 0, RPM ≈ idle | Closed (PID) | $\lambda = 1.00$ |
| Knock detected | KS signal > threshold | **Closed (retard)** | Retard spark 1° at a time |

> **Real-world utility:** This same structure is the foundation of any mechatronic system — refrigerator thermostat (temp closed-loop), ABS (wheel-slip closed-loop), drone altitude (PID). Understanding SI engine management is a template for ALL real-time feedback control engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Air Mass Flow Equation (Speed-Density Method)

The **Speed-Density method** computes the mass of air inducted into the cylinder from the **manifold absolute pressure (MAP)**, **intake air temperature (IAT)**, **engine speed (N)**, and **volumetric efficiency (ηᵥ)**.

We will derive it rigorously for a **4-stroke engine**.

### Step 1: Theoretical Volume Displaced per Second

In a 4-stroke engine, the crankshaft makes **2 revolutions per working cycle**. Therefore, the **number of working cycles per second** is:

$$
f_c = \frac{N}{2 \times 60} \quad [\text{cycles per second}]
$$

where $N$ is in revolutions per minute (RPM).

The **displacement volume per cylinder** is $V_d$ in m³. For a single cylinder, the theoretical volume of air drawn in per second is:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{theoretical}} = V_d \cdot f_c = \frac{V_d \cdot N}{2 \times 60} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

### Step 2: Apply Volumetric Efficiency

In reality, the cylinder does not fill completely due to throttling, residual gases, and valve timing. The **volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$** (typically 0.6–1.0) corrects this:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{actual}} = \eta_v \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{theoretical}} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{V_d \cdot N}{2 \times 60}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Ideal Gas Law to Convert Volume to Mass

Using $P V = m R T$, the density of intake air is:

$$
\rho_a = \frac{P_{\text{man}}}{R \cdot T_{\text{man}}} \quad [\text{kg}/\text{m}^3]
$$

Therefore, the **air mass flow rate** is:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{actual}}
$$

### Step 4: Combine the Equations

Substituting $\rho_a$ and $\dot{V}_{\text{actual}}$:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{\eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N}{2 \times 60} \cdot \frac{P_{\text{man}}}{R \cdot T_{\text{man}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{m}_a = \frac{\eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{\text{man}}}{120 \cdot R \cdot T_{\text{man}}}\;}
$$

**This is the speed-density equation used in every KTU board problem on engine management.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Injector Pulse Width (Fuel Calculation)

The ECU must command a fuel injector pulse duration such that the fuel mass delivered equals the **target fuel mass**.

### Step 1: Determine Target Fuel Mass

The target fuel mass per second is the air mass divided by the target AFR:

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\text{AFR}_{\text{target}}}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Closed-Loop Lambda Correction

If the upstream oxygen sensor reports a deviation, the ECU applies a **Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT)** correction factor $F_{\text{STFT}}$:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\text{corrected}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot (1 + F_{\text{STFT}})
$$

Long-term fuel trim $F_{\text{LTFT}}$ is also applied (learned over many cycles):

$$
\dot{m}_{f,\text{final}} = \dot{m}_{f,\text{corrected}} \cdot (1 + F_{\text{LTFT}})
$$

### Step 3: Determine Injector Pulse Width

Injectors are **flow devices** characterized by a **static flow rate $Q_{\text{inj}}$** in kg/s at a reference pressure. The required pulse width is:

$$
T_{\text{inj}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{f,\text{final}}}{Q_{\text{inj}}}
$$

### Step 4: Battery Voltage Compensation

At lower battery voltage, the injector solenoid opens more slowly, so an **injector dead-time $t_{d}(V_b)$** must be added:

$$
T_{\text{inj,final}} = T_{\text{inj}} + t_d(V_b)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical injector dead-time** is 0.8 ms at 14 V and ~3.0 ms at 8 V. The ECU stores a 2D lookup table $t_d(V_b, T_{\text{coolant}})$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical Board-Style)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2023 style):**
> A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has $V_d = 0.0005 \text{ m}^3$ per cylinder, $N = 3000 \text{ RPM}$, $\eta_v = 0.85$, $P_{\text{man}} = 50 \text{ kPa}$, $T_{\text{man}} = 320 \text{ K}$, and $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$. The target $\lambda = 1.0$. Calculate: (a) Air mass flow rate, (b) Fuel mass flow rate, (c) Total air mass inducted per cycle, (d) Total fuel mass inducted per cycle.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $V_d = 0.0005 \text{ m}^3$, $N = 3000$ RPM, $\eta_v = 0.85$
- $P_{\text{man}} = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_{\text{man}} = 320 \text{ K}$, $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$

**Part (a): Air mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{\eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{\text{man}}}{120 \cdot R \cdot T_{\text{man}}}
$$

Substitute all values:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{0.85 \times 0.0005 \times 3000 \times 50{,}000}{120 \times 287 \times 320}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
0.85 \times 0.0005 = 0.000425
$$
$$
0.000425 \times 3000 = 1.275
$$
$$
1.275 \times 50{,}000 = 63{,}750
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
120 \times 287 = 34{,}440
$$
$$
34{,}440 \times 320 = 11{,}020{,}800
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{63{,}750}{11{,}020{,}800} = 0.00578 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Part (b): Fuel mass flow rate**

For $\lambda = 1.0$, AFR = 14.7.

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{14.7} = \frac{0.00578}{14.7} = 0.000393 \text{ kg/s} = 0.393 \text{ g/s}
$$

**Part (c): Air mass per cycle**

Working cycles per second for 4-stroke:

$$
f_c = \frac{3000}{120} = 25 \text{ cycles/s (for 4 cylinders total)}
$$

Wait — clarify: For 4 cylinders, the **engine** runs 25 cycles/s in total (one cylinder fires every 2 revs/4 cyl). Air mass per **single** cylinder cycle:

$$
m_{a,\text{cyl}} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \cdot 4}{N} \cdot 60 \cdot 2
$$

Easier: Per cycle for one cylinder, inducted air mass:

$$
m_{a,\text{cyl}} = \rho_a \cdot \eta_v \cdot V_d
$$

$$
\rho_a = \frac{50{,}000}{287 \times 320} = 0.5443 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

$$
m_{a,\text{cyl}} = 0.5443 \times 0.85 \times 0.0005 = 0.0002313 \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Part (d): Fuel mass per cycle**

$$
m_{f,\text{cyl}} = \frac{m_{a,\text{cyl}}}{14.7} = \frac{0.0002313}{14.7} = 1.573 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for the Examiner:** Always specify whether the answer is **per cylinder per cycle** or **engine total per second**. Confusing these units is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Control Strategy Summary

| Operating Mode | AFR Target | Spark Advance | Throttle | EGR | Loop Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cranking | 2:1 – 6:1 | Fixed low | Closed | 0 % | Open |
| Cold Start | 10:1 – 12:1 | Fixed | Fast idle | 0 % | Open |
| Warm-Up | 13:1 | Reduced | Part open | Low | Open |
| Closed-Loop Idle | 14.7:1 | MBT map | Closed (PID) | 5 % | Closed |
| Part-Throttle Cruise | 14.7:1 | MBT map | Modulated | 15 % | Closed |
| Wide-Open Throttle | 12:1 – 12.5:1 | MBT – knock | Full | 0 % | Open |
| Decel Fuel Cut | Fuel = 0 | — | Closed | 0 % | Open |
| Knock Event | 14.7:1 | **Retard 1°/cycle** | Steady | — | Closed (KS feedback) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SI Engine Management

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete closed-loop mechatronic architecture** of a modern SI engine management system. It maps every sensor, ECU processing block, and actuator in a single functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Pedal Position Sensor] --> S1
    A2[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> S1
    A3[Camshaft Position Sensor] --> S1
    A4[MAP Sensor] --> S1
    A5[MAF Sensor] --> S1
    A6[Intake Air Temp Sensor] --> S1
    A7[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> S1
    A8[Lambda Oxygen Sensor] --> S1
    A9[Knock Sensor] --> S1
    A10[Battery Voltage Sensor] --> S1
    A11[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> S1

    S1[Sensor Signal Conditioning] --> ECU1
    ECU1[ECU Input Processing ADC] --> ECU2
    ECU2[Engine Speed Load Calculation] --> ECU3
    ECU3[Air Mass Estimation Speed-Density] --> ECU4
    ECU4[Fuel Mass Calculation Lambda Target] --> ECU5
    ECU5[Injector Pulse Width Computation] --> ECU6
    ECU6[Ignition Timing Map MBT Lookup] --> ECU7
    ECU7[Knock Correction Retard Logic] --> ECU8
    ECU8[Idle Speed PID Controller] --> ECU9
    ECU9[Throttle Position Controller] --> ECU10
    ECU10[OBD-II Diagnostic and MIL] --> O1

    ECU5 --> ACT1[Fuel Injector Driver]
    ECU6 --> ACT2[Ignition Coil Driver]
    ECU8 --> ACT3[Idle Air Control Valve]
    ECU9 --> ACT4[Electronic Throttle Body]
    ECU10 --> ACT5[EGR Valve Solenoid]
    ECU10 --> ACT6[VVT Solenoid]
    ECU10 --> ACT7[Evap Purge Valve]

    ACT1 --> ENG[SI Engine Cylinder]
    ACT2 --> ENG
    ACT3 --> ENG
    ACT4 --> ENG
    ACT5 --> ENG
    ACT6 --> ENG
    ACT7 --> ENG

    ENG --> EXH[Exhaust Manifold]
    EXH --> L1[Lambda O2 Sensor Feedback]
    L1 --> A8
    EXH --> CAT[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    CAT --> TAIL[Tailpipe Emissions]

    ENG --> KNK[Engine Block Vibration]
    KNK --> A9

    O1 --> D1[Malfunction Indicator Lamp MIL]
    O1 --> D2[OBD-II Diagnostic Port]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Trace any sensor (left) → signal conditioning → ECU processing block → actuator (right) → engine → back to exhaust → lambda sensor → ECU. This is the **canonical closed-loop mechatronic flow** for KTU 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.2 Control Loop Sub-Diagram — Closed-Loop Lambda Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Target Lambda = 1.00] --> C1[Summing Junction]
    M1[Measured Lambda from UEGO Sensor] --> C1
    C1{Sum Error e} --> C2[PI Controller]
    C2 --> C3[Injector Pulse Width Adjuster]
    C3 --> C4[Fuel Injector Actuator]
    C4 --> C5[Engine Cylinder Combustion]
    C5 --> C6[Exhaust Oxygen Reading]
    C6 --> M1
```

**PI Controller equation** (commonly tested):

$$
u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The Integral term $K_i \int e \, dt$ is what eliminates the **steady-state error** in lambda control. Without it, the AFR would always drift slightly rich or lean. KTU loves asking *"Why is integral action used in lambda control?"* — answer: to drive the mean error to zero so the three-way catalyst always operates in its conversion window.

---

## 4.3 Spark Timing Decision Tree (Logic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Engine Running] --> Q1{Crank Angle TDC?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> N2[Compute RPM and Load]
    Q1 -- No --> N1
    N2 --> N3[Lookup Base Spark from MBT Map]
    N3 --> N4[Add Coolant Correction]
    N4 --> N5[Add MAP / IAT Correction]
    N5 --> N6[Check Knock Sensor Window 7-15 kHz]
    N6 -- Knock Detected --> N7[Retard Spark by 2 deg]
    N6 -- No Knock --> N8[Re-advance Slowly by 0.5 deg per cycle]
    N7 --> N9[Fire Ignition Coil]
    N8 --> N9
    N9 --> N1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: List any three parameters that are controlled in an SI engine management system. Briefly state why each is controlled.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR / λ):** Controlled to maintain stoichiometric ratio (14.7:1) so that the three-way catalytic converter can simultaneously reduce CO, HC, and NOx emissions. (1 mark)
2. **Ignition Timing (Spark Advance):** Controlled to fire the spark at the MBT (Minimum advance for Best Torque) position so that peak cylinder pressure occurs ~15° ATDC, giving maximum torque without knock. (1 mark)
3. **Idle Speed:** Controlled to maintain a stable target RPM (~750 rpm) at closed throttle by adjusting the IAC valve or electronic throttle, compensating for accessory loads (A/C, alternator). (1 mark)

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the role of a lambda (oxygen) sensor in a closed-loop SI engine management system? What is the target lambda value during closed-loop operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The lambda sensor is mounted in the exhaust manifold **upstream of the catalytic converter** and generates a voltage signal proportional to the residual oxygen concentration in the exhaust gases. The ECU uses this signal as **feedback** to a PI controller that continuously trims the injector pulse width, keeping the actual AFR within ±1 % of stoichiometric. (2 marks)

**Target lambda value during closed-loop operation:** $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ (i.e., AFR = 14.7 ± 0.15). (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, and a student answers ONE of two internal choices. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]

**Q: With the help of a block diagram, explain the various parameters controlled in an SI engine management system. Discuss the role of sensors and actuators in detail.**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Overview of Parameters to be Controlled (7 marks)**

The SI engine management system is a **real-time, multi-variable, closed-loop mechatronic control system** built around the Engine Control Unit (ECU). It controls the following primary parameters:

| S.No | Parameter | Control Objective | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) | Maintain λ = 1.00 for TWC efficiency | Closed-loop |
| 2 | Ignition Timing | Achieve MBT without knock | Closed-loop |
| 3 | Throttle Position | Match driver demand + traction control | Closed-loop (PID) |
| 4 | Idle Speed | Stable 750 RPM at closed throttle | Closed-loop (PID) |
| 5 | EGR Rate | Reduce NOx at part load | Open/Closed |
| 6 | Variable Valve Timing | Optimize ηᵥ + EGR overlap | Closed |
| 7 | Cold-Start Enrichment | Smooth cranking below 60 °C | Open-loop |
| 8 | Decel Fuel Cut | Save fuel during engine braking | Open-loop |
| 9 | Evaporative Purge | Control fuel vapor emissions | Closed |
| 10 | Knock Correction | Retard spark if KS detects knock | Closed |

(Detailed explanation of each is expected — 5 marks for the table + 2 marks for the conceptual flow that "all parameters trace back to AFR, Spark, and Throttle as the three master variables".)

**Part (b) — Role of Sensors and Actuators (7 marks)**

**Sensors (3.5 marks):** The ECU continuously polls sensors and converts them to digital signals via the ADC. Key sensors include:

- **CKP & CMP:** Provide engine speed and cylinder identification; foundation for all timing decisions.
- **MAP / MAF:** Provide air mass for fuel calculation. Speed-density uses MAP + IAT; mass-flow uses MAF directly.
- **TPS:** Translates driver intent; feedforward input to throttle and fuel maps.
- **UEGO Lambda Sensor:** Exhaust O₂ feedback for closed-loop AFR trim. Switches from open-loop to closed-loop once hot (~600 °C).
- **Knock Sensor (Piezo):** Mounted on block; detects 7 kHz vibration signature of detonation. ECU retards spark 1° at a time.
- **CLT & IAT:** Temperature inputs for enrichment, EGR compensation, and spark advance.

**Actuators (3.5 marks):** The ECU drives actuators via PWM or H-bridge drivers. Key actuators include:

- **Fuel Injector:** Receives a precisely timed pulse (typically 2–4 ms at idle, 10–15 ms at WOT). Driver handles inductance and flyback.
- **Ignition Coil (Dwell-controlled):** ECU charges coil for a calculated "dwell time" then fires. Dwell is mapped against $V_b$ to maintain constant secondary energy.
- **Electronic Throttle Body:** DC servo motor with dual TPS feedback; closed-loop PID tracks target position.
- **IAC Valve / Stepper Motor:** Modulates bypass air at idle.
- **EGR Solenoid & VVT Solenoid:** PWM-controlled vacuum or oil pressure valves.
- **MIL Lamp & OBD-II Port:** Diagnostic actuators to alert driver and technician.

(Include the block diagram from Section 4.1 here for full 7 marks.)

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]

**Q: Derive the speed-density equation for calculating the air mass flow rate in a 4-stroke SI engine. A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has displacement per cylinder $V_d = 500 \text{ cm}^3$, runs at 3000 RPM with $\eta_v = 85\%$, MAP = 50 kPa, intake temperature = 320 K. Using R = 287 J/kg·K, calculate the air mass flow rate in kg/s. State any assumptions.**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation of Speed-Density Equation (7 marks)**

*(Write the full derivation from Section 3.1 here with all four steps: theoretical volume displaced → apply ηᵥ → apply ideal gas law → combine. Show final boxed equation.)*

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{\eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{\text{man}}}{120 \cdot R \cdot T_{\text{man}}}
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Intake air behaves as an ideal gas.
2. Steady-state flow.
3. Manifold pressure and temperature are spatially uniform.
4. Volumetric efficiency is constant at the given operating point.
5. No exhaust gas residual effects.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $V_d = 500 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.0005 \text{ m}^3$
- $N = 3000$ RPM
- $\eta_v = 0.85$
- $P_{\text{man}} = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_{\text{man}} = 320 \text{ K}$
- $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$

Substituting into the speed-density equation:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{0.85 \times 0.0005 \times 3000 \times 50{,}000}{120 \times 287 \times 320}
$$

**Numerator:** $0.85 \times 0.0005 \times 3000 \times 50{,}000 = 63{,}750$

**Denominator:** $120 \times 287 \times 320 = 11{,}020{,}800$

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{m}_a = \frac{63{,}750}{11{,}020{,}800} \approx 5.785 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 5.78 \text{ g/s}\;}
$$

If the target is $\lambda = 1.0$ (AFR = 14.7), fuel flow rate:

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{14.7} = \frac{0.00578}{14.7} \approx 3.93 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

(7 marks: 1 mark for substitution, 2 marks for numerator computation, 2 marks for denominator, 1 mark for final answer, 1 mark for fuel follow-up.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SI Engine Management Questions**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students often write $V_d$ in cm³ but plug into the speed-density equation assuming m³. Always convert: $1 \text{ cm}^3 = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2 in 4-stroke (–2 marks):** The 4-stroke cycle completes in **2 revolutions**, so the divisor is $2 \times 60 = 120$, not 60. Mixing up 2-stroke vs 4-stroke formulas is the single most common error.
> 3. **Writing "AFR" without specifying mass or mole basis (–1 mark):** Always say **"14.7:1 by mass"** for gasoline.
> 4. **Confusing λ with φ (–1 mark):** $\lambda > 1$ means lean; $\phi > 1$ means rich. They are reciprocals.
> 5. **Open-loop vs Closed-loop mix-up (–1 mark):** Cold start, WOT, and decel cut are **open-loop**; lambda and idle are **closed-loop**. Examiners always check this.
> 6. **Ignoring the "per cylinder" qualifier (–1 mark):** When asked for fuel per cycle, divide engine total by number of cylinders, not the whole engine.
> 7. **Forgetting knock sensor frequency range (–1 mark):** Specify **7–15 kHz bandpass filter** is used to isolate knock from normal combustion noise.
> 8. **Missing "MBT" definition (–1 mark):** Always expand MBT on first use: **Minimum advance for Best Torque**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final 5-Min Read Before Exam)**

- [x] **SI Engine Management** = real-time closed-loop mechatronic system around an ECU.
- [x] **Three Master Variables:** AFR, Spark Timing, Throttle (Air Mass).
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7 : 1 (by mass).**
- [x] **Lambda λ = AFR_actual / 14.7** → Closed-loop target is λ = 1.00 ± 0.01.
- [x] **MBT** = Minimum advance for Best Torque; spark fires before TDC; peak pressure ~15° ATDC.
- [x] **Speed-Density Equation:** $\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot P_{\text{man}}}{120 \cdot R \cdot T_{\text{man}}}$ (4-stroke).
- [x] **Fuel calculation:** $\dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_a / \text{AFR}_{\text{target}}$.
- [x] **Injector pulse width:** $T_{\text{inj}} = m_f / Q_{\text{inj}} + t_d(V_b)$.
- [x] **Sensors to memorize:** CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, TPS, UEGO, KS, CLT, IAT, VSS, Battery V.
- [x] **Actuators to memorize:** Injector, Ignition Coil, ETB, IAC, EGR, VVT, Purge, MIL.
- [x] **Knock Sensor** uses 7 kHz bandpass; ECU retards spark 1°/step until knock stops.
- [x] **EGR** displaces fresh charge → lowers flame T → suppresses NOx. 0 % at WOT, max ~20 % at part load.
- [x] **Open-loop modes:** Cranking, cold start, WOT, decel cut.
- [x] **Closed-loop modes:** Lambda control, idle (PID), knock correction, electronic throttle.
- [x] **PI Controller** used for lambda: integral term kills steady-state error → TWC always stays in conversion window.
- [x] **VVT** optimizes volumetric efficiency and internal EGR overlap.
- [x] **OBD-II / MIL** are part of the diagnostic actuator subsystem.
- [x] **TWC (Three-Way Catalyst)** works only near λ = 1 → that is the entire reason for closed-loop AFR control.
- [x] **Speed-Density vs Mass-Flow:** Speed-Density uses MAP + IAT; Mass-Flow uses MAF. MAF is more direct; Speed-Density is more robust to leaks.
- [x] **Units trap:** $V_d$ in m³, $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K, $N$ in RPM, $R = 287$ J/kg·K for air.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (OEMUT834)**
> This topic directly addresses **CO4** of the Automotive Mechatronics course: *"Apply mechatronics concepts to engine management subsystems including electronic fuel injection and ignition systems."*

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** is a microprocessor-controlled, electronically-actuated fuel delivery system in which one or more electromagnetic solenoid injectors meter and spray precisely atomized fuel into the engine's intake manifold, cylinder head ports, or directly into the combustion chamber, based on real-time inputs from multiple sensors and a pre-programmed Electronic Control Unit (ECU) map.

**Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary):**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **EFI** | Electronic Fuel Injection — replaces the mechanical carburetor with sensor-driven electronic metering. |
| **ECU / ECM** | Electronic Control Unit / Engine Control Module — the central brain (typically a **32-bit microcontroller**) that processes sensor signals and drives actuators. |
| **MAP Sensor** | Manifold Absolute Pressure sensor — measures engine load indirectly. |
| **MAF Sensor** | Mass Air Flow sensor — measures the **mass (kg/s)** of air entering the engine. |
| **TPS** | Throttle Position Sensor — detects driver pedal demand. |
| **Lambda ($\lambda$)** | Normalized air-fuel ratio: $\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ |

---

## 2. Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart IV Drip vs. The Bucket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the carburetor as a bucket of water (fuel) poured into the engine based purely on gravity and vacuum.** The amount poured depends on how hard you suck through a straw — imprecise, sluggish, and wasteful.

> **EFI is like a hospital IV drip controlled by a digital infusion pump.** A doctor (the ECU) monitors the patient's pulse, oxygen level, and body temperature (sensors) and continuously adjusts the drip rate (injector pulse width) to the exact milliliter per second required by the body (engine). The result is precision, repeatability, and adaptability to changing conditions (cold start, altitude, acceleration).

This analogy makes it clear why EFI outperforms carburetion in:
- **Cold-start fueling** (enrichment)
- **Altitude compensation** (barometric pressure correction)
- **Emissions control** (closed-loop lambda feedback)
- **Driveability** (instant throttle response)

---

## 3. Why EFI Replaced the Carburetor — The Engineering Motivation

- **Stoichiometric Compliance:** Modern 3-way catalytic converters demand $AFR = 14.7:1 \pm 1\%$ for gasoline; only closed-loop EFI achieves this consistently.
- **Emission Regulations:** BS-VI / Euro 6 norms mandate precise fueling that mechanical carburetors cannot deliver.
- **Fuel Economy:** Precision metering reduces fuel wastage by up to **15–20%** compared to carbureted systems.
- **Self-Diagnostics:** OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics) requires electronic fault monitoring — only EFI supports this natively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — EFI Architecture & Operating Modes

## 1. Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EFI System

The EFI system is a classic **closed-loop mechatronic system** comprising three sub-systems:

| Sub-system | Function | Key Components |
|------------|----------|----------------|
| **Sensing** | Measure operating parameters | MAP, MAF, TPS, CTS, CKP, O₂ sensor, KS |
| **Processing** | Compute fuel pulse width | ECU (CPU + ROM + RAM + ADC) |
| **Actuation** | Deliver fuel & manage engine | Fuel pump, Injector, Ignition coil, IACV, EVAP valve |

---

## 2. Operating Modes of an EFI System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ECU continuously switches between **multiple fuel maps** based on operating conditions. Each mode has a distinct fuel calculation strategy.

### Mode 1: **Cranking / Cold Start Mode**
- Triggered when starter motor is engaged.
- Injectors deliver **highly enriched** mixture: $AFR \approx 2:1$ to $8:1$.
- Enrichment factor depends on **coolant temperature** and **ambient air temperature**.
- Additional "after-start enrichment" pulse added for the first few seconds.

### Mode 2: **Idle Mode**
- Throttle closed or near-closed (TPS < 5°).
- Idle Air Control Valve (IACV) bypasses air around the throttle plate.
- Target idle speed: typically **700–900 rpm** (warm engine).

### Mode 3: **Part-Throttle / Cruise Mode**
- Normal driving conditions.
- Closed-loop operation using **O₂ sensor feedback** to maintain $\lambda = 1.000 \pm 0.02$.

### Mode 4: **Wide-Open Throttle (WOT) Mode**
- Maximum power demand (TPS > 70°).
- ECU switches to **open-loop mode**, enriching mixture to $AFR \approx 12:1$ for maximum power.

### Mode 5: **Deceleration / Fuel Cut-Off Mode**
- TPS closed + engine speed > 1800 rpm.
- Injectors **shut off** completely to save fuel and protect catalyst.

### Mode 6: **Warm-Up Enrichment**
- Until coolant reaches **80 °C**, additional fuel is injected proportional to $(T_{target} - T_{coolant})$.

---

## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU Part B (14-mark) and Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize with units.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio ($\approx 14.7$ for gasoline) |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Lambda (equivalence ratio inverse) |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Equivalence ratio ($\phi = 1$ stoichiometric, $\phi > 1$ rich) |
| 4 | $m_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot N}{2}$ (per cycle) | Air mass per engine cycle |
| 5 | $m_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{AFR_{target}}$ | Required fuel mass |
| 6 | $T_{i} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{q_{inj}}$ | **Injector Pulse Width (seconds)** |
| 7 | $q_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{n} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot \rho_{fuel}}$ | Injector flow rate (Bernoulli) |
| 8 | $P_{rail} = P_{manifold} + \Delta P_{reg}$ | Fuel rail pressure (typically **2.5–3.5 bar**) |
| 9 | $BPW = T_{base} \cdot f(T_{cool}, \lambda, TPS, RPM)$ | Base Pulse Width correction function |
| 10 | $EGO_{voltage} = f(\lambda)$ | Exhaust Gas Oxygen sensor output (0.1 V lean, 0.9 V rich) |

**Where:**
- $V_d$ = displacement volume (m³)
- $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency (0.7–0.95)
- $\rho_{air}$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at STP)
- $N$ = engine speed (rev/s)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (~**0.9**)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure differential across injector nozzle

---

## 4. Real-World Utility

- **Bosch L-Jetronic (1973):** First mass-production EFI, used in Volkswagen 911.
- **Toyota D-4 (Direct Injection):** Cylinder-direct injection with **350 bar** rail pressure.
- **Bosch GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection):** Stratified-charge operation for 25–30% fuel economy gains.
- **Common Rail Diesel (CRDi):** Bosch, Denso, Delphi systems with **2000+ bar** rail pressure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## Derivation 1: Injector Pulse Width Calculation (The Most Tested Problem in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** *"A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder gasoline engine has displacement 2000 cc, runs at 3000 rpm at part throttle. Volumetric efficiency = 85%. Injector flow = 2.5 g/s. Target AFR = 14.7. Calculate the injector pulse width per injection event."*

### Step 1: Calculate Air Mass per Cycle

Given:
- $V_d = 2000 \text{ cc} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ (total displacement)
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$
- $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $N = 3000 \text{ rpm} = 50 \text{ rev/s}$

For a **4-stroke** engine, air is inducted once every **2 revolutions** per cylinder.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air,total\_per\_sec} &= \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot N}{2} \\
m_{air,total\_per\_sec} &= \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.85) \cdot (1.225) \cdot (50)}{2} \\
m_{air,total\_per\_sec} &= \frac{0.104125}{2} \\
m_{air,total\_per\_sec} &= 0.05206 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Distribute Air Mass Across Cylinders

For **4 cylinders**, each cylinder gets equal share:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air,per\_cyl\_per\_sec} &= \frac{0.05206}{4} = 0.013015 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Required Fuel Mass per Second per Cylinder

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{fuel,per\_cyl\_per\_sec} &= \frac{m_{air,per\_cyl\_per\_sec}}{AFR} \\
m_{fuel,per\_cyl\_per\_sec} &= \frac{0.013015}{14.7} \\
m_{fuel,per\_cyl\_per\_sec} &= 8.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} \\
m_{fuel,per\_cyl\_per\_sec} &= 0.8854 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Injector Pulse Width

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{i} &= \frac{m_{fuel,per\_cyl\_per\_sec}}{q_{inj}} \\
T_{i} &= \frac{0.8854 \text{ g/s}}{2.5 \text{ g/s}} \\
T_{i} &= 0.3542 \text{ s} \\
T_{i} &= 354.2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** Real-world pulse widths are **2–10 ms** for port injection. The discrepancy here is because we used **3000 rpm**. At lower RPM (e.g., 1000 rpm idle), pulse width drops to ~3 ms. Always re-check your unit consistency.

---

## Derivation 2: Lambda Calculation from O₂ Sensor

**Problem:** O₂ sensor reads **0.85 V**. Determine whether the mixture is rich or lean and compute $\lambda$.

O₂ sensor characteristics (Zirconia type):
- Voltage $> 0.45 \text{ V}$ → **Rich** ($\lambda < 1$)
- Voltage $< 0.45 \text{ V}$ → **Lean** ($\lambda > 1$)

Since $V_{O_2} = 0.85 \text{ V} > 0.45 \text{ V}$, the mixture is **rich**.

Empirical correlation (Nernst equation approximation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &\approx 1.0 - k \cdot (V_{O_2} - 0.45) \\
\lambda &\approx 1.0 - 2.2 \cdot (0.85 - 0.45) \\
\lambda &\approx 1.0 - 0.88 \\
\lambda &\approx 0.12 \quad \text{(heavily rich — fault condition)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A healthy closed-loop system keeps $\lambda$ oscillating between **0.97 and 1.03**. Values outside this band (like 0.12) indicate sensor fault or fuel pressure regulator failure.

---

## Derivation 3: Fuel Rail Pressure Drop Compensation

**Problem:** At WOT, manifold vacuum drops to 0.2 bar. Fuel rail pressure regulator maintains $\Delta P = 2.8$ bar across injector. Calculate the change in injector flow rate.

From the Bernoulli-derived injector equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{inj} &= C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot \rho_{fuel}} \\
\rho_{fuel} &= 750 \text{ kg/m}^3 \text{ (gasoline)} \\
q_{inj,idle} &= (0.9) \cdot (A_n) \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 2.8 \times 10^5 \cdot 750} \\
q_{inj,idle} &= (0.9) \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{4.2 \times 10^8} \\
q_{inj,idle} &= (0.9) \cdot A_n \cdot (20493) \\
q_{inj,idle} &= 18444 \cdot A_n \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pressure regulator **maintains constant $\Delta P$** regardless of manifold vacuum — hence flow remains stable. This is a key engineering advantage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Block Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: EFI System Functional Architecture (Closed-Loop Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Throttle Position Sensor TPS]
    C[Air Intake] --> D[MAF Sensor]
    D --> E[Engine Intake Manifold]
    E --> F[MAP Sensor]
    G[Crankshaft Rotation] --> H[CKP Sensor]
    I[Coolant Temperature] --> J[CTS Sensor]
    K[Exhaust Gas] --> L[O2 Lambda Sensor]
    B --> M[ECU Engine Control Unit]
    F --> M
    H --> M
    J --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Fuel Injector Driver]
    N --> O[Fuel Injector]
    P[Electric Fuel Pump] --> Q[High Pressure Fuel Rail]
    Q --> O
    O --> R[Engine Cylinder Combustion]
    R --> K
    M --> S[Ignition Coil Driver]
    S --> T[Spark Plug]
    M --> U[Idle Air Control Valve IACV]
```

