# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 OEMUT834):** Part A carries 3 marks each (typically 4–5 questions per module); Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** between Q-A and Q-B. Total module weightage in ESE: ~20%.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Electronic Fuel Injection. List its advantages over a conventional carburetor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Electronic Fuel Injection is a fuel delivery system that uses electronic sensors and an Engine Control Unit (ECU) to meter fuel through solenoid-operated injectors based on real-time engine operating parameters.

**Advantages over Carburetor (2 marks — list any two):**
1. **Precise Air-Fuel Ratio Control:** Maintains stoichiometric AFR ($14.7:1$) within $\pm 1\%$ via closed-loop lambda feedback.
2. **Improved Cold Start & Altitude Compensation:** Sensors (CTS, MAP) automatically enrich mixture or compensate for low air density.
3. **Lower Emissions & Better Fuel Economy:** Enables efficient 3-way catalytic converter operation; saves 15–20% fuel.
4. **Better Driveability:** Instant throttle response, no accelerator pump lag, no float bowl flooding.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is meant by lambda ($\lambda$)? What is its significance in EFI?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Lambda is the normalized (dimensionless) air-fuel ratio:

$$
\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}
$$

For gasoline, $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7:1$. Therefore $\lambda = 1.0$ means stoichiometric mixture.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- $\lambda < 1$ → Rich mixture (excess fuel, used at WOT for power)
- $\lambda > 1$ → Lean mixture (excess air, used for fuel economy)
- $\lambda = 1.000 \pm 0.02$ → Required for 3-way catalytic converter to reduce CO, HC, and NOₓ simultaneously
- The ECU uses the **O₂ lambda sensor** in closed-loop mode to drive $\lambda \rightarrow 1$.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)** system. List the major sensors and actuators used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** [Refer to Diagram 1 in Section 4]

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**
1. Air enters through the air filter, mass flow measured by **MAF sensor**.
2. Throttle position (TPS) and crankshaft position (CKP) are continuously monitored.
3. The **ECU** computes the air mass per cylinder per cycle and looks up the required injector pulse width from the fuel map (stored in ROM).
4. A high-pressure electric fuel pump supplies fuel at **2.5–3.5 bar** to the fuel rail.
5. The **fuel pressure regulator** maintains constant pressure differential across the injectors.
6. Each injector is triggered by the ECU precisely during the **intake stroke** (sequential MPFI).
7. The **O₂ sensor** in the exhaust provides feedback; ECU adjusts pulse width to maintain $\lambda = 1$.

**Sensors & Actuators Table (1 mark):**

| Sensors | Actuators |
|---------|-----------|
| MAF, MAP, TPS, CTS, CKP, O₂, KS | Fuel Injectors, Fuel Pump, Ignition Coil, IACV |

**Q. (b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder SI engine has displacement 1.6 L, runs at 2500 rpm. Volumetric efficiency = 80%. Injector flow = 3.0 g/s. Calculate the **injector pulse width** for stoichiometric operation. Take AFR = 14.7, air density = 1.225 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert units (0.5 mark):**
$V_d = 1.6 \text{ L} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$; $N = 2500/60 = 41.67 \text{ rev/s}$

**Step 2 — Total air mass flow rate (2 marks):**

$$
m_{air,total} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot N}{2}
$$

$$
m_{air,total} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-3})(0.80)(1.225)(41.67)}{2} = 0.03265 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Air per cylinder (1 mark):**

$$
m_{air,per\_cyl} = \frac{0.03265}{4} = 8.163 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Required fuel mass (2 marks):**

$$
m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air,per\_cyl}}{AFR} = \frac{8.163 \times 10^{-3}}{14.7} = 5.553 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 0.5553 \text{ g/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Injector pulse width (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_i = \frac{m_{fuel}}{q_{inj}} = \frac{0.5553}{3.0} = 0.1851 \text{ s} \approx 185.1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_i \approx 185 \text{ ms}$ per injection event.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks): [Alternate Choice for Internal Option]

**Q. (a)** Explain the different **types of Electronic Fuel Injection systems** with sketches. Compare **SPFI, MPFI, and GDI**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Types of EFI (4 marks):**
- **Single-Point Fuel Injection (SPFI / TBI / CFI):** Uses a single injector (or two) mounted on the throttle body. Fuel sprays into the air stream above the intake manifold. Pressure: ~1 bar. Cost-effective, used in economy vehicles (e.g., Bosch Mono-Jetronic).
- **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI / PFI):** One injector per cylinder, mounted near the intake port. Pressure: 2.5–3.5 bar. Provides better cylinder-to-cylinder fuel distribution.
- **Direct Injection (GDI / D-4):** Injector placed in the combustion chamber, sprays directly onto piston crown. Pressure: **100–350 bar**. Enables stratified-charge operation for lean-burn (BS-VI compliant).

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | SPFI | MPFI | GDI |
|-----------|------|------|-----|
| Injector Location | Throttle body | Intake port | Combustion chamber |
| Rail Pressure | 1 bar | 2.5–3.5 bar | 100–350 bar |
| Fuel Atomization | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| Cold Start | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Fuel Economy | Fair | Good | Excellent (15–20% gain) |
| Emission Control | Basic | 3-way cat compatible | Best (lean NOₓ trap) |

**Q. (b)** Describe the role of the following sensors in EFI: **(i) MAF (ii) MAP (iii) O₂ Lambda (iv) CKP. (7 marks — 1.75 marks each)**

#### Model Solution:

**(i) MAF — Mass Air Flow Sensor (Hot-Film / Vane Meter):**
Measures the **mass (kg/s)** of air entering the engine. The ECU uses this as the primary input for fuel calculation. The hot-film type works by measuring current required to maintain a heated sensing element at constant temperature differential above intake air. More accurate than MAP-based speed-density systems.

**(ii) MAP — Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor:**
Measures pressure inside the intake manifold. Used as a load indicator. In **speed-density systems** (no MAF), MAP is the primary input: $m_{air} = f(MAP, RPM)$.

**(iii) O₂ Lambda Sensor (Heated Zirconia):**
Mounted in the exhaust manifold. Generates voltage based on oxygen concentration differential. Output switches abruptly around **0.45 V** at stoichiometric ($\lambda = 1$). Enables **closed-loop** fuel trim by PID controller in ECU.

**(iv) CKP — Crankshaft Position Sensor:**
Hall-effect or magnetic pickup sensor that detects **TDC** of cylinder 1 and engine RPM. Critical for **injection timing** (when to fire the injector) and **ignition timing**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert rpm to rev/s ($N/60$) or cc to m³ ($10^{-6}$) costs 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **4-Stroke vs 2-Stroke:** Always divide by 2 for 4-stroke engines (one intake per 2 revolutions). Wrong divisor = wrong answer.
> 3. **AFR Direction:** Students often write $m_{fuel} \times AFR$ instead of dividing. Correct formula is $m_{air}/AFR$.
> 4. **No Block Diagram = 0 Marks:** A question asking for "neat diagram" without a diagram gets zero for that part. Always draw with arrow labels.
> 5. **Stoichiometric Value:** Writing 14.7 without units is fine, but writing "AFR = 14.7" without specifying "for gasoline" loses 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Lambda Confusion:** Lambda $\lambda < 1$ means RICH, $\lambda > 1$ means LEAN. Reverse interpretation = -1 mark.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** EFI = sensor-driven, ECU-controlled fuel metering via solenoid injectors.
- **Key Components:** ECU, Fuel Pump, Fuel Rail, Injectors, Pressure Regulator, Sensors (MAF/MAP, TPS, CTS, CKP, O₂).
- **Three Types:** SPFI (throttle body), MPFI (port), GDI (direct).
- **Injection Strategies:** Simultaneous, Group, Sequential — sequential is best.
- **Operating Modes:** Cranking (rich), Idle, Cruise (closed-loop), WOT (open-loop rich), Deceleration (cut-off), Warm-up (enrichment).
- **Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline):** $14.7:1$ — critical for 3-way catalytic converter.
- **Lambda ($\lambda$):** Normalized AFR; closed-loop target = $1.000 \pm 0.02$.
- **Injector's Pulse Width Equation:** $T_i = m_{fuel}/q_{inj}$ — base value modified by temperature, TPS, lambda correction maps.
- **Fuel Rail Pressure:** 2.5–3.5 bar (PFI) or 100–350 bar (GDI); constant $\Delta P$ maintained across injectors.
- **Volumetric Efficiency:** Typically 0.7–0.95; affects air mass calculation per cycle.
- **Closed-Loop PID:** ECU uses O₂ sensor + PID controller to drive $\lambda \rightarrow 1.0$.
- **BS-VI / Euro 6 Compliance:** Only closed-loop sequential MPFI or GDI systems meet norms.
- **OBD-II Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs):** P0171 (system lean), P0172 (system rich), P0300 (random misfire) — all EFI-related.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for EFI Operating Modes: "C-I-P-W-D"** = **C**old start, **I**dle, **P**art-throttle, **W**ide-open, **D**eceleration. Recall in this order for viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Ignition System and its Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electronic Ignition System and its Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Electronic Ignition System and its Advantages

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electronic Ignition System (EIS)** is a microprocessor- or transistor-based ignition system in a spark-ignition (SI) engine that electronically controls the **timing**, **dwell angle**, and **energy delivery** of the high-voltage spark required to initiate combustion of the air-fuel mixture in the combustion chamber — replacing the mechanical breaker points, cam, and centrifugal/vacuum advance mechanisms of the conventional ignition system with solid-state switches, sensors, and an Electronic Control Unit (ECU).

The heart of an EIS is the **ignition coil** (essentially a step-up transformer operating in the **5,000 V – 40,000 V** range) whose primary winding current is interrupted by an **electronic switch** (BJT, Darlington pair, IGBT, or MOSFET) commanded by the **ECU** based on inputs from crank-angle, cam-angle, MAP, IAT, and knock sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** Students must be able to (i) describe the construction and working of an electronic ignition system, (ii) differentiate the subtypes (CDI, TCI, DIS), and (iii) enumerate the **advantages and limitations** of electronic ignition over conventional ignition.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the **conventional ignition system** as a hand-cranked film camera where a human finger mechanically decides *when* to open the shutter (breaker points) and *how long* the film is exposed (dwell time). The contact surfaces wear out, the timing drifts, and the shutter action becomes inconsistent after a few thousand shots.

Now imagine a **digital DSLR camera** that uses an **electronic sensor and a microcontroller** to decide exposure timing to the **microsecond**. No moving contacts to wear, no mechanical drift, perfect repeatability shot after shot — even at 10,000 frames per second.

The **Electronic Ignition System is the "DSLR" of engine ignition.** Instead of mechanical breaker points physically opening to collapse the magnetic field in the coil, a **transistor switch** does it **electronically**, on command from the ECU. The "shutter speed" (dwell time) and "timing" (ignition advance) are computed *digitally* every single combustion cycle based on real-time engine operating conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Hook for Exams:** *If a student can memorise just one line —* "An Electronic Ignition System uses solid-state switching (transistor/IGBT) controlled by an ECU to interrupt the primary current of the ignition coil, eliminating mechanical breaker-point wear and enabling precise, adaptive spark timing." *— that is worth at least 3 marks in any KTU Part A question.*

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (bolded)

- **Primary battery voltage:** 12 V (typical passenger car)
- **Secondary discharge voltage:** 10,000 V – 40,000 V (≈ **20 kV – 35 kV** is typical operational)
- **Primary current at dwell:** 5 A – 7 A
- **Spark duration:** 1 ms – 3 ms (inductive) or up to **0.5 ms – 1 ms** (CDI)
- **Dwell angle:** 30° – 60° of distributor rotation (older systems); variable in modern ECU-controlled systems
- **Engine speed tolerance:** up to **10,000 rpm** (vs ≈ 3,500 rpm for contact-breaker systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Working Principle

The electronic ignition system operates in **four sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Sensing the Crankshaft Position**
A **crank-angle sensor** (variable-reluctance, Hall-effect, or optical) mounted near the flywheel/timing gear generates a pulse every time a tooth passes the pickup. The **CAM sensor** identifies the compression stroke for sequential fuel injection engines. These pulses are sent to the **ECU** as square-wave trigger signals.

**Stage 2 — Computing the Ignition Timing**
The ECU consults its pre-loaded **3-D ignition maps** (look-up tables indexed by RPM and engine load) to determine the **optimal ignition advance angle** in degrees BTDC (Before Top Dead Centre). It may also apply real-time corrections based on:
- **Knock sensor** input (retards timing if knock detected)
- **Coolant temperature** (more advance when cold for idle stability)
- **Intake air temperature** and **manifold pressure**

**Stage 3 — Dwell Control & Primary Current Build-up**
The ECU energises the **electronic switch** (IGBT/MOSFET) for a calculated **dwell time** so that the current in the ignition coil primary builds up to a target value (typically **5 A – 7 A**). Because the switch is solid-state, it can be turned on/off millions of times with **zero mechanical wear**.

**Stage 4 — Spark Generation (Field Collapse)**
The ECU abruptly switches OFF the primary current. By **Lenz's Law**, the collapsing magnetic flux induces a high-voltage EMF in the secondary winding. This voltage (≈ **20 kV – 35 kV**) is routed (via distributor or directly, in DIS systems) to the correct cylinder's spark plug, where it ionises the air-fuel gap and produces the **ignition spark**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Formula** | **Description & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\, L_p\, I_p^{\,2}$ | Energy stored in primary winding; $L_p$ in H, $I_p$ in A, $E$ in J |
| 2 | $e_s = -\,M\,\dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Induced EMF in secondary; $M$ = mutual inductance (H) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{V_s}{V_p} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Turns ratio of ignition coil; $V_s$ up to 35 kV |
| 4 | $V_{s,peak} = I_p \,\sqrt{\dfrac{L_p}{C_p}}$ | Peak secondary voltage (ignoring losses); $C_p$ = primary distributed capacitance |
| 5 | $E_{spark} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,C_s\,V_s^{\,2}$ | Energy delivered across spark plug gap; $C_s$ in F, $E$ in J |
| 6 | $t_{dwell} = \dfrac{\theta_{dwell}}{6\,N}$ | Dwell time in seconds; $\theta_{dwell}$ in degrees, $N$ = engine rpm |
| 7 | $R_{breakdown} \approx \dfrac{V_{BD}}{d_{gap}}$ | Spark plug dielectric breakdown; $V_{BD} \approx 30\,\text{kV/cm}$ of gap |
| 8 | $f_{trigger} = \dfrac{N \cdot n_{cyl}}{120}$ | Trigger signal frequency; $N$ = rpm, $n_{cyl}$ = number of cylinders |
| 9 | $\theta_{adv} = f(N,\, \text{load},\, T_{coolant})$ | Ignition advance (function of RPM, MAP, coolant T) |
| 10 | $P_{spark} = \dfrac{E_{spark}}{t_{spark}}$ | Average power of spark discharge; typically **30 mJ – 80 mJ** delivered |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Rule for KTU:** Always write units in **SI base** (V, A, H, J, s, °). Examiners in the KTU valuation camp *do* deduct marks for missing or inconsistent units.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cold-start reliability:** Higher spark energy (≈ **50 mJ – 100 mJ** vs 20 mJ in conventional systems) reliably ignites lean cold-start mixtures, reducing HC emissions during the first 30 seconds — critical for meeting **BS-VI / Euro-6** norms.
- **High-RPM operation:** Above **4,000 rpm**, mechanical breaker points "bounce" and miss sparks. Solid-state switches operate reliably to **10,000+ rpm**, enabling modern high-performance engines.
- **Closed-loop knock control:** The ECU can **retard timing by 1°–3° per millisecond** when knock is detected, protecting pistons and allowing engines to run closer to the MBT (Minimum advance for Best Torque) point — a **3%–5% fuel-efficiency gain**.
- **OBD-II diagnostic compatibility:** Misfire detection, dwell-time fault codes, and ignition coil failure logging are all possible only with electronic ignition.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in the Ignition Coil Primary

We begin with the fundamental energy stored in an inductor carrying a current $I_p$.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous power being delivered to the inductor from the battery is

$$
P(t) = V_p(t) \cdot I_p(t)
$$

where $V_p$ is the voltage across the primary winding and $I_p$ is the primary current.

**Step 2:** For an ideal inductor, the voltage-current relation is

$$
V_p(t) = L_p\,\frac{dI_p}{dt}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute this into the power expression:

$$
P(t) = L_p\,I_p(t)\,\frac{dI_p}{dt}
$$

**Step 4:** The total energy stored from $t = 0$ (when $I_p = 0$) to time $t$ (when current reaches $I_p$) is the integral of power:

$$
E = \int_0^{t} P(t)\,dt = \int_0^{I_p} L_p\,I_p\,dI_p
$$

**Step 5:** $L_p$ is a constant, so we pull it out of the integral:

$$
E = L_p \int_0^{I_p} I_p\,dI_p = L_p \left[\frac{I_p^{\,2}}{2}\right]_0^{I_p}
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluating the limits:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}\,L_p\,I_p^{\,2}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,L_p\,I_p^{\,2}\,}
$$

> This is the energy available to be transferred to the secondary winding and ultimately discharged across the spark plug gap. With $L_p = 5\,\text{mH}$ and $I_p = 6\,\text{A}$, $E = 0.5 \times 0.005 \times 36 = 0.090\,\text{J} = 90\,\text{mJ}$ — a typical modern EIS spark energy.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Peak Secondary Voltage (Turns Ratio Method)

The ignition coil is a step-up transformer. Applying Faraday's law to both windings:

**Step 1:** The induced EMF in any winding equals the number of turns times the rate of change of flux linkage:

$$
e = -N\,\frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

**Step 2:** Therefore for the primary ($N_p$ turns) and secondary ($N_s$ turns) windings, both linked by the same flux $\Phi$:

$$
e_p = -N_p\,\frac{d\Phi}{dt}, \qquad e_s = -N_s\,\frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide the two expressions, side by side, to eliminate the flux term:

$$
\frac{e_s}{e_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p}
$$

**Step 4:** The primary voltage $e_p$ is approximately the supply (battery) voltage, typically **12 V**. The turns ratio $\dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ in a typical automotive ignition coil is in the range **80 : 1 to 200 : 1**.

**Step 5:** Taking a representative turns ratio of $N_s / N_p = 200$:

$$
e_s = 200 \times 12\,\text{V} = 2{,}400\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 6 — Applying the Lenz's-Law "Field Collapse" Multiplication:** During the actual spark (switch-OFF) event, the flux change is not slow and continuous — it is **abrupt** because the ECU cuts the primary current in **≈ 1 μs – 10 μs**. The induced EMF is *not* simply $V_{bat} \times (N_s/N_p)$; the rapid $\dfrac{di}{dt}$ multiplies the secondary voltage by an additional factor. Empirically, a modern coil with $N_s/N_p = 200$ and 12 V battery can deliver **20 kV – 35 kV** at the spark plug.

**Practical engineer's formula used in industry:**

$$
V_{s,peak} \approx V_{bat} \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot k_{mult}
$$

where $k_{mult}$ (a multiplier between 5 and 15) accounts for the rapid collapse and the primary distributed capacitance ringing. With $k_{mult} \approx 12$:

$$
V_{s,peak} \approx 12 \cdot 200 \cdot 12 \approx 28{,}800\,\text{V} \approx 29\,\text{kV}
$$

**Result — in clean KTU form:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{s,peak} = V_{bat}\cdot\dfrac{N_s}{N_p}\cdot k_{mult} \approx (20\text{ to }40)\,\text{kV}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dwell Time from Engine Speed

**Step 1:** Dwell angle $\theta_{dwell}$ is the number of crank degrees during which the electronic switch is ON and primary current is building up.

**Step 2:** The time corresponding to one crank-shaft revolution is

$$
T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N}\;\text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3:** The time corresponding to one crank-shaft degree is therefore

$$
T_{deg} = \frac{60}{360\,N} = \frac{1}{6N}\;\text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4:** Dwell time is the product of the dwell angle and the time per degree:

$$
t_{dwell} = \theta_{dwell} \times \frac{1}{6N} = \frac{\theta_{dwell}}{6N}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{dwell} = \dfrac{\theta_{dwell}}{6N}\,}
$$

**Numerical example (exam-favourite):** For a 4-stroke engine running at $N = 3000\,\text{rpm}$ with $\theta_{dwell} = 36°$:

$$
t_{dwell} = \frac{36}{6 \times 3000} = \frac{36}{18000} = 0.002\,\text{s} = 2\,\text{ms}
$$

This 2 ms is the time available for the coil primary current to ramp up — fully sufficient for a typical $L_p = 5\,\text{mH}$ coil to reach saturation current.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Conventional vs Electronic Ignition

| **Parameter** | **Conventional (Breaker-Point) Ignition** | **Electronic Ignition System (EIS)** |
|---|---|---|
| Switching element | Mechanical contact set + cam | **Transistor / IGBT / MOSFET** |
| Wear-prone parts | Contact points, rubbing block | **None** (no contact wear) |
| Spark energy | ≈ 20 mJ | **50 mJ – 100 mJ** |
| Peak secondary voltage | ≈ 15 kV – 20 kV | **25 kV – 40 kV** |
| RPM limit | ≈ 3,500 rpm | **8,000 – 10,000 rpm** |
| Timing accuracy | ± 3° (mechanical drift) | **± 0.5°** (ECU-controlled) |
| Dwell angle | Fixed by cam profile | **Variable**, adaptive (Closed Loop) |
| Ignition advance | Mechanical (centrifugal + vacuum) | **3-D mapped**, with knock correction |
| Maintenance | Re-point / re-gap every ≈ 15,000 km | **Adjust-free** for life of vehicle |
| Misfire detection | Not possible | **Yes — OBD-II coded** |
| Cold-start reliability | Marginal | **Excellent** |
| Cost | Low | Higher (offset by long-term savings) |
| Pollutant emissions | Higher (timing drift) | **Lower HC, NOx** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Modern Electronic Ignition System (DIS / Coil-on-Plug Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT["12V Battery"] --> KEY["Ignition Key Switch"]
    KEY --> ECU1["Engine Control Unit ECU"]
    CAS["Crank Angle Sensor Reluctor or Hall Effect"] --> ECU1
    CMS["Cam Position Sensor"] --> ECU1
    MAP["MAP Sensor"] --> ECU1
    KNO["Knock Sensor"] --> ECU1
    ECU1 -->|"Dwell and Timing Command Signal"| IGBT["IGBT Ignition Driver Switch"]
    IGBT -->|"Switches Primary Current 0 to 5A in 2 ms"| COIL["Ignition Coil Step Up Transformer 12V to 30kV"]
    COIL -->|"High Voltage HT Lead"| SPK["Spark Plug Air Fuel Gap Breakdown"]
    SPK -->|"Flame Kernel Initiates Combustion"| CYL["Combustion Chamber"]
    ECU1 -.->|"Misfire and Knock Feedback"| DTCP["OBD II Diagnostic Port"]
```

### 4.2 Operational State Machine — ECU Ignition Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SenseTrigger
    SenseTrigger --> ComputeAdvance : Read crank cam MAP IAT sensors
    ComputeAdvance --> DwellOn : Turn ON IGBT for calculated dwell time
    DwellOn --> PrimaryCharging : Current ramps 0 to 6 A in coil
    PrimaryCharging --> SparkFire : IGBT switched OFF abrupt collapse
    SparkFire --> KnockCheck : Read knock sensor within 5 ms window
    KnockCheck --> Retard : If knock retard by 1 to 3 degrees
    KnockCheck --> Advance : If no knock move toward MBT
    Retard --> SenseTrigger
    Advance --> SenseTrigger
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Ignition Coil (Step-Up Transformer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PRI["Primary Winding Np turns Copper 12 V side"] --> CORE["Laminated Iron Core Shared Magnetic Path"]
    CORE --> SEC["Secondary Winding Ns = 200 x Np turns Fine Wire 30 kV side"]
    BAT2["12 V DC Supply"] --> PRI
    SEC --> HT["HT Output to Spark Plug"]
    SW["Electronic Switch IGBT"] -.->|"Interrupts Ip"| PRI
```

### 4.4 Sub-type Comparison Flow — CDI vs TCI vs DIS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EIS["Electronic Ignition System EIS"] --> CDI["Capacitor Discharge Ignition CDI"]
    EIS --> TCI["Transistor Coil Ignition TCI Inductive"]
    EIS --> DIS["Distributorless Ignition System DIS"]
    CDI --> CDI_USES["Used in 2 stroke scooters and small engines Fast rising voltage short spark 0.5 ms"]
    TCI --> TCI_USES["Used in 4 stroke cars Long spark duration 2 to 3 ms high energy"]
    DIS --> DIS_USES["One coil per cylinder or per pair No moving distributor best accuracy"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagrammatic Rule for KTU Exam Scripts:** Even if you cannot draw perfectly, draw a **labelled rectangular block diagram** with arrows showing power flow, signal flow, and sensor feedback. Examiners award up to **2 marks** for a neat labelled diagram in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Slot B]`  
> **Define the term "Electronic Ignition System" and list any **three** advantages it offers over the conventional contact-breaker ignition system. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

An **Electronic Ignition System (EIS)** is a solid-state ignition system in which an electronic switch (transistor, IGBT, or MOSFET) controlled by the Engine Control Unit (ECU) interrupts the primary current of the ignition coil to generate a high-voltage spark, thereby replacing the mechanical contact-breaker points of a conventional system.

**Three advantages:**

1. **No contact wear** — the switch is solid-state, so the system requires almost no maintenance.
2. **Higher and more consistent spark energy** (≈ 50 – 100 mJ vs 20 mJ), enabling reliable cold starts and complete combustion.
3. **Better timing accuracy** (within ± 0.5°) and the ability to **adapt advance angle in real time** based on engine speed, load, and knock feedback, leading to lower emissions and higher fuel economy.

*(Examiner's note: any three valid points earn 1 mark each; definition earns up to 1 mark.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`  
> **What is meant by "dwell angle" in an electronic ignition system? Why is precise control of dwell angle important? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **dwell angle** is the angular duration (in degrees of distributor/crankshaft rotation) during which the electronic switch is kept ON, allowing primary current to build up in the ignition coil.

Precise dwell control is important because:
1. **Insufficient dwell** → primary current does not reach saturation → weak spark → misfire.
2. **Excessive dwell** → coil saturates, wastes energy, overheats the IGBT switch, and reduces switch life.
3. The ECU varies dwell with **RPM and battery voltage** to keep spark energy constant across all operating conditions.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With the help of a **labelled block diagram**, explain the **construction and working of a transistorized (inductive) electronic ignition system** used in a modern 4-stroke petrol engine. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **peak secondary voltage** generated by the ignition coil and calculate its value for a coil with turns ratio $N_s / N_p = 150$, battery voltage $V_{bat} = 12\,\text{V}$ and collapse-multiplier $k_{mult} = 18$. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Construction** (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):
- **Battery (12 V)** — primary power source.
- **ECU** — receives crank-angle, cam-angle, MAP, IAT, knock sensor inputs.
- **Pickup / Trigger sensor** — variable-reluctance or Hall-effect type mounted on the distributor shaft.
- **Electronic switch** — IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor) acts as a fast ON/OFF switch for the primary coil current.
- **Ignition coil** — step-up transformer with primary (≈ 100 turns of thick wire) and secondary (≈ 15,000 – 25,000 turns of fine wire) on a common laminated iron core.
- **Distributor + HT leads** (in older systems) — route the high-voltage pulse to the correct spark plug; **eliminated in DIS systems**.
- **Spark plugs** — one per cylinder; gap ≈ 0.7 mm – 1.1 mm.

**Working** (sequential):
1. ECU reads trigger pulse and computes spark timing + dwell.
2. ECU turns ON the IGBT → current flows in primary winding, building magnetic flux.
3. After the dwell interval (≈ 2 – 3 ms), ECU abruptly turns OFF the IGBT.
4. The collapsing flux induces a high voltage in the secondary (≈ 20 – 35 kV).
5. High voltage is delivered to the spark plug, ionising the gap and igniting the air-fuel mixture.

*(Block diagram — 2 marks; component description — 3 marks; working steps — 2 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1:** Faraday's law for primary and secondary windings linked by the same core:

$$
e_p = -N_p\,\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \quad\text{and}\quad e_s = -N_s\,\frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

**Step 2:** Dividing eliminates the flux term:

$$
\frac{e_s}{e_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p}
$$

**Step 3:** Primary voltage is the battery voltage (12 V), so the *static* secondary EMF is

$$
e_{s,static} = V_{bat} \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_p} = 12 \times 150 = 1{,}800\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4:** During the actual spark event, the rapid $\dfrac{di}{dt}$ (≈ $10^{6}\,\text{A/s}$) multiplies the EMF by an empirical factor $k_{mult}$:

$$
V_{s,peak} = V_{bat} \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot k_{mult}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the given values:

$$
V_{s,peak} = 12 \cdot 150 \cdot 18
$$

$$
V_{s,peak} = 1{,}800 \times 18
$$

$$
V_{s,peak} = 32{,}400\,\text{V} \approx 32.4\,\text{kV}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{s,peak} \approx 32.4\,\text{kV}\,}
$$

This is well within the 25 – 40 kV operating range of a modern electronic ignition coil and is more than sufficient to ionise a 1.0 mm spark-plug gap (which needs ≈ 30 kV/cm × 0.1 cm = 3 kV minimum, plus a safety margin of × 10 for fouled plugs and high cylinder pressures).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **CDI, TCI, and DIS** electronic ignition systems in a tabular form, highlighting their typical applications. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An ignition coil has primary inductance $L_p = 6\,\text{mH}$ and is energised by a primary current $I_p = 5.5\,\text{A}$. Calculate (i) the energy stored in the primary winding, and (ii) the dwell time required when the engine runs at $N = 2{,}500\,\text{rpm}$ with a dwell angle of $42°$. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

| **Feature** | **CDI (Capacitor Discharge)** | **TCI (Transistor Coil / Inductive)** | **DIS (Distributorless)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy storage | Capacitor charged to 400 V | Magnetic field in coil primary | Magnetic field in coil (one per cyl) |
| Spark duration | **0.5 ms – 1 ms** (short) | **2 ms – 3 ms** (long) | **2 ms – 3 ms** (long) |
| Spark rise time | Very fast (μs) | Moderate (≈ 100 μs) | Moderate |
| Best suited for | **2-stroke scooters, small engines, lawn mowers, marine outboards** | **4-stroke passenger cars, MUVs, light commercial** | **Modern multi-cylinder engines (>4 cyl)** |
| Distributor | Yes (or electronic trigger) | Yes (older) / ECU (newer) | **None** — one coil per cylinder |
| Cold start | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| RFI noise | High (fast dv/dt) | Moderate | Low (controlled) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Typical spark energy | 30 – 50 mJ | 50 – 100 mJ | 50 – 100 mJ |

*(Correct identification of three systems and any 5 distinguishing parameters — 5 marks; typical application statement — 2 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Part (i) — Energy stored in primary winding:**

We use the standard inductor energy formula (derived in Section 3.1):

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}\,L_p\,I_p^{\,2}
$$

Substituting $L_p = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{H}$ and $I_p = 5.5\,\text{A}$:

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times (5.5)^2
$$

$$
E = 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 30.25
$$

$$
E = 0.09075\,\text{J} \approx 90.75\,\text{mJ}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,E \approx 90.8\,\text{mJ}\,}
$$

This is more than four times the spark energy of a conventional ignition system (≈ 20 mJ), which is why modern EIS engines can reliably ignite even lean air-fuel mixtures.

---

**Part (ii) — Dwell time:**

Using the dwell-time formula derived in Section 3.3:

$$
t_{dwell} = \frac{\theta_{dwell}}{6\,N}
$$

Substituting $\theta_{dwell} = 42°$ and $N = 2{,}500\,\text{rpm}$:

$$
t_{dwell} = \frac{42}{6 \times 2{,}500}
$$

$$
t_{dwell} = \frac{42}{15{,}000}
$$

$$
t_{dwell} = 2.8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 2.8\,\text{ms}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{dwell} = 2.8\,\text{ms}\,}
$$

This 2.8 ms is sufficient for a 6 mH coil to charge the primary current to ≈ 5.5 A at 12 V (theoretically $I = V \cdot t / L = 12 \times 2.8 \times 10^{-3} / 6 \times 10^{-3} = 5.6\,\text{A}$ — consistent with the given value).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons for Losing Marks on EIS Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** Always write V, mH, mJ, ms, kV. Missing units = 0.5 to 1 mark lost per sub-question.
> 2. **Drawing the diagram unlabelled.** A block diagram with no labels (Battery, ECU, Coil, Spark Plug) is treated as a *decorative* diagram and earns only 0–1 of the allocated 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing "ignition coil increases voltage" without quantifying.** KTU examiners expect either a numerical value (e.g., ≈ 30 kV) or the turns ratio in the answer.
> 4. **Confusing CDI with TCI.** Many students swap the *spark duration* values. Memorise: **CDI → short spark (μs–ms), TCI → long spark (ms).**
> 5. **Omitting the role of the ECU.** Any 14-mark question on EIS that does not mention the **ECU's role in adaptive timing and dwell control** typically loses 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition (1-liner):** Solid-state switching + ECU-controlled timing of the ignition coil's primary current to generate the spark.
- **Three main subtypes:** CDI (capacitor discharge), TCI (inductive / transistor coil), DIS (distributorless, one coil per cylinder).
- **Peak secondary voltage:** ≈ 20 kV – 40 kV, derived from $V_{s} = V_{bat} \cdot (N_s/N_p) \cdot k_{mult}$.
- **Spark energy stored in coil:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,L_p\,I_p^{\,2}$; typical modern value = 50 – 100 mJ.
- **Dwell time formula:** $t_{dwell} = \theta_{dwell} / (6N)$ — decreases as engine RPM increases.
- **No moving contact parts** = no wear = no periodic maintenance = better long-term reliability.
- **Knock sensor closed-loop** allows the ECU to advance/retard timing by 1°–3° for MBT operation.
- **High-RPM capability:** Solid-state switches work reliably above 8,000 rpm; breaker points fail above ≈ 3,500 rpm.
- **Cold-start advantage:** Higher spark energy reliably ignites lean cold-start mixtures → lower HC emissions → BS-VI / Euro-6 compliance.
- **Energy flow summary:** Battery → ECU-controlled IGBT → Coil primary (stores $E$) → Field collapse → Coil secondary (≈ 30 kV) → Spark plug gap → Flame kernel → Combustion.
- **Diagnostic capability:** Misfire, dwell fault, coil failure, knock retard — all logged as OBD-II / OBD-BS-VI codes; conventional systems offer none of this.
- **One-line advantage summary (must-memorise for 3-mark Part A):** *No contact wear, higher spark energy, precise & adaptive timing, better cold start, lower emissions, higher RPM capability, OBD-II diagnostics.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Control Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Control Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuel Control Map** (also called a **Fuel Map** or **Fuel Calibration Map**) is a multi-dimensional look-up table (LUT) permanently stored in the non-volatile memory (typically Flash/EEPROM) of the Engine Control Unit (ECU). It defines the quantitative relationship between measured engine operating parameters — primarily engine speed (RPM) and engine load — and the required fuel quantity to be injected per engine cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEMUT834):**
> "Fuel Control Maps are pre-calibrated, multi-dimensional data structures in the ECU that translate engine operating conditions (RPM, Load, Temperature) into optimum fuel injection commands to achieve target air-fuel ratios across the entire operating envelope."

In modern Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) systems, the ECU does *not* calculate fuel from first principles every combustion cycle. Instead, it **interpolates** between calibrated map cells to determine the correct injector pulse width (often expressed in milliseconds) for the current engine state.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chef's Recipe Book

Imagine you are a chef who must cook a dish instantly every time a customer orders, but you don't have time to think. You keep a **recipe book** at your station:

- **Rows** of the book = engine speed (low RPM, mid RPM, high RPM)
- **Columns** = how hungry the customer is (light load vs heavy load)
- **Each cell** = the exact quantity of spices (fuel) to add

When a customer arrives (the driver presses the accelerator), you simply glance at the row and column matching the order, and you know **instantly** how much fuel to dispense. You never "calculate" — you **look up** and **adjust** slightly for the environment (cold kitchen, hot kitchen = cold-start enrichment, warm-up enrichment).

> [!NOTE]
> The ECU is the chef. The fuel map is the recipe book. The sensors (MAP, MAF, TPS, CTS, IAT) are the customer's order slip.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following **bold** constants are the foundational numbers every KTU examiner expects a student to memorize for fuel map calculations:

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol/Gasoline):** **14.7 : 1** (by mass)
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel):** **14.5 : 1** (by mass; operates lean → 18:1 to 70:1 in real operation)
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (CNG):** **17.2 : 1**
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (LPG):** **15.5 : 1**
- **Density of Air at STP:** **1.225 kg/m³**
- **Gas Constant for Air (R):** **287 J/(kg·K)**
- **Volumetric Efficiency (ηᵥ) Range:** **0.60 to 1.00** (dimensionless)
- **Lambda (λ) target — Petrol with 3-way catalyst:** **λ = 1.000 ± 0.010**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambda (λ) is the most critical normalized fuel metric:**
> $$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoichiometric}}$$
>
> - **λ < 1** → Rich mixture (excess fuel, e.g., power enrichment)
> - **λ = 1** → Stoichiometric (catalyst efficiency peak)
> - **λ > 1** → Lean mixture (excess air, e.g., lean cruise for economy)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Fuel Control Map

A fuel control map is mathematically a **function of two or more independent variables**:

$$Q_{fuel} = f(N, L, T_{coolant}, T_{intake}, \lambda_{target})$$

Where:
- $Q_{fuel}$ = Fuel quantity per injection (mg/stroke or mm³/stroke)
- $N$ = Engine speed (RPM)
- $L$ = Engine load (MAP in kPa, MAF in kg/s, or TPS in %)
- $T_{coolant}$ = Coolant temperature (°C)
- $T_{intake}$ = Intake air temperature (°C)
- $\lambda_{target}$ = Target lambda value (dimensionless)

### 2.2 Hierarchical Map Architecture in a Modern ECU

Modern ECUs do **not** use a single map. They use a **layered, multiplicative map strategy** where each map contributes one specific correction. The final injector pulse width is:

$$T_{inj} = T_{base} \cdot K_{cool} \cdot K_{warm} \cdot K_{accel} \cdot K_{batt} \cdot K_{air} \cdot (1 + K_{transient})$$

This modular structure allows calibration engineers to tune each correction independently — a critical KTU 14-mark question theme.

### 2.3 Classification of Fuel Maps

**A. By Dimensionality:**

| Map Type | Independent Variables | Application | Memory Footprint |
|----------|----------------------|-------------|------------------|
| **1-D Map** | One (e.g., Coolant Temp only) | Cold-start enrichment, warm-up fuel adder | Very small |
| **2-D Map** | Two (RPM × Load) | Base fuel quantity — the "spine" of fuel control | Medium |
| **3-D Map** | Three (RPM × Load × Target λ) | Multi-mode engines (economy, normal, power) | Large |
| **4-D / 5-D Maps** | RPM × Load × Temp × λ × Altitude | Premium engines with altitude & flex-fuel compensation | Very large |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** "Explain the difference between a 2-D and 3-D fuel map with suitable examples" — appearing almost every cycle.

**B. By Functional Role:**

- **Base Fuel Map (Main Map):** Defines the stoichiometric fuel for fully warmed-up, steady-state operation.
- **Cold-Start Enrichment Map:** Adds extra fuel when $T_{coolant} < 40°C$ because fuel condenses on cold intake walls.
- **Warm-Up Enrichment Map:** Gradually reduces enrichment as coolant temperature rises from 40°C to 90°C.
- **Acceleration Enrichment (Tip-In) Map:** Adds a transient fuel pulse when TPS changes rapidly (acceleration pump replacement).
- **Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO) Map:** Cuts fuel when TPS = 0 and RPM > idle threshold.
- **Battery Voltage Compensation Map:** Lengthens pulse width at low voltage (slow injector opening).
- **Volumetric Efficiency Map:** Computes actual air mass entering the cylinder to back-calculate required fuel.

### 2.4 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Fuel Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a guaranteed KTU 14-mark question every year.**

**Open-Loop Control:**
- ECU calculates fuel **purely from map values**.
- No feedback from exhaust oxygen sensor.
- Used during: cold start, wide-open throttle (WOT), idle, transient tip-in/out, and fault conditions.

**Closed-Loop Control:**
- ECU uses the **Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen (HEGO / UEGO) sensor** voltage feedback.
- ECU adjusts fuel continuously to maintain λ = 1.000.
- Uses an integral (PI) controller with proportional and integral gains stored as maps.
- Active only in a narrow RPM/load window (light cruise).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **DO NOT use the pipe symbol `|` in tables** — use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units | Used For |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | $m_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{N / (2 \cdot 60)}$ | Air mass per cylinder per cycle | kg | MAP-based systems |
| 2 | $m_{air} = \dot{m}_{MAF} / (N / (2 \cdot 60))$ | Air mass from Mass Air Flow sensor | kg | MAF-based systems |
| 3 | $m_{fuel} = m_{air} / AFR_{target}$ | Stoichiometric fuel mass | kg | Base fuel calculation |
| 4 | $m_{fuel} = m_{air} / (\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich})$ | Lambda-controlled fuel mass | kg | Closed-loop fuel |
| 5 | $T_{inj} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{q_{inj}}$ | Injector pulse width | s | Final injection command |
| 6 | $q_{inj} = \dfrac{Q_{static}}{\Delta P_{static}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{rail}}{\rho_{fuel}}}$ | Effective injector flow rate | kg/s | Injector dynamics |
| 7 | $\lambda = AFR_{actual} \cdot AFR_{stoich}^{-1}$ | Normalized equivalence ratio | dimensionless | Feedback control |
| 8 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{air,actual}}{m_{air,theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless | MAP-based correction |
| 9 | $T_{inj,cold} = T_{inj,base} \cdot K_{cool}(T_{coolant})$ | Cold-start pulse width | s | Cold enrichment |
| 10 | $K_{accel} = K_{accel} \left( \dfrac{dTPS}{dt} \right)$ | Acceleration enrichment factor | dimensionless | Transient fueling |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

Fuel maps are the **heart of every modern vehicle** — from a Maruti Alto to a BMW M-series to a Caterpillar mining truck. They directly determine:

1. **Emissions compliance** — meeting BS-VI / Euro 7 standards requires λ = 1.000 ± 1% across the entire WLTP cycle.
2. **Fuel economy** — lean cruise (λ ≈ 1.05) saves 5–10% fuel on highways.
3. **Power output** — WOT enrichment (λ ≈ 0.78) prevents detonation and cools combustion chambers.
4. **Drivability** — smooth tip-in response depends on precisely tuned acceleration maps.
5. **Cold-start drivability** — without cold maps, no car would start below 10°C.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Base Fuel Pulse Width from First Principles

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU 2024 Scheme examination on this topic. We derive the injector pulse width required for stoichiometric combustion.

**Step 1 — Air Mass per Cylinder per Cycle (MAP-based Speed-Density Method):**

In a 4-stroke engine, one complete working cycle takes **two crank revolutions (720°)**. Hence, the number of fresh intake events per cylinder per second is $N / (2 \cdot 60)$ where N is in RPM.

The theoretical air mass that would fill the displacement at atmospheric density is $\rho_{air} \cdot V_d$. However, real engines do not fully fill the cylinder due to throttling losses, residual gases, and temperature effects — captured by volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$.

$$m_{air, per\, cycle} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{(N / (2 \cdot 60))}$$

**Step 2 — Fuel Mass Required for Stoichiometric Combustion:**

By definition of AFR, $m_{fuel} = m_{air} / AFR$. For lambda control:

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$$

**Step 3 — Injector Pulse Width Calculation:**

The injector delivers fuel at a constant effective flow rate $q_{inj}$ (kg/s) once energized. To deliver $m_{fuel}$, the ECU must energize the injector for time $T_{inj}$:

$$T_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{q_{inj}}$$

**Step 4 — Combining All Three Steps:**

$$T_{inj} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{q_{inj} \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{N / (2 \cdot 60)}$$

$$T_{inj} = \frac{120 \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{q_{inj} \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \lambda \cdot N}$$

This final equation is the **base pulse width** that would be stored in the ECU's base fuel map cell corresponding to (N, MAP) → which gives $\eta_v$ via the volumetric efficiency map.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has displacement $V_d = 1.6$ L (total), operates at $N = 3000$ RPM, MAP = 60 kPa, volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.85$, target λ = 1.00. Injector flow rate $q_{inj} = 2.5$ g/s. Calculate the injector pulse width per injection event. Assume stoichiometric AFR = 14.7.

**Step 1: Convert units and compute air mass per cylinder per cycle.**

$$V_d = 1.6 \text{ L} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\rho_{air} = \frac{P_{MAP}}{R \cdot T_{intake}} = \frac{60{,}000}{287 \times 300} = 0.697 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\text{Cycles/sec per cylinder} = \frac{3000}{2 \times 60} = 25 \text{ Hz}$$

$$m_{air, per\, cyl,\, per\, cycle} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.697}{25}$$

$$m_{air} = \frac{9.479 \times 10^{-4}}{25} = 3.79 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg} = 37.9 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2: Compute fuel mass per cylinder per cycle.**

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{37.9 \text{ mg}}{1.00 \cdot 14.7} = 2.58 \text{ mg per cycle}$$

**Step 3: Compute injector pulse width.**

$$T_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{q_{inj}} = \frac{2.58 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}}$$

$$T_{inj} = 1.032 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.032 \text{ ms}$$

**Answer:** $T_{inj} \approx 1.03$ ms per injection event at the given operating point.

### 3.3 Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency Map Compensation

The MAP sensor measures manifold absolute pressure, but this only equals cylinder pressure if there are no pressure drops across the throttle, intake manifold, and valves. Volumetric efficiency captures this:

$$\eta_v(N,\, MAP,\, TPS) = \frac{m_{air, actual}}{m_{air, theoretical}}$$

Stored as a 3-D map: $\eta_v = f(N, MAP)$ with possible TPS or cam-phasing override. The ECU divides the MAP-based air mass by this map value to correct for real breathing.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Map Correction Strategy

The table below shows how each auxiliary map **multiplies** the base pulse width. KTU examiners love asking students to draw or explain this hierarchy.

| Layer # | Map Name | Variable Input | Multiplier Symbol | Typical Range |
|---------|----------|----------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Base Fuel | RPM × Load | $T_{base}$ | 0.5 – 15 ms |
| 2 | Coolant Temp Enrichment | $T_{coolant}$ | $K_{cool}$ | 1.0 (warm) – 2.5 (cold) |
| 3 | Warm-Up Enrichment | $T_{coolant}$ + time since start | $K_{warm}$ | 1.0 – 1.4 |
| 4 | Acceleration Enrichment | $dTPS / dt$ | $K_{accel}$ | 1.0 – 1.6 |
| 5 | Battery Voltage Compensation | $V_{batt}$ | $K_{batt}$ | 1.0 – 1.25 |
| 6 | Air Temp / Density Correction | $T_{intake}$ | $K_{air}$ | 0.85 – 1.15 |
| 7 | Closed-Loop Lambda Trim | $\lambda_{sensor}$ | $K_{\lambda}$ | 0.85 – 1.15 |
| 8 | Injector Dead-Time Adder | $V_{batt}$ | $t_{dead}$ | 0.5 – 1.5 ms (additive) |

**Final Equation (Comprehensive):**

$$T_{inj,final} = T_{base} \cdot K_{cool} \cdot K_{warm} \cdot K_{accel} \cdot K_{batt} \cdot K_{air} \cdot K_{\lambda} + t_{dead}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fuel Map Lookup — Functional Flow Architecture

This Mermaid block diagram represents how the ECU interrogates the fuel map hierarchy in real-time. Every node ID is alphanumeric and follows the safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start of Injection Cycle ISR] --> A2[Read Crank Position Sensor CPS]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Engine Speed N in RPM]
    A3 --> A4[Read MAP Sensor Manifold Pressure]
    A4 --> A5[Read MAF Sensor Air Mass Flow]
    A5 --> A6[Read TPS Throttle Position]
    A6 --> A7[Read CTS Coolant Temperature]
    A7 --> A8[Read IAT Intake Air Temperature]
    A8 --> A9[Read Battery Voltage Vbatt]
    A9 --> B1{Lookup Base Fuel Map 2D}
    B1 --> B2[Interpolate Tbase from RPM x Load]
    B2 --> C1{Lookup Cold Start Enrichment}
    C1 --> C2[Apply Kcool from Coolant Temp]
    C2 --> D1{Lookup Warm Up Enrichment}
    D1 --> D2[Apply Kwarm from Coolant and Time]
    D2 --> E1{Detect TPS Rate of Change}
    E1 --> E2[Apply Kaccel Acceleration Enrichment]
    E2 --> F1{Lookup Lambda Target Map}
    F1 --> F2{Engine in Closed Loop Window}
    F2 -->|Yes| F3[Read HEGO Sensor Lambda Actual]
    F3 --> F4[PI Controller Adjusts Klambda]
    F2 -->|No| F5[Use Open Loop Lambda Target 1]
    F4 --> G1
    F5 --> G1
    G1[Multiply All Factors] --> G2[Add Injector Dead Time tdead]
    G2 --> G3[Final Injector Pulse Width Tinj final]
    G3 --> H1[Trigger INJ Power Transistor]
    H1 --> I1[Injector Opens and Sprays Fuel]
    I1 --> J1[End of Cycle ISR Return]
```

### 4.2 2-D Base Fuel Map — Cell Representation

A typical 16×16 base fuel map is shown below as a Mermaid heat-map representation. The ECU stores these values in non-volatile Flash memory.

```mermaid
graph LR
    M1[RPM 800 Load 20 kPa Tbase 1.2 ms] --> M2[RPM 800 Load 40 kPa Tbase 1.8 ms]
    M2 --> M3[RPM 800 Load 60 kPa Tbase 2.4 ms]
    M3 --> M4[RPM 800 Load 80 kPa Tbase 3.1 ms]
    M4 --> M5[RPM 800 Load 100 kPa Tbase 3.8 ms]
    M5 --> M6[RPM 2000 Load 20 kPa Tbase 1.0 ms]
    M6 --> M7[RPM 2000 Load 40 kPa Tbase 1.5 ms]
    M7 --> M8[RPM 2000 Load 60 kPa Tbase 2.1 ms]
    M8 --> M9[RPM 2000 Load 80 kPa Tbase 2.7 ms]
    M9 --> M10[RPM 2000 Load 100 kPa Tbase 3.3 ms]
    M10 --> M11[RPM 4000 Load 20 kPa Tbase 0.9 ms]
    M11 --> M12[RPM 4000 Load 40 kPa Tbase 1.3 ms]
    M12 --> M13[RPM 4000 Load 60 kPa Tbase 1.9 ms]
    M13 --> M14[RPM 4000 Load 80 kPa Tbase 2.4 ms]
    M14 --> M15[RPM 4000 Load 100 kPa Tbase 3.0 ms]
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop vs Open-Loop Mode Selection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    S1[Engine Cranking] --> S2[Open Loop Cold Start Mode]
    S2 --> S3[Open Loop Warm Up Mode]
    S3 --> S4{All Conditions Met}
    S4 --> S5[Closed Loop Active Mode]
    S5 --> S6{Any Condition Violated}
    S6 --> S7[Open Loop Power Enrichment Mode]
    S7 --> S4
    S6 --> S8[Open Loop Idle Mode]
    S8 --> S4
    S6 --> S9[Open Loop Deceleration Fuel Cut Off]
    S9 --> S4
```

**Conditions for Closed-Loop Activation (typical):**

1. Coolant temperature > 70°C
2. Engine speed between 1200 RPM and 3500 RPM
3. Load between 25% and 70%
4. No HEGO sensor fault (P0130–P0135 not set)
5. Time since start > 60 seconds
6. No active misfire or catalyst damage fault

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define the term **"Fuel Control Map"** as used in automotive engine management systems. Mention any two physical quantities that form its axes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Fuel Control Map is a pre-calibrated look-up table stored in the ECU's non-volatile memory that relates engine operating parameters to the required fuel quantity per combustion cycle.

The two principal axes are:
1. **Engine speed (N)** in revolutions per minute (RPM)
2. **Engine load** — typically measured as manifold absolute pressure (MAP) in kPa, mass air flow (MAF) in kg/s, or throttle position (TPS) in %.

The ECU performs **bi-linear interpolation** between adjacent map cells to determine the precise injector pulse width for the current operating point. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark per axis = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

State the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** for petrol and distinguish between **open-loop** and **closed-loop** fuel control.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for petrol (gasoline) is **14.7 : 1** by mass — meaning 14.7 kg of air chemically combines with 1 kg of petrol.

| Aspect | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback sensor | None | HEGO / UEGO oxygen sensor |
| Lambda accuracy | ±5% | ±1% |
| Operating window | Cold start, WOT, idle, transient | Light cruise only |
| Adjustment mechanism | Fixed map value | Continuous PI trim |

*(1 mark stoichiometric value + 2 marks distinction = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **complete fuel map hierarchy** used in a modern multi-point electronic fuel injection (MPFI) system. List **at least six** correction maps and state the physical variable each one responds to. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a total displacement of 2.0 L. At an operating point of 2500 RPM and MAP of 70 kPa, the volumetric efficiency is 0.88. Intake air temperature is 35°C. The injector static flow rate is 220 cm³/min at 3 bar differential pressure. Calculate the **injector pulse width** required for **stoichiometric operation**. Assume universal gas constant R = 287 J/(kg·K) and stoichiometric AFR = 14.7. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fuel Map Hierarchy (7 marks):**

The ECU in an MPFI system does not use a single fuel map. Instead, it uses a **layered multiplicative hierarchy** of multiple maps. The base fuel quantity is determined by the 2-D base fuel map indexed by RPM and MAP. This base value is then sequentially multiplied by several correction factors, each obtained from its own dedicated map:

1. **Coolant Temperature Enrichment Map** — variable: $T_{coolant}$ (°C); adds fuel during cold start.
2. **Warm-Up Enrichment Map** — variable: $T_{coolant}$ and elapsed time; gradually reduces enrichment.
3. **Acceleration Enrichment Map** — variable: $dTPS/dt$; adds fuel during tip-in transients.
4. **Battery Voltage Compensation Map** — variable: $V_{batt}$; compensates for slow injector opening.
5. **Intake Air Temperature / Density Correction Map** — variable: $T_{intake}$; corrects for air density variation.
6. **Closed-Loop Lambda Trim Map** — variable: HEGO sensor voltage; fine-tunes to λ = 1.000.
7. **Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off Map** — variable: TPS = 0% and RPM > threshold; cuts fuel completely.

The final pulse width is:
$$T_{inj} = T_{base} \cdot K_{cool} \cdot K_{warm} \cdot K_{accel} \cdot K_{batt} \cdot K_{air} \cdot K_{\lambda} + t_{dead}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $V_d = 2.0 \text{ L} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- $N = 2500 \text{ RPM}$, $\eta_v = 0.88$
- MAP $= 70 \text{ kPa} = 70{,}000 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_{intake} = 35°C = 308 \text{ K}$
- $q_{inj} = 220 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$
- $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$, $\lambda = 1.00$

**Step 1 — Air density:**
$$\rho_{air} = \frac{P}{R \cdot T} = \frac{70{,}000}{287 \times 308} = 0.792 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Intake events per second per cylinder:**
$$f_{intake} = \frac{N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{2500}{120} = 20.833 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Air mass per cylinder per cycle:**
$$m_{air} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{f_{intake}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.88 \cdot 0.792}{20.833}$$
$$m_{air} = \frac{1.3939 \times 10^{-3}}{20.833} = 6.691 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg} = 66.91 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 4 — Fuel mass per cycle:**
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{66.91}{1.00 \times 14.7} = 4.552 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 5 — Convert injector flow to kg/s and find pulse width:**
$$q_{inj} = \frac{220 \text{ cm}^3}{60 \text{ s}} = 3.667 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

Using fuel density $\rho_{fuel} = 0.745 \text{ kg/L} = 0.745 \text{ g/cm}^3$:

$$q_{inj} = 3.667 \times 0.745 = 2.732 \text{ g/s} = 2.732 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$T_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{q_{inj}} = \frac{4.552 \times 10^{-6}}{2.732 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.666 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{inj} = 1.67 \text{ ms per injection event}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with suitable sketches the difference between **2-D and 3-D fuel maps**. Discuss how the ECU performs **bi-linear interpolation** between adjacent cells of a 2-D map when the actual operating point does not coincide with a discrete node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **closed-loop fuel control strategy** using a **HEGO (Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen) sensor**. Draw the block diagram of the PI controller and describe how the integral term ensures long-term lambda accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 2-D vs 3-D Fuel Maps (7 marks):**

A **2-D fuel map** has two independent input axes — typically engine speed (RPM) on one axis and engine load (MAP or MAF) on the other. Each cell contains a single output value: the base fuel pulse width. A typical production 2-D map has 16×16 = 256 cells.

A **3-D fuel map** adds a third dimension — commonly the target lambda value or coolant temperature. The map is essentially a stack of multiple 2-D maps, one per value of the third variable. A 16×16×8 3-D map contains 2048 cells.

**Bi-linear Interpolation Procedure:**

Suppose the engine is operating at $N = 2800$ RPM and MAP = 55 kPa, but the map has discrete nodes at (2000, 40), (2000, 60), (3000, 40), and (3000, 60). The ECU:

1. Identifies the four surrounding map nodes.
2. Performs linear interpolation along the load axis at fixed $N = 2000$ RPM → value $V_1$.
3. Performs linear interpolation along the load axis at fixed $N = 3000$ RPM → value $V_2$.
4. Performs linear interpolation along the speed axis between $V_1$ and $V_2$:

$$T_{base} = V_1 + (V_2 - V_1) \cdot \frac{N - 2000}{3000 - 2000}$$

This bi-linear interpolation gives a smooth, continuous fuel pulse width across the entire operating envelope.

**(b) Closed-Loop Control with HEGO Sensor (7 marks):**

In closed-loop mode, the **HEGO (lambda) sensor** mounted in the exhaust manifold produces a voltage that switches sharply between **~0.1 V** (lean) and **~0.9 V** (rich) around the stoichiometric point.

**Block Diagram Description:**

The ECU implements a **Proportional-Integral (PI) controller**:
- **Error signal:** $e(t) = \lambda_{target} - \lambda_{actual}$
- **Proportional term:** $K_P \cdot e(t)$ — provides immediate response.
- **Integral term:** $K_I \cdot \int e(t) \, dt$ — eliminates steady-state offset.

The output of the PI controller is the lambda trim multiplier $K_\lambda$, which continuously multiplies the base fuel pulse width:

$$T_{inj,closed} = T_{base} \cdot K_\lambda$$

**Why the Integral Term is Critical:**

The proportional term alone would leave a permanent offset (lambda slightly off from 1.000) because injector aging, air leaks, and altitude changes introduce constant disturbances. The integral term **accumulates the error over time** and forces $K_\lambda$ to settle at the exact value required for $\lambda = 1.000$, even if the base map was slightly miscalibrated.

**Operating Window:** Closed loop is active only between ~1200–3500 RPM and ~25–70% load to protect the catalyst from rich excursions.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Fuel Control Map Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for 4-stroke engines** — students often write $N/60$ instead of $N/120$, losing **2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Using wrong AFR** — diesel AFR is 14.5, petrol is 14.7, CNG is 17.2. Mixing these costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing up volumetric efficiency** — $\eta_v$ multiplies $V_d \cdot \rho_{air}$, not divides. A common sign error.
> 4. **Wrong injector flow units** — g/s vs cm³/min vs kg/h. Always convert to **consistent SI units before dividing**.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram** in 14-mark questions — examiners explicitly mention a "neat block diagram" — without it, **2 marks are forfeited**.
> 6. **Confusing open-loop with closed-loop window conditions** — closed loop is NOT active at WOT or cold start. Many students write the opposite.
> 7. **Not stating lambda target explicitly** — every fuel calculation should end with the λ value used.
> 8. **Ignoring fuel density** in injector flow conversion — 1 cm³ ≠ 1 g for fuel. Use $\rho_{fuel} = 745$ kg/m³ for petrol.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Fuel Control Map** is a multi-dimensional LUT stored in ECU flash memory that maps (RPM, Load, Temperature) → injector pulse width.
- The **stoichiometric AFR for petrol is 14.7 : 1** and lambda target with 3-way catalyst is **λ = 1.000 ± 0.01**.
- The **base fuel equation** is $T_{inj} = \dfrac{120 \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{q_{inj} \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \lambda \cdot N}$.
- For **4-stroke engines**, the cycle frequency is $N / (2 \cdot 60)$, not $N / 60$.
- **Maps are interpolated**, not stepped — the ECU uses **bi-linear interpolation** between four surrounding cells.
- **Open-loop control** uses map values only; **closed-loop control** uses HEGO sensor feedback through a **PI controller**.
- Modern ECUs use **multiple stacked maps** (base + cold + warm + accel + battery + lambda), multiplied together.
- **Volumetric efficiency** ($\eta_v$) corrects for incomplete cylinder filling — typically 0.60–1.00.
- **Cold-start enrichment** can multiply base fuel by up to **2.5×** when coolant is at -20°C.
- **WOT enrichment** drives lambda to **~0.78** to cool combustion and prevent knock.
- **Deceleration fuel cut-off (DFCO)** completely cuts fuel above ~1500 RPM with TPS = 0%.
- **Battery voltage compensation** adds 0.5–1.5 ms when $V_{batt}$ drops below 11 V.
- The **lambda trim multiplier** $K_\lambda$ in closed loop typically varies between **0.85 and 1.15**.
- BS-VI / Euro 6 emissions compliance is **impossible** without precisely calibrated fuel maps.
- The ECU's PI controller has both $K_P$ and $K_I$ gains stored as maps indexed by RPM and load.
- **Wall-wetting compensation** — when fuel condenses on cold port walls, transient maps inject extra fuel; when walls warm and re-evaporate fuel, transient maps subtract fuel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# CI Engine Management - Parameters to be Controlled

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Automotive Mechatronics (OEMUT834)**, **CI (Compression Ignition) Engine Management** refers to the closed-loop and open-loop electronic control of a diesel (compression-ignition) powertrain through an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** that monitors, processes, and actuates multiple subsystems in real time to deliver optimal torque, fuel economy, emissions, and drivability.

A **parameter to be controlled** in a CI engine is any measurable or commanded physical variable (e.g., injection timing, rail pressure, EGR rate, boost pressure, air mass, swirl ratio) whose set-point is dynamically adjusted by the Engine Management System (EMS) using inputs from sensors and outputs to actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly identifies the CI engine management stack as a **multi-parameter, multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) mechatronic system**, where the ECU must reconcile **combustion quality**, **emissions compliance (BS-VI/Euro-VI)**, and **transient driveability** simultaneously.

The canonical CI engine management parameter set is grouped into the following five domains:

1. **Fuel Delivery Domain** — Injection pressure, injection timing, injection quantity, number of injections (pilot/main/after).
2. **Air-Path Domain** — Boost pressure, throttle position, EGR rate, swirl/tumble, intake manifold pressure.
3. **Combustion Domain** — Start of combustion, peak cylinder pressure, pressure rise rate (dP/dθ), ignition delay.
4. **Emissions Aftertreatment Domain** — DPF regeneration, SCR dosing, NOx trap loading, lambda control.
5. **Auxiliary Domain** — Idle speed, coolant temperature warm-up, cold-start fuelling, governor action, fan control.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor:**
> Imagine a CI engine as an **orchestra** of subsystems (fuel pump, injectors, turbocharger, EGR valve, throttle, aftertreatment). The **ECU is the conductor**. Each musician (actuator) can play too loud or too soft, too fast or too slow. The conductor reads the sheet music (target torque/emissions map) and the sounds from the hall (sensor feedback) and continuously cues each musician. A parameter to be controlled is just one musician's volume/timing — but all parameters must be tuned **together** because raising one affects the others (e.g., more fuel demands more air, which alters EGR and emissions).

Equivalently, picture the engine as a **kitchen stove with multiple knobs**:
- One knob is the fuel quantity,
- Another is the timing (when fuel enters),
- Another is the air supply,
- Another is the exhaust recirculation.

If you turn any one knob, the dish (combustion) changes. The **ECU is the chef who turns all knobs simultaneously** to keep the dish perfect under varying "room" conditions (load, speed, ambient temperature, altitude).

## 1.3 Why Parameters Must Be Controlled

The combustion process in a CI engine is governed by the **diesel cycle**, where the **compression ratio** (typically **15:1 to 22:1**) raises air temperatures to **700–900 °C**, igniting injected diesel auto-genously. Unlike SI engines, there is **no throttle plate** to control load — load is controlled by **fuel quantity alone (Q_f)**. This makes the parameter control problem fundamentally different from SI engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant for KTU:**
> Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) for diesel ≈ **14.5 : 1** (mass basis). However, CI engines normally operate **lean** with overall excess air ratio **λ ≈ 1.2 to 4.0** at part load to control smoke, NOx, and fuel economy.

## 1.4 Sensors and Actuators — The Boundary of Control

| Direction | Device | Typical Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP) | Engine speed (N), crank angle (θ) |
| Input | Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP) | Injection phasing, cylinder ID |
| Input | Rail Pressure Sensor | Common-rail pressure (P_rail) |
| Input | Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAF) | Air mass (m_air) |
| Input | Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) | Boost pressure (P_boost) |
| Input | Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS) | Engine temperature (T_co) |
| Input | Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APP) | Driver demand (α%) |
| Input | Lambda / UEGO Sensor | λ (excess air ratio) |
| Input | NOx Sensor | Tailpipe NOx concentration |
| Input | DPF Pressure Sensor | DPF loading (ΔP across DPF) |
| Output | Fuel Injector (Solenoid/Piezo) | Injection events |
| Output | HP Fuel Pump / Pressure Control Valve | Rail pressure |
| Output | Variable Geometry Turbocharger (VGT) | Boost pressure |
| Output | EGR Valve (electrical/pneumatic) | EGR mass flow |
| Output | Throttle Valve (electronic) | Intake air restriction |
| Output | Glow Plug Control Unit | Cold-start aid |
| Output | SCR Dosing Module (AdBlue®) | DEF injection |

# Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Parameter Control Architecture

## 2.1 Hierarchical Control Logic

KTU 2024 emphasises that modern CI engine management follows a **hierarchical (layered) control structure**, similar to industrial automation. Each parameter belongs to a control loop with a specific bandwidth (response time) and priority.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three-Layer Control Hierarchy:**
> 1. **Upper-Level (Torque/Driver Demand Layer)** — Slowest, decides target torque from pedal & cruise control.
> 2. **Mid-Level (Coordination Layer)** — Translates torque request into sub-targets for each parameter (fuel Q, boost P, EGR).
> 3. **Low-Level (Servo/Tracking Layer)** — Fastest, drives individual actuators (injector solenoid, VGT vane, PCV) using PID control.

### 2.1.1 Upper Layer — Driver Demand to Torque Request

The accelerator pedal position α (in %) is mapped (via a look-up table) into a **desired torque T_req**, which is then converted into a **desired indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP_req)**:

$$T_{req} = f(\alpha, N, T_{co}, T_{amb})$$

$$IMEP_{req} = \frac{2\pi \cdot T_{req}}{V_d \cdot N}$$

where **V_d** is the displaced volume per revolution.

### 2.1.2 Mid Layer — Torque Split

Torque in a CI engine is dominated by fuel quantity, but refined by:

$$T_{ind} \approx \eta_{c} \cdot Q_{f} \cdot LHV$$

where **LHV** is the lower heating value of diesel (≈ **42.5 MJ/kg**), and **η_c** is the combustion efficiency.

The ECU splits T_req into:
- **Combustion torque** (from Q_f, timing, EGR).
- **Pumping torque loss** (from throttle, boost).
- **Mechanical friction torque** (speed/temperature dependent).
- **Auxiliary torque loads** (alternator, A/C compressor).

### 2.1.3 Low Layer — Servo Control

Each parameter is regulated by a **PID controller**:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where **e(t) = y_set − y_measured** is the tracking error.

## 2.2 The Major Controlled Parameters — Engineering Detail

### 2.2.1 Fuel Injection Pressure (Rail Pressure, P_rail)

The **Common-Rail** system maintains rail pressure independent of engine speed. Modern piezo injectors operate up to **2500 bar (250 MPa)**.

$$P_{rail} = f(Q_{inj}, N, T_{co})$$

Higher P_rail → finer atomisation → shorter ignition delay → lower soot.

### 2.2.2 Injection Timing (Start of Injection, SOI)

SOI is defined relative to TDC (Top Dead Centre). For diesel, optimal SOI is typically **8° to 12° BTDC**.

$$\theta_{SOI} = \theta_{base} + \Delta\theta_{temp} + \Delta\theta_{load} + \Delta\theta_{emissions}$$

- Advancing SOI → more time for premixed combustion → higher NOx, higher peak pressure.
- Retarding SOI → lower peak pressure, lower NOx, but higher soot and fuel consumption.

### 2.2.3 Injection Quantity (Q_f)

Determined by the **injector energising duration (t_inj)** and **rail pressure**:

$$Q_f = A_{nozzle} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot (P_{rail} - P_{cyl})}{\rho_f}} \cdot t_{inj} \cdot C_d$$

where:
- **A_nozzle** = nozzle flow area,
- **P_cyl** = in-cylinder pressure during injection,
- **ρ_f** = fuel density (≈ **835 kg/m³**),
- **C_d** = discharge coefficient (≈ **0.85–0.95**).

### 2.2.4 Number of Injections (Pilot / Main / After / Post)

Modern common-rail systems execute **multiple injection events per cycle** to control:
- **Pilot (small, early)** — raises P_cyl, lowers ignition delay, reduces combustion noise (rate of pressure rise dP/dθ < 5 bar/°).
- **Main** — delivers majority of energy.
- **After / Post** — raises exhaust temperature for **DPF regeneration** and reduces soot.

### 2.2.5 Boost Pressure (P_boost)

Provided by **Variable Geometry Turbocharger (VGT)** or **wastegate**. The VGT vane angle φ is controlled:

$$\varphi_{vgt} = K_p (P_{boost,set} - P_{boost}) + K_i \int (P_{boost,set} - P_{boost})\, dt$$

Boost pressure directly sets air mass flow, hence the **relative air-fuel ratio λ**:

$$\lambda = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel} \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$$

### 2.2.6 EGR Rate

Exhaust Gas Recirculation lowers combustion temperature (specific heat of CO₂ and H₂O > N₂) and **reduces thermal NOx formation**.

$$\dot{m}_{EGR} = \dot{m}_{intake} \cdot X_{EGR}$$

where **X_EGR** is the EGR mass fraction (typically 5–40%).

NOx formation (Zel'dovich mechanism) follows:

$$\frac{d[NOx]}{dt} \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{flame}}\right)$$

A **10 K drop in flame temperature** reduces NOx by ~7–10%.

### 2.2.7 Idle Speed Control

Without a throttle, idle is maintained by adjusting **fuel quantity and VGT position** to overcome friction torque:

$$N_{idle,set} = f(T_{co}, T_{amb}, accessories)$$

Target idle ranges from **650 rpm (warm)** to **1100 rpm (cold start)**.

### 2.2.8 Aftertreatment Coordination

The ECU coordinates SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) dosing with engine-out NOx:

$$\dot{m}_{DEF} = \frac{\dot{m}_{NOx,engine} \cdot M_{DEF}}{2 \cdot M_{NOx} \cdot \eta_{SCR}}$$

where DEF is Diesel Exhaust Fluid (AdBlue®, 32.5% urea in water).

DPF regeneration is triggered when ΔP across the DPF exceeds a threshold (typically **15–25 kPa**), injecting post-fuel to raise exhaust temperature to **~600 °C**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Table for KTU Board Exam.** Use `\vert` notation for absolute values to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | Parameter | Governing Equation | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Indicated Torque | $T_{ind} = \dfrac{IMEP \cdot V_d}{2\pi}$ | 100 – 600 N·m |
| 2 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}$ | 200 – 260 g/kWh |
| 3 | Air-Fuel Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel} \cdot AFR_{st}}$ | 1.2 – 4.0 (CI) |
| 4 | Injection Quantity | $Q_f = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_{rail} - P_{cyl})}{\rho_f}} \cdot t_{inj}$ | 5 – 80 mm³/stroke |
| 5 | Rail Pressure | $P_{rail,set} = f(Q_f, N, T_{co})$ | 250 – 2500 bar |
| 6 | Start of Injection | $\theta_{SOI} = \theta_{opt}(N, Q_f) + \Delta_{emiss}$ | 4° – 18° BTDC |
| 7 | Boost Pressure | $P_{boost,set} = f(N, Q_f, \alpha)$ | 0.8 – 3.5 bar (abs) |
| 8 | EGR Rate | $\dot{m}_{EGR} = \dot{m}_{int} \cdot X_{EGR}$ | 5 – 40 % |
| 9 | NOx (Zel'dovich) | $\dot{[NOx]} \propto \exp(-E_a / R T)$ | Function of T |
| 10 | Pressure Rise Rate | $\left(\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} \le 5$ bar/° | Combustion noise limit |
| 11 | Friction Torque | $T_{fric} = a + bN + cN^2$ | Empirical (Chen-Flynn) |
| 12 | DEF Dosing | $\dot{m}_{DEF} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{NOx,eng} M_{DEF}}{2 M_{NOx} \eta_{SCR}}$ | kg/h |
| 13 | DPF Regen Trigger | $\Delta P_{DPF} > \Delta P_{threshold}$ | 15 – 25 kPa |
| 14 | PID Output | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Servo law |
| 15 | Indicated Mean Eff. Pressure | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{ind}}{V_d}$ | 5 – 25 bar |
| 16 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{in}}$ | 0.95 – 0.99 |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** **"F-I-B-E-A"** → Fuel, Injection, Boost, EGR, Aftertreatment are the five control domains.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

In practice, these parameters must be controlled within **±2–5% tolerance** to:
- Meet **BS-VI / Euro-VI emission norms** (NOx ≤ **80 mg/km**, PM ≤ **4.5 mg/km**).
- Limit combustion noise (< **85 dB**).
- Deliver fuel efficiency of **42–48% (diesel)** vs **35–40% (petrol)**.
- Provide transient response to **0–100 km/h in < 10 s** for commercial vehicles.
