# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Driver Pedal to Injector Pulse Width

This is the **single most important derivation** for a CI engine management question in KTU exams.

**Problem Statement:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine has displacement **V_d = 2.0 L per cylinder**, crankshaft speed **N = 2000 rpm**, and driver demand requires **brake torque T_brake = 250 N·m**. Given mechanical efficiency **η_m = 0.85**, BSFC target = **220 g/kWh**, find the **required injector pulse width** if rail pressure is 1600 bar and in-cylinder pressure at injection is 40 bar.

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2000}{60} = 209.44 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute brake power:**

$$P_{brake} = T_{brake} \cdot \omega = 250 \cdot 209.44 = 52{,}360 \text{ W} = 52.36 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute indicated power using η_m = P_ind/P_brake:**

$$P_{ind} = \frac{P_{brake}}{\eta_m} = \frac{52.36}{0.85} = 61.60 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute fuel mass flow rate using BSFC:**

$$\dot{m}_f = BSFC \cdot P_{brake} = 0.220 \cdot 52.36 = 11.52 \text{ kg/h} = 3.20 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute fuel quantity per cylinder per cycle (4-stroke ⇒ 1 cycle / 2 revolutions):**

$$\text{Cycles per second per cylinder} = \frac{N/2}{60} = \frac{1000}{60} = 16.67 \text{ Hz}$$

$$Q_f = \frac{\dot{m}_f / n_{cyl}}{\text{cycles/s per cyl}} = \frac{3.20 \text{ g/s} / 4}{16.67 \text{ Hz}} = 0.048 \text{ g/stroke} = 48 \text{ mg/stroke}$$

Converting to volume using ρ_f = 835 kg/m³:

$$V_{inj} = \frac{Q_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{0.048 \cdot 10^{-3}}{835} = 5.75 \cdot 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3 = 57.5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 6 — Injector flow equation (derivation of pulse width t_inj):**

Starting from continuity for a nozzle:

$$Q_f = C_d \cdot A_{nozzle} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_{rail} - P_{cyl})}{\rho_f}}$$

For volumetric flow per stroke:

$$V_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{nozzle} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_{rail} - P_{cyl})}{\rho_f}} \cdot t_{inj}$$

Solving for t_inj:

$$t_{inj} = \frac{V_{inj}}{C_d \cdot A_{nozzle}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_f}{2(P_{rail} - P_{cyl})}}$$

**Step 7 — Plug in values** (assume C_d = 0.9, A_nozzle = 0.20 mm² = 2×10⁻⁷ m², P_rail = 1600 bar = 160 MPa, P_cyl = 40 bar = 4 MPa):

$$P_{rail} - P_{cyl} = 160 - 4 = 156 \text{ MPa} = 156 \cdot 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{2(P_{rail}-P_{cyl})}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 156 \cdot 10^6}{835}} = \sqrt{3.74 \cdot 10^5} = 611.4 \text{ m/s}$$

$$C_d \cdot A \cdot v_{inj} = 0.9 \cdot 2 \cdot 10^{-7} \cdot 611.4 = 1.10 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$t_{inj} = \frac{V_{inj}}{1.10 \cdot 10^{-4}} = \frac{5.75 \cdot 10^{-8}}{1.10 \cdot 10^{-4}} = 5.23 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 523 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 8 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{t_{inj} \approx 523\ \mu s,\quad Q_f = 48\ \text{mg/stroke},\quad V_{inj} \approx 57.5\ \text{mm}^3}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — EGR Effect on NOx (First-Order Reduction Model)

**Problem:** A diesel engine without EGR emits NOx = 800 ppm at full load. When 25% EGR is introduced, flame temperature drops by 60 K. Using the Zel'dovich correlation, estimate the percentage reduction in NOx. (Assume E_a/R = 65,000 K.)

**Zel'dovich Arrhenius form (lumped):**

$$[NOx] \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{flame}}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute original NOx exponent:**

$$-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_1} = -\frac{65000}{2300} = -28.26$$

$$[NOx]_1 \propto e^{-28.26} = 5.21 \cdot 10^{-13}$$

**Step 2 — Compute new flame temperature after EGR:**

$$T_2 = T_1 - 60 = 2240 \text{ K}$$

$$-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_2} = -\frac{65000}{2240} = -29.02$$

$$[NOx]_2 \propto e^{-29.02} = 2.22 \cdot 10^{-13}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio (percentage remaining):**

$$\frac{[NOx]_2}{[NOx]_1} = e^{(-29.02) - (-28.26)} = e^{-0.76} = 0.467$$

**Step 4 — Reduction:**

$$\Delta NOx \% = (1 - 0.467) \cdot 100\% = 53.3\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{EGR reduces NOx by approximately } 53\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Reality Check:** The rule of thumb — **10 K reduction in flame temperature → 7–10% reduction in NOx** — is validated here: 60 K × 0.9 ≈ 54%.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Map

| Increase ↓ in Parameter | NOx | Smoke (PM) | Fuel Economy | Combustion Noise | BSFC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Injection Pressure | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| ↑ Injection Quantity | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Advance SOI | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑↑ | ↓ |
| Retard SOI | ↓ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| ↑ EGR Rate | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| ↑ Boost Pressure | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Pilot Injection | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓↓ | ↑ |
| Post Injection | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ |

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is a KTU favourite.** Examiners often ask: *"Trade-off between NOx and soot in a CI engine."* The answer hinges on **EGR (lowers NOx, raises soot)** and **boost pressure (lowers soot, can raise NOx)** — the so-called **NOx-soot trade-off**.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical — Boost Controller Tuning

**Problem:** A VGT must maintain P_boost = 2.0 bar (gauge). Sensor returns P_boost = 1.78 bar. K_p = 4 (deg/% error), K_i = 0.6 (deg/(%·s)), error = 22%. Compute the vane command u after 1 s using parallel PID.

**Step 1 — Proportional term:**

$$u_P = K_p \cdot e = 4 \cdot 22 = 88°$$

**Step 2 — Integral term:**

$$u_I = K_i \int_0^{1} e(t)\, dt \approx K_i \cdot e \cdot \Delta t = 0.6 \cdot 22 \cdot 1 = 13.2°$$

**Step 3 — Total command:**

$$u(t) = u_P + u_I = 88 + 13.2 = 101.2°$$

The vane is moved toward **closing** (boost up). Since 101.2° > 90°, the VGT will saturate — proving why **anti-windup** must be implemented in the CI ECU.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CI Engine Management — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    driver["Driver Pedal APP Sensor"]
    ckp["CKP and CMP Sensors"]
    maf["Mass Air Flow Sensor"]
    map["MAP and Boost Sensor"]
    tcool["Coolant Temperature Sensor"]
    lambda["Lambda UEGO Sensor"]
    noxsense["Tailpipe NOx Sensor"]
    dpfp["DPF Differential Pressure"]
    ecumain["ECU Master Processor"]

    torgtrq["Upper Layer Torque Request Layer"]
    coordlyr["Mid Layer Torque Coordinator"]
    fuellyr["Low Layer Fuel Injection Servo"]
    airlyr["Low Layer Air Path Servo"]
    egrlyr["Low Layer EGR Servo"]
    boostlyr["Low Layer VGT Boost Servo"]
    emlyr["Aftertreatment Coordinator"]

    hppump["High Pressure Fuel Pump"]
    injectors["Solenoid or Piezo Injectors"]
    vgtact["VGT Vane Actuator"]
    egrv["EGR Valve Actuator"]
    thrv["Electronic Throttle Valve"]
    dpf["DPF and SCR Modules"]
    scrpump["AdBlue DEF Dosing Pump"]

    driver --> ecumain
    ckp --> ecumain
    maf --> ecumain
    map --> ecumain
    tcool --> ecumain
    lambda --> ecumain
    noxsense --> ecumain
    dpfp --> ecumain

    ecumain --> torgtrq
    torgtrq --> coordlyr
    coordlyr --> fuellyr
    coordlyr --> airlyr
    coordlyr --> egrlyr
    coordlyr --> boostlyr
    coordlyr --> emlyr

    fuellyr --> injectors
    injectors --> hppump
    airlyr --> thrv
    boostlyr --> vgtact
    egrlyr --> egrv
    emlyr --> scrpump
    emlyr --> dpf
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Driver input and sensor data flow into the **ECU master processor**, which feeds the **upper-layer torque request**. The torque request is then split among **five parallel servo loops** — fuel, air, EGR, boost, and aftertreatment. Each loop independently drives its actuator with closed-loop PID feedback.

## 4.2 Parameter Control State Machine (Cold-Start to Steady-State)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> KeyOff
    KeyOff --> Cranking : Ignition ON
    Cranking --> ColdStart : T_cool < 40 C
    ColdStart --> WarmUp : Glow Plug OFF and N stabilised
    WarmUp --> IdleControl : N within idle band
    IdleControl --> PartLoad : Pedal applied
    PartLoad --> FullLoad : Alpha over 70 percent
    FullLoad --> PartLoad : Pedal released
    PartLoad --> IdleControl : Pedal at zero
    IdleControl --> DFPRegeneration : Delta P over DPF exceeds threshold
    DFPRegeneration --> PartLoad : Post injection complete
    PartLoad --> EngineOff : Key OFF
    EngineOff --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Be ready to draw or explain this state machine. The **DFP regeneration** state is unique to diesel — it injects **post-fuel** that combusts over the DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst) to raise exhaust temperature to **~600 °C**.

## 4.3 Closed-Loop PID Servo Block — Generic Parameter Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r["Setpoint y_set"] --> sum
    sum["Sum Junction"] --> e["Error e t"]
    e --> kp["K_p Gain"]
    e --> ki["K_i Integrator"]
    e --> kd["K_d Differentiator"]
    kp --> add["Adder"]
    ki --> add
    kd --> add
    add --> u["Control Output u"]
    u --> plant["Plant Actuator"]
    plant --> y["Measured y"]
    y --> fb["Feedback Path"]
    fb --> sum
```

This generic block applies to **every parameter** — fuel pressure (PCV), boost (VGT), EGR (EGR valve), lambda (aftertreatment dosing).

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**"List the major parameters that must be controlled in a modern CI engine management system."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Fuel delivery:** injection pressure (rail pressure), injection timing (SOI), injection quantity, number/duration of injection events (pilot, main, after). **(1 mark)**
2. **Air path:** boost pressure (VGT position), intake air mass/throttle position, swirl ratio. **(1 mark)**
3. **Emissions and auxiliary:** EGR rate, DPF regeneration temperature, SCR/DEF dosing, idle speed, coolant warm-up. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**"Why is rail pressure considered the most important controlled parameter in a common-rail diesel system?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Rail pressure directly controls **fuel atomisation quality** — higher pressure produces finer droplets, shorter ignition delay, lower smoke and lower unburned hydrocarbon emissions. **(1.5 marks)**
2. Rail pressure also affects **injection quantity** and **multiple injection capability** (pilot/post) — without sufficient pressure, the ECU cannot execute pilot injections required for noise and NOx control. **(1 mark)**
3. Therefore, rail pressure is a **prerequisite enabler** for almost every other CI control parameter. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"With the help of a block diagram, explain the major parameters controlled in a CI engine management system. Discuss the role of the EGR and VGT in detail. (14 marks)"**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Parameter List (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** above for the canonical CI engine management functional architecture. The student should draw:
- A **driver/pedal sensor** feeding into the ECU.
- An **ECU** that splits the torque demand into **five servo loops**.
- Each loop closes through an **actuator** (injector, VGT, EGR, throttle, aftertreatment dosing pump) and a **sensor** for feedback.

**Five Parameter Domains (4 marks):**

| Domain | Parameters | Actuators |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel Delivery | Rail pressure, SOI, Q_f, pilot/main/post timing | HP pump, injectors |
| Air Path | Boost, MAP, intake air mass, swirl | VGT, throttle |
| Combustion | Ignition delay, peak P, dP/dθ, EGR rate | EGR valve |
| Aftertreatment | DEF dosing, DPF regen temp, λ | SCR pump, post-injection |
| Auxiliary | Idle N, glow plug, fan | Stepper, relay |

#### Part (b) — Role of EGR and VGT (7 marks)

**EGR — Exhaust Gas Recirculation (3.5 marks):**

- EGR diverts a portion (typically **5–40%**) of exhaust gas back into the intake manifold.
- EGR gas is inert (CO₂, H₂O) and has **higher specific heat** than air → lowers adiabatic flame temperature.
- A 60 K drop in flame temperature reduces NOx by **~50%** (Zel'dovich mechanism).
- **Controlled by:** an electrically actuated EGR valve modulated by ECU using PID with feedback from MAP and EGR cooler temperature.
- **Trade-off:** higher EGR raises smoke and BSFC; ECU therefore optimises EGR as a function of load and speed.

**VGT — Variable Geometry Turbocharger (3.5 marks):**

- VGT uses adjustable vanes at the turbine inlet to vary the effective A/R ratio.
- At low engine speed, vanes close (high boost) → eliminates **turbo lag**.
- At high engine speed, vanes open → prevents over-boost.
- VGT position is controlled by a **vacuum or electric actuator** commanded by ECU using PID with MAP sensor feedback.
- VGT sets boost pressure, which sets air mass m_air, which sets λ, which influences both smoke and NOx.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**"Explain in detail the role of fuel injection timing and multiple injection strategies in CI engine management. Show how the injection pulse width is calculated for a given torque demand. (14 marks)"**

#### Part (a) — Injection Timing and Multiple Injections (7 marks)

**Start of Injection — SOI (3 marks):**
- SOI defines **when** the injector solenoid energises relative to TDC.
- For diesel, SOI is **4°–18° BTDC**, decreasing with speed and load.
- **Advancing SOI:** more premixed combustion → higher peak pressure and **NOx**, lower smoke.
- **Retarding SOI:** lower peak pressure, lower NOx, higher smoke, higher fuel consumption.

**Multiple Injection Strategy (4 marks):**

| Injection | Quantity | Timing | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pilot | 1–3 mm³ | 20°–40° BTDC | Raise P_cyl, reduce dP/dθ, reduce noise |
| Main | 60–80 % of total | 8°–12° BTDC | Deliver majority of energy |
| After | 5–10 % | 20°–40° ATDC | Raise exhaust temp for DPF regen |
| Post | 2–5 % | 60°–90° ATDC | Reduce PM by late oxidation |

**Benefits:**
- Pilot reduces **combustion noise** by limiting dP/dθ to **< 5 bar/°**.
- After/post enables **DPF regeneration** without extra fuel burner.
- Multiple injections reduce **peak cylinder pressure** and improve **BSFC by 2–4%**.

#### Part (b) — Pulse Width Derivation (7 marks)

**Given:** 4-cyl, 4-stroke diesel, V_d = 1.6 L/cyl, N = 2500 rpm, T_brake = 200 N·m, η_m = 0.88, BSFC target = 220 g/kWh, ρ_f = 835 kg/m³, C_d = 0.9, A_nozzle = 0.18 mm², P_rail = 1800 bar, P_cyl = 45 bar.

**Step 1 — Angular speed:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2500}{60} = 261.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Brake power:**

$$P_{brake} = 200 \cdot 261.80 = 52{,}360 \text{ W} = 52.36 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Fuel flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_f = 0.220 \cdot 52.36 = 11.52 \text{ kg/h} = 3.20 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 4 — Per cylinder per cycle:**

$$f_{cycle} = \frac{N/2}{60} = 20.83 \text{ Hz}$$

$$Q_f = \frac{3.20 \cdot 10^{-3}/4}{20.83} = 3.84 \cdot 10^{-5} \text{ kg} = 38.4 \text{ mg/stroke}$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric injection:**

$$V_{inj} = \frac{38.4 \cdot 10^{-6}}{835} = 4.60 \cdot 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3 = 46.0 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 6 — Velocity term:**

$$\Delta P = 1800 - 45 = 1755 \text{ bar} = 1.755 \cdot 10^8 \text{ Pa}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 1.755 \cdot 10^8}{835}} = 648.3 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Pulse width:**

$$t_{inj} = \frac{V_{inj}}{C_d \cdot A \cdot v} = \frac{4.60 \cdot 10^{-8}}{0.9 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 10^{-7} \cdot 648.3} = \frac{4.60 \cdot 10^{-8}}{1.05 \cdot 10^{-4}} = 4.38 \cdot 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{inj} \approx 438\ \mu s}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in 4-stroke cycles.** Many students compute per-revolution instead of per-cycle (1 cycle = 2 revolutions). This causes a 2× error in injection quantity.
> 2. **Using BSFC with indicated power instead of brake power.** BSFC is always defined on **brake** power. Always divide by η_m to get indicated power only if computing IMEP.
> 3. **Ignoring the C_d factor.** Using A directly (without C_d ≈ 0.9) gives a pulse width that is 10–15% too short.
> 4. **Confusing gauge vs absolute pressure.** In-cylinder pressure must be **absolute**, not gauge, when used in the nozzle equation.
> 5. **Omitting the trade-off discussion.** When asked about parameters, students often list only fuel items and forget EGR, boost, and aftertreatment — losing up to **4 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — CI Engine Management:**

- **Core principle:** CI engines control load via **fuel quantity only**, not throttle. This makes the parameter control problem fundamentally a **fuel + air coordination** problem.
- **Five controlled parameter domains:** Fuel delivery, Air path, Combustion, Aftertreatment, Auxiliary. Mnemonic: **F-A-C-A-A**.
- **Common-Rail pressure:** 250 – **2500 bar**; controlled by **Pressure Control Valve (PCV)** and HP pump.
- **Start of Injection (SOI):** Typically **4°–18° BTDC**; advance raises NOx, retard raises soot.
- **Pilot injection:** Limits **dP/dθ ≤ 5 bar/°** to suppress combustion knock.
- **EGR rate:** 5–40%; each **10 K reduction** in flame temperature reduces NOx by **7–10%**.
- **VGT boost:** Range **0.8–3.5 bar**; controlled by PID with MAP feedback.
- **Stoichiometric AFR for diesel ≈ 14.5 : 1** but operating λ = **1.2–4.0** (lean).
- **BSFC target:** 200–260 g/kWh.
- **DPF regen trigger:** ΔP across DPF > **15–25 kPa**; uses **post injection** to raise exhaust temp to **~600 °C**.
- **SCR dosing:** $\dot{m}_{DEF} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{NOx,eng} M_{DEF}}{2 M_{NOx} \eta_{SCR}}$
- **Combustion noise metric:** $\left(\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} \le 5$ bar/°.
- **Hierarchy:** Torque layer → Coordination layer → Servo layer (each with PID).
- **Injection quantity equation:** $Q_f = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_{rail} - P_{cyl})}{\rho_f}} \cdot t_{inj}$
- **NOx-soot trade-off** is the central problem — every control parameter is a knob in this trade-off.
- **Sensors to remember:** CKP, CMP, MAF, MAP, CTS, APP, UEGO (λ), NOx, DPF ΔP.
- **Actuators to remember:** Solenoid/piezo injector, HP pump, PCV, VGT vane, EGR valve, electronic throttle, glow plug, DEF pump.
- **Examiner-favourite equations:** torque, BSFC, injection quantity, EGR-NOx Arrhenius, PID servo law.
- **Always convert** rpm → rad/s, mm³ → m³, bar → Pa before substituting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Injection System, Parameters Affecting Combustion, Noise and Emissions in CI Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Injection System, Parameters Affecting Combustion, Noise and Emissions in CI Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Fuel Injection, Combustion, Noise & Emissions in CI Engines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Compression Ignition (CI) Engine — Formal Definition
A **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine**, universally known as the **Diesel Engine** (named after Rudolf Diesel, **1892**), is an internal combustion engine in which ignition of the fuel charge is achieved purely by the high temperature of compressed air — *without* an external spark. Air is compressed to a high pressure (typically **30:1 to 25:1** for passenger cars and **18:1 to 14:1** for large marine engines), heating it to temperatures exceeding **750 K**, after which finely atomized fuel is injected directly (or indirectly) into this hot air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):** *A CI engine is a reciprocating internal combustion engine in which the air charge is compressed to a temperature above the auto-ignition temperature of the fuel, and combustion is initiated by injecting fuel into the hot compressed air at the appropriate point in the cycle.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker vs. The Matchbox"
Imagine two ways to light a campfire:
- **The Matchbox (SI Engine)**: You gather wood (air+fuel mixture), and a small spark (spark plug) starts the fire. Easy to start, but controlled.
- **The Pressure Cooker (CI Engine)**: You pressurize air alone in a chamber until it becomes incredibly hot (squeeze the air molecules together). Then you squirt fuel (diesel) into this super-hot air — *and it auto-ignites*. No spark needed, but the pressure and timing must be EXACT.

This is the entire essence of a CI engine: **control the air, inject the fuel**.

### 1.3 Why Fuel Injection is CRITICAL in CI Engines
Unlike SI engines where a carburetor can roughly mix fuel and air, CI engines demand **precise, high-pressure, timed fuel delivery** because:
- Combustion begins **the moment** fuel contacts hot air (no spark buffer).
- Poor atomization → poor mixing → smoke, noise, low efficiency.
- Injection timing dictates the entire combustion phasing (premixed vs. diffusion burn).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core takeaway:** In SI engines, the *mixture* is prepared before the cylinder. In CI engines, **the mixture is prepared INSIDE the cylinder** during combustion. This is why fuel injection is the heart of a diesel engine.

### 1.4 Classification of CI Engine Fuel Injection Systems

| Generation | System | Peak Pressure | Era |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | **Inline (Jerk) Pump** with Individual Injectors | 350–600 bar | 1927–1990s |
| 2nd | **Rotary / Distributor (VE) Pump** | 350–1,000 bar | 1980s–2000s |
| 3rd | **Unit Injector (UI) / Unit Pump (UP)** | 1,200–2,050 bar | 1990s–2010s |
| 4th | **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** | 1,600–2,500 bar (modern: 2,500+ bar) | 1997–present |

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics & Constants
- **Cetane Number (CN)** of diesel fuel: **40 to 55** (higher = shorter ignition delay, smoother combustion).
- **Auto-ignition temperature of diesel**: **~250 °C (523 K)**, but practical in-cylinder temperatures are **750 K – 900 K**.
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) for diesel**: **~14.5 : 1** (vs. gasoline's 14.7 : 1).
- **Typical excess air factor (λ)** in diesel: **1.3 to 2.5** (always lean burn).
- **Diesel fuel density**: **~840 kg/m³** at 15 °C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modern Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) — The Dominant System

The **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** system, pioneered by Bosch (**1997**), is the industry standard. It decouples **pressure generation** from **injection timing** — solving the biggest weakness of older jerk-pump systems.

### 2.1.1 Major Sub-Systems
1. **Supply Pump (HP Pump)**: A radial-piston pump (often 3-piston) driven by the camshaft. Draws fuel from the tank, boosts it to rail pressure (often **1,600–2,500 bar**).
2. **Rail (Accumulator)**: A high-pressure reservoir (volume ~20–60 cm³) that maintains nearly **constant pressure** to all injectors. The pressure is regulated by the **Pressure Control Valve (PCV)** and monitored by the **Rail Pressure Sensor**.
3. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**: A mechatronic brain. Reads crank position, cam position, coolant temp, rail pressure, accelerator pedal position, and air mass. Computes injection quantity, timing, and number of pilot injections.
4. **Solenoid / Piezo Injector**: The actuator. ECU sends a pulse → injector solenoid opens → fuel sprays through **6 to 9 micro-nozzle holes** (~100–200 µm diameter) into the cylinder.

### 2.1.2 Why "Common Rail"? — Operational Logic
- **OLD (Inline pump)**: Pressure is generated *per injection event*. Pressure is highest at start, then drops as fuel leaks. Result: non-uniform spray, poor atomization.
- **NEW (Common Rail)**: Pressure is *always* available in the rail. The injector just opens like a valve. Result: **flexible, multi-pulse injection** (pilot + main + after).

### 2.2 Multiple Injection Strategy — The Mechatronic Magic
Modern CRDi systems perform **up to 9 injections per cycle**:
1. **Pilot Injection(s)** (small, 1–4 mm³): ~5–30° BTDC. Lifts piston slightly, raises in-cylinder T, shortens ignition delay of main charge → **reduces diesel knock and NOx**.
2. **Main Injection**: 70–90% of fuel, 0–10° BTDC.
3. **After Injection(s)**: Used for **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) regeneration** — burns accumulated soot by raising exhaust temperature.

### 2.3 Parameters Affecting Combustion in CI Engines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** The examiner LOVES questions on "Discuss the parameters affecting combustion in CI engines." Memorize the 6–8 parameters below.

| # | Parameter | Effect on Combustion |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Injection Pressure** | Higher pressure → finer atomization → shorter ignition delay → smoother burn → more PM (over-pressure) |
| 2 | **Injection Timing (Start of Injection, SOI)** | Advanced timing → more premixed burn → higher peak pressure → higher **NOx** and noise. Retarded → more diffusion burn → more **smoke/PM** and lower efficiency |
| 3 | **Injection Duration / Rate Shape** | Shorter duration → higher mean effective pressure. "Boot-shaped" or "rate-shaped" injection reduces noise |
| 4 | **Spray Pattern / Nozzle Geometry** | Multi-hole vs. swirl vs. slit. Affects spray penetration and air entrainment |
| 5 | **Fuel Properties (Cetane Number, Viscosity, Density)** | Higher CN → shorter ignition delay. Higher viscosity → poor atomization → more smoke |
| 6 | **Compression Ratio** | Higher CR → higher peak temperature → better efficiency, but more NOx |
| 7 | **Load / EGR / Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** | Higher load → higher in-cylinder T → more NOx. Higher EGR → lower flame T → less NOx but more PM |
| 8 | **Engine Speed** | Higher speed → less time for mixing → shorter ignition delay in degrees, longer in ms. Affects spray tip penetration |

### 2.4 Noise in CI Engines — "Diesel Knock"

Diesel engines are notoriously **noisier** than gasoline engines (typically **8–10 dB(A) louder** at idle). The dominant source is **combustion noise**, often called **"diesel knock"** or **"combustion knock."**

#### 2.4.1 Why CI Engines Knock
- **Ignition Delay (τᵢd)**: The time between start of injection (SOI) and start of combustion. During this delay, fuel accumulates in the chamber.
- When combustion finally begins, all this accumulated fuel **ignites almost simultaneously** → a rapid, uncontrolled **premixed burn** → sharp rise in cylinder pressure → **mechanical shock waves** → **knock**.

#### 2.4.2 Pressure Rise Rate (dP/dθ) — The Noise Metric
The peak rate of pressure rise is the **direct predictor of combustion noise**:

$$
\left(\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} \propto \frac{\Delta P_{premixed}}{\theta_{premixed}}
$$

where $\Delta P_{premixed}$ is the pressure rise from the premixed burn and $\theta_{premixed}$ is the crank angle duration of the premixed burn.

**Typical acceptable values**: 4–6 bar/°CA. Values > 8–10 bar/°CA cause severe knock.

#### 2.4.3 Methods to Reduce Diesel Knock
1. **Pilot injection** (most effective modern method).
2. **Increase injection pressure** (shorter delay).
3. **Use higher Cetane Number fuel** (shorter delay).
4. **Rate-shaped injection** (slower initial rate).
5. **Retard SOI slightly** (trades efficiency for noise).
6. **Better atomization** (multi-hole nozzle).
7. **EGR** (lowers flame temperature, slows reaction).

### 2.5 Emissions in CI Engines

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Students often say "diesel engines produce CO₂ only." WRONG. Diesel engines produce **NOx, PM (soot), CO, HC, and CO₂** — but in *different proportions* than SI engines. The classic diesel trade-off is **NOx vs. PM** (you cannot easily reduce both at once).

#### 2.5.1 Major Pollutants

| Pollutant | Formation Mechanism | Why CI has it |
|---|---|---|
| **NOx (NO + NO₂)** | Zeldovich mechanism at high T (>1,800 K) | High peak flame T (EGR-sensitive) |
| **PM / Soot** | Fuel-rich pockets in diffusion burn (rich zones) | Diffusion burn, not premixed |
| **CO** | Incomplete combustion (locally rich) | Cold start, low load |
| **HC** | Fuel trapped in crevices, wall-quenching | Over-mixed fuel near walls |
| **CO₂** | Complete combustion — greenhouse gas | Always produced |

#### 2.5.2 The NOx–PM Trade-Off (KTU Favorite)
This is a thermodynamic dead-end: measures that **lower peak flame temperature** (to cut NOx) **increase fuel-rich zones** (to increase PM), and vice-versa.

```
         Lower EGR / Advanced Timing  →  Higher T → More NOx, Less PM
         Higher EGR / Retarded Timing →  Lower T  → Less NOx, More PM
```

The only way to break this trade-off is **after-treatment**:
- **NOx**: SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) using AdBlue/DEF (urea), or **LNT** (Lean NOx Trap).
- **PM**: DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter), DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst).

#### 2.5.3 Emission Norms (India)
- **BS-IV (2017)**, **BS-VI (2020)** — equivalent to Euro 6.
- BS-VI for diesel passenger cars: **NOx limit 80 mg/km** (vs. 250 mg/km in BS-IV).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto cycle efficiency (not directly CI, used for comparison) | — |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | Dual cycle efficiency (Diesel cycle) | — |
| 3 | $\tau_{id} = \text{SOI to SOC crank angle}$ | Ignition delay (key to knock) | °CA or ms |
| 4 | $AFR_{st} \approx 14.5$ | Stoichiometric for diesel | — |
| 5 | $\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Excess air ratio (1.3–2.5 in CI) | — |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot \frac{1}{AFR}$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 7 | $\left(\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max}$ | Combustion noise indicator | bar/°CA |
| 8 | $EGR\% = \frac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{air} + \dot{m}_{EGR}} \times 100$ | EGR fraction definition | % |
| 9 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \frac{\text{Energy of CO+HC+PM+CO unburnt}}{\text{Energy of fuel input}}$ | Combustion efficiency | — |
| 10 | $BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always write the units in KTU numerical problems. A correct answer without units = **0 marks** for that step.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dual (Sabathé) Cycle Efficiency — The CI Engine Approximation

The CI engine cycle is modeled as the **Dual Cycle** (also called the **Limited Pressure cycle** or **Sabathé cycle**), which combines a constant-volume heat addition (premixed burn) with a constant-pressure heat addition (diffusion burn). It is the most realistic model for a diesel engine.

### 3.1.1 The Four Processes
1. **Process 1→2**: Isentropic compression of air.
2. **Process 2→3**: Constant-volume heat addition (models premixed burn).
3. **Process 3→4**: Constant-pressure heat addition (models diffusion burn).
4. **Process 4→5**: Isentropic expansion.
5. **Process 5→1**: Constant-volume heat rejection.

### 3.1.2 Define Key Ratios
- **Compression ratio:** $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$
- **Cut-off ratio:** $\rho = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3}$ (ratio at constant-pressure heat addition)
- **Pressure ratio (explosion):** $r_p = \dfrac{P_3}{P_2}$ (ratio at constant-volume heat addition)

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Write the efficiency of any cycle as:
$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{5-1}}{Q_{2-3} + Q_{3-4}}
$$

**Step 2** — Apply 1st Law to constant-volume heat addition (process 2→3):
$$
Q_{2-3} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 3** — Apply 1st Law to constant-pressure heat addition (process 3→4):
$$
Q_{3-4} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_4 - T_3)
$$

**Step 4** — Apply 1st Law to constant-volume heat rejection (process 5→1):
$$
Q_{5-1} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_5 - T_1)
$$

**Step 5** — Apply isentropic relation for process 1→2:
$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Step 6** — Apply isentropic relation for process 4→5:
$$
T_5 = T_4 \cdot \left(\frac{V_4}{V_5}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_4 \cdot \left(\frac{V_4}{V_1}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_4 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Step 7** — Define pressure ratio $r_p = P_3/P_2$. Using ideal gas for 2→3 (constant V):
$$
\frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{P_3}{P_2} = r_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_3 = r_p \cdot T_2
$$

**Step 8** — Use constant-pressure process 3→4:
$$
\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \frac{V_4}{V_3} = \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_4 = \rho \cdot T_3 = \rho \cdot r_p \cdot T_2
$$

**Step 9** — Substitute $T_2$ from Step 5 into Step 7 and Step 8:
$$
T_3 = r_p \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}
$$

$$
T_4 = \rho \cdot r_p \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}
$$

**Step 10** — Substitute $T_5$ from Step 6 using $T_4$:
$$
T_5 = \rho \cdot r_p \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}
$$

$$
T_5 = \rho \cdot r_p \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma-1}}{r^{\gamma-1}}
$$

$$
T_5 = \rho^{\gamma} \cdot r_p \cdot T_1
$$

**Step 11** — Compute the ratio $Q_{5-1}/Q_{in}$:

$$
\frac{Q_{5-1}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{c_v (T_5 - T_1)}{c_v(T_3 - T_2) + c_p(T_4 - T_3)} = \frac{T_5 - T_1}{(T_3 - T_2) + \gamma(T_4 - T_3)}
$$

**Step 12** — Substitute the temperatures in terms of $T_1$, $r$, $\rho$, $r_p$, $\gamma$:

Numerator: $T_5 - T_1 = T_1 (\rho^{\gamma} \cdot r_p - 1)$

Denominator: 
$$
T_3 - T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}(r_p - 1)
$$

$$
T_4 - T_3 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \cdot r_p(\rho - 1)
$$

**Step 13** — Final efficiency expression:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,\,Dual} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_p \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (\rho - 1)}}
$$

**Special Case 1**: If $r_p = 1$ (no constant-volume part), this reduces to the **Diesel cycle**:
$$
\eta_{th,\,Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}
$$

**Special Case 2**: If $\rho = 1$ (no constant-pressure part), this reduces to the **Otto cycle**:
$$
\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Ignition Delay Equation (Arrhenius Form)

The ignition delay of diesel fuel is empirically modeled as:

$$
\tau_{id} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant
- $p$ = in-cylinder pressure at SOI
- $n$ ≈ 1.0–1.5 (pressure exponent)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (~170 kJ/kmol for diesel)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) (universal gas constant)
- $T$ = in-cylinder temperature (K)

**Interpretation**: Higher $T$ and higher $p$ → **shorter** $\tau_{id}$ → **less fuel accumulation** → **less knock**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Fuel Mass per Cycle from CRDi Strategy

For a multi-pulse CRDi injection with pilot + main + after:

$$
m_{fuel,total} = m_{pilot} + m_{main} + m_{after}
$$

Each pulse mass is calculated from injector flow:

$$
m_{pulse} = C_d \cdot A_{nozzle} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \Delta P_{rail}} \cdot \Delta t_{pulse}
$$

where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (~0.85)
- $A_{nozzle}$ = total nozzle hole area
- $\Delta P_{rail}$ = rail pressure minus combustion chamber back-pressure
- $\Delta t_{pulse}$ = solenoid energization time

**Typical values**: $C_d = 0.85$, $A_{nozzle} = 6 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(150 \times 10^{-6})^2 \approx 1.06 \times 10^{-7}$ m², $\Delta P_{rail} = 160$ MPa, $\rho_{fuel} = 840$ kg/m³.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trade-Off Map for CI Combustion Parameters

| Parameter | ↑ Increases What? | ↓ Decreases What? | Engineering Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Injection Pressure** | Atomization, NOx (slightly) | Smoke, HC | Pump parasitic loss, nozzle wear |
| **EGR Rate** | PM, BSFC slightly | NOx, peak T | Soot deposits, valve coking |
| **Pilot Injection** | Noise (lowers), smoothness | System complexity | ECU computation |
| **Cetane Number** | Cold start, low noise | NOₓ (slightly higher) | Fuel cost |
| **EGR Cooler (↓EGR T)** | NOx reduction | PM, system cost | Heat exchanger |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TankA[FUEL TANK] -->|Low-pressure line 4 bar| FeedPump[FEED PUMP]
    FeedPump -->|4-6 bar| FilterA[FUEL FILTER]
    FilterA -->|Filtered| HPPump[HIGH PRESSURE PUMP 3-piston radial]
    HPPump -->|up to 2500 bar| RailAcc[COMMON RAIL ACCUMULATOR]
    RailAcc -->|Constant Pressure| Inj1[Injector Cyl 1]
    RailAcc -->|Constant Pressure| Inj2[Injector Cyl 2]
    RailAcc -->|Constant Pressure| Inj3[Injector Cyl 3]
    RailAcc -->|Constant Pressure| Inj4[Injector Cyl 4]
    Inj1 -->|Spray into Cyl| Comb1[Combustion Chamber Cyl 1]
    Inj2 -->|Spray into Cyl| Comb2[Combustion Chamber Cyl 2]
    Inj3 -->|Spray into Cyl| Comb3[Combustion Chamber Cyl 3]
    Inj4 -->|Spray into Cyl| Comb4[Combustion Chamber Cyl 4]
    PCV[Pressure Control Valve on Rail] -.->|Modulates| RailAcc
    RailSensor[Rail Pressure Sensor] -.->|Feedback to ECU| ECU
    CrankS[Crankshaft Position Sensor] -.->|RPM and angle| ECU
    CamS[Camshaft Position Sensor] -.->|Phase reference| ECU
    MAPS[Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor] -.->|Air mass estimation| ECU
    APS[Accelerator Pedal Sensor] -.->|Driver demand| ECU
    CoolT[Coolant Temperature Sensor] -.->|Thermal state| ECU
    ECU[ENGINE CONTROL UNIT ECU] -->|PWM Command| Inj1
    ECU -->|PWM Command| Inj2
    ECU -->|PWM Command| Inj3
    ECU -->|PWM Command| Inj4
    ECU -->|Duty Cycle| PCV
    Comb1 -->|Exhaust gas| EGRValve[EGR Valve]
    EGRValve -->|Recirculated Exhaust| InletMan[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    InletMan -->|Air + EGR| Comb1
    Comb1 -->|Exhaust| DPFA[DIESEL PARTICULATE FILTER DPF]
    DPFA -->|Cleaned exhaust| SCRSys[SELECTIVE CATALYTIC REDUCTION SCR]
    SCRSys -->|NOx cleaned| MufA[MUFFLER TAIL PIPE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = fluid flow (fuel/exhaust/air). Dotted arrows = electrical signals. The ECU is the **central mechatronic brain** that closes all loops.

## 4.2 Combustion Phases in a CI Engine — Heat Release Rate Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SoiA[START OF INJECTION SOI] -->|Ignition Delay τid| PhyA[PHASE 1 Ignition Delay]
    PhyA -->|Rapid pressure rise| PhyB[PHASE 2 Premixed Combustion]
    PhyB -->|Controlled diffusion| PhyC[PHASE 3 Diffusion Combustion]
    PhyC -->|Late burn| PhyD[PHASE 4 Afterburn]
    PhyD -->|End of combustion| EocA[END OF COMBUSTION EOC]
    PhyA -.->|Fuel accumulation - noise risk| NoiseA[HIGH dP/dθ KNOCK ZONE]
    PhyB -.->|Peak heat release| PmaxA[Peak Pressure Pmax]
    PhyC -.->|Soot formation zone| SootA[PM SOOT FORMATION]
    PhyD -.->|Late heat release| EfffA[Lower efficiency if too long]
```

## 4.3 CRDi Multi-Injection Strategy — Block Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CrankDeg[Crank Angle Degrees BTDC] --> P1
    P1[1 PILOT 1 µL at 30 deg BTDC] --> GapA[gap 5-10 deg CA]
    GapA --> P2[2 PILOT 2 µL at 20 deg BTDC]
    P2 --> GapB[gap 5-10 deg CA]
    GapB --> Main[3 MAIN INJECTION 25-40 mm3 at 5 deg BTDC]
    Main --> GapC[small gap]
    GapC --> After[4 AFTER INJECTION 5 mm3 at 30 deg ATDC for DPF regen]
    P1 -.->|Purpose raises T and P in chamber| GoalA[Reduces ignition delay of main]
    P2 -.->|Purpose further smoothing| GoalB[Lower combustion noise]
    Main -.->|Main power production| GoalC[Maximum heat release]
    After -.->|DPF regeneration| GoalD[Raises exhaust T to burn soot]
```

## 4.4 Emission After-Treatment System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EngA[DIESEL ENGINE] --> ExhA[EXHAUST MANIFOLD]
    ExhA --> DOCA[DIESEL OXIDATION CATALYST DOC]
    DOCA -->|Reduces CO and HC| DPFA[DIESEL PARTICULATE FILTER DPF]
    DPFA -->|Traps PM soot| SCRSys[SCR UNIT with AdBlue DEF injection]
    SCRSys -->|Reduces NOx to N2| ASCSys[AMMONIA SLIP CATALYST ASC]
    ASCSys -->|Clean exhaust| TailA[TAIL PIPE]
    DPFNeeds[DPF needs periodic regen] -.->|Triggered by ECU| AfterA[After injection raises T to 600 C]
    AdBlueTank[AdBlue DEF tank with urea] -.->|Dosed by pump| SCRSys
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is meant by "ignition delay" in a CI engine, and how does it relate to diesel knock?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ignition delay is the time interval (expressed in crank angle degrees, °CA, or milliseconds) between the **start of fuel injection (SOI)** and the **start of combustion (SOC)**. During this delay, fuel droplets enter the chamber, vaporize, and mix with hot compressed air, but no significant reaction occurs. Once the auto-ignition conditions are met, all the fuel accumulated during the delay **burns almost simultaneously** in a rapid, premixed reaction. This rapid pressure rise produces a sharp pressure wave — the **diesel knock**. The longer the ignition delay, the more fuel accumulates, and the louder the knock. Hence, modern CRDi systems use **pilot injection** to shorten the delay of the main charge and reduce knock. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Cetane Number. Why is it the most important fuel property for CI engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Cetane Number (CN)** is a measure of the **ignition quality of diesel fuel** — the shorter the ignition delay, the higher the CN. It is determined by comparing the fuel against a blend of **n-cetane (CN = 100, very short delay)** and **alpha-methyl-naphthalene (CN = 0, very long delay)**.
It is critical for CI engines because **combustion is initiated by auto-ignition** (no spark). A higher CN means:
1. **Shorter ignition delay** → less knock and smoother running.
2. **Better cold start** performance.
3. **More complete combustion** → less smoke and HC.

Typical Indian diesel has a CN of **48–52**, which is regulated by BIS. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students write "diesel engines produce less CO₂ than petrol" — this is **FALSE** in CO₂/km. Diesel engines have higher BSFC, so they often emit **more** CO₂/km despite higher thermal efficiency. They emit **less CO₂ per kWh** of work.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Students confuse **PM (particulate matter)** with **smoke (visible black exhaust)**. PM includes sub-micron soot and is **measured in mg/km**, not by visual smoke.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Many students write "more EGR = less PM" — the **correct** relationship is **more EGR = more PM, less NOx**. Memorize the direction.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting to mention **Cetane Number** in any CI engine question loses at least 1 mark. It is the examiner's favorite "hidden" mark.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Explain the working of a Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) system with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of the rail, ECU, and solenoid injector in mechatronic terms.**

**Model Solution:**

The CRDi system is a **fully electronic, mechatronic fuel injection system** used in modern diesel engines. Unlike conventional jerk-pump systems where pressure is generated per stroke, CRDi **decouples** pressure generation from injection events.

**Working Principle — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Suction and Pre-Filtering:**
A **feed (supply) pump**, usually an electric or mechanical vane/gear pump, draws diesel from the tank and delivers it at low pressure (~4 bar) through a **fine fuel filter** to remove particulates and water. Any contamination entering the high-pressure side can destroy the injectors. **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2 — High-Pressure Generation:**
A **3-piston radial high-pressure pump**, driven by the camshaft, compresses the fuel to a high pressure (typically **1,600 to 2,500 bar**). The pump has a cam ring that pushes 3 pistons radially outward once per camshaft revolution (3 pumping events per cam rev). A **fuel metering valve** (or **PCV** on the rail) regulates the volume pumped, controlling rail pressure. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Rail Accumulation:**
The high-pressure fuel enters the **common rail** — a high-pressure accumulator with internal volume of ~20–60 cm³. The rail's function is to **store the high-pressure fuel** and supply it to all injectors at **near-constant pressure**, independent of engine speed. A **rail pressure sensor** continuously monitors the pressure and sends feedback to the ECU. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — ECU-Based Injection Decision:**
The **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** receives inputs from:
- Crankshaft position sensor (engine speed and crank angle)
- Camshaft position sensor (cylinder identification)
- Accelerator pedal position sensor (driver demand)
- Coolant temperature sensor
- Intake air mass flow sensor
- Rail pressure sensor

The ECU computes the **injection quantity, start of injection (SOI), number of pilot injections, and injection duration** using calibrated maps. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Solenoid/Piezo Injector Actuation:**
The ECU sends a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulated) signal** to the **solenoid actuator** (or piezo crystal in advanced systems) of the injector. The actuator lifts the needle valve against spring force, allowing high-pressure fuel to spray through **6–9 micro-holes** (each ~100–200 µm diameter) into the combustion chamber. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Multi-Pulse Injection (Mechatronic Advantage):**
The ECU can command **multiple injection events per cycle**: 1–2 **pilot injections** (small, 1–4 mm³, before TDC) followed by the **main injection** and optionally an **after injection** for DPF regeneration. **(1 mark)**

**Mechatronic Roles Summary:**

| Component | Mechatronic Role |
|---|---|
| **Common Rail** | Mechanical accumulator (stores hydraulic energy) |
| **ECU** | Microprocessor-based decision maker (sensors → algorithm → actuators) |
| **Solenoid Injector** | Electrohydraulic actuator (electrical pulse → mechanical needle lift → fluid spray) |
| **Sensors** | Feedback elements (crank, cam, rail pressure, pedal, temp) |

**Conclusion:** CRDi is the **pinnacle of mechatronic integration in CI engines** — achieving < 0.5 ms injection precision, ~2,500 bar pressure, and < 5 bar/°CA pressure rise rate, simultaneously reducing noise, smoke, and fuel consumption. **(1.5 marks for diagram, neatness, conclusion)**

#### **(b) [7 marks] Discuss in detail the parameters affecting combustion in CI engines. How does each parameter influence the premixed and diffusion phases of combustion?**

**Model Solution:**

CI engine combustion consists of **four phases**: ignition delay, premixed burn, diffusion burn, and afterburn. The combustion quality — and therefore noise, emissions, and efficiency — is highly sensitive to the following parameters:

**1. Injection Pressure (1.5 marks):**
Higher injection pressure (1,600 → 2,500 bar) produces **smaller fuel droplets** (Sauter Mean Diameter decreases). Smaller droplets evaporate faster → **shorter ignition delay** → **less fuel accumulation** → **lower knock** and lower HC. However, over-pressure can cause spray to impinge on the cylinder walls → wall wetting → more PM and CO. Optimal: 1,800–2,200 bar for passenger cars.

**2. Injection Timing (Start of Injection, SOI) (1.5 marks):**
- **Advanced SOI** (e.g., 15° BTDC) → more fuel injected before TDC → longer time for premixing → more **premixed burn** → **higher peak pressure, more NOx, more knock, higher efficiency**.
- **Retarded SOI** (e.g., 5° ATDC) → less premixing → more **diffusion burn** → **lower efficiency, more smoke, more HC, less NOx**.
- Modern ECUs use **SOI mapping** as a primary knob for the NOx–PM trade-off.

**3. Spray Pattern and Nozzle Geometry (1 mark):**
Multi-hole nozzles (6–9 holes) produce fine, well-distributed sprays. Spray angle, hole diameter, and **spray tip penetration** (L = √(2·ΔP·t²/ρ_fuel)) determine the air-fuel mixing rate. Better mixing → shorter delay → less PM.

**4. Fuel Properties (Cetane Number, Viscosity, Density) (1 mark):**
- **Cetane Number**: Higher CN → shorter ignition delay → less knock, less PM. Indian diesel: CN = 48–52.
- **Viscosity**: Too high → poor atomization → large droplets → long delay → smoke. Too low → leakage, lubrication issues.
- **Density**: Affects mass per injection volume; affects spray momentum.

**5. Compression Ratio (1 mark):**
Higher CR → higher peak temperature and pressure → shorter ignition delay, better efficiency. But higher CR also → higher peak flame T → **more NOx**. Modern CRDi engines use **CR = 14:1 to 17:1** (down from 22:1 in old designs) to control NOx.

**6. Engine Load and Speed (1 mark):**
- **Load**: Higher load → more fuel injected → more diffusion burn → more PM and NOx.
- **Speed**: Higher speed → less time for heat loss → higher peak T → more NOx. Also affects the crank-angle duration of each phase.

**7. EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) (1 mark):**
Inert exhaust gas dilutes the charge → **lowers adiabatic flame temperature** → **less NOx**. But it also **reduces oxygen concentration** → fuel-rich pockets → **more PM, more smoke, slight BSFC penalty**. Typical EGR rates: 5–25% depending on operating point.

**Conclusion:**
CI combustion is a **multi-parameter optimization problem**. Modern engines use **electronic control of injection pressure, timing, EGR, and VGT (Variable Geometry Turbocharger)** to simultaneously meet BS-VI/Euro-6 emissions and acceptable noise, while delivering > 42% thermal efficiency. **(Bonus 0.5 mark for integration/commentary)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **(a) [7 marks] With the help of a heat release rate diagram, explain the four phases of combustion in a CI engine. How is the rate of pressure rise (dP/dθ) related to diesel knock?**

**Model Solution:**

**The Four Phases of CI Combustion:**

**Phase 1 — Ignition Delay (1.5 marks):**
From **Start of Injection (SOI)** to **Start of Combustion (SOC)**. During this period, fuel is injected, atomized, vaporized, and mixed with hot compressed air. Very little energy is released, but **fuel accumulates** in the chamber. The end of this phase is marked by **cool flame reactions** and finally hot ignition. Duration: **0.5–2 ms** in modern engines. This is the **NO-knock period**.

**Phase 2 — Premixed Combustion (1.5 marks):**
At SOC, the accumulated fuel-air mixture burns **almost instantaneously** in an uncontrolled, volumetric reaction. The heat release rate (HRR) shows a **sharp spike**. This phase is responsible for:
- The **highest dP/dθ** (rate of pressure rise).
- The **combustion knock** sound.
- A large fraction of **NOx formation** (peak temperature).

Mathematically:
$$
\left(\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} = \frac{P_{max} - P_{SOC}}{\theta_{max} - \theta_{SOC}}
$$

**Phase 3 — Diffusion Combustion (1.5 marks):**
After the premixed fuel is consumed, combustion continues as a **rate-controlled diffusion process** where fuel and air mix at the flame front. HRR is **moderate and sustained**. This is the **major soot/PM formation phase** because local fuel-rich pockets exist. Most of the useful work is produced here.

**Phase 4 — Afterburn (1 mark):**
Late-cycle combustion of unburned fuel and CO as the piston descends. HRR tapers off. This phase affects the **expansion line** and therefore the indicated work. Long afterburn reduces efficiency.

**Relation Between dP/dθ and Diesel Knock (1.5 marks):**
The combustion knock sound is essentially a **mechanical vibration induced by the high-frequency pressure oscillations** during the premixed phase. The louder the knock, the higher the dP/dθ. Acceptance limits:
- **Acceptable**: dP/dθ ≤ 6 bar/°CA.
- **Objectionable**: dP/dθ > 8 bar/°CA.
- **Severe knock**: dP/dθ > 12 bar/°CA (mechanical damage risk).

Knock reduction techniques:
1. **Pilot injection** (most effective).
2. Increase injection pressure.
3. Use higher CN fuel.
4. Retard SOI slightly.
5. Add EGR.

**(0.5 mark for neat HRR diagram + 0.5 mark for conclusion)**

#### **(b) [7 marks] Explain the various emissions from a CI engine. Discuss the formation mechanism of NOx and PM, and explain the NOx–PM trade-off with suitable diagram. Mention any two after-treatment devices.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. CI Engine Emissions (1 mark):**
A CI engine emits **NOx, PM (soot), CO, HC, CO₂**, and to a lesser extent **SO₂** (from sulfur in fuel). Due to lean operation, **CO and HC emissions are low**, but **NOx and PM are high** compared to a stoichiometric SI engine.

**2. NOx Formation — Extended Zeldovich Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
Thermal NOx is formed when in-cylinder gas temperature exceeds **1,800 K** via:
1. $O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N$ (slow, rate-limiting)
2. $N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O$
3. $N + OH \rightarrow NO + H$

The rate is highly sensitive to T (Arrhenius-type: ~doubled for every 50 K rise). Peak in-cylinder T in a diesel can reach **2,500 K**, leading to high NOx. NOx increases with **higher load, advanced timing, low EGR, and high CR**.

**3. PM (Soot) Formation — Diffusion Burn Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
PM is formed in **fuel-rich zones** of the diffusion flame where there is insufficient oxygen:
1. **Nucleation**: Pyrolysis of fuel forms small PAH (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons) particles.
2. **Surface growth**: PAHs grow by adding more fuel molecules.
3. **Aggregation**: Particles coagulate into chains.
4. **Oxidation**: Some soot burns out if mixed with air; residual soot is emitted.

PM increases with **high load, retarded timing, high EGR, low injection pressure, and poor atomization**.

**4. The NOx–PM Trade-Off (1.5 marks):**
This is the central problem in diesel calibration:

```
                Effect of Various Levers on NOx and PM

   Lever                          NOx       PM
   --------------------------------------------------------------
   Increase EGR rate              DECREASES INCREASES
   Advance SOI                    INCREASES DECREASES
   Increase injection pressure    -         DECREASES
   Increase EGR cooling           DECREASES INCREASES
   Use high Cetane Number         DECREASES -
```

The thermodynamic reason: any lever that **lowers peak flame temperature** (e.g., EGR) reduces thermal NOx but **starves the diffusion flame of oxygen**, producing more soot. Conversely, any lever that **enriches the mixture locally** (e.g., retarded injection) reduces NOx (less premixed burn) but increases soot.

**5. After-Treatment Devices (1.5 marks):**

**a) Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):**
A wall-flow ceramic filter (typically silicon carbide or cordierite) with alternating plugged channels. Exhaust must pass through the porous wall; PM is trapped. Periodically, **DPF regeneration** is required — the ECU commands an after-injection to raise exhaust T above **600 °C**, oxidizing the trapped soot into CO₂.

**b) Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):**
A catalytic converter (usually V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂ or Cu-zeolite) that reduces NOx using **urea (AdBlue/DEF)** as a reducing agent:
$$
4 NO + 2 (NH_2)_2 CO + O_2 \rightarrow 4 N_2 + 4 H_2O + 2 CO_2
$$
Modern SCR systems can achieve > 95% NOx reduction, enabling BS-VI compliance.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The NOx–PM trade-off cannot be solved in-cylinder alone. **Mechanism-based in-cylinder reduction (EGR, pilot injection, rate shaping) + after-treatment (DPF + SCR)** is the modern holistic approach.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: CI Engine Combustion, Noise & Emissions**

- [x] **CI engines** auto-ignite fuel by compressing air to **>750 K**; no spark plug.
- [x] **CRDi** is the modern mechatronic injection system; rail pressure **1,600–2,500 bar**.
- [x] **Three main sub-systems**: High-Pressure Pump, Common Rail, ECU-driven Injectors.
- [x] **Multi-pulse injection**: Pilot (reduces noise) + Main (power) + After (DPF regen).
- [x] **8 combustion parameters**: injection pressure, SOI, spray pattern, CN, viscosity, CR, load/speed, EGR.
- [x] **Ignition delay** is the crank-angle interval between SOI and SOC — it is **THE** key parameter for diesel knock.
- [x] **dP/dθ (rate of pressure rise)** is the **direct metric** for combustion knock noise.
- [x] **Pilot injection** is the **best modern method** to reduce diesel knock.
- [x] **NOx** forms via Zeldovich mechanism at **T > 1,800 K** (thermal NOx).
- [x] **PM/soot** forms in **fuel-rich zones** of the diffusion flame.
- [x] **NOx–PM trade-off**: in-cylinder EGR helps NOx but hurts PM; **after-treatment** breaks the trade-off.
- [x] **DPF** removes PM by filtration + periodic regeneration at **>600 °C**.
- [x] **SCR** removes NOx using **AdBlue/DEF (urea)** — converts NOx to N₂.
- [x] **Cetane Number** for Indian diesel = **48–52**; higher = shorter delay.
- [x] **Diesel cycle efficiency**: $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$.
- [x] **Dual cycle** (more accurate for modern CI) adds constant-volume premixed burn term.
- [x] **Excess air factor (λ) = 1.3 to 2.5** in CI (always lean burn).
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR for diesel ≈ 14.5** (vs. gasoline 14.7).
- [x] **BS-VI / Euro-6 NOx limit for diesel cars = 80 mg/km**.
- [x] **Mechatronics integration**: ECU + crank/cam sensor + rail pressure sensor + solenoid injector = closed-loop control of the combustion process.

> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** For any 7-mark question on CI engines, **always structure your answer as**: (i) Definition, (ii) Working/Process, (iii) Mechatronic component, (iv) Diagram, (v) Conclusion with real-world example (e.g., Bosch CRDi in a Maruti Swift Diesel). Examiners reward **maturity and application**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pilot, Main, Advanced, Post Injection and Retarded Post Injection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT834 - AUTOMOTIVE MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pilot, Main, Advanced, Post Injection and Retarded Post Injection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Injection Strategy Family in Common-Rail Diesel Engines

In a modern electronically controlled **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)** diesel engine, fuel is *not* delivered as a single slug per cycle. The Engine Control Unit (ECU) slices the demanded fuel mass into a precisely timed **multi-event injection schedule** executed by the piezo-electric or solenoid injectors at rail pressures typically between **600 bar** and **2000 bar**. The five canonical events are **Pilot, Main, Advanced (Main), Post Injection, and Retarded Post Injection**. Each event is defined by four ECU-controlled parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Start of Injection (timing) | SOI | –30° to +60° aTDC |
| Injection Quantity per event | Q | 1 to 80 mm³/stroke |
| Rail Pressure | p_rail | 600 – 2000 bar |
| Number of events | N_ev | 1 to 9 (latest systems) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *"Explain pilot, main, advanced, post and retarded post injection in CRDI engines with reference to combustion, emissions and aftertreatment."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "A Symphony of Pulses"

Imagine you are blowing out **100 birthday candles** on a cake:
- **Pilot injection** is like gently *warming up the air* around the candles with a tiny breath — the room gets oxygen-rich, so when the real flame-killing gust comes, it works silently and smoothly.
- **Main injection** is the *powerful main gust* — the actual combustion that delivers torque.
- **Advanced main injection** is delivering that gust *earlier* (before everyone leans in to blow), so the room has more time to push the candle flames out completely.
- **Post injection** is a small *follow-up breath* thrown *while the candles are still cooling*, reheating the exhaust gases to burn soot stuck on a filter.
- **Retarded post injection** is the *very last, very tiny breath* thrown at almost the exit door — it sacrifices a little fuel just to make sure the exhaust is hot enough to clean the catalytic trap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics highlighted for KTU Examiners:** Rail pressure **1800 bar**, SOI precision **±0.1° CA**, Pilot quantity **2–4 mm³/stroke**, Post injection heat window **300–600° C** for DPF regeneration.

---

### 1.3 Quick-Reference Definitions Box

> [!DEFINITION]
> - **Pilot Injection:** A small, low-energy fuel pulse (≈ 2–4 mm³) delivered *before* the main event to preheat the combustion chamber, reduce ignition delay of the main pulse, and lower peak pressure rise rate (dP/dθ).
> - **Main Injection:** The principal fuel mass event that produces the dominant pressure rise and torque output.
> - **Advanced (Main) Injection:** Main event *timed earlier* (greater °BTDC value) to extend the premixed burn fraction, improve thermal efficiency, and lower smoke.
> - **Post Injection:** A secondary fuel pulse occurring *during the expansion stroke* (typically 60°–140° aTDC) used primarily to raise exhaust temperature for Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) regeneration.
> - **Retarded Post Injection:** A very late post pulse (typically 180°–240° aTDC, near exhaust valve opening) used to maximise tail-pipe exhaust temperature with minimum torque penalty.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pilot Injection — The "Whisper Before the Storm"

#### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

1. The ECU commands the injector to open at a crank angle $\theta_p$ between **30° BTDC and 10° BTDC**.
2. A *small* metered quantity $Q_p$ (typically 2–4 mm³) is injected at a moderate rail pressure (400–800 bar for the pilot).
3. This small fuel charge undergoes a **delayed ignition** of its own (auto-ignition after 4–8° CA), producing a mild pressure bump.
4. The combustion products and elevated local temperature create a **thermal cushion** that *shortens* the ignition delay of the upcoming main injection.

#### 2.1.2 Benefits (The "Why")

- Reduces the **peak rate of pressure rise** $\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}$ — directly lowering combustion noise (1–3 dB(A) reduction).
- Lowers peak in-cylinder temperature → reduces **NOₓ** by 15–30 %.
- Improves cold-start HC and smoke stability.
- Pilot ratio $r_p = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_{total}}$ is typically **0.02 – 0.07**.

#### 2.1.3 Drawbacks
- Excess pilot quantity → *diesel knock* (audible combustion harshness).
- Excess pilot timing advance → heat loss to walls, increased HC.

### 2.2 Main Injection — The Torque Producer

This is the *energy-delivery event*. The ECU calculates the main mass $Q_m$ from the accelerator pedal position, engine speed, and torque demand:

$$Q_{total} = Q_p + Q_m + Q_{post} + Q_{ret-post}$$

The main event is governed by the **Wiebe heat-release law** for the premixed and diffusion phases:

$$\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = \frac{a}{\Delta\theta_b}(m+1)\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_{ig}}{\Delta\theta_b}\right)^m \exp\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta - \theta_{ig}}{\Delta\theta_b}\right)^{m+1}\right]$$

where $x_b$ = burned mass fraction, $\theta_{ig}$ = ignition crank angle, $\Delta\theta_b$ = burn duration, $m$ = shape factor (Wiebe), $a$ = efficiency factor (≈ 5 for SI, 6.9 for CI).

### 2.3 Advanced (Main) Injection — Moving the Main Event Earlier

"Advanced" refers to **advancing the SOI** (larger °BTDC value). Effects:

| Parameter | When SOI is Advanced |
|---|---|
| Peak pressure | ↑ Increases |
| Indicated thermal efficiency | ↑ Improves up to optimum |
| Smoke / PM | ↓ Decreases (more time for mixing) |
| NOx | ↑ Increases (higher T_peak) |
| BSFC | ↓ Improves (optimum) |
| Engine-out HC | ↑ Slight increase (wall quench) |

There exists an **optimum SOI** for every (speed, load) point that maximises efficiency without exceeding the **smoke limit** or **NOₓ** limit. The ECU stores this in a 16-bit calibration map: **SOI_opt = f(n, BMEP)**.

### 2.4 Post Injection — Heating the Exhaust

#### 2.4.1 Why Post Injection?
The DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) must reach **~ 600 °C** to passively/actively burn trapped soot. Tail-pipe exhaust gas is normally only 200–400 °C during cruise. So a small extra fuel dose is injected, which:

1. Partially combusts in-cylinder (extra energy release during expansion).
2. Survives combustion and reacts across the **DOC (Diesel Oxidation Catalyst)** as **HC + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O**, releasing heat into the exhaust.
3. The hot gas then flows through the DPF, raising its temperature to **> 600 °C** — soot oxidation rate follows Arrhenius:

$$r_{soot} = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_{DPF}}\right) \cdot C_{soot}$$

#### 2.4.2 Engine Penalty of Post Injection
- **Increases BSFC** by 3–8 % during active regeneration.
- **Slightly lowers torque** because some post fuel is unburned.
- **Late post** is closer to exhaust valve opening → more HC slip → DOC works harder.

### 2.5 Retarded Post Injection — Maximising Tail-Pipe Temperature, Minimising Torque Loss

Occurring very late (180°–240° aTDC), most of this fuel does *not* combust in-cylinder; it travels across the **DOC** as unburned HC and gets oxidised, releasing heat *downstream* of the cylinder. This is the preferred strategy when the engine is under light load but DPF regeneration is required.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_p = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_{total}}$ | Pilot mass ratio | – |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\big|_{max} \le 6\text{ bar/°CA}$ | Combustion noise limit | bar/°CA |
| 3 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | g/kWh |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{P_{ind}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | Indicated thermal efficiency | – |
| 5 | $r_{soot} = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Soot oxidation Arrhenius rate | g/s |
| 6 | $T_{ex} \approx T_{cyc} - \Delta T_{cool}$ | Tail-pipe temperature approx | K |
| 7 | $\text{NO}_x \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{NO}}{RT_{flame}}\right)$ | Zeldovich thermal NOx | ppm |
| 8 | $\Delta\theta_{id} = f(T_{cyl},\,p_{cyl})$ | Ignition delay (correlates with Cetane) | °CA |
| 9 | $Q_{inj} = A_{nozzle}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta p}{\rho_f}} \cdot \Delta t$ | Injector flow equation | mm³ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bosch, Delphi, Denso, Continental** CRDI systems all use these 5 event types in their production ECU calibrations.
- Critical for **BS-VI / Euro VI** emission compliance (Indian passenger cars from **April 2020** onwards).
- Enables **OBD-II** detection of DPF failure via post-injection fuel quantity monitoring.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pilot Injection Reduces Peak Pressure Rise Rate

**Step 1 — Without pilot injection**, the main fuel mass $Q_m$ enters at SOI = θ_m and ignites after an **ignition delay** $\Delta\theta_{id}$. The accumulated premixed fuel mass burns almost instantaneously:

$$\left.\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right|_{max,\,no-pilot} \approx \frac{\gamma \cdot P_0 \cdot Q_m}{V_0 \cdot \Delta\theta_{id}}$$

**Step 2 — With pilot injection**, a small charge $Q_p$ ignites first and produces a pre-pressure $P_p$. The main charge now has a *shorter* delay $\Delta\theta_{id}'$ because of the elevated $T_p$ and $P_p$:

$$\Delta\theta_{id}' = \Delta\theta_{id} \cdot \left(\frac{P_0}{P_p}\right)^{n_1} \cdot \left(\frac{T_0}{T_p}\right)^{n_2}$$

where $n_1 \approx 1.5$ and $n_2 \approx 1.0$ are Arrhenius-type exponents.

**Step 3 — The new maximum pressure rise rate** is:

$$\left.\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right|_{max,\,with-pilot} = \frac{\gamma \cdot P_p \cdot (Q_m - Q_{p,reacted})}{\;V_p \cdot \Delta\theta_{id}'\;}$$

**Step 4 — Take the ratio** of step 3 to step 1. After cancelling cylinder-volume terms and noting that $Q_{p,reacted} \ll Q_m$:

$$\frac{(dP/d\theta)_{with}}{(dP/d\theta)_{without}} = \left(\frac{P_p}{P_0}\right)^{1-n_1} \cdot \left(\frac{T_0}{T_p}\right)^{n_2}$$

**Step 5 — Numerically**, for a typical diesel at 1500 rpm with pilot raising $T$ by 80 K and $P$ by 4 bar:

$$\text{Ratio} = \left(\frac{46}{42}\right)^{1-1.5} \cdot \left(\frac{850}{930}\right)^{1.0} \approx (1.095)^{-0.5}(0.914) \approx 0.873$$

So pressure rise rate drops by **≈ 13 %**, directly reducing combustion noise.

> [!NOTE]
> The $P_p / P_0$ exponents carry a *negative* sign when $n_1 > 1$, confirming pilot pressure *rise* paradoxically *lowers* peak dP/dθ — because it shortens the catastrophic premixed burst.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Engine Response to Each Injection Event

| Performance Metric | Pilot ↑ | Main SOI Advanced ↑ | Post Injection ↑ | Retarded Post ↑ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak Cylinder Pressure | ↓ slightly | ↑ significantly | ↓ slightly | ↓ (negligible) |
| Pressure Rise Rate (dP/dθ) | **↓ strongly** | ↑ | ↓ | ≈ 0 |
| BSFC | ≈ 0 (small ↑) | **↓ improves** | ↑ 3–8 % | ↑ 1–3 % |
| NOx | ↓ 15–30 % | **↑ strongly** | ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 |
| Smoke (PM) | ≈ 0 | **↓ strongly** | ↓ slightly | ↓ slightly |
| Combustion Noise | **↓ 1–3 dB(A)** | ↑ | ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 |
| HC Emissions | ↑ at low load | ↑ (wall quench) | **↑ strongly** | **↑↑ strongly** |
| CO Emissions | ≈ 0 | ↑ slightly | ↑ | ↑ |
| Exhaust Temp | ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 | **↑ to ~ 600 °C** | **↑↑ to > 650 °C** |
| DPF Regeneration | none | none | **effective** | **most effective** |
| Engine Torque | ≈ 0 | ↑ (until optimum) | ↓ 1–3 % | ↓ < 1 % |

### 3.3 Energy Balance Around Post Injection (DPF Heating)

**Step 1** — Post injection energy content:

$$E_{post} = Q_{post} \cdot \rho_f \cdot \text{LHV}_f \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

**Step 2** — Fraction $f_{DOC}$ oxidised across DOC (0.6 – 0.9 for HC at T_DOC > 250 °C):

$$E_{ox} = f_{DOC} \cdot E_{post}$$

**Step 3** — Heat transferred to DPF substrate:

$$Q_{DPF} = E_{ox} - Q_{loss} = h_{conv}\,A_{DPF}(T_{gas} - T_{DPF})\,t_{reg}$$

**Step 4** — DPF temperature rise:

$$\Delta T_{DPF} = \frac{Q_{DPF}}{m_{DPF} \cdot c_{p,\,DPF}}$$

This is how retarded post injection *quantitatively* drives DPF regeneration.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** Define **pilot injection** in a CRDI diesel engine and state two benefits.

> **Model Answer:**
> Pilot injection is a small fuel pulse (typically 2–4 mm³) injected by the ECU into the combustion chamber *before* the main injection event, usually between 30° BTDC and 10° BTDC at a lower rail pressure of 400–800 bar. The pilot quantity auto-ignites first and produces a mild pressure rise that elevates local in-cylinder temperature and pressure.
>
> **Two benefits:**
> 1. It shortens the ignition delay of the main injection, reducing peak rate of pressure rise (dP/dθ) and therefore combustion noise by 1–3 dB(A).
> 2. It lowers peak in-cylinder temperature, reducing thermal NOx formation by 15–30 %.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** What is the function of **retarded post injection** in modern diesel engines?

> **Model Answer:**
> Retarded post injection is a small fuel pulse delivered very late in the expansion stroke, typically between 180° aTDC and 240° aTDC, near the exhaust valve opening event. Its primary function is to introduce unburned hydrocarbons into the exhaust stream so that the **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** oxidises them exothermically, raising tail-pipe exhaust temperature to **> 600 °C**. This temperature window enables active regeneration of the **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** by oxidising accumulated soot. Retarded post injection is preferred over earlier post injection because it delivers maximum exhaust heating with **minimum torque penalty** (< 1 %) since most of the fuel does not combust in-cylinder.

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

> **Question A (14 marks):**
> **(a)** With the help of a crank-angle timing diagram, explain the **five events** — pilot, main, advanced, post and retarded post injection — in a common-rail diesel engine. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss how each of these injection events influences **combustion noise, BSFC, NOx and DPF regeneration**. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — Timing Diagram & Event Description (7 marks)**

| Event | SOI Range (° CA) | Quantity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pilot | 30 BTDC → 10 BTDC | 2–4 mm³ | Pre-condition chamber |
| Main (Advanced) | 15 BTDC → 5 BTDC | 30–70 mm³ | Deliver torque |
| Post | 60 aTDC → 140 aTDC | 4–8 mm³ | Heat exhaust |
| Retarded Post | 180 aTDC → 240 aTDC | 2–4 mm³ | Maximise T_ex |

*Timing diagram reference:* see Section 4.1 of these notes.

The events are triggered sequentially by the **ECU event scheduler**. The injector is a solenoid- or piezo-actuated 7-orifice nozzle; rail pressure is held constant at 600–2000 bar throughout. The pilot and main are *in-cylinder combustion events*, while post and retarded post partially combust in-cylinder and partially release heat across the DOC.

**Part (b) — Influence on Parameters (7 marks)**

- **Combustion noise:** Pilot **strongly reduces** dP/dθ (≈ 13 % from Section 3.1). Advanced main *increases* dP/dθ slightly. Post and retarded post have negligible noise effect.
- **BSFC:** Advanced main injection **lowers** BSFC by 5–10 % at part load due to longer premixed burn. Post injection raises BSFC by 3–8 % (regeneration penalty). Retarded post raises BSFC by 1–3 %.
- **NOx:** Pilot reduces NOx by 15–30 %; advanced main *increases* NOx (higher T_peak); post/retarded post have negligible direct NOx effect but improve aftertreatment efficiency.
- **DPF regeneration:** Achieved almost exclusively by post and retarded post injection; retarded post is **most effective** because most unburned HC reaches the DOC, producing peak exhaust temperature > 650 °C and triggering soot oxidation per Arrhenius kinetics.

---

> **Question B (14 marks, alternative choice):**
> **(a)** Derive the relationship showing how pilot injection **reduces peak rate of pressure rise** in a diesel engine. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram how the **ECU schedules the multiple injection events** in a CRDI system. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)** — *Direct reference: Section 3.1 of these notes, fully worked.*

Key result obtained:

$$\frac{(dP/d\theta)_{with}}{(dP/d\theta)_{without}} = \left(\frac{P_p}{P_0}\right)^{1-n_1} \cdot \left(\frac{T_0}{T_p}\right)^{n_2}$$

With $n_1 \approx 1.5$, $n_2 \approx 1.0$, a typical pilot gives $P_p/P_0 = 46/42$ and $T_p/T_0 = 930/850$, yielding a **13 % reduction in dP/dθ** — directly lowering combustion noise.

**Part (b) — ECU Scheduling (7 marks)** — *Direct reference: Section 4.2 of these notes.*

The ECU executes the following loop every 10 ms:

1. **Read inputs:** APP (%), engine speed (rpm), coolant temp (°C), rail pressure (bar), MAF (kg/h), torque demand (Nm).
2. **Look up maps:** total quantity Q_total from main map, optimum SOI from advance map, pilot quantity from pilot map, post quantity from DPF-temperature map.
3. **Compute event schedule:** SOI_pilot, SOI_main, SOI_post, SOI_retarded — all relative to a fixed TDC reference.
4. **Drive actuators:** rail-pressure control valve (SCV) regulates pump; injector driver (boost converter) commands piezo/solenoid injector at scheduled SOIs.
5. **Close loop:** monitor cylinder pressure sensor (if present), O₂ in exhaust, DPF differential pressure → adjust next cycle's maps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (HIGH WEIGHTAGE):**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "Advanced Injection" with the *advance of timing*. "Advanced" is simply an alternative name for *main injection timed early*; many students lose 2 marks by treating it as a separate *fifth* event.
> - **Do NOT write** absolute value `|x|` inside markdown tables — this *breaks table rendering* and the examiner may not award method marks. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`.
> - **Always mention** specific numeric values (e.g., rail pressure **1800 bar**, pilot quantity **2–4 mm³**) — vague answers lose marks under KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment.
> - **Always link** post/retarded post injection to **DPF regeneration**, not just "to reduce emissions" — generic statements fetch only 1/7 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Pilot injection** = small pre-pulse; **reduces** dP/dθ, NOx, noise.
- ✅ **Main injection** = torque producer; quantity maps with (n, BMEP).
- ✅ **Advanced injection** = main event *moved earlier*; improves BSFC, lowers smoke, **raises** NOx.
- ✅ **Post injection** = expansion stroke pulse; raises T_ex; **enables DPF regeneration**.
- ✅ **Retarded post injection** = very late pulse near EVO; **maximum** T_ex, **minimum** torque penalty.
- ✅ Rail pressure **600–2000 bar**; pilot ratio **0.02–0.07**.
- ✅ DPF regeneration needs **> 600 °C**; Soot oxidation follows **Arrhenius** law.
- ✅ DOC oxidises HC from post injection → heat → DPF soot burn-off.
- ✅ ECU scheduling is **closed-loop, map-based, multi-event**.
- ✅ Wiebe function $x_b(\theta)$ describes premixed + diffusion burn phases.
- ✅ Optimum SOI exists for every (n, BMEP); ECU stores 16-bit calibration map.
- ✅ Key exam trap: Advanced ≠ a separate event — it is the **timing state** of the main event.
